revision.comment: /* Sample Lagerstätten */ [[User:Monkbot/task 21: Replace page(s) with article-number|Monkbot/task 21: Replace page(s) with article-number]];
revision.text:
{{Short description|Sedimentary deposit with well-preserved extraordinary fossils}} {{Use dmy dates|date=April 2020}} [[File:Opabinia_smithsonian.JPG|thumb|Well-preserved basal arthropod ''[[Opabinia]]'' from [[Burgess Shale]] ''Lagerstätte'' (Middle [[Cambrian]]) ]] {{Paleontology}} A '''Fossil-Lagerstätte''' ({{IPA|de|ˈlaːɡɐˌʃtɛtə}} {{endash}} from ''[[wikt:Lager|Lager]]'' 'storage, lair' and ''[[wikt:Stätte|Stätte]]'' 'place'; {{plural form}} {{lang|de|'''Lagerstätten'''}}) is a [[Stratigraphic unit|sedimentary deposit]] that preserves an exceptionally high amount of [[Paleontology|palaeontological]] information.{{cite journal| last=Shields |first=Graham |title=What are Lagerstätten |journal=Lethaia |year=1998 |volume=31 |issue=2 |page=124 |doi=10.1111/j.1502-3931.1998.tb00498.x |bibcode=1998Letha..31..124S |url=https://www.idunn.no/doi/pdf/10.1111/j.1502-3931.1998.tb00498.x}} '''''Konzentrat-Lagerstätten''''' preserve a high concentration of [[fossil]]s, while '''''Konservat-Lagerstätten''''' offer exceptional fossil preservation, sometimes including preserved soft tissues. ''Konservat-Lagerstätten'' may have resulted from carcass burial in an [[hypoxia (environmental)|anoxic]] environment with minimal bacteria, thus delaying the decomposition of both gross and fine biological features until long after a durable impression was created in the surrounding matrix. ''Fossil-Lagerstätten'' span [[geology|geological]] time from the [[Neoproterozoic]] era to the [[Holocene|present]]. Worldwide, some of the best examples of near-perfect fossilization are the [[Cambrian]] [[Maotianshan shales]] and [[Burgess Shale]], the [[Ordovician]] [[Fezouata|Fezouata Biota]], [[Beecher's Trilobite Bed]], and [[Soom Shale]], the [[Silurian]] [[Waukesha Biota]], the [[Devonian]] [[Hunsrück Slates]] and [[Gogo Formation]], the [[Carboniferous]] [[Mazon Creek fossil beds|Mazon Creek]], the [[Triassic]] [[Madygen Formation]], the [[Jurassic]] [[Posidonia Shale]] and [[Solnhofen Limestone]], the [[Cretaceous]] [[Yixian Formation|Yixian]], [[Santana Formation|Santana]], and [[Agua Nueva Formation|Agua Nueva]] formations and the [[Tanis (fossil site)|Tanis Fossil Site]], the [[Eocene]] [[Fur Formation]], [[Green River Formation]], [[Messel Formation]] and [[Monte Bolca]], the [[Miocene]] [[Foulden Maar]] and [[Ashfall Fossil Beds]], the [[Pliocene]] [[Gray Fossil Site]], and the [[Pleistocene]] [[Naracoorte Caves]] and [[La Brea Tar Pits]]. ==Types== [[Palaeontologist]]s distinguish two major kinds:{{refn| group=nb|Additionally, some authors have proposed the usage of a third type of ''Lagerstätten: "'''Liberation Lagerstätten'''".'' This proposed type of ''Lagerstätten'' has had little acknowledgement or acceptance within the rest of the literature, however. These sites are characterized by a potential high number of fossils and good preservation, but low-grade lithification. In other words, sites that undergone little [[diagenesis]] during the fossilization process.{{Cite journal |last1=Ernst |first1=Andrej |last2=Claussen |first2=Anna Lene |last3=Suess |first3=Barbara |last4=Jackson |first4=Patrick N. Wyse |date=2022-05-23 |title=Stenolaemate bryozoans from the Graham Formation, Pennsylvanian (Virgilian) at Lost Creek Lake, Texas, USA |url=https://palaeo-electronica.org/content/2022/3608-pennsylvanian-bryozoan-fauna |access-date=2025-10-07 |journal=Palaeontologia Electronica |doi=10.26879/1174 |language=en|doi-access=free }}}}The term was originally coined by [[Adolf Seilacher]] in: {{cite journal | author = Seilacher, A. | year = 1970 | title = ''Begriff und Bedeutung der Fossil-Lagerstätten: Neues Jahrbuch fur Geologie und Paläontologie'' | journal = Monatshefte | volume = 1970 | pages = 34–39 | language=de }}The term was redefined by [[Julien Kimmig]] and [[James D. Schiffbauer]] in: {{cite journal |last=Kimmig |first=Julien |author2=James D. Schiffbauer |date=25 April 2024 |title=A modern definition of Fossil-Lagerstätten |url=https://www.cell.com/trends/ecology-evolution/abstract/S0169-5347(24)00086-7 |journal=[[Trends in Ecology and Evolution]] |volume=39 |issue=6 |pages=621–624 |bibcode=2024TEcoE..39..621K |doi=10.1016/j.tree.2024.04.004 |pmid=38670863 |url-access=subscription}} # '''''Konzentrat-Lagerstätten''''' (concentration ''Lagerstätten'') are deposits with a particular "concentration" of disarticulated organic hard parts, such as a bone bed. These ''Lagerstätten'' are less spectacular than the more famous ''Konservat-Lagerstätten''. Their contents invariably display a large degree of time averaging, as the accumulation of bones in the absence of other sediment takes some time. Deposits with a high concentration of fossils that represent an ''in situ'' community, such as [[reef]]s or [[oyster]] beds, are not considered ''Lagerstätten''. # '''''Konservat-Lagerstätten''''' (conservation ''Lagerstätten'') are deposits known for the exceptional preservation of fossilized organisms or traces. The individual [[taphonomy]] of the fossils varies with the sites. Conservation ''Lagerstätten'' are crucial in elucidating important moments in the history and [[evolution]] of life. For example, the [[Burgess Shale]] of British Columbia is associated with the [[Cambrian explosion]], and the [[Solnhofen limestone]] with the earliest known [[bird]], ''[[Archaeopteryx]]''. ==Preservation== ''Konservat-Lagerstätten'' preserve lightly [[Sclerosis (medicine)|sclerotized]] and soft-bodied organisms or traces of organisms that are not otherwise preserved in the usual shelly and bony fossil record; thus, they offer more complete records of ancient biodiversity and behavior and enable some reconstruction of the [[palaeoecology]] of ancient aquatic communities. In 1986, [[Simon Conway Morris]] calculated only about 14% of genera in the Burgess Shale had possessed [[Biomineralisation|biomineralized]] tissues in life. The affinities of the shelly elements of [[conodont]]s were mysterious until the associated soft tissues were discovered near Edinburgh, Scotland, in the Granton Lower [[Oil Shale]] of the [[Carboniferous]].Briggs et al. 1983; Aldridge et al. 1993.{{full citation needed|date=January 2022}} Information from the broader range of organisms found in ''Lagerstätten'' have contributed to recent [[phylogenetic]] reconstructions of some major [[metazoan]] groups. ''Lagerstätten'' seem to be temporally autocorrelated, perhaps because global environmental factors such as climate might affect their deposition.{{Cite journal | last1 = Retallack | first1 = G. J. | doi = 10.1016/j.palaeo.2011.04.023 | title = Exceptional fossil preservation during CO2 greenhouse crises? | journal = [[Palaeogeography, Palaeoclimatology, Palaeoecology]] | year = 2011 | volume=307 | issue = 1–4 | pages=59–74| bibcode = 2011PPP...307...59R }} A number of [[taphonomic]] pathways may produce ''Konservat-Lagerstätten'':{{Cite journal |last1=Kimmig |first1=Julien |last2=Schiffbauer |first2=James D. |date=2024 |title=A modern definition of Fossil-Lagerstätten |url=https://linkinghub.elsevier.com/retrieve/pii/S0169534724000867 |journal=Trends in Ecology & Evolution |volume=39 |issue=7 |pages=621–624 |language=en |doi=10.1016/j.tree.2024.04.004|pmid=38670863 |bibcode=2024TEcoE..39..621K |url-access=subscription }} * [[Phosphatization]] (replacing soft tissues with [[Phosphate mineral|phosphate]], such as [[Orsten|Orsten-type]] and [[Doushantuo type preservation|Doushantuo-type preservations]]). * [[Silicification]] (replacing or entombing soft tissues with silica, such as [[petrified wood]] or [[Bitter Springs type preservation|Bitter Springs-type preservation]]). * Kerogenization (soft tissues converted into inert [[Carbonaceous film (paleontology)|carbonaceous films]], as found in [[Burgess Shale type preservation|Burgess Shale-type preservation]]). * [[Aluminosilification]] (replacing or coating soft tissues with films of [[aluminosilicate]] minerals). * [[Pyritization]] (replacing soft tissues with [[pyrite]], such as the exquisite detail found in [[Beecher's Trilobite type preservation|Beecher's trilobite-type preservation]]). * Calcification (replacing or soft tissues with [[calcite]] minerals). * [[Siderite]] or calcite [[Nodule (geology)|nodules]] (chemicals released by decaying soft tissue modify the surrounding sediment into a siderite [[concretion]] or [[coal ball]] encasing the fossil, as found in the [[Mazon Creek fossil beds]]). * Rapid sediment [[Cementation (geology)|cementation]] (impressions of soft tissue preserved through [[Fossil#Casts and molds|casts and molds]] in the surrounding sediment, such as [[Ediacaran type preservation|Ediacaran-type preservation]] facilitated by [[microbial mat]]s). * [[Amber]] (soft tissues encased in hardened tree resin). The identification of a fossil site as a ''Konservat-Lagerstätte'' may be based on a number of different factors which constitute "exceptional preservation". These may include the completeness of specimens, soft tissue preservation, fine-scale detail, [[Taxonomy|taxonomic]] richness, distinctive taphonomic pathways (often multiple at the same site), the extent of the fossil layer in time and space, and particular sediment [[facies]] encouraging preservation. ==Sample Lagerstätten== {{Main list|List of lagerstätten}} {| class="wikitable" align="center" width="100%" |- !Site(s) !Age !Location !Significance !Notable fossils/organisms |- | [[Doushantuo Formation]] | 600–555 Ma | [[Guizhou|Guizhou Province]], {{Flag|China}} | Spans the poorly understood interval between the end of the [[Cryogenian]] [[geological period|period]] and the late Ediacaran [[Avalon explosion]]. |[[File:Ediacaran embryo-like fossil.jpg|thumb|center|upright|An Ediacaran [[embryo]]-like fossil]] |- | [[Mistaken Point]] (including the [[Drook Formation|Drook]], [[Briscal Formation|Briscal]], [[Mistaken Point Formation|Mistaken Point]], [[Trepassey Formation|Trepassey]], and [[Fermeuse Formation|Fermeuse]] Formations.){{Cite journal |last1=Liu |first1=Alexander G. |last2=Matthews |first2=Jack J. |date=2017-07-21 |title=Great Canadian Lagerstätten 6. Mistaken Point Ecological Reserve, Southeast Newfoundland |url=https://journals.lib.unb.ca/index.php/GC/article/view/25296 |journal=Geoscience Canada |volume=44 |issue=2 |language=en |pages=63–76 |doi=10.12789/geocanj.2017.44.117 |bibcode=2017GeosC..44...63L |issn=1911-4850}} | 565 Ma | Newfoundland, {{Flag|Canada}} | This site contains one of the most diverse and well-preserved collections of [[Precambrian]] [[fossils]]. | [[File:Ediacaran fossils Mistaken Point Newfoundland.jpg|thumb|center|upright|[[Frondose]] ediacaran organisms]] |- | [[Ediacara Hills]] | 555 Ma | South {{Flag|Australia}} | The type location the [[Ediacaran]] period, and has preserved a significant amount of fossils from that time. | [[File:Spriggina floundersi - MUSE.jpg|thumb|center|upright|''[[Spriggina|Spriggina floundersi]]'', a worm-like organism]] |- |[[Maotianshan Shales]] (Chengjiang) |518 Ma |[[Yunnan]], {{Flag|China}} |The preservation of an extremely diverse faunal assemblage renders the Maotianshan Shales the world's most important formation for understanding the evolution of early [[Multicellular organism|multi-cellular]] life. Microscopic animals like ''[[Yicaris]]'' are preserved here, showing the presence of an Orsten-type deposit within the formation. This site also includes the Xiazhuang biota.{{Cite journal |last1=Yang |first1=Bing |last2=Zong |first2=Ruiwen |last3=Xia |first3=Haodong |last4=Zhang |first4=Xinzhi |last5=Li |first5=Weitong |last6=Sun |first6=Siyuan |date=2025 |title=New occurrence of the Guanshan Biota (Cambrian, Stage 4) in the Malong-Yiliang area, eastern Yunnan, South China |journal=Historical Biology |volume=0 |pages=1–11 |doi=10.1080/08912963.2025.2539302 |issn=0891-2963}}{{cite journal |last1=Zhang |first1=Xi-guang |last2=Siveter |first2=David J. |last3=Waloszek |first3=Dieter |last4=Maas |first4=Andreas |date=4 October 2007 |title=An epipodite-bearing crown-group crustacean from the Lower Cambrian |journal=Nature |volume=449 |issue=7162 |pages=595–598 |bibcode=2007Natur.449..595Z |doi=10.1038/nature06138 |pmid=17914395}} |[[File:Haikouichthys4.png|thumb|center|upright|''[[Haikouichthys]]'', a primitive [[craniate]]]] |- |[[Sirius Passet]] |523-518 Ma |[[Greenland]], {{Flag|Denmark}} |A site known for its fauna, and that they were most likely preserved by a [[Microbial mat|death mask]]. It is a part of the larger [[Buen Formation]], and has a fauna similar to the Maotianshan shales. |[[File:Kerygmachela kierkegaardi. MGUH 32054.jpg|thumb|center|upright|''[[Kerygmachela]]'', a [[lobopodia]]n-like [[Dinocaridida|dinocaridid]]]] |- |[[Burgess Shale]] |508 Ma |British Columbia, {{Flag|Canada}} |One of the most famous fossil localities in the world. It is famous for the exceptional preservation of the soft parts of its fossils. At 508 million years old ([[Wuliuan|middle Cambrian]]), it is one of the earliest fossil beds containing soft-part imprints. |[[File:Aria et al. 2020 Anomalocaris canadensis head ROMIP 51212.png|thumb|center|upright|''[[Anomalocaris]]'', a predatory [[Radiodonta|radiodont]]]] |- | [[Kinnekulle]] [[Orsten]] and [[Alum]] [[Shale]] | 500 Ma | {{Flag|Sweden}} | The Orsten sites reveals the oldest well-documented [[benthic]] [[meiofauna]] in the fossil record. Fossils such as microfossils of arthropods like free-living [[pentastomid]]s are known. Multiple "Orsten-type" ''lagerstätten'' are also known from other countries. |[[File:Cambropachycope.png|thumb|center|upright|''[[Cambropachycope]]'', a stem-group [[Mandibulata|mandibulate]] arthropod]] |- |[[Fezouata Formation]]{{cite journal |last1=Van Roy |first1=Peter |last2=Briggs |first2=Derek E. G. |last3=Gaines |first3=Robert R. |date=2015 |title=The Fezouata fossils of Morocco; an extraordinary record of marine life in the Early Ordovician |url=https://www.lyellcollection.org/doi/10.1144/jgs2015-017 |journal=Journal of the Geological Society |volume=172 |issue=5 |pages=541–549 |bibcode=2015JGSoc.172..541V |doi=10.1144/jgs2015-017 |hdl=1854/LU-8714212 |issn=0016-7649 |access-date=2025-10-14|hdl-access=free }} |about 485 Ma |Draa Valley, {{Flag|Morocco}} |It was deposited in a marine environment, and is known for its exceptionally preserved fossils, filling an important preservational window beyond the earlier and more common Cambrian [[Burgess shale-type deposits]]. |[[File:20191205 Aegirocassis benmoulai Aegirocassis benmoulae.png|thumb|center|upright|''[[Aegirocassis]]'', a giant [[hurdiid]] radiodont]] |- |[[Winneshiek Shale]] |460 Ma |[[Decorah]], [[Iowa]], {{Flag|US}} |A Middle Ordovician site confined to a large impact Crater that is known for exceptionally exquisite preservation of conodonts, bivalved arthropods, and the earliest eurypterids in the fossil record.{{cite journal |last1=Liu |first1=Huaibao P. |last2=Bergström |first2=Stig M. |last3=Witzke |first3=Brian J. |last4=Briggs |first4=Derek E. G. |last5=McKay |first5=Robert M. |last6=Ferretti |first6=Annalisa |date=28 February 2017 |title=Exceptionally preserved conodont apparatuses with giant elements from the Middle Ordovician Winneshiek Konservat-Lagerstätte, Iowa, USA |journal=[[Journal of Paleontology]] |volume=91 |issue=3 |pages=493–511 |bibcode=2017JPal...91..493L |doi=10.1017/jpa.2016.155 |s2cid=132698401 |doi-access=free|hdl=11380/1114523 |hdl-access=free }} |[[File:Eurypterids Pentecopterus Vertical.jpg|center|thumb|upright|''[[Pentecopterus]]'', the oldest known eurypterid]] |- |[[Beecher's Trilobite Bed]] |460? Ma |[[New York (state)|New York]], {{Flag|US}} |Noted exceptionally preserved trilobites with soft tissue preserved by [[pyrite]] replacement.
Pyritisation allows the use of X-rays to study fine detail of preserved soft body parts. |[[File:Triarthrus.jpg|thumb|center|upright|A pair of ''[[Triarthrus eatoni|Triarthrus]]'' trilobites with pyritized soft-tissue]] |- |[[Soom Shale]] |450? Ma |{{Flag|South Africa}} |Known for its remarkable preservation of soft-tissue in fossil material. Deposited in still waters, the unit lacks [[bioturbation]], perhaps indicating [[Anoxic event|anoxic]] conditions. |[[File:Promissum NT small.jpg|thumb|center|upright|''[[Promissum]]'', a [[conodont]] known from rare soft-tissues]] |- |[[Waukesha Biota]] ([[Brandon Bridge Formation]]) |~435 Ma
[[Early Silurian]] |[[Wisconsin]], {{Flag|US}} |Well-studied site known for the exceptional preservation of its diverse, soft-bodied and lightly skeletonized fauna, includes many major [[taxa]] found nowhere else in strata of similar age. It was one of the first fossil sites with soft bodied preservation known to science. |[[File:20211029 Parioscorpio venator.png|thumb|center|upright|''[[Parioscorpio]]'', an enigmatic arthropod]] |- |Herefordshire Lagerstätte ([[Coalbrookdale Formation]]) |~430 Ma |[[Herefordshire]], {{Flag|UK}} |Known for the well-preserved fossils of various [[Invertebrate|invertebrate animals]] many of which are in their three-dimensional structures. Fossils are preserved within volcanic ash, because of that sometimes this site has been compared to [[Pompeii]].{{Cite web |last=BBC |title=Fossils found in 425 million year old 'Pompeii' |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/herefordandworcester/content/articles/2009/06/01/herefordshire_fossils_feature.shtml |access-date=2023-10-07 |website=www.bbc.co.uk |language=en-gb}} Some of the fossils are regarded as earliest evidences and evolutionary origin of some of the major groups of modern animals. |[[File:20200810 Offacolus kingi.png|thumb|center|upright|''[[Offacolus]]'', a [[Chelicerata|euchelicerate]]]] |- |[[Bertie Group]] |422.9-416 Ma |[[Ontario]] & [[New York (state)|New York State]], {{Flag|Canada}} and {{Flag|US}} |This limestone have produced thousands of fossil [[eurypterid]]s, such as giant ''[[Acutiramus]]'' and well-known ''[[Eurypterus]]'', as well as other fauna like scorpions and fish. |[[File:Nerepisacanthus.png|thumb|center|upright|''[[Nerepisacanthus]]'', an [[acanthodian]]]] |- |[[Rhynie chert]] |400 Ma |[[Scotland]], {{Flag|UK}} |The Rhynie chert contains exceptionally preserved plant, fungus, [[lichen]] and animal material (euthycarcinoids, branchiopods, arachnids, hexapods, etc.) preserved in place by an overlying [[Volcanic rock|volcanic deposit]] and hot springs. As well as one of the first known fully terrestrial ecosystems. |[[File:Asteroxylon_life_restoration.jpg|center|thumb|200x200px|''[[Asteroxylon]]'', an early [[vascular plant]] related to [[lycopods]]]] |- |[[Hunsrück Slates]] (Bundenbach) | |Rheinland-Pfalz, {{Flag|Germany}} |The Hunsrück slates are one of the few marine Devonian ''lagerstätte'' having soft tissue preservation, and in many cases fossils are coated by a pyritic surface layer. |[[File:20210708 Schinderhannes bartelsi diagrammatic reconstruction.png|center|thumb|200x200px|''[[Schinderhannes bartelsi]]'', the youngest known radiodont]] |- |[[Gogo Formation]] |380 Ma ([[Frasnian]]) |[[Western Australia|Western]] {{Flag|Australia}} |The fossils of the Gogo Formation display three-dimensional soft-tissue preservation of tissues as fragile as nerves and embryos with umbilical cords. Over fifty species of [[fish]] have been described from the formation, and arthropods. |[[File:Materpiscis.png|center|thumb|200x200px|''[[Materpiscis]]'', a [[ptyctodontid]] [[Placodermi|placoderm]] fish that is the oldest [[vertebrate]] known to give [[Viviparity|live birth]]]] |- |[[Miguasha National Park]] ([[Escuminac Formation]]) |370 Ma |[[Quebec|Québec]], {{Flag|Canada}} |Some of the fish, fauna, and spore fossils found at Miguasha are rare and ancient species. For example, ''[[Eusthenopteron]]'' is sarcopterygian that shares characters with early [[tetrapoda|tetrapods]]. |[[File:Scaumenacia_curta_&_Bothriolepis_canadensis.JPG|center|thumb|200x200px|Fossil of lungfish ''[[Scaumenacia]]'' and [[antiarch]] placoderm ''[[Bothriolepis]]'']] |- |[[Waterloo Farm lagerstätte|Waterloo Farm Lagerstätte]] (Witpoort Formation) |360 Ma |{{Flag|South Africa}} |Important site that providing the only record of a high latitude (near [[Polar regions of Earth|polar]]) coastal ecosystem, overturning numerous assumptions about high latitude conditions during the latest Devonian. |[[File:Priscomyzon_riniensis02.jpg|center|thumb|200x200px|''[[Priscomyzon]]'', the oldest known genus of [[lamprey]]]] |- |[[East Kirkton Quarry]]{{Cite book |last1=Fraser |first1=Nicholas C. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=JysFngEACAAJ |title=Terrestrial Conservation Lagerstätten: Windows Into the Evolution of Life on Land |last2=Sues |first2=Hans-Dieter |date=2017 |publisher=Dunedin Academic Press |isbn=978-1-78046-014-7 |language=en}} |335 Ma |[[West Lothian]], [[Scotland]], {{Flag|UK}} |This site has produced numerous well-preserved fossils of early tetrapods like [[temnospondyls]] or [[Reptiliomorpha|reptiliomorphs]], and large arthropods like scorpions or eurypterids. |[[File:Silvanerpeton1DB.jpg|center|thumb|200x200px|''[[Silvanerpeton]]'', a possible [[reptiliomorph]]]] |- |[[Bear Gulch Limestone]] |324 Ma |[[Montana]], {{Flag|US}} |A [[limestone]]-rich [[Lens (geology)|geological lens]] in central [[Montana]]. It is renowned for its unusual and ecologically diverse fossil composition of [[Chondrichthyes|chondrichthyans]], the group of cartilaginous fish containing modern [[shark]]s, [[Batoidea|rays]], and [[chimaera]]s. Other animals like brachiopods, [[ray finned fish]], arthropods, and the possible mollusk ''[[Typhloesus]]'' are also known from the site. |[[File:Falcatus_falcatus_01.JPG|center|thumb|200x200px|''[[Falcatus]]'', a [[holocephalian]] which males had large fin spine]] |- |[[Joggins Fossil Cliffs]] ([[Joggins Formation]]) |315 Ma |[[Nova Scotia]], {{Flag|Canada}} |A fossil site that preserves a diverse terrestrial ecosystem consisting of plants like lycopsids, giant arthropods, fish, and the oldest known [[Sauropsida|sauropsid]], ''[[Hylonomus]]''. |[[File:Hylonomus_lyelli_-_MUSE.jpg|center|thumb|200x200px|''Hylonomus'', the oldest known sauropsid in the fossil record]] |- |[[Mazon Creek]] |310 Ma |[[Illinois]], {{Flag|US}} |A conservation ''lagerstätte'' found near [[Morris, Illinois|Morris]], in [[Grundy County, Illinois]]. The fossils from this site are preserved in [[ironstone]] [[concretion]]s with exceptional detail. The fossils were preserved in a large delta system that covered much of the area. The state fossil of Illinois, the enigmatic animal ''[[Tullimonstrum]]'', is only known from these deposits. |[[File:Tullimonstrum_gregarium_343.JPG|center|thumb|200x200px|''Tullimonstrum'', an enigmatic animal]] |- |[[Montceau-les-Mines lagerstätte|Montceau-les-Mines]] |300 Ma |{{Flag|France}} |Exceptional preservation of [[Pennsylvanian (geology)|Late Carboniferous]] fossil biota are known, including various vertebrates and arthropods, as well as plants.{{cite journal |last1=Perrier |first1=V. |last2=Charbonnier |first2=S. |year=2014 |title=The Montceau-les-Mines Lagerstätte (Late Carboniferous, France) |journal=[[Comptes Rendus Palevol]] |volume=13 |issue=5 |pages=353–67 |bibcode=2014CRPal..13..353P |doi=10.1016/j.crpv.2014.03.002}}{{Cite magazine |last1=Heyler |first1=Daniel |last2=Poplin |first2=Cecile M. |date=1988 |title=The Fossils of Montceau-les-Mines |magazine=Scientific American |pages=104–111 |volume=259 |issue=3 |bibcode=1988SciAm.259c.104H |doi=10.1038/scientificamerican0988-104 |issn=0036-8733 |jstor=24989233}} |[[File:Idmonarachne_brasieri_reconstruction.jpg|center|thumb|200x200px|''[[Idmonarachne]]'', an [[arachnid]] that is related to [[spider]]s]] |- |[[Chemnitz petrified forest]] |291 Ma |[[Saxony]], {{Flag|Germany}} |A [[Petrified wood|petrified forest]] in Germany that is composed of ''[[Arthropitys bistriata]]'', a type of [[Calamites]], giant [[horsetails]] that are ancestors of modern [[Equisetum|horsetails]], found on this location with never seen multiple branches. Many more plants and animals from this excavation are still in an ongoing research.{{cite journal |last1=Luthardt |first1=Ludwig |last2=Rößler |first2=Ronny |last3=Schneider |first3=Joerg W. |date=1 January 2016 |title=Palaeoclimatic and site-specific conditions in the early Permian fossil forest of Chemnitz—Sedimentological, geochemical and palaeobotanical evidence |url=https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0031018215005775 |journal=[[Palaeogeography, Palaeoclimatology, Palaeoecology]] |volume=441 |issue= |pages=627–652 |bibcode=2016PPP...441..627L |doi=10.1016/j.palaeo.2015.10.015 |url-access=subscription |access-date=8 June 2022}} |[[File:Bundesarchiv Bild 183-C1113-0001-001, Chemnitz, Versteinerte Bäume.jpg|center|thumb|200x200px|Large trunks of ''Arthropitys'' at Chemnitz]] |- |[[Kupferschiefer]] |259–255 Ma |(North-Central [[Europe]]) {{Flag|Denmark}}, {{Flag|Germany}}, {{Flag|Lithuania}}, {{Flag|Netherlands}}, {{Flag|Poland}}, {{Flag|Russia}} |This site deposited in an open marine and shallow marine environment provides fossils of reptiles as well as many fish. |[[File:Weigeltisaurus_reconstruction.png|center|thumb|200x200px|''[[Weigeltisaurus]]'', gliding [[weigeltisaurid]] reptile]] |- |Luoping Biota ([[Guanling Formation]]){{Cite journal |last1=Hu Ash from a [[Yellowstone hotspot]] eruption 10-12 million years ago created these fossilized [[bone bed]]s. |[[File:Ashfall Fossil Beds - Teleoceras and Cormohipparion.jpg|thumb|200x200px|A bone-bed containing the fossils of the basal rhino ''[[Teleoceras]]'' and the three-toed horse ''[[Cormohipparion]]''.|center]] |- |[[Gray Fossil Site]] |4.9-4.5 Ma |[[Tennessee]], {{Flag|US}} |As the first site of its age known from the [[Appalachia]]n region, the Gray Fossil Site is a unique window into the past. Research at the site has yielded many surprising discoveries, including new species of [[red panda]], [[rhinoceros]], [[Emydidae|pond turtle]], [[Hickory|hickory tree]], and more. The site also hosts the world's largest known assemblage of fossil [[tapir]]s. |[[File:Skull of Pristinailurus bristoli.jpg|thumb|Fossil skull of ''[[Pristinailurus]]'', a North American relative of the modern Red Panda|center]] |- |[[The Mammoth Site]] |26 [[kiloannum|Ka]] |[[South Dakota]], {{Flag|US}} |The facility encloses a prehistoric [[sinkhole]] that formed and was slowly filled with [[sediment]]s during the [[Pleistocene]] era. As of 2016, the remains of 61 [[mammoth]]s, including 58 North American [[Columbian mammoth|Columbian]] and 3 [[woolly mammoth]]s had been recovered. Mammoth bones were found at the site in 1974, and a museum and building enclosing the site were established. |[[File:Arctodus_Simus,_Hot_Springs,_South_Dakota.jpg|alt=Fossil skeleton of Arctodus simus, a large species of "short-faced" bear that was one of North America's largest predators during the Pleistocene|thumb|200x200px|Fossil skeleton of ''[[Arctodus simus]]'', a large species of "short-faced" bear that was one of North America's largest predators during the Pleistocene.|center]] |- |Rancho [[La Brea Tar Pits]] |40–12 Ka |[[California]], {{Flag|US}} |A group of [[tar pit]]s where natural [[Bitumen|asphalt]] (also called asphaltum, bitumen, or pitch; ''brea'' in Spanish) has seeped up from the ground for tens of thousands of years. Over many centuries, the bones of trapped animals have been preserved. Among the prehistoric species associated with the La Brea Tar Pits are [[Pleistocene]] mammoths, [[Dire wolf|dire wolves]], [[Arctodus|short-faced bears]], [[American lion]]s, [[ground sloth]]s, and, the [[State Fossil|state fossil]] of [[California]], the saber-toothed cat (''[[Smilodon fatalis]]''). |[[File:Mammuthus columbi Page.jpg|thumb|200x200px|Fossil skeleton of ''[[Mammuthus columbi]]'' excavated from the tar pits|center]] |- |[[Naracoorte Caves]] |500-1 [[kiloannum|Ka]] |[[South Australia]], {{Flag|Australia}} |A series of caves that preserve numerous pleistocene [[megafauna]], like ''Thylacoleo'', and is recognized as a World heritage site alongside the older, but geographically similar Riversleigh site. |[[File:Thylacoleo skeleton in Naracoorte Caves.jpg|thumb|200x200px|Skeleton of ''Thylacoleo'' at the Naracoorte Caves|center]] |} ==See also== * [[List of fossil sites]] (with link directory) * [[Hoard]], a concentration of human artifacts useful for similar reasons in archaeology ==Notes== {{reflist|group=nb}} ==References== {{Reflist}} ==Further reading== * Penney, D. (ed.) 2010. ''Biodiversity of Fossils in Amber from the Major World Deposits''. Siri Scienfic Press, Manchester, 304 pp. * {{cite web | year=2003 | url=http://palaeo.gly.bris.ac.uk/Palaeofiles/Lagerstatten/ | title=Fossil Lagerstätten | publisher=Department of Earth Sciences, University of Bristol | access-date=2005-11-21 | archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070612022023/http://palaeo.gly.bris.ac.uk/Palaeofiles/Lagerstatten/ | archive-date=12 June 2007 }} – A catalogue of sites of exceptional fossil preservation produced by MSc palaeobiology students at University of Bristol's Department of Earth Sciences. * {{cite journal |doi= 10.1144/jgs.157.1.173 |last= Orr|first= Patrick J.|author2=David J. Siveter|date=1 January 2000|title= Three-dimensional preservation of a non-biomineralized arthropod in concretions in Silurian volcaniclastic rocks from Herefordshire, England|journal= [[Journal of the Geological Society]]|volume= 157|issue = 1|pages= 173–86|bibcode= 2000JGSoc.157..173O|s2cid= 129635160|url= http://jgs.geoscienceworld.org/cgi/content/abstract/157/1/173|access-date= 2006-10-26 |author-link2= David J. Siveter}} * {{cite journal |doi= 10.1016/j.tree.2024.04.004 |last= Kimmig|first= Julien|author2=James D. Schiffbauer|date=25 April 2024|title= A modern definition of Fossil-Lagerstätten|journal= [[Trends in Ecology and Evolution]]|volume= 39|issue = 6|pages= 621–624|pmid= 38670863|bibcode= 2024TEcoE..39..621K|url= https://www.cell.com/trends/ecology-evolution/abstract/S0169-5347(24)00086-7|access-date= 2024-05-05|url-access= subscription}} {{Burgess shale type preservation}} {{Authority control}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Lagerstatte}} [[Category:Lagerstätten| ]]

{{Short description|None}} {{Use dmy dates|date=October 2025}}
[[File:Patagotitan vs Mammals Scale Diagram SVG Steveoc86.svg|thumb|upright=2.0|From left to right: a [[polar bear]] (''[[Ursus maritimus]]''), a [[giraffe]] (''[[Giraffa camelopardalis]]''), †''[[Paraceratherium transouralicum]]'', †''[[Patagotitan mayorum]]'', two [[human]]s (''[[Homo sapiens]]''), †''[[Palaeoloxodon recki]]'', an [[African bush elephant]] (''[[Loxodonta africana]]'') and a [[white rhino]] (''[[Ceratotherium simum]]'')]] The '''largest prehistoric animals''' include both [[vertebrate]] and [[invertebrate]] species. Many of them are described below, along with their typical range of size (for the general dates of extinction, see the link to each). Many species mentioned might not actually be the largest representative of their clade due to the incompleteness of the [[fossil record]] and many of the sizes given are merely estimates since no complete specimen have been found. Their body mass, especially, is largely conjecture because soft tissue was rarely fossilized. Generally, the size of extinct species was subject to energetic<ref>{{cite journal | last1=Carbone | first1=Chris | last2=Teacher | first2=Amber | last3=Rowcliffe | first3=J |title=The Costs of Carnivory |journal=PLOS Biology |volume=5 |issue=2 |article-number=e22 | doi=10.1371/journal.pbio.0050022 | year=2007 | pmid=17227145 | pmc=1769424 | doi-access=free }}</ref> and biomechanical constraints.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Hokkanen |first1=J.E.I. |title=The size of the largest land animal |journal=Journal of Theoretical Biology |date=February 1986 |volume=118 |issue=4 |pages=491–499 |doi=10.1016/S0022-5193(86)80167-9 |pmid=3713220 |bibcode=1986JThBi.118..491H }}</ref> {{TOC limit|3}}
== Non-mammalian synapsids (Synapsida) == {{further|Synapsida}}
=== Caseasaurs (Caseasauria) === The herbivorous ''[[Alierasaurus]]'' was the largest [[Caseidae|caseid]] and the largest [[amniote]] to have lived at the time, with an estimated length around {{cvt|6–7|m|0}}.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Romano |first1=Marco |last2=Citton |first2=Paolo |last3=Maganuco |first3=Simone |last4=Sacchi |first4=Eva |last5=Caratelli |first5=Martina |last6=Ronchi |first6=Ausonio |last7=Nicosia |first7=Umberto |title=New basal synapsid discovery at the Permian outcrop of Torre del Porticciolo (Alghero, Italy) |journal=Geological Journal |date=May 2019 |volume=54 |issue=3 |pages=1554–1566 |doi=10.1002/gj.3250 |bibcode=2019GeolJ..54.1554R |s2cid=133755506 }}</ref> ''[[Cotylorhynchus|Cotylorhynchus hancocki]]'' is also large, with an estimated length and weight of at least {{cvt|6|m|0}}<ref>{{cite web |title=Subcommission on Permian Stratigraphy |publisher=International Commission on Stratigraphy International Union of Geological Sciences |url=http://permian.stratigraphy.org/files/20180825094514307.pdf |access-date=8 September 2022 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20181203104036/http://permian.stratigraphy.org/files/20180825094514307.pdf|archive-date=3 December 2018 |website=permian.stratigraphy.org}}</ref> and more than {{cvt|500|kg}}.<ref>{{Cite journal|title=The Oldest Caseid Synapsid from the Late Pennsylvanian of Kansas, and the Evolution of Herbivory in Terrestrial Vertebrates|first1=Robert R. |last1=Reisz|first2=Jörg|last2=Fröbisch |date=16 April 2014 |journal=PLOS ONE|volume=9|issue=4|article-number=e94518 |doi=10.1371/journal.pone.0094518|pmid=24739998|pmc=3989228 |bibcode=2014PLoSO...994518R|doi-access=free}}</ref>
=== Edaphosaurids (Edaphosauridae) === [[File:Edaphosaurus Scale.svg|thumb|Size comparison of some species of ''[[Edaphosaurus]]'']] The largest [[Edaphosauridae|edaphosaurids]] were ''[[Lupeosaurus]]'' at {{cvt|3|m}} long<ref>{{cite journal |first1=A.S. |last1=Romer |first2= L.W. |last2=Price |year=1940 |title=Review of the Pelycosauria |journal=Geological Society of America Special Papers |number=28 | pages=400, 403 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=hCg71-cYFQMC&pg=PA400}}</ref> and ''[[Edaphosaurus]]'', which could reach even more than {{cvt|3|m}} in length.<ref>{{cite web|title=Edaphosaurus |url=http://palaeos.com/vertebrates/synapsida/edaphosauridae2.html#Edaphosaurus|website=Palaeos|access-date=9 September 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220220162206/http://palaeos.com/vertebrates/synapsida/edaphosauridae2.html|archive-date=20 February 2022}}</ref>
=== Sphenacodontids (Sphenacodontidae) === The biggest carnivorous synapsid of the [[Early Permian]] was ''[[Dimetrodon]]'', which could reach {{cvt|4.6|m|0}} and {{cvt|250|kg}}.<ref>{{cite journal |last=Berman |first=D.S. |author2=Reisz, R.R. |author3=Martens, T. |author4= Henrici, A.C. |year=2001 |title=A new species of ''Dimetrodon'' (Synapsida: Sphenacodontidae) from the Lower Permian of Germany records first occurrence of genus outside of North America |journal=Canadian Journal of Earth Sciences |volume=38|issue=5 |pages=803–812 |doi=10.1139/cjes-38-5-803 |url=http://www.stuartsumida.com/BIOL680-09/BermanEtAl2001.pdf|bibcode=2001CaJES..38..803B }}</ref> The largest members of the genus ''Dimetrodon'' were also the world's first fully terrestrial [[apex predator]]s.<ref>{{Cite journal|title=Hidden dental diversity in the oldest terrestrial apex predator Dimetrodon|first1=Kirstin S.|last1=Brink|first2=Robert R.|last2=Reisz|date=16 October 2014|journal=Nature Communications|volume=5|article-number=3269|doi=10.1038/ncomms4269|pmid=24509889|bibcode = 2014NatCo...5.3269B|doi-access=free}}</ref>
=== Tappenosauridae === The [[Middle Permian]] ''[[Tappenosaurus]]'' was estimated at {{cvt|5.5|m}} in length, nearly as large as the largest [[dinocephalia]]ns.<ref>{{cite journal |last=Olson |first=E.C. |year=1955 |title=Parallelism in the evolution of the Permian reptilian faunas of the Old and New Worlds |journal=Fieldiana |volume=37 |issue=13 |page=395 |url=https://archive.org/stream/parallelisminevo3713olso#page/n5/mode/2up |access-date=8 September 2022}}</ref>
=== Therapsids (Therapsida) === ==== Anomodonts (Anomodontia) ==== [[File:Lisowicia Size Comparison.svg|thumb|''[[Lisowicia]]'' compared to a human]] The plant-eating [[dicynodont]] ''[[Lisowicia|Lisowicia bojani]]'' is the largest-known of all non-mammalian [[Synapsida|synapsids]], at about {{cvt|4.5|m}} long, {{cvt|2.6|m}} tall, and {{cvt|9000|kg}} in body mass.<ref name="NYT-20190104">{{cite news |last=St. Fleur |first=Nicholas |title=An Elephant-Size Relative of Mammals That Grazed Alongside Dinosaurs |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2019/01/04/science/dicynodonts-fossils-poland.html |date=4 January 2019 |work=[[The New York Times]] |access-date=9 September 2022 }}</ref><ref name="SM-20190104">{{cite journal |last1=Sulej |first1=Tomasz |last2=Niedzwiedzki |first2=Grzegorz |title=An elephant-sized Late Triassic synapsid with erect limbs |date=4 January 2019 |journal=[[Science (journal)|Science]] |volume=363 |issue=6422 |pages=78–80 |doi=10.1126/science.aal4853|pmid=30467179 |bibcode=2019Sci...363...78S |doi-access=free }}</ref><ref name="SD-20181123">{{cite press release |title=Gigantic mammal 'cousin' discovered |url=https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2018/11/181123134400.htm |work=ScienceDaily |publisher=Uppsala University |date=23 November 2018 }}</ref> However, in 2019 its weight was later more reliably estimated by modelling its mass from the estimated total volume of its body. These estimates varied depending on the girth of its rib cage and the amount of soft tissue modelled around the skeleton, with an overall average weight of 5.9 metric tons (6.5 short tons), and a lowermost estimate with minimal body fat and other tissues at 4.9 metric tons (5.4 short tons) and a maximum of 7 metric tons (7.7 short tons) at its bulkiest.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Romano |first1=Marco |last2=Manucci |first2=Fabio |date=3 April 2021 |title=Resizing Lisowicia bojani: volumetric body mass estimate and 3D reconstruction of the giant Late Triassic dicynodont |url=https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/08912963.2019.1631819 |journal=Historical Biology |language=en |volume=33 |issue=4 |pages=474–479 |doi=10.1080/08912963.2019.1631819 |bibcode=2021HBio...33..474R |issn=0891-2963|url-access=subscription }}</ref>
==== Biarmosuchians (Biarmosuchia) ==== The [[Late Permian]] ''[[Eotitanosuchus]]'' (a possible synonym to ''[[Biarmosuchus]]''<ref name=Eotitanosuchidae>{{cite web|title=Eotitanosuchidae |url=http://www.kheper.net/evolution/therapsida/Eotitanosuchidae.html|website=Kheper|publisher=M.Alan Kazlev |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190806075958/http://www.kheper.net/evolution/therapsida/Eotitanosuchidae.html |archive-date=6 August 2019 |access-date=9 September 2022}}</ref>) may have been over {{cvt|2.5|m}} in length, possibly up to {{cvt|6|m}} and more than {{cvt|600|kg}} in weight for adult specimens.<ref name=Eotitanosuchidae/>
==== Dinocephalians (Dinocephalia) ==== [[File:Anteosaurus in landscape.jpg|thumb|left|''[[Anteosaurus]]'' overviewing the landscape]]
* Perhaps the largest known [[Dinocephalia|dinocephalian]] was the [[Titanosuchidae|titanosuchid]] ''[[Jonkeria|Jonkeria truculenta]]'', with volumetric models suggesting it could have weighed {{convert|985|kg|lb|abbr=on}}.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Benoit |first1=Julien |last2=Midzuk |first2=A. J. |date=January 2024 |title=Estimating the endocranial volume and body mass of Anteosaurus, Jonkeria, and Moschops (Dinocephalia, Therapsida) using 3D sculpting |url=https://palaeo-electronica.org/content/pdfs/1377.pdf |journal=Palaeontologia Electronica |volume=27 |issue=2 |doi=10.26879/1377}}</ref> ''[[Tapinocaninus pamelae]]'' was slightly smaller, volumetric models suggesting it weighed {{convert|892.63|kg|lb|abbr=on}}.<ref>Romano, M., & Rubidge, B. (2019). First 3D reconstruction and volumetric body mass estimate of the tapinocephalid dinocephalian Tapinocaninus pamelae (Synapsida: Therapsida). Historical Biology, 33(4), 498–505. https://doi.org/10.1080/08912963.2019.1640219</ref> * The largest carnivorous non-mammalian synapsids was the [[Anteosauridae|anteosaurid]] ''[[Anteosaurus]]'', which was {{cvt|5|-|6|m}} long, and weighed {{cvt|500|-|600|kg}}.<ref>van Valkenburgh, Blaire; Jenkins, Ian (2002). "Evolutionary Patterns in the History of Permo-Triassic and Cenozoic synapsid predators". Paleontological Society Papers 8: 267–288.</ref><ref name="Kheper">{{cite web|title=Brithopodidae / Anteosauridae|url=http://www.kheper.net/evolution/therapsida/Brithopodidae.html|website=Kheper|publisher=M.Alan Kazlev|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190812220358/https://www.kheper.net/evolution/therapsida/Brithopodidae.html |archive-date=12 August 2019 |access-date=9 September 2022}}</ref> Fully grown ''[[Titanophoneus]]'' from the same family [[Anteosauridae]] likely had a skull of {{cvt|1|m|ft}} long.<ref name="Kheper" />
==== Gorgonopsians (Gorgonopsia) ==== [[File:Inostrancevia A6 digital.jpg|thumb|Photo-reconstruction of ''[[Inostrancevia]]'']] ''[[Inostrancevia|Inostrancevia latifrons]]'' is the largest known [[gorgonopsia]]n, with a skull length of more than {{cvt|60|cm}}, a total length approaching {{cvt|3.5|m}} and a mass of {{cvt|300|kg|lb}}.<ref>{{cite book |last=Prothero |first=Donald R. |chapter=20. Synapsids: The Origin of Mammals|title=Vertebrate Evolution: From Origins to Dinosaurs and Beyond |date=18 April 2022 |publisher=CRC Press |location=Boca Raton |isbn=978-0-367-47316-7 |doi=10.1201/9781003128205-4 |s2cid=246318785 |language=en}}</ref> ''[[Rubidgea atrox]]'' is the largest African gorgonopsian, with skull of nearly {{cvt|45|cm}} long.<ref name=Kammerer2016>{{cite journal |last1=Kammerer |first1=Christian F. |title=Systematics of the Rubidgeinae (Therapsida: Gorgonopsia) |journal=PeerJ |date=2016 |volume=4 |article-number=e1608 |doi=10.7717/peerj.1608|pmid=26823998 |pmc=4730894 |doi-access=free }}</ref> Other large gorgonopsians include ''[[Dinogorgon]]'' with skull of ~{{cvt|40|cm}} long,<ref>Pages 158–159 in: {{cite book |doi=10.1515/9783110341553-005 |chapter=Non-Mammalian synapsids: The deep roots of the mammalian family tree |title=Mammalian Evolution, Diversity and Systematics |date=2018 |last1=Angielczyk |first1=Kenneth D. |last2=Kammerer |first2=Christian F. |pages=117–198 |isbn=978-3-11-034155-3 }}</ref> ''[[Leontosaurus]]'' with skull of almost {{cvt|40|cm}} long,<ref name=Kammerer2016/> and ''[[Sycosaurus]]'' with skull of ~{{cvt|38|cm}} long.<ref name=Kammerer2016/>
==== Therocephalians (Therocephalia) ==== The largest of [[therocephalia]]ns is ''[[Scymnosaurus]]'',<ref>{{Citation|title=Dissertation Presented for the Degree of Doctor of Philosophy at the University of Stellenbosch|date=1987|author=J. Van Den Heever}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Lydekker |first1=R. |title=The Year's Vertebrate Palæontology |journal=Science Progress in the Twentieth Century (1906-1916) |date=1908 |volume=2 |issue=7 |pages=501–524 |jstor=43776634 }}</ref> which reached a size of the modern [[hyena]].<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Broom|first=Robert|date=1903|title=On Some New Primitive Theriodonts|journal=Annals of the South African Museum|volume=4}}</ref>
==== Non-mammalian cynodonts (Cynodontia) ==== * The largest known non-mammalian [[Cynodontia|cynodont]], as well as the largest member of [[Cynognathia]], is ''[[Scalenodontoides]]'', a [[traversodontid]], which had a maximum skull length of approximately {{convert|61.7|cm|in}} based on a fragmentary specimen.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Tolchard |first1=Frederick |last2=Kammerer |first2=Christian F. |last3=Butler |first3=Richard J. |last4=Hendrickx |first4=Christophe |last5=Benoit |first5=Julien |last6=Abdala |first6=Fernando |last7=Choiniere |first7=Jonah N. |title=A new large gomphodont from the Triassic of South Africa and its implications for Gondwanan biostratigraphy |journal=Journal of Vertebrate Paleontology |date=4 March 2021 |volume=41 |issue=2 |article-number=e1929265 |doi=10.1080/02724634.2021.1929265 |bibcode=2021JVPal..41E9265T |url=http://pure-oai.bham.ac.uk/ws/files/136125806/JVP_2020_0119.R1_Proof_hi.pdf }}</ref> * ''[[Paceyodon davidi]]'' was the largest of [[morganucodonta]]ns, cynodonts close to mammals. It is known by a right lower molariform {{cvt|3.3|mm}} in length, which is bigger than molariforms of all other morganucodontans.<ref>{{cite journal |author=William A. Clemens |year=2011 |title=New morganucodontans from an Early Jurassic fissure filling in Wales (United Kingdom) |journal=Palaeontology |volume=54 |issue=5 |pages=1139–1156 |doi=10.1111/j.1475-4983.2011.01094.x |bibcode=2011Palgy..54.1139C |doi-access=free }}</ref> * The largest known [[Docodonta|docodont]] was ''[[Castorocauda]]'', almost {{cvt|50|cm}} in length.{{sfn|Rose|2006|p=56}}
== Mammals (Mammalia) == {{further|Mammal}}
=== Non-therian mammals ===
==== Gobiconodonts (Gobiconodonta) ==== [[File:Repenomamus BW.jpg|thumb|left|A reconstruction of ''[[Repenomamus]]'']] The largest [[Gobiconodonta|gobiconodont]] and the largest well-known [[Mesozoic]] mammal was ''[[Repenomamus]]''.<ref>{{cite book | url = https://books.google.com/books?id=DUli1Mb8j7QC | title = Evolution of Fossil Ecosystems. Second edition | publisher = Academic Press | date = 19 September 2012 | access-date = 25 August 2022 | page = 178 |author=Paul Selden |author2=John Nudds | isbn = 978-0-12-404629-0 }}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |author=[[Zofia Kielan-Jaworowska]] |author2=Richard L. Cifelli |author3=Zhe-Xi Luo | url = https://books.google.com/books?id=qJGcX3X0EaMC | title = Mammals from the Age of Dinosaurs: origins, evolution, and structure | publisher = Columbia University Press | location = New York | year = 2004 | page = 243 | chapter = Chapter 12: Metatherians | isbn = 978-0-231-11918-4 }}</ref><ref name=ProtheroRepenomamus>{{cite book | url = https://books.google.com/books?id=tWiYDwAAQBAJ&dq=repenomamus+largest&pg=PA33 | title = The Princeton Field Guide to Prehistoric Mammals | publisher = Princeton University Press | date = 15 November 2016 | access-date = 22 September 2022 | page = 33 | first = Donald R. | last = Prothero | isbn = 978-0-691-15682-8 }}</ref><ref>{{cite book | url = https://books.google.com/books?id=dQd2DwAAQBAJ&dq=repenomamus+largest&pg=PA224 | title = Mammalian Evolution, Diversity and Systematics | publisher = De Gruyter | date = 22 October 2018 | access-date = 25 August 2022 | page = 224 | author = Frank Zachos, Robert Asher | isbn = 978-3-11-034155-3 }}</ref>{{sfn|Rose|2006|p=62}}<ref name="Zofia2013">{{cite book | url = https://books.google.com/books?id=wuwtxuJoX6IC&dq=repenomamus+largest&pg=PA715 | title = In Pursuit of Early Mammals | publisher = Indiana University Press | date = 2013 | access-date = 25 August 2022 | page = 115 | first = Zofia | last = Kielan-Jaworowska | isbn = 978-0-253-00824-4 }}</ref> The known adult of ''Repenomamus giganticus'' reached a total length of around {{convert|1|m|abbr=on|sigfig=2}} and an estimated mass of {{convert|12|-|14|kg|abbr=on|sigfig=2}}.<ref name=ProtheroRepenomamus/> With such parameters it surpassed in size several small [[theropod]] dinosaurs of the [[Early Cretaceous]].<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Hu |first1=Yaoming |last2=Meng |first2=Jin |last3=Wang |first3=Yuanqing |last4=Li |first4=Chuankui |title=Large Mesozoic mammals fed on young dinosaurs |journal=Nature |date=13 January 2005 |volume=433 |issue=7022 |pages=149–152 |doi=10.1038/nature03102 |pmid=15650737 |bibcode=2005Natur.433..149H |url=http://doc.rero.ch/record/15291/files/PAL_E2590.pdf }}</ref> ''[[Gobiconodon]]'' was also a large mammal,{{sfn|Rose|2006|p=62}}<ref name="Zofia2013"/> it weighed {{Convert|5.4|kg}},<ref name=ProtheroRepenomamus/> had a skull of {{cvt|10|cm}} in length, and had {{cvt|35|cm}} in presacral body length.<ref>{{cite book |isbn = 978-0-19-850761-1 |url = https://books.google.com/books?id=GswTDAAAQBAJ&dq=Gobiconodon+kg&pg=PA183 |title = The Origin and Evolution of Mammals |publisher = Oxford University Press, USA |date = 2005 |access-date = 22 September 2022 |page = 183 |author = T. S. Kemp}}</ref>
==== Multituberculates (Multituberculata) ==== The largest [[Multituberculata|multituberculate]],{{sfn|Rose|2006|p=60}} ''[[Taeniolabis|Taeniolabis taoensis]]'' is the largest non-[[theria]]n mammal known, at a weight possibly exceeding {{cvt|100|kg|lb}}.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Williamson |first1=Thomas E. |last2=Brusatte |first2=Stephen L. |last3=Secord |first3=Ross |last4=Shelley |first4=Sarah |title=A new taeniolabidoid multituberculate (Mammalia) from the middle Puercan of the Nacimiento Formation, New Mexico, and a revision of taeniolabidoid systematics and phylogeny: Revision of Taeniolabidoidea |journal=Zoological Journal of the Linnean Society |date=May 2016 |volume=177 |issue=1 |pages=183–208 |doi=10.1111/zoj.12336 }}</ref>
==== Monotremes (Monotremata) ==== [[File:Zaglossus hacketti A6 digital.jpg|thumb|Photo-reconstruction of ''Murrayglossus hacketti'' (''Zaglossus hacketti'') by paleoartist [https://prehistoric-fauna.com/Zaglossus-hacketti Roman Uchytel]]] * The largest known [[monotreme]] (egg-laying mammal) ever was the extinct [[long-beaked echidna]] species known as ''[[Murrayglossus|Murrayglossus hacketti]]'', known from a couple of bones found in Western Australia. It was the size of a sheep, weighing probably up to {{cvt|30|kg}}.<ref name="TourismWA">{{cite web |author=<!--Not stated--> |title =''Zaglossus hacketti'' – extinct giant echidna |url=http://www.megafauna.com.au/view/megafauna/zaglossus-hacketti |work=Tourism Western Australia |access-date=28 April 2014 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140126010215/http://www.megafauna.com.au/view/megafauna/zaglossus-hacketti |archive-date=26 January 2014}}</ref> * The largest known [[Ornithorhynchidae|ornithorhynchid]] is ''[[Obdurodon|Obdurodon tharalkooschild]]''.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Pian |first1=Rebecca |last2=Archer |first2=Michael |last3=Hand |first3=Suzanne J. |date=1 November 2013 |title=A new, giant platypus, Obdurodon tharalkooschild, sp. nov. (Monotremata, Ornithorhynchidae), from the Riversleigh World Heritage Area, Australia |journal=Journal of Vertebrate Paleontology |volume=33 |issue=6 |pages=1255–1259 |doi=10.1080/02724634.2013.782876 |bibcode=2013JVPal..33.1255P |s2cid=85776473 }}</ref> * ''[[Kollikodon|Kollikodon ritchiei]]'' was likely the largest monotreme in the [[Mesozoic]]. Its body length could be up to a {{cvt|1|m}}.<ref>{{cite journal |first=Anne |last=Weil |title=Mammalian palaeobiology: Living large in the Cretaceous |journal=Nature |volume=433 |pages=116–117 |date=2005|issue=7022 |doi=10.1038/433116b|pmid=15650725 |bibcode=2005Natur.433..116W |s2cid=52869101 |publication-date=12 January 2005}}</ref>
=== Metatherians (Metatheria) === [[File:Thylacosmilus Size Comparison.svg|thumb|''[[Thylacosmilus]]'' compared to a human]] * The largest non-marsupial [[metatherian]], as well as the largest carnivorous metatherian, was ''[[Proborhyaena|Proborhyaena gigantea]]'', which is estimated to have weighed weigh {{convert|50-200|kg|lb|abbr=on}}.<ref>{{Cite report |url=https://digitallibrary.amnh.org/handle/2246/7235 |title=Eomakhaira molossus, a new saber-toothed sparassodont (Metatheria: Thylacosmilinae) from the early Oligocene (?Tinguirirican) Cachapoal locality, Andean Main Range, Chile. D. |author2=Ladevèze, S. |author3=Prevosti, F. |bibcode=2022NatSR..12.1224T |doi=10.1038/s41598-022-05266-z |pmc=8786871 |pmid=35075186 |doi-access=free}}</ref> Another large metatherian was ''[[Thylacosmilus|Thylacosmilus atrox]]'', weighing {{convert|80|to|120|kg}},<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Prevosti |first1=Francisco J. |last2=Forasiepi |first2=Analía |last3=Zimicz |first3=Natalia |title=The Evolution of the Cenozoic Terrestrial Mammalian Predator Guild in South America: Competition or Replacement? |journal=Journal of Mammalian Evolution |date=5 November 2011 |volume=20 |issue=1 |pages=3–21 |doi=10.1007/s10914-011-9175-9 Sparassodonta (Mammalia, Metatheria) de Edad Santacrucense (Mioceno Temprano) a Partir del Tamaño del Centroide de los Elementos Apendiculares: Inferencias Paleoecológicas |trans-title=Mass Estimation of the Holy Cross (Early Miocene) Sparassodonta (Mammalia, Metatheria) Species from the Centroid Size of the Appendicular Elements: Paleoecological Inferences |language=es |journal=Ameghiniana |date=1 |pages=333–347 |doi=10.1111/j.1502-3931.2007.00091.x |bibcode=2008Letha..41..333S }}</ref> ''[[Australohyaena]]'' is another large metatherian, weighing up to {{convert|70|kg}}.<ref name=Forasiepi2014>{{cite journal |last1=Forasiepi |first1=Analía M. |last2=Judith Babot |first2=M. |last3=Zimicz |first3=Natalia |title=Australohyaena antiqua (Mammalia, Metatheria, Sparassodonta), a |bibcode=2015JSPal..13..503F |hdl=11336/59430 |hdl-access=free }}</ref> * [[Stagodontidae|Stagodontid]] mammal ''[[Didelphodon]]'' was one of the largest [[Mesozoic]] metatherians and all [[Cretaceous]] mammals.{{sfn|Rose|2006|p=78}} Its skull could reach over {{convert|10|cm}} in length<ref>{{Cite book |author=[[Zofia Kielan-Jaworowska]] |author2=Richard L. Cifelli |author3=Zhe-Xi Luo |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=qJGcX3X0EaMC |title=Mammals from the Age of Dinosaurs: origins, evolution, and structure |publisher=Columbia University Press |location=New York |year=2004 |page=428 |chapter=Chapter 12: Metatherians |isbn=978-0-231-11918-4}}</ref> and a weight of complete animal was {{convert|5.2|kg}}.<ref>{{cite journal |title=A large carnivorous mammal from the Late Cretaceous and the North American origin of marsupials |journal=Nature Communications |volume=7 |article-number=13734 |date=2016 |first1=G.P. |last1=Wilson |first2=E.G. |last2=Ekdale |first3=J.W. |last3=Hoganson |first4=J.J. |last4=Calede |first5=A.V.| last5=Linden |doi=10.1038/ncomms13734 |pmid=27929063 |pmc=5155139 |bibcode=2016NatCo...713734W}}</ref>
==== Marsupials (Marsupialia) ==== [[File:Diprotodon_optatum_(2).jpg|thumb|''[[Diprotodon]]'' the largest marsupial]] * The largest known [[marsupial]], and the largest [[metatherian]], is the extinct ''[[Diprotodon]]'', about {{cvt|3|m}} long, standing {{cvt|2|m}} tall and weighing up to {{cvt|2786|kg}}.<ref name="smh">{{cite web |title=Ice Age Marsupial Topped Three Tons, Scientists Say |url=http://news.nationalgeographic.com/news/2003/10/1016_031017_giantmarsupial.html |archive-date=13 April 2010 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100413090600/https://news.nationalgeographic.com/news/2003/10/1016_031017_giantmarsupial.html}}</ref> Fellow [[Vombatiformes|vombatiform]] ''[[Palorchestes|Palorchestes azael]]'' was similar in length being around {{cvt|2.5|m|ft}}, with body mass estimates indicating it could exceed {{cvt|1,000|kg|lb}}.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Richards|first1=Hazel L.|last2=Wells|first2=Rod T. |last3=Evans|first3=Alistair R.|last4=Fitzgerald|first4=Erich M. G.|last5=Adams|first5=Justin W.|date=13 September 2019|title=The extraordinary osteology and functional morphology of the limbs in Palorchestidae, a family of strange extinct marsupial giants |journal=PLOS ONE |volume=14 |issue=9|article-number=e0221824 |doi=10.1371/journal.pone.0221824|pmid=31518353|pmc=6744111 |bibcode=2019PLoSO..1421824R |doi-access=free}}</ref> * The largest known carnivorous marsupial was ''[[Thylacoleo carnifex]]''. Measurements taken from a number of specimens show they averaged {{cvt|101|to|164|kg|lb}} in weight.<ref>{{cite journal | doi=10.1007/s10914-013-9228-3 | volume=20 | issue=3 | title=The Bony Labyrinth in Diprotodontian Marsupial Mammals: Diversity in Extant and Extinct Forms and Relationships with Size and Phylogeny | journal=Journal of Mammalian Evolution | pages=191–198|year = 2013|last1 = Alloing-Séguier|first1 = Léanie| last2=Sánchez-Villagra | first2=Marcelo R. | last3=Lee | first3=Michael S. Y. | last4=Lebrun | first4=Renaud | s2cid=16385939}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Wroe |first1=S. |last2=Myers |first2=T. J. |last3=Wells |first3=R. T. |last4=Gillespie |first4=A. |date=1999 |title=Estimating the weight of the Pleistocene marsupial lion, Thylacoleo carnifex (Thylacoleonidae:Marsupialia): implications for the ecomorphology of a marsupial super-predator and hypotheses of impoverishment of Australian marsupial carnivore faunas |journal=Australian Journal of Zoology |volume=47 |issue=5 |page=489 |doi=10.1071/ZO99006}}</ref> The largest known koala is the [[Giant koala]] (''Phascolarctos stitorni'') are modern [[Koala]] (''Phascolarctos cinerus'') and has an estimated weight of {{convert|13|kg|lb|abbr=on|lk=off}}, which is the same weight as a large contemporary male koala.<ref name=Prideaux>{{Cite FTP | author = Prideaux, Gavin J. | title = Mammalian responses to Pleistocene climate change in southeastern Australia | url = ftp://rock.geosociety.org/pub/reposit/2007/2007016.pdf | year = 2007 | volume = 35 | page = 33 | doi = 10.1130/G23070A.1 | last2 = Roberts | first2 = Richard G. | last3 = Megirian | first3 = ever ''[[Sarcophilus laniarius]]'' which were around 15% larger and 50% heavier than modern devils.<ref name=o35>Owen and Pemberton, p. 35.</ref> * The largest known [[kangaroo]] was an as yet unnamed{{Update inline|date=April 2025}} species of ''[[Macropus]]'', estimated to weigh {{cvt|274|kg}},<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Hocknull |first1=Scott A. |last2=Lewis |first2=Richard |last3=Arnold |first3=Lee J. |last4=Pietsch |first4=Tim |last5=Joannes-Boyau |first5=Renaud |last6=Price |first6=Gilbert J. |last7=Moss |first7=Patrick |last8=Wood |first8=Rachel |last9=Dosseto |first9=Anthony known specimen of ''[[Procoptodon]]'', which could grow up to {{cvt|2|m}} and weigh {{cvt|230|kg}}.<ref>{{cite journal | author1 = Helgen, K. M. |author2= Wells, R. T.| author3= Kear, B. P. | author4= Gerdtz, W. R.| author5= Flannery, T. F. | year = 2006 | title = Ecological and evolutionary significance of sizes of giant extinct kangaroos | journal = Australian Journal of Zoology | volume=54 | issue = 4 | pages=293–303 | doi = 10.1071/ZO05077}}</ref> Some species from the genus ''[[Sthenurus]]'' were similar in size or a bit larger than the extant [[grey kangaroo]] (''Macropus giganteus'').<ref>{{cite journal| last1 = Janis| first1 = CM | last2 = Buttrill| first2 = K| last3 = Figueirido| first3 = B| date = 2014| title = Locomotion in Extinct Giant Kangaroos: Were Sthenurines Hop-Less Monsters?| journal = PLOS ONE |volume = 9| issue = 10| article-number = e109888| doi = 10.1371/journal.pone.0109888| pmid=25333823| pmc=4198187| bibcode = 2014PLoSO...9j9888J | doi-access = free}}</ref> The largest ever tree kangaroo, ''[[Bohra (mammal)|Bohra]]'', had an estimated body mass of {{convert|35-47|kg|lbs|abbr=on}}.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Prideaux |first1=Gavin J. |last2=Warburton |first2=Natalie M. |date=7
