which was nearly 30% bigger than the largest living species and weighed up to {{cvt|10|kg}}.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Long |first1=John A. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=LwMkO0M1mPQC|title=Prehistoric Mammals of Australia and New Guinea: One Hundred Million Years of Evolution |date=2002 |publisher=Johns Hopkins University Press |isbn=978-0-8018-7223-5 |page=174}}</ref> * The largest member of the [[Thylacinidae]] is ''[[Thylacinus megiriani]]'', which is somewhat reasonably larger than the Tasmanian wolf (''[[Thylacine|Thylacinus cynocephalus]]''), and bigger than its fellow Miocene relative ''[[Thylacinus potens]]'', usually being 57.3 kilograms in weight.<ref name="Yates2015">{{cite journal |last1=Yates |first1=A.M. |author-link1=Adam M. Yates |title=''Thylacinus'' (Marsupialia: Thylacinidae) from the Mio-Pliocene boundary and the diversity of Late Neogene thylacinids in Australia |journal=PeerJ |date=12 May 2015 |volume=3 |article-number=e931 |doi=10.7717/peerj.931|pmid=26019996 |pmc=4435473 |doi-access=free }}</ref>
=== Non-placental eutherians === [[File:Knight Coryphodon.jpg|thumb|Restoration of ''[[Coryphodon]]s'']]
==== Cimolestans (Cimolesta) ==== The largest known [[cimolesta]]n is ''[[Coryphodon]]'', {{cvt|1|m}} high at the shoulder, {{cvt|2.5|m}} long<ref>{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=4na5gY-lXwgC|title = Mammals. An Outline of Theriology|publisher = Polish Scientific Publishers|date = 1976|access-date = 17 September 2022|page = 442|author = Kazimierz Kowalski}}</ref><ref>{{cite book|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=ltpNtGCHUAUC|title = Dinosaurs, Sharks, and Woolly Mammoths|publisher = State Historical Society of North Dakota|date = 2007|access-date = 17 September 2022|page = 36|author = John W. Hoganson|isbn=978-1-891419-33-1}}</ref> and up to {{Convert|700|kg|abbr=on}} of mass.<ref>{{Cite journal | last1 = Uhen | first1 = Mark D. | last2 = Gingerich | first2 = Philip D. | author2-link = Philip D. Gingerich | title = Evolution of ''Coryphodon'' (Mammalia, Pantodonta) in the Late Paleocene and Early Eocene of Northwestern Wyoming | year = 1995 | journal = Contributions from the Museum of Paleontology, University of Michigan | volume = 29 | issue = 10 | page = 264 | hdl=2027.42/48649 |hdl-access=free | oclc = 742731820 }}</ref> ''[[Barylambda]]'' was also a huge mammal, at {{cvt|650|kg|lb}}.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.paleocene-mammals.de/large_herbivores.htm|title = Paleocene mammals of the world|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220911060514/http://www.paleocene-mammals.de/large_herbivores.htm|archive-date=11 September 2022}}</ref> ''[[Wortmania]]'' and ''[[Psittacotherium]]'' from the group [[Taeniodonta]] were among the largest mammals of the Early [[Paleocene]].<ref name=Taeniodonts>{{cite web|title=Paleocene mammals of the world|url=http://www.paleocene-mammals.de/taeniodonts.htm|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220803122704/http://www.paleocene-mammals.de/taeniodonts.htm|archive-date=3 August 2022|access-date=17 September 2022|website=www.paleocene-mammals.de}}</ref> Lived as soon as half a million years after [[K–Pg boundary]], ''Wortmania'' reached {{cvt|20|kg}} in body mass. ''Psittacotherium'', which appeared two million years later, reached {{cvt|50|kg}}.<ref name=Taeniodonts/>
==== Leptictids (Leptictida) ==== The largest [[Leptictida|leptictid]] ever discovered is ''[[Leptictidium tobieni]]'' from the [[Middle Eocene]] of [[Germany]]. It had a skull {{cvt|101|mm}} long, head with trunk {{cvt|375|mm}} long, and tail {{cvt|500|mm}} long.<ref name=Meehan>{{cite journal |author=TJ Meehan, Larry D. Martin |date=1 September 2012 |title =New Large Leptictid Insectivore from the Late Paleogene of South Dakota, USA |journal=Acta Palaeontologica Polonica |volume=57 |issue=3 |pages=509–518 |doi=10.4202/app.2011.0035 |s2cid=129358395 |doi-access=free }}</ref> Close European relatives from the same family [[Pseudorhyncocyonidae]] had skulls of {{cvt|67|-|101|mm}} in length.<ref name=Meehan/>
=== Tenrecs and allies (Afroscida) === The larger of the two species of [[Plesiorycteropus|bibymalagasy]] (''Plesiorycteropus madagascariensis''), extinct [[tenrec]] relatives from [[Madagascar]], is estimated to have weighed from 10 to 18 kilograms (21 to 40 lb).<ref>{{cite journal |hdl=2246/828 |title=Morphology, adaptations, and relationships of Plesiorycteropus: And a diagnosis of a new order of eutherian mammals |journal=Bulletin of the American Museum of Natural History |issue=220 |date=1994 |last1=MacPhee |first1=R. D. E. |page=148 }}</ref>
=== Even-toed ungulates (Artiodactyla) === [[File:Hippopotamus gorgops e.jpg|thumb|The extinct ''[[Hippopotamus gorgops]]'' is the most massive of the fossil even-toed ungulates]] * The largest known land-dwelling [[Artiodactyla|artiodactyl]] was ''[[Hippopotamus gorgops]]'' estimated to have weighed over {{cvt|4,000|kg|lb}},<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Hutchinson |first=John R. |date=1 June 2021 |title=The evolutionary biomechanics of locomotor function in giant land animals |journal=Journal of Experimental Biology |language=en |volume=224 |issue=11 |article-number=jeb217463 |doi=10.1242/jeb.217463 |issn=0022-0949 |pmc=8214834 |pmid=34100541|bibcode=2021JExpB.224B7463H }}</ref> with its closely related European descendant, ''[[Hippopotamus antiquus]]'', possibly rivaling it, estimated to be 14.1 ft (4.3 m) in length and {{cvt|3,500-4,200|kg|lb}} in weight.<ref name="hippo">{{Cite journal |last1=Martino |first1=Roberta |last2=Ríos |first2=Maria Ibanez |last3=Mateus |first3=Octavio |last4=Pandolfi |first4=Luca |date=December 2022 |title=Taxonomy, chronology, and dispersal patterns of Western European Quaternary hippopotamuses: New insight from Portuguese fossil material |url=https://linkinghub.elsevier.com/retrieve/pii/S1040618222003901 |journal=Quaternary International |volume=674-675 |language=en |pages=121–137|doi=10.1016/j.quaint.2022.12.010|s2cid=255029640 |url-access=subscription }}</ref> However, volumetric models suggests it was slightly smaller, weighing {{Convert|3174|kg}}.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Romano |first1=Marco |last2=Manucci |first2=Fabio |last3=Bellucci |first3=Luca |date=30 July 2024 |title=Body mass estimate and in-vivo reconstruction of Hippopotamus antiquus from Figline, Upper Valdarno (Tuscany) |url=https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/08912963.2024.2380358 |journal=Historical Biology |volume=37 |issue=6 |language=en |pages=1541–1552 |doi=10.1080/08912963.2024.2380358 |issn=0891-2963|url-access=subscription }}</ref> * ''[[Daeodon]]'' and similar in size and morphology ''[[Paraentelodon]]''<ref name=Prothero> {{cite book | isbn = 978-0-8018-8735-2 | url = https://books.google.com/books?id=qO8H_alEofAC&q=paraentelodon+%22tooth%22&pg=PA129 | title = The Evolution of Artiodactyls | publisher = The Johns Hopkins University Press | date = 2007 | access-date = 26 September 2020 | pages = 128–129 | author = [[Donald Prothero|Donald R. Prothero]], Scott E. Foss }}</ref> were the largest-known [[Entelodontidae|entelodonts]] that ever lived, at {{cvt|12|ft|order=flip}} long and {{cvt|1.77|m|ft}} high at the shoulder.<ref name="Peterson1909">{{cite journal|last=Peterson|first=O. A.|title=A revision of the Entelodontidae|journal=Memoirs of the Carnegie Museum|year=1909|volume=4|issue=3|pages=41–158|doi=10.5962/p.234831|url= https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/bibliography/46733#/summary |hdl=2027/mdp.39015017493571|s2cid=247000277|hdl-access=free}}</ref> The huge ''[[Andrewsarchus]]'' from the [[Eocene]] of [[Inner Mongolia]] had a skull about {{cvt|83.4|cm|in}} long<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Osborn |first1=Henry Fairfield |last2=Olsen |first2=George |author3=Central Asiatic Expeditions |title=Andrewsarchus, giant mesonychid of Mongolia |journal=American Museum Novitates |date=1924 |issue=146 |hdl=2246/3226 |hdl-access=free }}</ref> though the taxonomy of this genus is disputed.<ref>{{cite journal |doi=10.1038/nature07775 |title=Thewissen et al. Reply |journal=Nature |volume=458 |issue=7236 |article-number=E5 |year=2009 |last1=Thewissen |author-link=Hans Thewissen |first1=J. G. M. |last2=Cooper |first2=Lisa Noelle |last3=Clementz |first3=Mark T. |last4=Bajpai |first4=Sunil |last5=Tiwari |first5=B. N. |bibcode=2009Natur.458....5T |s2cid=4431497 |doi-access=free }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Tabuce |first1=Rodolphe |last2=Clavel |first2=Julien |last3=Antunes |first3=Miguel Telles |title=A structural intermediate between triisodontids and mesonychians (Mammalia, Acreodi) from the earliest Eocene of Portugal |journal=Naturwissenschaften |date=February 2011 |volume=98 |issue=2 |pages=145–155 |doi=10.1007/s00114-010-0747-y |pmid=21181109 |bibcode=2011NW.....98..145T }}</ref> [[File:Giant bison.jpg|thumb|left|A skeleton of ''[[Bison latifrons]]'']] * The possibly of [[Bovinae]] as well as the largest [[Bovidae|bovid]] was ''[[Bison latifrons]]''. It reached a weight from {{convert|1250|kg|lb|abbr=on}}<ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=-QxQAAAAQBAJ |title=The Dream Hunters Epoch: The Paleo Indians Series |isbn=978-1-4653-9694-5 |last1=East |first1=Shirley G. |date=29 December 2011 |publisher=Xlibris Corporation}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=kUAKgNfiAvoC |title=Megafauna: Giant Beasts of Pleistocene South America |isbn=978-0-253-00719-3 |last1=Fariña |first1=Richard A. |last2=Vizcaíno |first2=Sergio F. |last3=Iuliis |first3=Gerry De |date=22 May 2013 |publisher=Indiana University Press}}</ref> to possibly {{convert|2,000|kg|lb|abbr=on}},<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Froese |first1=Duane |last2=Stiller |first2=Mathias |last3=Heintzman |first3=Peter D. |last4=Reyes |first4=Alberto V. |last5=Zazula |first5=Grant D. |last6=Soares |first6=André E. R. |last7=Meyer |first7=Matthias |last8=Hall |first8=Elizabeth |last9=Jensen |first9=Britta J. L. |last10=Arnold |first10=Lee J. |last11=MacPhee |first11=Ross D. E. |last12=Shapiro |first12=Beth |title=Fossil and genomic evidence constrains the timing of bison arrival in North America |journal=Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences |date=28 March 2017 |volume=114 |issue=13 |pages=3457–3462 |doi=10.1073/pnas.1620754114 |pmid=28289222 |pmc=5380047 |bibcode=2017PNAS..114.3457F |doi-access=free }}</ref> {{cvt|4.75|m|ft}} in length, shoulder height of {{convert|2.31|m|ft|abbr=on}},<ref name=SanDiego>{{cite web |url=http://ielc.libguides.com/sdzg/factsheets/extinctlonghorned-ancientbison|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210115170010/http://ielc.libguides.com/sdzg/factsheets/extinctlonghorned-ancientbison |archive-date=15 January 2021|title=Extinct Long-horned Bison & Ancient Bison (Bison latifrons and B. antiquus) Fact Sheet: Summary. San Diego Zoo Global Library}}</ref> and had horns that spanned {{cvt|2.13|m}}.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Kurten |first1=B. |last2=Anderson |first2=E. |title=Pleistocene mammals of North America|edition=1st|chapter=Order Artiodactyla|pages=295–339|publisher=Columbia University Press|location=New York|year=1980|isbn=0-231-03733-3|chapter-url=https://books.google.com/books?id=f8pviaxPIKEC&q=%22long+horn-cores%2C+which+have+a+maximum+span+of+213+cm%22&pg=PA337}}</ref> The African giant buffalo (''[[Syncerus antiquus]]'') may have rivaled ''Bison latifrons'' in size. It reached weight from {{convert|1200|kg|lb}} to {{convert|2000|kg|lb}},<ref>{{cite book | isbn = 9780691156828 | url = https://books.google.com/books?id=tWiYDwAAQBAJ | title = The Princeton Field Guide to Prehistoric Mammals | publisher = Princeton University Press | date = 2016 | access-date = 31 August 2022 | pages = 184 | author = [[Donald Prothero|Donald R. Prothero]] }}</ref> measured {{cvt|3|m|ft}} in length from muzzle to the end of the tail, {{cvt|1.85|m|ft}} in height at the [[withers]], {{cvt|1.7|m|ft}} in height at the hindquarters,<ref name=Berbere1992>{{cite encyclopedia |last=Camps |first=Gabriel |editor-last=Camps |editor-first=Gabriel |encyclopedia=Encyclopédie Berbère |title=Bubalus antiquus |url=http://journals.openedition.org/encyclopedieberbere/1875 |date=1992 |volume= 11|issue=11 |pages=1642–1647 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200718185339/http://journals.openedition.org/encyclopedieberbere/1875 |url-status=live|archive-date=18 July 2020 |language=fr |location=Aix-en-Provence |publisher=Edisud |doi=10.4000/encyclopedieberbere.1875 |access-date=31 August 2022|doi-access=free }}</ref><ref>{{cite book |last=Pomel |first=Auguste |author-link1=Auguste Pomel |date=1893 |title=''Bubalus antiquus'' |url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/13747484|series=Carte de Géologie de l'Algérie – Paléontologie Monographies de Vertébrés |language=fr |location=Algiers |publisher=imprimerie P. Fontana |pages=1–94, pl.1–10 |doi=10.5962/bhl.title.13867}}</ref> and the distance between the tips of its horns was as large as {{cvt|2.4|m}}.<ref name=Berbere1992/> The African ''[[Pelorovis]]'' reached {{cvt|2|t|ST}} in weight and had bony cores of the horns about {{cvt|1|m}} long.{{sfn|Palmer|1999|p=281}} The North American ''[[Bison antiquus]]'' reached up to {{cvt|4.6|m|ft}} long, {{cvt|2.27|m|ft}} tall, weight of {{cvt|1588|kg|lb}},<ref>{{cite web |url=http://www.warkworthwesternweekend.com/competitors.php |title=Warkworth Western Weekend Rodeo {{pipe}} Competitors |access-date=30 May 2012 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110821104228/http://warkworthwesternweekend.com/competitors.php |archive-date=21 August 2011 }}</ref> and horn span of {{cvt|1|m|ft}}.<ref name=SanDiego/> Aside from local populations and subspecies of extant species, such as the gaur population in Sri Lanka, European bison in British Isles, [[Caucasian wisent]] and [[Carpathian wisent]], the largest modern extinct bovid is [[aurochs]] (''Bos primigenius'') with an average height at the shoulders of {{cvt|155|-|180|cm}} in bulls and {{cvt|135|-|155|cm}} in cows, while aurochs populations in Hungary had bulls reaching {{cvt|155|–|160|cm}}.<ref>{{cite journal| last1=Kysely |first1=René |title=Aurochs and potential crossbreeding with domestic cattle in Central Europe in the Eneolithic period. A metric analysis of bones from the archaeological site of Kutná Hora-Denemark (Czech Republic) |journal=Anthropozoologica |volume=43 |issue=2| page=2008}}</ref> The [[kouprey]] (''[[Bos sauveli]]''), reaching {{cvt|1.7|-|1.9|m}} in shoulder height,<ref>{{cite web |url=http://www.arkive.org/kouprey/bos-sauveli/#text=Facts|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110810180727/http://www.arkive.org/kouprey/bos-sauveli/#text=Facts|archive-date=10 August 2011 |title=Kouprey (Bos sauveli)}}</ref><ref>{{cite book |editor-last1=Burnie |editor-first1=D. |editor-last2=Wilson |editor-first2=D.E. |title=Animal: The Definitive Visual Guide to the World's Wildlife |publisher=DK Adult |year=2005 |isbn=0-7894-7764-5}}</ref> has existed since the [[Middle Pleistocene]]<ref>{{cite journal |author=K. Suraprasit |author2=J.-J. Jaegar |author3=Y. Chaimanee |author4=O. Chavasseau |author5=C. Yamee |author6=P. Tian |author7=S. Panha|title=The Middle Pleistocene vertebrate fauna from Khok Sung (Nakhon Ratchasima, Thailand): biochronological and paleobiogeographical implications|journal=ZooKeys |date=2016|issue=613 |pages=1–157|doi=10.3897/zookeys.613.8309 |pmid=27667928 |pmc=5027644 |doi-access=free |bibcode=2016ZooK..613....1S}}</ref> and is also considered to be possibly extinct.<ref>{{cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=t4jjBwAAQBAJ&dq=kouprey+Bos+sauveli&pg=PA446 |title=Van Nostrand's Scientific Encyclopedia |publisher=Springer US|date=2013|access-date=28 August 2022|page=446|author1=Douglas M. Considine |author2=Glenn D. Considine|isbn=978-1-4757-6918-0}}</ref><ref>{{cite book |first=Andy D. |last=Herring |date=2014 |title=Beef Cattle Production Systems |publisher=CABI |page=22 |isbn=978-1-78064-507-0 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=NSExBQAAQBAJ&dq=kouprey+Bos+sauveli&pg=PA22 |access-date=28 August 2022}}</ref> * The long-legged ''[[Megalotragus]]'' is possibly the largest known [[Alcelaphinae|alcelaphine]] bovid,<ref>{{cite book | isbn = 978-90-481-9962-4 | url = https://books.google.com/books?id=s9Lj-EJEbgsC | title = Paleontology and Geology of Laetoli: Human Evolution in Context. Volume 2: Fossil Hominins and the Associated Fauna | publisher = Springer Netherlands | date = 27 January 2011 | access-date = 17 September 2022 | page = 404 | author = Terry Harrison }}</ref> bigger than the extant [[wildebeest]].<ref>{{cite book | url = https://books.google.com/books?id=U-dJAAAAYAAJ | title = Discovery. Magazine of the Peabody Museum of Natural History, Yale University. Volumes 13–16 | publisher = Peabody Museum of Natural History | date = 1978 | access-date = 17 September 2022 | page = 9 }}</ref> The tips of horns of ''M. priscus'' were located at a distance of about {{cvt|1.2|m}} from each other.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Churchill |first1=S.E. |last2=Brink |first2=J.S. |last3=Berger |first3=L.R. |last4=Hutchison |first4=R.A. |last5=Rossouw |first5=L. |last6=Stynder |first6=D. |last7=Hancox |first7=P.J. |last8=Brandt |first8=D. |last9=Woodborne |first9=S. |last10=Loock |first10=J.C. |last11=Scott |first11=L. |last12=Ungar |first12=P. |title=Erfkroon: a new Florisian fossil locality from fluvial contexts in the western Free State, South Africa |journal=South African Journal of Science |volume=96 |issue=4 |date=2000 |pages=161–163 |hdl=10520/AJA00382353_8897}}</ref> [[File:WLANL - Urville Djasim - Iers reuzenhert - Irish elk (2).jpg|thumb|''[[Megaloceros giganteus]]'']] * The extinct [[Cervidae|cervid]] [[Irish elk]] (''Megaloceros giganteus'') reached over {{cvt|7|ft|m|order=flip}} in height, {{cvt|1500|lb|kg|order=flip}} in mass and could have antlers spanning up to {{cvt|4.3|m}} across, about twice the maximum span for a moose's antlers.<ref>{{cite web|author=David Petersen |date=1989|title=Of Moose, Megaloceros and Miracles |url=http://www.motherearthnews.com/Nature-Community/1989-03-01/Of-Moose-Megaloceros-and-Miracles.aspx |website=Motherearthnews.com |archive-date=4 May 2017 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170504045414/http://www.motherearthnews.com/nature-and-environment/deer-elk-and-moose-zmaz89mazraw}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Moen |first1=Ron A. |first2=John |last2=Pastor |first3=Yosef |last3=Cohen |title=Antler growth and extinction of Irish elk |journal=Evolutionary Ecology Research |year=1999 |pages=235–249 |url=https://www.evolutionary-ecology.com/issues/v01n02/iiar1026.pdf }}</ref> The [[giant moose]] (''[[Cervalces latifrons]]'') reached {{cvt|2.1|to|2.4|m|ft}} high<ref>{{cite conference |last1=Kevrekidis |first1=Charalampos |last2=Kostopoulos |first2=Dimitris S. |title=The southernmost occurrence of Cervalces latifrons (Johnson, 1874) (Artiodactyla: Cervidae) in Europe |date=2017 |doi=10.13140/RG.2.2.24751.53928 |doi-access=free |conference=44. Treffen des Arbeitskreises Wirbeltierpaläontologie, 24-26.3.2017, Münster}}</ref> and was twice as heavy as the Irish elk but its antler span at {{cvt|2.5|m|ft}} was smaller than that of ''Megaloceros''.<ref name=Geist>{{Cite book |first=Valerius |last=Geist |title=Deer of the world: their evolution, behaviour, and ecology |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=bcWZX-IMEVkC&pg=PA122 |isbn=978-0-8117-0496-0 |year=1998 |publisher=Stackpole Books |pages=111, 126, 247–250}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |author=Breda, Marzia |date=2010 |url=http://www.nhm.ac.uk/nature-online/species-of-the-day/evolution/cervalces-latifrons/ |title=''Cervalces latifrons'' |publisher=Natural History Museum |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140104110532/http://www.nhm.ac.uk/nature-online/species-of-the-day/evolution/cervalces-latifrons/ |archive-date=4 January 2014}}</ref> North American [[stag-moose]] (''[[Cervalces scotti]]'') reached {{convert|2.5|m|ft}} in length and a weight of {{convert|708.5|kg|lb}}.<ref>{{cite web |last=Strauss |first=Bob |url=https://www.thoughtco.com/stag-moose-cervalces-scotti-1093143 |title=Stag Moose – Facts and Figures |publisher=Thoughtco.com |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211104202804/https://www.thoughtco.com/stag-moose-cervalces-scotti-1093143 |archive-date=4 November 2021|access-date=11 October 2022}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |url=http://laignoranciadelconocimiento.blogspot.com.es/2011/12/cervalces.html |title=Cervalces |website=Escenarios prehistóricos |publisher=Laignoranciadelconocimiento.blogspot.com.es |date=23 December 2011 |language=es |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220127114501/https://laignoranciadelconocimiento.blogspot.com/2011/12/cervalces.html |archive-date=27 January 2022 |access-date=11 October 2022}}</ref> * The largest known [[Giraffidae|giraffid]], aside from the extant giraffe, is ''[[Sivatherium]]'', with a body weight of {{cvt|1250|kg}}.<ref>{{cite journal | title = The extinct, giant giraffid ''Sivatherium giganteum'': skeletal reconstruction and body mass estimation | journal = [[Biology Letters]] | date = January 2016 | volume = 12 | issue = 1 | article-number = 20150940 | doi = 10.1098/rsbl.2015.0940 |pmid = 26763212| pmc = 4785933 | first1 = Christopher | last1 = Basu | first2 = Peter L. | last2 = Falkingham | first3 = John R. | last3 = Hutchinson}}</ref> * The largest [[Protoceratidae|protoceratid]] was ''[[Synthetoceras]]'', it reached {{cvt|2|m}} long and {{cvt|150|–|200|kg}} in mass.{{sfn|Palmer|1999|p=273}}<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Janis |first1=Christine M. |last2=Theodor |first2=Jessica M. |last3=Boisvert |first3=Bethany |title=Locomotor evolution in camels revisited: a quantitative analysis of pedal anatomy and the acquisition of the pacing gait |journal=Journal of Vertebrate Paleontology |date=14 March 2002 |volume=22 |issue=1 |pages=110–121 |doi=10.1671/0272-4634(2002)022[0110:LEICRA]2.0.CO;2 }}</ref> * The largest known wild [[suidae|suid]] to ever exist was ''[[Kubanochoerus|Kubanochoerus gigas]]'', having measured up to {{cvt|500|kg}} and stood around {{cvt|1|m}} tall at the shoulder.<ref>[https://archive.today/20130213095333/http://www.tesorosnaturales.es/en/catalogo.php?categoria=9&producto=141 Teeth: Kubanochoerus gigas lii (GUAN)]. tesorosnaturales.es</ref> ''[[Megalochoerus]]'' could be similar in size, possibly weighing {{cvt|303|kg}} or {{cvt|526|kg}}.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Pickford |first1=M. |title=New suoid specimens from Gebel Zelten, Libya |journal=Estudios Geológicos |date=2006 |volume=62 |issue=1 |pages=499–514 |doi=10.3989/egeol.0662147|doi-access=free }}</ref> * The largest [[tayassuidae|tayassuid]] extinct ''[[Platygonus]]'' species were similar in size to modern peccaries especially [[giant peccary]], at around {{convert|1|m|ft|abbr=on}} in body length, and had long legs, allowing them to run well. They also had a [[pig]]-like snout and long [[tusk]]s which were probably used to fend off predators.{{sfn|Palmer|1999|p=269}} * The largest [[camelidae|camelid]] was ''[[Titanotylopus]]'' from the [[Miocene]] of North America. It possibly reached {{cvt|2485.6|kg}} and a shoulder height of over {{cvt|3.4|m}}.<ref>[http://www.intechinc.com/disappeared-species/giant-camel.html Giant Camel Disappeared Species] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131110150125/http://www.intechinc.com/disappeared-species/giant-camel.html |date=10 November 2013 }}. Intechinc.com (5 July 2011)</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Mendoza |first1=M. |last2=Janis |first2=C. M. |last3=Palmqvist |first3=P. |title=Estimating the body mass of extinct ungulates: a study on the use of multiple regression |journal=Journal of Zoology |date=September 2006 |volume=270 |issue=1 |pages=90–101 |doi=10.1111/j.1469-7998.2006.00094.x }}</ref> The [[Syrian camel]] was twice as big as the modern camels.<ref name=GiantCamel2006/> It was {{cvt|3|m}} at the shoulder<ref>{{cite book | url = https://books.google.com/books?id=XhNzEAAAQBAJ&dq=Camelus+moreli+3&pg=PT119 | title = Néandertal, un parent: À la découverte de nos origines | publisher = Delachaux et Niestlé | date = 17 June 2022 | access-date = 28 August 2022 | author = Rebecca Wragg Sykes| isbn = 978-2-603-02968-8 | author-link = Rebecca Wragg Sykes }}</ref> and {{cvt|4|m}} tall.<ref name=GiantCamel2006>{{cite news|title=Giant camel fossil found in Syria |url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/2/hi/science/nature/6035113.stm|access-date=17 April 2013|newspaper=[[BBC News]] |date=10 October 2006|archive-date=27 September 2019|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190927080959/http://news.bbc.co.uk/2/hi/science/nature/6035113.stm|url-status=live}}</ref> ''[[Camelops]]'' had legs 20% longer than that of the [[dromedary]] and was about {{convert|2.3|m|ftin|abbr=on}} tall at the shoulder, weighing about {{convert|1000|kg|lb|abbr=on}}.<ref>Anthony J. Stuart, 2021, ''Vanished Giants: The Lost World of the Ice Age'', "6.17 Yesterday's Camel: Camelops Hesternus", p.99, [[University of Chicago Press]]</ref> * The [[anoplotheriid]] ''[[Anoplotherium]]'' is thought to have been capable of reaching up to {{cvt|271|kg}} in the case of ''A. commune'' and {{cvt|229|kg}} in the case of ''A. latipes''.<ref>{{cite journal|last1=Badiola|first1=Ainara|last2=De Vicuña|first2=Nahia Jiménez|last3=Perales-Gogenola|first3=Leire|last4=Gómez-Olivencia|first4=Asier|year=2023|title=First clear evidence of Anoplotherium (Mammalia, Artiodactyla) in the Iberian Peninsula: an update on the Iberian anoplotheriines|journal=The Anatomical Record: Advances in Integrative Anatomy and Evolutionary Biology|volume=308 |issue=6 |pages=1783–1800 |doi=10.1002/ar.25238|pmid=37221992 |s2cid=258864256 |doi-access=free|pmc=12062563}}</ref> ''A. latipes'' in particular could have measured more than {{cvt|2.5|m}} in length and {{cvt|1.25|m}} in shoulder height. Because it was probably capable of [[facultative bipedalism]], it could have been capable of standing over {{cvt|3|m}} tall.<ref>{{cite journal|last=Hooker|first=Jerry J.|year=2007|title=Bipedal browsing adaptations of the unusual Late Eocene–earliest Oligocene tylopod Anoplotherium (Artiodactyla, Mammalia)|journal=Zoological Journal of the Linnean Society |volume=151 |issue=3|pages=609–659|doi=10.1111/j.1096-3642.2007.00352.x|doi-access=free}}</ref>
==== Cetaceans (Cetacea) ==== {{further|Cetacean}} [[File:Perucetus colossus Holotype skeletal drawing.png|thumb|''[[Perucetus]]'' was the heaviest [[Archaeoceti|archeocete]]]] [[File:Basilosaurus SIZE 01.png|thumb|Size comparison between a human and two species of ''[[Basilosaurus]]'', ''B. cetiodes'' (dark blue) and ''B. isis'']] [[File:Livyatan melvillei skull.jpg|thumb|''[[Livyatan]]'' was a huge ancient sperm whale, the largest of the [[macroraptorial sperm whale]]s.]] * The heaviest [[Archaeoceti|archeocete]] was ''[[Perucetus]]'', with weight estimated at {{convert|85-340|t|abbr=on}}, while length is estimated at {{convert|17.0-20.1|m|ft|sp=us}}.<ref name="AT23">{{Cite journal |last1=Bianucci |first1=G. |last2=Lambert |first2=O. |last3=Urbina |first3=M. |last4=Merella |first4=M. |last5=Collareta |first5=A. |last6=Bennion |first6=R. |last7=Salas-Gismondi |first7=R. |last8=Benites-Palomino |first8=A. |last9=Post |first9=K. |last10=de Muizon |first10=C. |last11=Bosio |first11=G. |last12=Di Celma |first12=C. |last13=Malinverno |first13=E. |last14=Pierantoni |first14=P.P. |last15=Villa |first15=I.M. |year=2023 |title=A heavyweight early whale pushes the boundaries of vertebrate morphology |journal=Nature |doi=10.1038/s41586-023-06381-1 |last16=Amson |first16=E.|volume=620 |issue=7975 |pages=824–829 |pmid=37532931 |bibcode=2023Natur.620..824B |s2cid=260433513 |hdl=10281/434998 |hdl-access=free }}</ref> However, Motani and Pyenson in 2024 argued that it is extremely difficult for Perucetus to rival or exceed the blue whale in weight. They discussed that since Perucetus is much shorter than the blue whale in length, it should be at least 3.375 times denser or 1.83 times fatter to weigh heavier, which is impossible for vertebrates whose whole-body density range from 0.75 to 1.2. Motani and Pyenson tested the hypotheses of Bianucci and colleagues by performing various body mass estimation methods: the regression-based and volumetric mass estimation resulted in {{cvt|60–114|t|LT ST}} for a length range of {{cvt|17–20|m|ft}}, though the likely body mass range would fall within {{cvt|60–70|t|LT ST}} . They also claimed that the previous estimation is inflated by assumed isometry, and that the effect from pachyostosis on the estimation of body mass is not negligible as it resulted in underestimation.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Motani |first1=Ryosuke |last2=Pyenson |first2=Nicholas D. |date=29 February 2024 |title=Downsizing a heavyweight: factors and methods that revise weight estimates of the giant fossil whale Perucetus colossus |journal=PeerJ |language=en |volume=12 |article-number=e16978 |doi=10.7717/peerj.16978 |doi-access=free |pmid=38436015 |pmc=10909350 |issn=2167-8359}}</ref> The longest of known Eocene archeocete whales was ''[[Basilosaurus]]'' at {{Convert|17|-|20|m|abbr=on|adj=on}} in length.<ref name="New Archaeoceti 2027">{{Cite journal| last1 = Gingerich | first1 = P. D.| last2 = Arif | first2 = M| last3 = Bhatti | first3 = M Akram| last4 = Anwar | first4 = M| last5 = Sanders | first5 = William J| title = ''Basilosaurus drazindai'' and ''Basiloterus hussaini'', New Archaeoceti (Mammalia, Cetacea) from the Middle Eocene Drazinda Formation, with a Revised Interpretation of Ages of Whale-Bearing Strata in the Kirthar Group of the Sulaiman Range, Punjab (Pakistan)| year = 1997 | journal = Contributions from the Museum of Paleontology, University of Michigan | volume = 30 | issue = 2 | pages = 55–81| oclc = 742731913 | hdl = 2027.42/48652}}</ref><ref>Kellogg R. A review of the Archaeoceti. Carnegie Institution of Washington Publications. 1936; 482: 1–366.</ref><ref name=Basilo2019>{{cite journal |last1=Voss |first1=Manja |last2=Antar |first2=Mohammed Sameh M. |last3=Zalmout |first3=Iyad S. |last4=Gingerich |first4=Philip D. |title=Stomach contents of the archaeocete ''Basilosaurus isis'': Apex predator in oceans of the late Eocene |journal=PLOS ONE |volume=14 |issue=1 |at=e0209021 |year=2019 |doi=10.1371/journal.pone.0209021 |pmid=30625131 |pmc=6326415 |bibcode=2019PLoSO..1409021V |doi-access=free }}</ref> * The largest [[Squalodelphinidae|squalodelphinid]] was ''[[Macrosqualodelphis]]'' at {{cvt|3.5|m|ft}} in length.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Bianucci |first1=Giovanni |last2=Bosio |first2=Giulia |last3=Malinverno |first3=Elisa |last4=de Muizon |first4=Christian |last5=Villa |first5=Igor M. |last6=Urbina |first6=Mario |last7=Lambert |first7=Olivier |title=A new large squalodelphinid (Cetacea, Odontoceti) from Peru sheds light on the Early Miocene platanistoid disparity and ecology |journal=Royal Society Open Science |date=April 2018 |volume=5 |issue=4 |article-number=172302 |doi=10.1098/rsos.172302 |pmid=29765678 |pmc=5936943 |bibcode=2018RSOS....572302B |hdl=11568/955787 |hdl-access=free }}</ref> * Some [[Neogene]] [[rorqual]]s were comparable in size to modern huge relatives. ''[[Parabalaenoptera]]'' was estimated to be about the size of the modern [[gray whale]],<ref name=Demere2005>{{cite journal |last1=Deméré |first1=T.A. |last2=Berta |first2=A. |last3=McGowen |first3=M.R. |year=2005 |title=The taxonomic and evolutionary history of fossil and modern balaenopteroid mysticetes |journal=Journal of Mammalian Evolution |volume=12 |issue=1/2 |pages=99–143 |doi=10.1007/s10914-005-6944-3|s2cid=90231}}</ref> about {{cvt|16|m}} long. Some balaenopterids perhaps rivaled the [[blue whale]] in terms of size,<ref name=Demere2005/> though other studies disagree that any baleen whale grew that large in the [[Miocene]].<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Slater|first1=Graham J.|last2=Goldbogen|first2=Jeremy A.|author3-link=Nicholas Pyenson |last3=Pyenson |first3=Nicholas D.|date=31 May 2017 |title=Independent evolution of baleen whale gigantism linked to Plio-Pleistocene ocean dynamics |journal=Proceedings of the Royal Society B: Biological Sciences |volume=284|issue=1855|at=20170546 |doi=10.1098/rspb.2017.0546 |pmid=28539520 |pmc=5454272}}</ref> * The largest [[macroraptorial sperm whale]] is ''[[Livyatan]]'', with an estimated length of 44–57 ft (13.5–17.5 m) and an estimated weight of 62.8 short tons (57 tonnes).<ref name="GB">{{Cite journal| last1 = Lambert| first1 = Olivier| first2 = Giovanni | last2 = Bianucci | first3 = Klaas | last3 = Post | first4 = Christian | last4 = de Muizon | first5 = Rodolfo | last5 = Salas-Gismondi | first6 = Mario | last6 = Urbina | first7 = Jelle | last7 = Reumer| title = The giant bite of a new raptorial sperm whale from the Miocene epoch of Peru| journal = Nature| volume = 466| issue = 7302| pages = 105–108| year = 2010| doi = 10.1038/nature09067| pmid=20596020| url = https://pdfs.semanticscholar.org/6269/31c84504b36f1b2dff7f3a308873e525df6a.pdf| archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20171201033504/https://pdfs.semanticscholar.org/6269/31c84504b36f1b2dff7f3a308873e525df6a.pdf| archive-date = 1 December 2017| bibcode = 2010Natur.466..105L| s2cid = 4369352}}</ref>
=== Odd-toed ungulates (Perissodactyla) === [[File:Rhino sizes English.png|thumb|Relative sizes of †''[[Paraceratherium]]'', †''[[Elasmotherium]]'', [[white rhino]], [[Indian rhino]], [[black rhino]] and [[Sumatran rhino]] compared to a human]] [[File:Moropus elatus life restoration.jpg|thumb|Life restoration of ''[[Moropus elatus]]'']] * One of the largest known [[Perissodactyla|perissodactyls]], and the second largest land mammal (see ''[[Palaeoloxodon namadicus]]'') an unnamed species of [[Dzungariotherium]] may have been even larger than been estimated to be around {{cvt|20.6|tonne|ST|1}}.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Li |first1=Shijie |last2=Jiangzuo |first2=Qigao |last3=Deng |first3=Tao |date=6 July 2022 |title=Body mass of the giant rhinos (Paraceratheriinae, Mammalia) and its tendency in evolution |url=https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/08912963.2022.2095908 |journal=Historical Biology |language=en |pages=1–12 |doi=10.1080/08912963.2022.2095908 |issn=0891-2963 |url-access=subscription}}</ref> It’s more famous relatively, ''[[Paraceratherium]]'', also grew to large sizes. The largest individual known from the species ''Paraceratherium transouralicum'', was estimated at {{cvt|4.8|m|1}} tall at the shoulders, {{cvt|7.4|m|1}} in length from nose to rump, and {{cvt|17|tonne|ST|1}} in weight.<ref name="probos_mass">{{cite journal |last1=Larramendi |first1=Asier |title=Proboscideans: Shoulder Height, Body Mass and Shape |journal=Acta Palaeontologica Polonica |date=2015 |doi=10.4202/app.00136.2014 |doi-access=free }}</ref><ref name="Fortelius">{{Cite journal | doi = 10.1111/j.1096-3642.1993.tb02560.x| title = The largest land mammal ever imagined| journal = Zoological Journal of the Linnean Society| volume = 108| pages = 85–101| year = 1993| last1 = Fortelius | first1 = M. | last2 = Kappelman | first2 = J.}}</ref> * Some prehistoric horned [[Rhinoceros|rhinos]] also grew to large sizes. The biggest ''[[Elasmotherium]]'' reached up to {{cvt|5|-|5.2|m}} long,<ref name=Zhegallo2005>{{cite journal |last1=Zhegallo |first1=V. |last2=Kalandadze |first2=N. |last3=Shapovalov |first3=A. |last4=Bessudnova |first4=Z. |last5=Noskova |first5=N. |last6=Tesakova |first6=E. |year=2005 |title=On the fossil rhinoceros ''Elasmotherium'' (including the collections of the Russian Academy of Sciences) |journal=Cranium |volume=22 |issue=1 |pages=17–40 |url=http://www.rhinoresourcecenter.com/pdf_files/142/1429150376.pdf }}</ref> {{cvt|2.5|m}} high<ref name=Schvyreva2016>{{Cite book|last=Schvyreva|first=A.K.|date=2016|url=https://www.geokniga.org/bookfiles/geokniga-elasmoterii-pleystocena-evrazii.pdf|title=Эласмотерии плейстоцена Евразии|language=ru|pages=103–105}}</ref> and weighed {{cvt|3.5|-|5|t|ST}}.<ref name=Kosintsev2018>{{cite journal|last1= Kosintsev|first1= P.|last2= Mitchell|first2=K. J.|last3= Devièse|first3= T.|last4=van der Plicht|first4= J.|last5= Kuitems|first5= M.|last6= Petrova|first6= E.|last7= Tikhonov|first7= A.|last8= Higham|first8= T.|last9= Comeskey|first9= D.|last10= Turney|first10= C.|last11= Cooper|first11= A.|last12=van Kolfschoten|first12= T.|last13= Stuart|first13=A. J.|last14= Lister|first14=A. M.|title= Evolution and extinction of the giant rhinoceros ''Elasmotherium sibiricum'' sheds light on late Quaternary megafaunal extinctions|journal= Nature Ecology & Evolution|volume= 3|issue= 1|pages= 31–38|year= 2019|doi= 10.1038/s41559-018-0722-0|pmid= 30478308|s2cid= 53726338|url= https://ora.ox.ac.uk/objects/uuid:ae9eac15-c4a7-4adc-9ac2-ff3345b59489|hdl= 11370/78889dd1-9d08-40f1-99a4-0e93c72fccf3|hdl-access= free}}</ref><ref name=Zhegallo2005/><ref name=Schvyreva2016/> Such parameters make it the largest rhino of the [[Quaternary]].<ref name=Kosintsev2018/> [[Woolly rhinoceros]] (''[[Coelodonta antiquitatis]]'') of the same time reached {{convert|1100|-|1500|kg|lb|abbr=on}}<ref>{{cite web | url= http://www.hanskrause.de/HKHPE/hkhpe_07_02.htm | archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220828104116/http://www.hanskrause.de/HKHPE/hkhpe_07_02.htm | archive-date=28 August 2022 | title = Hkhpe 07 02 | first= Hans | last= Krause | work= hanskrause.de | year=2011 | access-date=10 September 2022 }}</ref> or {{convert|2000|kg|lb|abbr=on}},<ref>{{cite book | isbn = 978-0-306-46092-0 | url = https://books.google.com/books?id=UZLuF1YXYTcC | title = Extinctions in Near Time. Causes, Contexts, and Consequences | publisher = Springer US | date = 30 June 1999 | access-date = 17 September 2022 | page = 262 | author = Hans-Dieter Sues, Ross D.E. MacPhee }}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal | last1 = Boeskorov | first1 = G. G. | title = Some specific morphological and ecological features of the fossil woolly rhinoceros (''Coelodonta antiquitatis'' Blumenbach 1799) | doi = 10.1134/S106235901208002X | journal = Biology Bulletin | volume = 39 | issue = 8 | pages = 692–707 | year = 2012 | bibcode = 2012BioBu..39..692B | s2cid = 24868968 }}</ref> {{cvt|1.93|m}} at the shoulder height and {{cvt|4.6|m}} in length.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.trieboldpaleontology.com/specimens/mammals/cenozoic/coelodonta-antiquitatis-mammal/|title=Coelodonta antiquitatis (Mammal)|publisher=Triebold Paleontology, Inc|access-date=28 August 2022|language=en|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120602074449/http://www.trieboldpaleontology.com/specimens/mammals/cenozoic/coelodonta-antiquitatis-mammal/|archive-date=2 June 2012}}</ref> * ''[[Metamynodon]]'', an [[Amynodontidae|amynodontid]], reached {{cvt|4|m}} in length, comparable to ''Hippopotamus'' in measurement and shape.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://hoopermuseum.earthsci.carleton.ca/balucatherium/rhino3.htm|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20060908012918/https://hoopermuseum.earthsci.carleton.ca/balucatherium/rhino3.htm|archive-date=8 September 2006|title=Amynodonts}}</ref> * The [[giant tapir]] (''[[Tapirus augustus]]'') was the largest tapir ever, at about {{cvt|623|kg|lb}}<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Maclaren |first1=Jamie A |last2=Hulbert |first2=Richard C |last3=Wallace |first3=Steven C |last4=Nauwelaerts |first4=Sandra |title=A morphometric analysis of the forelimb in the genus Tapirus (Perissodactyla: Tapiridae) reveals influences of habitat, phylogeny and size through time and across geographical space |journal=Zoological Journal of the Linnean Society |date=5 October 2018 |volume=184 |issue=2 |pages=499–515 |doi=10.1093/zoolinnean/zly019 }}</ref> and {{cvt|1|m|ft}} tall at the shoulders.<ref name=DrobyshevskyCenozoic>{{cite book | isbn = 978-5-04-380567-6 | url = https://books.google.com/books?id=diNTEAAAQBAJ | title = Палеонтология антрополога. Том 3. Кайнозой (Paleontology of anthropologist. Volume 3. Cenozoic) | publisher = LitRes | date = 2021 | access-date = 19 September 2022 | pages = | author = [[Stanislav Drobyshevsky]] }}</ref> Earlier, this mammal was estimated even bigger, at {{cvt|1.5|m|ft}} tall, and assigned to the separate genus ''Megatapirus''.<ref name=DrobyshevskyCenozoic/> * The largest known [[lophiodont]] is ''[[Lophiodon]]'', with ''L. lautricense'' being estimated to reach more than {{cvt|2000|kg}} in weight.<ref>{{cite journal|last1=Robinet |first1=Céline |last2=Remy |first2=Jean A. |last3=Laurent |first3=Yves |last4=Danilo |first4=Laure |last5=Lihoreau |first5=Fabrice |year=2015 |title=A new genus of Lophiodontidae (Perissodactyla, Mammalia) from the early Eocene of La Borie (Southern France) and the origin of the genus Lophiodon Cuvier, 1822 |journal=Geobios |volume=48 |issue=1 |pages=25–38 |doi=10.1016/j.geobios.2014.11.003 |bibcode=2015Geobi..48...25R}}</ref> * One of the biggest [[chalicothere]]s was ''[[Moropus]]''.<ref>{{cite book | url = https://books.google.com/books?id=QCC9DwAAQBAJ&dq=Moropus+largest&pg=PA573 | title = The Fossil Book: A Record of Prehistoric Life | publisher = Dover Publications | date = 15 January 2020 | access-date = 25 August 2022 | page = 573 |author=[[Patricia Vickers-Rich|Patricia Vickers Rich]] |author2=Thomas Hewitt Rich |author3=[[Mildred Adams Fenton]] |author4=Carroll Lane | isbn = 978-0-486-83855-7 }}</ref> It stood about {{convert|8|ft|m|order=flip}} tall at the shoulder.<ref>Agate Fossils National Monument NPS Natural Resource Report NPS/NRPC/GRD/NRR-2009/080 (J. Graham, March 2009)</ref> * Late Eocene perissodactyls from the family [[Brontotheriidae]] attained huge sizes. The North American ''[[Megacerops]]'' (also known as ''Brontotherium''<ref name=Megacerops/>) reached {{cvt|2.5|m}} tall at the shoulders,<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.prehistory.com/bronto.htm|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130526204913/http://www.prehistory.com/bronto.htm|archive-date=26 May 2013|title=Brontotherium – Titanothere – Oligocene epoch}}</ref> {{cvt|5|m}} in length,<ref name=Megacerops>{{cite web|url=https://www.thoughtco.com/brontotherium-megacerops-1093175|title=Overview of Brontotherium (Megacerops)|author=Bob Strauss|work=About|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211121174908/https://www.thoughtco.com/brontotherium-megacerops-1093175|archive-date=21 November 2021}}</ref> and {{cvt|3-3.5|t|lb}} in weight.<ref name=Leahy1994/><ref>{{cite book |last=Fletcher |first=Tom |date=19 October 2023 |editor-last=Bright |editor-first=Michael|title=Life On Our Planet: A Stunning Re-examination of Prehistoric Life on Earth|publisher=Sourcebooks |page=259|chapter=8 The Age Of Ice And Fire|isbn=978-1-4642-1611-4}}</ref>''[[Embolotherium]]'' from Asia was equal in size.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://research.amnh.org/paleontology/perissodactyl/evolution/groups/brontotheriidae|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211120122610/https://research.amnh.org/paleontology/perissodactyl/evolution/groups/brontotheriidae|archive-date=20 November 2021|title=Brontotheriidae. American Museum of Natural History}}</ref> * The largest prehistoric horse was ''[[Equus giganteus]]'' of North America. It was estimated to grow to more than {{cvt|1250|kg|ST}} and {{cvt|2|m}} at the shoulders.<ref>{{cite journal |last=Eisenmann |first=Vera |title=Gigantic horses |journal=Advances in Vertebrate Paleontology |date=2003 |url=https://vera-eisenmann.com//IMG/pdf/136.Gigantic_horses.pdf}}</ref> The largest [[Anchitheriinae|anchitherine]] equid was ''[[Hypohippus]]'' at {{cvt|403|to|600|kg|lb}}, comparable to large modern [[domestic horse]]s.<ref name=MacFadden1992>{{cite book | isbn = 0-521-47708-5 | url = https://books.google.com/books?id=K1upTamSEW0C&dq=megahippus+kg&pg=PA284 | title = Fossil Horses: Systematics, Paleobiology, and Evolution of the Family Equidae | publisher = Cambridge University Press | date = 1992 | access-date = 21 September 2022 | page = 284 | author = Bruce J. MacFadden }}</ref><ref>{{cite book | url = https://books.google.com/books?id=I-RgojcDyWYC&dq=Megahippus&pg=PA545 | title = Evolution of Tertiary Mammals of North America: Volume 1, Terrestrial, Carnivores, Ungulates and Ungulatel-like mammals | publisher = Cambridge University Press | date = 1998 | access-date = 21 September 2022 | page = 545 | author = Kathleen M. Janis | isbn = 978-0-521-35519-3 }}</ref> ''[[Megahippus]]'' is another large anchitheriine. With the body mass of {{cvt|266.2|kg|lb}} it was much heavier than most of its close relatives.<ref name=MacFadden1992/> * Among the largest-sized genera of [[palaeotheres]], close relatives of horses, is ''[[Palaeotherium]]'', with ''P. giganteum'' being estimated to reach weights of more than {{cvt|700|kg}}.<ref name="iberian">{{cite journal |last1=Badiola |first1=Ainara |last2=Perales-Gogenola |first2=Leire |last3=Astibia |first3=Humberto |last4=Suberbiola |first4=Xabier Pereda |year=2022 |title=A synthesis of Eocene equoids (Perissodactyla, Mammalia) from the Iberian Peninsula: new signs of endemism|journal=Historical Biology|volume=34|issue=8|pages=1623–1631 |doi=10.1080/08912963.2022.2060098 |bibcode=2022HBio...34.1623B |s2cid=248164842 }}</ref> Previously until the naming of ''P. giganteum'' in 1994, ''P. magnum'' was considered the largest species of ''Palaeotherium'',<ref name="revisions">{{cite thesis |type=Inaugural Dissertation |last=Franzen|first=Jens L.|year=1968 |title=Revision der Gattung Palaeotherium Cuvier, 1804 (Palaeotheriidae, Perissodactyla, Mammalia) |volume=1 |publisher=University of Freiburg}}</ref> potentially reaching {{cvt|1.3|m}} in shoulder height and {{cvt|2.52|m}} in length.<ref name="skeleton2">{{cite book|last=Roman|first=Frédéric |year=1922|title=Monographie de la faune de mammifères de Mormoiron (Vaucluse) ludien supérieur|publisher=Société géologique de France |url=https://patrimoine.sorbonne-universite.fr/fonds/item/2894-monographie-de-la-faune-de-mammiferes-de-mormoiron-vaucluse-ludien-superieur}}</ref> Another palaeothere ''[[Cantabrotherium]]'' is estimated to have weighed about {{cvt|600|kg}}.<ref name="iberian"/>
=== Phenacodontids (Phenacodontidae) === The largest known [[Phenacodontidae|phenacodontid]] is ''[[Phenacodus]]''. It was {{cvt|1.5|m}} long<ref>{{cite book | isbn = 978-0-19-154517-7 | url = https://books.google.com/books?id=iBRREAAAQBAJ&dq=Phenacodus+1.5+m&pg=PA237 | title = The Origin and Evolution of Mammals | publisher = Oxford University Press, USA | date = 4 November 2004 | access-date = 21 September 2022 | page = 237 | author = T. S. Kemp }}</ref> and weighed up to {{cvt|56|kg}}.<ref>{{cite book |doi=10.1016/B0-12-370878-8/00065-3 |chapter=What Fossils Tell Us about the Evolution of the Neocortex |title=Evolution of Nervous Systems |date=2007 |last1=Jerison |first1=H.J. |pages=1–12 |isbn=978-0-12-370878-6 }}</ref>
=== Dinoceratans (Dinocerata) === [[File:Eobasileus.jpg|thumb|''[[Eobasileus]]'' was the largest of the [[Dinocerata|Dinoceratans]]]] The largest known [[dinocerata]]n was ''[[Eobasileus]]'' with skull length of {{cvt|102|cm}}, {{cvt|2.1|m}} tall at the back and {{cvt|1.5|m}} tall at the shoulder.<ref name=Rich2020>{{cite book | isbn = 978-0-486-83855-7 | url = https://books.google.com/books?id=QCC9DwAAQBAJ&dq=Eobasileus+weight&pg=PA555 | title = The Fossil Book: A Record of Prehistoric Life | publisher = Dover Publications | date = 15 January 2020 | access-date = 4 September 2022 | page = 555 |author=Patricia Vickers Rich |author2=Thomas Hewitt Rich |author3=Mildred Adams Fenton |author4=Carroll Lane Fenton }}</ref> Another huge animal of this group was ''[[Uintatherium]]'', with skull length of {{cvt|76|cm}}, {{cvt|1.5|m}} tall at the shoulder,<ref name=Rich2020/> {{cvt|4|m}} in length and {{cvt|2.25|t|ST}}, the size of a [[rhinoceros]].<ref name=Iceagemammals/> Despite their large size, ''Eobasileus'' as well as ''Uintatherium'' had a very small brain.<ref name=Rich2020/><ref name=Iceagemammals/>
=== Carnivores (Carnivora) === ==== Caniformia ==== [[File:ArctodusSimusReconstruct.jpg|thumb|''[[Arctodus simus]]'' reconstruction]] [[File:Chapalmalania.jpg|thumb|''[[Chapalmalania]]'', the giant [[Procyonidae|procyonid]]]] [[File:Epicyon haydeni LACM.jpg|thumb|Skeletal mount of ''[[Epicyon haydeni]]'']] * The largest terrestrial mammalian predator, as well the largest known [[bear]] and terrestrial carnivoran of all time, was ''[[Arctotherium angustidens]]'', the giant South American short-faced bear. A [[humerus]] of ''A. angustidens'' from [[Buenos Aires]] indicates that the males of the species could have weighed {{cvt|1,588|-|1,749|kg}} and stood at least {{cvt|11|ft|order=flip}} tall on their hind-limbs.<ref name="Soibelzon2">{{cite journal |last1=Soibelzon |first1=Leopoldo H. |last2=Schubert |first2=Blaine W. |title=The largest known bear, Arctotherium angustidens , from the early Pleistocene Pampean region of Argentina: with a discussion of size and diet trends in bears |journal=Journal of Paleontology |date=January 2011 |volume=85 |issue=1 |pages=69–75 |doi=10.1666/10-037.1 |hdl=11336/104215 |hdl-access=free }}</ref><ref>Dell'Amore, C. (2011): [https://archive.today/20120715031957/http://news.nationalgeographic.com/news/2011/02/110203-biggest-bear-largest-giant-short-faced-animals-science/ Biggest Bear Ever Found], [[National Geographic Society|National Geographic]] News, Published 3 February 2011</ref> Another huge bear was the giant North American short-faced bear (''[[Arctodus simus]]''), with the average weight of {{cvt|625|kg|lb}} and the maximum estimated at {{cvt|957|kg|lb}}.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Figueirido |first1=Borja |last2=Pérez-Claros |first2=Juan A. |last3=Torregrosa |first3=Vanessa |last4=Martín-Serra |first4=Alberto |last5=Palmqvist |first5=Paul |title=Demythologizing Arctodus simus , the 'short-faced' long-legged and predaceous bear that never was |journal=Journal of Vertebrate Paleontology |date=29 January 2010 |volume=30 |issue=1 |pages=262–275 |doi=10.1080/02724630903416027 |bibcode=2010JVPal..30..262F |hdl=10630/33066 |hdl-access=free }}</ref> There is a guess that the largest individuals of this species could reached even larger mass, up to {{cvt|1200|kg|lb}}.<ref name="Soibelzon2"/> The extinct [[cave bear]] (''[[Ursus spelaeus]]'') was also heavier than many recent bears. Largest males weighed as much as {{cvt|1000|kg|lb}}.<ref>{{cite news |title=Huge Cave Bears: When and Why They Disappeared |url=https://www.livescience.com/7622-huge-cave-bears-disappeared.html |work=Live Science |date=25 November 2008 }}</ref> The largest males of the possibly disrupted subspecies of [[brown bear]] (''Ursus arctos''), [[steppe brown bear]] (''Ursus arctos priscus''), is also estimated to have weighed {{cvt|1000|kg|lb}}.<ref name="estimate2">Marciszak, A., Schouwenburg, C., Lipecki, G., Talamo, S., Shpansky, A., Malikov, D., & Gornig, W. (2019). Steppe brown bear Ursus arctos "priscus" from the Late Pleistocene of Europe. Quaternary International, 534, 158–170. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.quaint.2019.02.042</ref> Another large bear was ''[[Agriotherium africanum]]'', this species was estimated to have initially estimated to have weighed {{convert|750|kg|sigfig=3}},<ref>{{cite book |title=The Marshall Illustrated Encyclopedia of Dinosaurs and Prehistoric Animals |publisher=Marshall Editions |year=1999 |isbn=1-84028-152-9 |editor=Palmer, D. |location=London |page=217}}</ref><ref>Burness, G. P., Diamond, J., & Flannery, T. (2001). Dinosaurs, dragons, and dwarfs: The evolution of maximal body size. ''Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences, '''98'''''(25), 14518–14523. https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.251548698</ref> but more recent estimates suggest it could've weighed {{convert|317|–|540|kg|sigfig=3}}.<ref name="bite">{{Cite journal |last1=Oldfield |first1=C. C. |last2=McHenry |first2=C. R. |last3=Clausen |first3=P. D. |last4=Chamoli |first4=U. |last5=Parr |first5=W. C. H. |last6=Stynder |first6=D. D. |last7=Wroe |first7=S. |date=2012 |title=Finite element analysis of ursid cranial mechanics and the prediction of feeding behaviour in the extinct giant Agriotherium africanum |journal=Journal of Zoology |language=en |volume=286 |issue=2 |page=171 |doi=10.1111/j.1469-7998.2011.00862.x |issn=1469-7998 |doi-access=}}</ref><ref name="Sorkin" /> ''[[Ailuropoda baconi]]'' from the Pleistocene was larger than the modern [[giant panda]] (''[[Ailuropoda melanoleuca]]'').<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Jin |first1=Changzhu |last2=Ciochon |first2=Russell L. |last3=Dong |first3=Wei |last4=Hunt |first4=Robert M. |last5=Liu |first5=Jinyi |last6=Jaeger |first6=Marc |last7=Zhu |first7=Qizhi |title=The first skull of the earliest giant panda |journal=Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences |date=26 June 2007 |volume=104 |issue=26 |pages=10932–10937 |doi=10.1073/pnas.0704198104 |doi-access=free |pmid=17578912 |pmc=1904166 |bibcode=2007PNAS..10410932J }}</ref> * The biggest [[Odobenidae|odobenid]] and one of the biggest [[pinniped]]s to have ever existed is ''[[Pontolis|Pontolis magnus]]'', with a skull length of {{cvt|60|cm}} (twice as large as the skulls of modern male [[walrus]]es)<ref name=Berta2017/> and having a total body length of more than {{cvt|4|m}}.<ref name="Xénia2021"/><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Churchill |first1=Morgan |last2=Clementz |first2=Mark T. |last3=Kohno |first3=Naoki |title=Cope's rule and the evolution of body size in Pinnipedimorpha (Mammalia: Carnivora) |journal=Evolution |date=January 2015 |volume=69 |issue=1 |pages=201–215 |doi=10.1111/evo.12560 |pmid=25355195 }}</ref> Only the modern male [[elephant seal]]s (''[[Mirounga]]'') reach similar sizes.<ref name="Xénia2021"> {{cite book | url = https://books.google.com/books?id=DeXEDwAAQBAJ&dq=largest+pinniped+%22pontolis%22&pg=PA17 | title = The Atlantic Walrus: Multidisciplinary Insights into Human-Animal Interactions | publisher = Charlotte Cockle | date = 2021 | access-date = 21 August 2022 | page = 17 |author=Xénia Keighley |author2=Morten Tange Olsen |author3=Peter Jordan |author4=Sean P.A. Desjardins | isbn = 978-0-12-817431-9 }}</ref> The second largest prehistoric pinniped is ''[[Gomphotaria pugnax]]'' with a skull length of nearly {{cvt|47|cm}}.<ref name=Berta2017> {{cite book | url = https://books.google.com/books?id=mHw6DwAAQBAJ&dq=Gomphotaria+skull+47+cm&pg=PA110 | title = The Rise of Marine Mammals: 50 Million Years of Evolution | publisher = Johns Hopkins University Press | date = 2017 | access-date = 21 August 2022 | page = 110 | first = Annalisa | last = Berta | isbn = 978-1-4214-2325-8 }}</ref> * One of the largest of prehistoric [[Otariidae|otariids]] is ''[[Thalassoleon]]'', comparable in size to the biggest extant [[fur seal]]s. An estimated weight of ''T. mexicanus'' is no less than {{cvt|295|–|318|kg|lb|sigfig=3}}.<ref name=Charles1977> {{cite book | url = https://books.google.com/books?id=lLxUAAAAYAAJ&dq=Thalassoleon+weight&pg=PA62 | title = Otarioid seals of the Neogene. Geological Survey Professional Paper, Volume 992 | publisher = Geological Survey (U.S.) | date = 1977 | access-date = 21 August 2022 | pages = 61–62 |author=István Fozy |author2=István Szente |author3=Gareth Dyke }}</ref> * The biggest known [[mustelid]] to ever exist was likely the giant otter, ''[[Enhydriodon|Enhydriodon omoensis]]''. It exceeded {{cvt|3|m}} in length, and would have weighed in at around {{cvt|200|kg}}, much larger than any other known mustelid, living or extinct.<ref name=Camille2022>{{cite journal|last1=Grohe|first1=Camille|last2=Uno|first2=Kevin|last3=Boisserie|first3=Jean-Renaud|year=2022|title=Lutrinae Bonaparte, 1838 (Carnivora, Mustelidae) from the Plio-Pleistocene of the Lower Omo Valley, southwestern Ethiopia: systematics and new insights into the paleoecology and paleobiogeography of the Turkana otters|language=French|journal=Comptes Rendus Palevol|volume=30|issue=30 |pages=684–693|doi=10.5852/cr-palevol2022v21a30|s2cid=252106648 |doi-access=free}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|doi=10.1080/02724634.2011.550356 | volume=31 | issue=2 | title=Enhydriodon dikikae, sp. nov. (Carnivora: Mammalia), a gigantic otter from the Pliocene of Dikika, Lower Awash, Ethiopia | journal=Journal of Vertebrate Paleontology | pages=447–453| year=2011 | last1=Geraads | first1=Denis | last2=Alemseged | first2=Zeresenay | last3=Bobe | first3=René | last4=Reed | first4=Denné | bibcode=2011JVPal..31..447G | s2cid=84797296 }}</ref><ref>{{cite magazine|url=https://www.wired.com/2011/03/the-bear-otter/|title=The Bear Otter|magazine=Wired|date=26 March 2011}}</ref> There were other giant otters, like ''[[Siamogale]]'', at around {{cvt|50|kg}}<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.sci-news.com/paleontology/siamogale-melilutra-giant-otter-04557.html|title=Siamogale melilutra: Giant Otter Fossils Reveal New Species – Paleontology – Sci-News.com|website=sci-news.com}}</ref> and ''[[Megalenhydris]]'', which was larger than a modern-day giant river otter.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://scienceblogs.com/tetrapodzoology/2007/12/20/island-endemic-otters-canids/|title=Islands of otters and strange foxes|website=scienceblogs.com}}{{self-published inline|date=August 2024}}</ref> ''[[Megalictis]]'' was the largest purely terrestrial mustelid<ref name=Valenciano2016>{{cite journal |last1=Valenciano |first1=Alberto |last2=Baskin |first2=Jon A. |last3=Abella |first3=Juan |last4=Pérez-Ramos |first4=Alejandro |last5=Álvarez-Sierra |first5=M. Ángeles |last6=Morales |first6=Jorge |last7=Hartstone-Rose |first7=Adam |title=Megalictis, the Bone-Crushing Giant Mustelid (Carnivora, Mustelidae, Oligobuninae) from the Early Miocene of North America |journal=PLOS ONE |date=7 April 2016 |volume=11 |issue=4 |article-number=e0152430 |doi=10.1371/journal.pone.0152430 |pmc=4824437 |pmid=27054570 |bibcode=2016PLoSO..1152430V |doi-access=free }}</ref> (although ''Enhydriodon'' had recently been mentioned as the largest mustelid that also happens to be a terrestrial predator<ref name=Camille2022/>). Similar in size to the [[jaguar]], ''Megalictis ferox'' had even wider skull, almost as wide as of the [[Ursus americanus|black bear]].<ref name=Valenciano2016/> Another large-bodied mustelid was the superficially cat-like ''[[Ekorus]]'' from the [[Miocene]] of Africa. At almost {{cvt|44|kg}}, the long-legged ''Ekorus'' was about the size of a [[wolf]].<ref> {{cite book | isbn = 978-1-4008-8445-2 | url = https://books.google.com/books?id=eiftDAAAQBAJ&dq=Ekorus++size&pg=PA144 | title = The Princeton Field Guide to Prehistoric Mammals | publisher = Princeton University Press | date = 2016 | access-date = 28 August 2022 | page = 144 | first = Donald R. | last = Prothero }}</ref> Other huge mustelids include ''[[Perunium]]''<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Orlov |first1=Jury A. |title=Perunium Ursogulo Orlov, A New Gigantic Extinct Mustelid (A Contribution to the Morphology of the Skull and Brain and to the Phylogeny of Mustelidae) |journal=Acta Zoologica |date=June 1948 |volume=29 |issue=1 |pages=63–105 |doi=10.1111/j.1463-6395.1948.tb00028.x }}</ref> and [[hypercarnivore|hypercarnivorous]] ''[[Eomellivora]]'', both from the [[Late Miocene]].<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Valenciano |first1=Alberto |last2=Abella |first2=Juan |last3=Sanisidro |first3=Oscar |last4=Hartstone-Rose |first4=Adam |last5=Álvarez-Sierra |first5=María Ángeles |last6=Morales |first6=Jorge |title=Complete description of the skull and mandible of the giant mustelid Eomellivora piveteaui Ozansoy, 1965 (Mammalia, Carnivora, Mustelidae), from Batallones (MN10), late Miocene (Madrid, Spain) |journal=Journal of Vertebrate Paleontology |date=4 July 2015 |volume=35 |issue=4 |article-number=e934570 |doi=10.1080/02724634.2014.934570 |bibcode=2015JVPal..35E4570V }}</ref> * The heaviest [[Procyonidae|procyonid]] was possibly the South American ''[[Chapalmalania]]''. It reached {{convert|1.5|m|ft}} in body length with a short tail and {{convert|150|kg|lbs}}, comparable in size to an [[American black bear]] (''Ursus americanus'').<ref>{{cite book |last1=Forasiepi |first1=Analía M. |last2=Prevosti |first2=Francisco J. |title=Evolution of South American mammalian predators during the Cenozoic: paleobiogeographic and paleoenvironmental contingencies |date=2018 |publisher=Springer |location=Cham |isbn=978-3-319-03701-1 |pages=124–125}}</ref> Another huge procyonid was ''[[Cyonasua]]'', which weighted about {{cvt|15|–|25|kg}}, about the same size as a medium-sized dog.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Tarquini |first1=Juliana |last2=Toledo |first2=Néstor |last3=Soibelzon |first3=Leopoldo H. |last4=Morgan |first4=Cecilia C. |title=Body mass estimation for †''Cyonasua'' (Procyonidae, Carnivora) and related taxa based on postcranial skeleton |journal=Historical Biology |date=March 2017 |volume=30 |issue=4 |pages=496–506 |doi=10.1080/08912963.2017.1295042|s2cid=90408657 |url=https://figshare.com/articles/journal_contribution/6073349 |hdl=11336/49670 |hdl-access=free }}</ref> * The largest [[canid]] of all time was ''[[Epicyon haydeni]]'', which stood {{cvt|90|cm}} tall at the shoulder, had a body length of {{cvt|2.4|m|ft}} and weighed {{cvt|100|–|125|kg|lb}},<ref>Díaz-Sibaja, R. (2010). "Titanes Vol. 1 Mamíferos." ''Fósil Revista de Paleontología''. {{ISSN|0717-9235}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last=Andersson|first=Ki|year=2005|title=Were there pack-hunting canids in the Tertiary, and how can we know?|journal=Paleobiology |volume=41|issue=4|pages=333–347|doi=10.1666/0094-8373(2005)031<0056:WTPCIT>2.0.CO;2 |bibcode=2005Pbio...31...56A |s2cid=85306826}}</ref><ref>{{cite book |last1=Wang |first1=Xiaoming |author-link1=Xiaoming Wang (paleontologist) |last2=Tedford |first2=Richard H. |author-link2=Richard H. Tedford |date=2008 |title=Dogs: Their Fossil Relatives & Evolutionary History}}</ref> with the heaviest known specimen weighing up to {{convert|170|kg|abbr=on}}.<ref name = "Sorkin"/> The extinct [[dire wolf]] (''Aenocyon dirus'') reached {{cvt|1.5|m|ft}} in length and weighed between {{cvt|50|and|110|kg|lb|sigfig=3}}.<ref name="Sorkin" /><ref>{{cite web |title=Wolves, Coyotes, and Dogs (Genus ''Canis'')|publisher=Museum.state.il.us |url=http://www.museum.state.il.us/exhibits/larson/canis.html |access-date=23 October 2011}}</ref> The largest [[wolf]] (''Canis lupus'') subspecies ever existed in Europe is the ''[[Pleistocene wolf#Canis lupus maximus|Canis lupus maximus]]'' from the [[Late Pleistocene]] of [[France]]. Its long bones are 10% larger than those of extant [[Subspecies of Canis lupus|European wolves]] and 20% longer than those of ''C. l. lunellensis''.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Boudadi-Maligne |first1=Myriam |title=Une nouvelle sous-espèce de loup (Canis lupus maximus nov. subsp.) dans le Pléistocène supérieur d'Europe occidentale |journal=Comptes Rendus Palevol |date=October 2012 |volume=11 |issue=7 |pages=475–484 |doi=10.1016/j.crpv.2012.04.003 |bibcode=2012CRPal..11..475B }}</ref> The Late Pleistocene [[Pleistocene wolf#Italian wolves|Italian wolf]] was morphometrically close to ''C. l. maximus''.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=F.BERTÈ |first1=DAVIDE |last2=PANDOLFI |first2=LUCA |title=Canis Lupus (Mammalia, Canidae) from the Late Pleistocene Deposit of Avetrana (Taranto, Southern Italy) |journal=Rivista italiana di Paleontologia e Stratigrafia |date=30 November 2014 |volume=120 |doi=10.13130/2039-4942/6079 }}</ref> * The largest [[Amphicyonidae|amphicyonid]] (bear-dogs) was a species of ''[[Pseudocyon]]'' weighing around {{cvt|773|kg}}, representing a very large individual.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Figueirido |first1=Borja |last2=Pérez-Claros |first2=Juan A. |last3=Hunt |first3=Robert M. |last4=Palmqvist |first4=Paul |title=Body Mass Estimation in Amphicyonid Carnivoran Mammals: A Multiple Regression Approach from the Skull and Skeleton |journal=Acta Palaeontologica Polonica |date=June 2011 |volume=56 |issue=2 |pages=225–246 |doi=10.4202/app.2010.0005 |doi-access=free }}</ref>
==== Feliformia ==== [[File:Male machairodus giganteus and felis catus.png|thumb|Comparison between ''[[Amphimachairodus]] giganteus'' and the modern [[domestic cat]]]] [[File:Panthera atrox La Brea.jpg|thumb|Skeleton of ''[[Panthera atrox]]'' at the La Brea Tar Pits Museum]] * The largest [[barbourofelinae]], as well as the largest [[Nimravidae|nimravid]], was ''[[Barbourofelis fricki]]'', with the shoulder height of {{convert|90|cm|in|abbr=on}} and could've weighed {{cvt|328|kg}}.<ref name="Barret 2021" /><ref>{{cite book |last=Antón |first=Mauricio |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=dVcqAAAAQBAJ&dq=barbourofelis+largest&pg=PA104 |title=Sabertooth |date=22 November 2013 |publisher=Indiana University Press |isbn=978-0-253-01049-0 |pages=104–107 |access-date=26 August 2022}}</ref> The largest [[nimravinae]] was ''[[Quercylurus|Quercylurus major]]'' as its fossils suggest it weighed {{cvt|200|kg}} similar in size to the modern-day [[lion]] and was [[scansorial]]. The closely related ''[[Dinailurictis|Dinailurictis bondali]]'' was slightly smaller weighing {{cvt|130|kg}}, making it as large as a lioness.<ref name="Peigne3">{{cite journal |last1=Peigne |first1=Stephane |date=May 2003 |title=Systematic review of European Nimravinae (Mammalia, Carnivora, Nimravidae) and the phylogenetic relationships of Palaeogene Nimravidae |url=https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/abs/10.1046/j.1463-6409.2003.00116.x |journal=Zoologica Scripta |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=199–229 |doi=10.1046/j.1463-6409.2003.00116.x |s2cid=86827900|url-access=subscription }}</ref> ''[[Eusmilus|Eusmilus adelos]]'' is considered the largest member of the [[Hoplophoninae|hoplophonine]] subfamily, reaching a weight of {{cvt|111|kg}}, comparable to a small [[African lion]].<ref name="Barret 2021">{{cite journal |last1=Barrett |first1=Paul Zachary |title=The largest hoplophonine and a complex new hypothesis of nimravid evolution |journal=Scientific Reports |date=26 October 2021 |volume=11 |issue=1 |article-number=21078 |doi=10.1038/s41598-021-00521-1 |pmid=34702935 |pmc=8548586 |bibcode=2021NatSR..1121078B |s2cid=240000358 }}</ref> While it was assumed that ''[[Hoplophoneus|Hoplophoneus occidentalis]]'' could've weighed {{cvt|160|kg}},<ref name=Sorkin /> other experts suggested it was smaller,<ref name="Barret 2021" /> being about the size of a [[leopard]].<ref name="Turner">{{cite book |last=Turner |first=Alan |url=https://archive.org/details/bigcatstheirfoss00turn |title=The Big Cats and their Fossil Relatives: an illustrated guide |publisher=Columbia University Press |year=1997 |isbn=978-0-231-10228-5 |location=New York |pages=[https://archive.org/details/bigcatstheirfoss00turn/page/n267 234] |url-access=limited}}</ref> A 2012 study suggested this species could've weighed {{cvt|65|kg}}.<ref name=":03">{{cite journal |last=Meachen |first=J. A. |year=2012 |title=Morphological convergence of the prey-killing arsenal of sabertooth predators |url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/261974255 |journal=Paleobiology |volume=38 |issue=1 |pages=1–14 |doi=10.1666/10036.1 |jstor=41432156 }}</ref> * The largest [[Machairodontinae|machairodont]] (saber-toothed cats) [[Felidae|felid]] was ''[[Amphimachairodus|Amphimachairodus kabir]]'', with the males possibly reaching {{cvt|350|-|490|kg}}.<ref name="Peignéde2005">{{cite journal |last1=Peigné |first1=S. |last2=de Bonis |first2=L. |last3=Likius |first3=A. |last4=Mackaye |first4=H. T. |last5=Vignaud |first5=P. |last6=Brunet |first6=M. |year=2005 |title=A new machairodontine (Carnivora, Felidae) from the Late Miocene hominid locality of TM 266, Toros-Menalla, Chad |journal=Comptes Rendus Palevol |volume=4 |issue=3 |pages=243–253 |bibcode=2005CRPal...4..243P |doi=10.1016/j.crpv.2004.10.002}}</ref> ''[[Smilodon|Smilodon populator]]'' was a close contender, with males weighing {{cvt|220|-|450|kg}}.<ref name=Sorkin /><ref name="Sherani2016"/> Another large member of the ''Amphimachairodus'' genus, ''A. horribilis'',<ref name=RuizRamoni2019>{{Cite journal|doi=10.1080/08912963.2019.1583750 |title=Taxonomic revision of a Machairodontinae (Felidae) from the Late Hemphillian of México |date=2020 |last1=Ruiz-Ramoni |first1=Damián |last2=Rincón |first2=Ascanio D. |last3=Montellano-Ballesteros |first3=Marisol |journal=Historical Biology |volume=32 |issue=10 |pages=1312–1319 |bibcode=2020HBio...32.1312R |s2cid=91277834 }}</ref><ref name=Tseng2023>{{Cite journal|doi=10.1080/08912963.2022.2067756 |title=Fast spread followed by anagenetic evolution in Eurasian and North American ''Amphimachairodus'' |date=2023 |last1=Wang |first1=Xiaoming |last2=Carranza-Castañeda |first2=Oscar |last3=Tseng |first3=Z. Jack |journal=Historical Biology |volume=35 |issue=5 |pages=780–798 |bibcode=2023HBio...35..780W |s2cid=248597661 |url=https://escholarship.org/uc/item/3nd8t0tq |url-access=subscription }}</ref> is estimated to weigh {{convert|405|kg|lb|abbr=on}}.<ref name="Deng 2016">{{cite journal |last1=Deng |first1=Tao |last2=Zhang |first2=Y. X |last3=Tseng |first3= Z. J |last4=Kuan-Su |first4= Hou |date=October 2016 |title=A skull of Machairodus horribilis and new evidence for gigantism as a mode of msaic evolution in machairodonts (Felidae, Carnivora) |journal=Vertebrata PalAsiatica |volume=54 |issue=4 |pages=302–318 |url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/309409331}}</ref> Another contender is ''[[Nimravides|Nimravides catocopis]]''<ref name="Jiangzuo2022">{{Cite journal |last1=Jiangzuo |first1=Qigao |last2=Li |first2=Shijie |last3=Deng |first3=Tao |date=2022 |title=Parallelism and lineage replacement of the late Miocene scimitar-toothed cats from the old and New World |journal=iScience |volume=25 |issue=12 |article-number=105637 |doi=10.1016/j.isci.2022.105637 |pmid=36505925 |pmc=9730133 |bibcode=2022iSci...25j5637J |url=https://www.cell.com/iscience/pdf/S2589-0042(22)01909-5.pdf}}</ref> with the largest specimen weighing up to {{convert|427|kg|lb|abbr=on}}.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Orcutt |first1=John D. |last2=Calede |first2=Jonathan J.M. |title=Quantitative Analyses of Feliform Humeri Reveal the Existence of a Very Large Cat in North America During the Miocene |journal=Journal of Mammalian Evolution |date=September 2021 |volume=28 |issue=3 |pages=729–751 |doi=10.1007/s10914-021-09540-1 }}</ref> An unnamed species of ''[[Xenosmilus]]'' thought to have weighed between {{convert|347|-|410|kg|lb}}.<ref name=":2">{{Cite journal |last1=Manzuetti |first1=Aldo |last2=Jones |first2=Washington |last3=Rinderknecht |first3=Andrés |last4=Ubilla |first4=Martín |last5=Perea journal |last1=Manthi |first1=Fredrick K. |last2=Brown |first2=Francis H. |last3=Plavcan |first3=Michael J. |last4=Werdelin |first4=Lars |title=Gigantic lion, Panthera leo , from the Pleistocene of Natodomeri, eastern Africa |journal=Journal of Paleontology |date=March 2018 |volume=92 |issue=2 |pages=305–312 |doi=10.1017/jpa.2017.68 |bibcode=2018JPal...92..305M }}</ref> The [[Panthera tigris soloensis|Ngangdong tiger]] (''Panthera tigris soloensis''), was estimated to have weighed up to {{cvt|486|kg}},<ref name="Sherani2016">{{cite journal |last=Sherani |first=Shaheer |title=A new specimen-dependent method of estimating felid body mass |journal=PeerJ Preprints |year=2016 |page=16 |doi=10.7287/peerj.preprints.2327v2 |url=https://peerj.com/preprints/2327.pdf |doi-access=free
