September 2022 | page = 30 |author=Anne Schmidt-Kuentzel |author2=Laurie Marker |author3=Lorraine K. Boast }}</ref> The North American ''[[Pratifelis]]'' was larger than the extant [[cougar]].<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Lane |first1=H. H. |title=Survey of the Fossil Vertebrates of Kansas: Part V: The Mammals (Continued) |journal=Transactions of the Kansas Academy of Science |date=1947 |volume=50 |issue=3/4 |pages=273–314 |doi=10.2307/3625600 |jstor=3625600 }}</ref>
* The largest [[viverrid]] known to have existed is ''[[Viverra leakeyi]]'', which was around the size of a [[wolf]] or [[Amur leopard|small leopard]] at {{cvt|41|kg}}.<ref>Alan Turner, ''National Geographic Prehistoric Mammals'' National Geographic, 2004, {{ISBN|0-7922-7134-3}}</ref> * The largest known extinct [[hyena]] was the [[Percrocutidae|percrocutid]] hyena<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Pérez-Claros |first=Juan A. |date=26 April 2023 |title=An ecomorphological characterization of the percrocutoid hyaenids: a multivariate approach using postcanine dentition |url=https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/02724634.2023.2197972 |journal=Journal of Vertebrate Paleontology |volume=42 |issue=5|article-number=e2197972 |doi=10.1080/02724634.2023.2197972 |url-access=subscription }}</ref><ref name="Xiong 2022">{{Cite journal |last=Xiong |first=Wuyang |date=1 May 2022 |title=New species of Percrocuta (Carnivora, Hyaenidae) from the early middle Miocene of Tongxin, China |journal=Historical Biology |volume=35 |issue=5 |pages=799–820 |doi=10.1080/08912963.2022.2067757 |issn=0891-2963 |s2cid=248627038}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last=Wuyang |first=Xiong |date=October 2019 |title=Basicranial morphology of Late Miocene Dinocrocuta gigantea (Carnivora: Hyaenidae) from Fugu, Shaanxi |url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/336578352 |journal=Vertebrata PalAsiatica |volume=57 |issue=4 |pages=274–307}}</ref>'', [[Dinocrocuta|Dinocrocuta gigantea]]''. It was originally estimated to have weighed {{Convert|380|kg|abbr=on}}.<ref>{{cite journal |last=Deng |first=Tao |author2=Tseng, Zhijie J. |year=2010 |title=Osteological evidence for predatory behavior of the giant percrocutid (Dinocrocuta gigantea) as an active hunter |journal=Chinese Science Bulletin |volume=55 |issue=17 |pages=1790–1794 |bibcode=2010ChSBu..55.1790D |doi=10.1007/s11434-010-3031-9 |s2cid=84720997}}</ref> However, recent weight estimates may suggest it may have weighed less. An individual with a skull length of {{Convert|32.2|cm|abbr=on}}, is estimated to have weighed {{Convert|200|kg|abbr=on}}.<ref name=":1">{{Cite journal |last1=Tseng |first1=Z. J. |year=2008 |title=Cranial function in a late Miocene Dinocrocuta gigantea (Mammalia: Carnivora) revealed by comparative finite element analysis |journal=Biological Journal of the Linnean Society |volume=96 |pages=51–67 |doi=10.1111/j.1095-8312.2008.01095.x |doi-access=free}}</ref> One specimen is reported to have a skull length of {{Convert|40|cm|abbr=on}}.<ref name=":4">{{Cite web |last=Antón |first=Mauricio |author-link=Mauricio Antón |date=12 January 2016 |title=SABERTOOTH'S BANE: INTRODUCING DINOCROCUTA |url=https://chasingsabretooths.wordpress.com/2016/01/12/sabertooths-bane-introducing-dinocrocuta/#:~:text=There%20was%20one%20monstrous%20carnivore,zoological%20family%20as%20modern%20hyenas. |access-date=12 January 2025 |website=chasing sabretooths |language=en}}</ref> ''[[Pachycrocuta|Pachycrocuta brevirostris]]'' was another large extinct hyena. It's estimated at {{convert|90|-|100|cm|in|abbr=on}} at the shoulder<ref>{{cite journal |last=Turner |first=Alan |author2=Antón, Mauricio |year=1996 |title=The giant hyaena ''Pachycrocuta brevirostris'' (Mammalia, Carnivora, Hyaenidae) |journal=Geobios |volume=29 |issue=#4 |pages=455–468 |doi=10.1016/S0016-6995(96)80005-2 |bibcode=1996Geobi..29..455T }}</ref> and {{cvt|190|kg}} weight.<ref name = "Sorkin"/> However, other experts leaned towards {{cvt|150|kg}} representing the upper end of the species.<ref name="Liu2021">{{cite journal |last1=Liu |first1= Jinyi |last2=Liu |first2= Jinyuan |last3=Zhang |first3=Hanwen |display-authors=et. al|date=2021|title=The giant short-faced hyena Pachycrocuta brevirostris (Mammalia, Carnivora, Hyaenidae) from Northeast Asia: A reinterpretation of subspecies differentiation and intercontinental dispersal |doi=10.1016/j.quaint.2020.12.031 |journal=Quaternary International |volume=577 |pages=29–51|bibcode= 2021QuInt.577...29L }}</ref> ''Crocuta'' hyenas have also been known large sizes, even larger than the extant spotted hyena, most notably the extinct [[spotted hyena]] subspecies, the [[cave hyena]], with average individuals weighing {{cvt|88|kg}}.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Sauqué |first1=V |last2=Rabal-Garcés |first2=R |last3=Madurell-Malaperia |first3=J |last4=Gisbert |first4=M |last5=Zamora |first5=S |last6=Torres |first6=T |last7=Ortíz |first7=J |last8=Cuenca-Bescós |first8=G |date=2017 |title=Pleistocene cave hyenas in the Iberian Peninsula: New insights from Los Aprendices cave (Moncayo, Zaragoza |url=http://palaeo-electronica.org/content/2017/1786-crocuta-spelaea-in-iberias |journal=Palaeontologia Electronica |language=en |doi=10.26879/622 |issn=1094-8074}}</ref> ''[[Crocuta eturno]]'' was another large ''Crocuta'' species with estimates suggesting this species could've weighed {{cvt|85|kg}}.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Hopley |first1=Philip J. |last2=Cerling |first2=Thure E. |last3=Crété |first3=Lucile |display-authors=et al. |date=2023 |title=Stable isotope analysis of carnivores from the Turkana Basin, Kenya: Evidence for temporally-mixed fossil assemblages |url=https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1040618222001276 |journal=Quaternary International |volume=650 |pages=12–27 |bibcode=2023QuInt.650...12H |doi=10.1016/j.quaint.2022.04.004}}</ref> * The extinct [[giant fossa]] (''[[Cryptoprocta spelea]]'') had a body mass in range from {{cvt|17|kg}}<ref>Personal communication from R. Dewer in Burness et al., 2001, table 1</ref> to {{cvt|20|kg}},<ref>Wroe et al., 2004, p. 297</ref> much larger than the modern [[Fossa (animal)|fossa]] weighs (up to {{cvt|8.6|kg}} for adult males<ref>{{cite book | editor1-last = Wilson | editor1-first = D. | editor2-last = Mittermeier | editor2-first = R. | editor2-link = Russell Mittermeier | title = Handbook of the Mammals of the World. Volume 1: Carnivores | year = 2009 | publisher = [[Lynx Edicions]] | isbn = 978-84-96553-49-1 | last1 = Goodman | first1 = S. | contribution = Family Eupleridae (Madagascar Carnivores) | url = http://www.lynxeds.com/hmw/species-accounts/hmw-1-species-accounts-red-panda-ailurus-fulgens | access-date = 31 May 2010 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20110725081653/http://www.lynxeds.com/hmw/species-accounts/hmw-1-species-accounts-red-panda-ailurus-fulgens | archive-date = 25 July 2011 }}</ref>).
=== Hyaenodonts (Hyaenodonta) ===
* The largest [[Hyainailouridae|hyainailourid]], as well as the largest [[Hyaenodonta|hyaenodont]] was ''[[Megistotherium|Megistotherium osteothlastes]]'' at {{cvt|500-880|kg|lb}}.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Savage |first=R. J. G. |date=1973 |title=Megistotherium, gigantic hyaenodont from Miocene of Gebel Zelten, Libya |url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/36949982#page/661/mode/1up |journal=Bulletin of the British Museum (Natural History), Geology |volume=22 |issue=7 |pages=483–511 |doi=10.5962/p.150151}}</ref><ref name="Sorkin" /> While its close relative, ''[[Simbakubwa|Simbakubwa kutokaafrika]]'', was estimated to reach {{cvt|208-1,554|kg|lb}}, the same regression suggested ''Megistotherium'' was larger than ''Simbakubwa''.<ref name=":3">{{cite journal |last1=Borths |first1=M. R. |last2=Stevens |first2=N. J. |year=2019 |title=''Simbakubwa kutokaafrika'', gen. et sp. nov. (Hyainailourinae, Hyaenodonta, "Creodonta," Mammalia), a gigantic carnivore from the earliest Miocene of Kenya |url=https://figshare.com/articles/journal_contribution/8009951 |journal=Journal of Vertebrate Paleontology |volume=39 |issue=1 |article-number=e1570222 |bibcode=2019JVPal..39E0222B |doi=10.1080/02724634.2019.1570222 |s2cid=145972918 |url-access=subscription}}</ref> ''[[Hyainailouros]]'' was another large hyainailourid. The largest species in the genus, ''H. bugtiensis'', which could've weighed between {{cvt|267-1744|kg|lb}}.<ref name=":3" /> * The largest [[Hyaenodontidae|hyaenodontid]] was ''[[Hyaenodon gigas]]'', weighing in at around {{cvt|378|kg}}.<ref name="Wang2008">{{Cite book |last1=Wang |first1=Xiaoming |title=Dogs: Their Fossil Relatives and Evolutionary History |last2=Tedford |first2=Richard H. |publisher=Columbia University Press |year=2008 |isbn=978-0-231-13528-3 |location=New York |page=1 |doi=10.7312/wang13528 |jstor=10.7312/wang13528}}</ref>
=== Oxyaenids (Oxyaenidae) === [[File:SarkastodonDB.jpg|thumb|right|''[[Sarkastodon]]'']] The largest known [[Oxyaenidae|oxyaenid]] was ''[[Sarkastodon]]'' weighing in at {{cvt|800|kg}}.<ref name = "Sorkin"/>
=== Mesonychians (Mesonychia) === Some [[mesonychia]]ns reached a size of a bear. Such large were ''[[Mongolonyx]]'' from Asia<ref name=Kramarenko>{{cite book | url = https://books.google.com/books?id=HMoRAQAAMAAJ | title = Зоогеография палеогена Азии |trans-title=Zoogeography of Paleogene of Asia |language=ru | publisher = Publishing office "Nauka" | date = 1974 | access-date = 18 September 2022 | pages = 113–114 | author = N. N. Kramarenko }}</ref> and ''[[Ankalagon]]'' from North America.<ref>{{cite journal |doi=10.1671/0272-4634(2000)020[0387:ANSOAM]2.0.CO;2 |jstor=4524103 |year=2000 |volume=20 |issue=2 |pages=387–93 |title=A new specimen of ''Ankalagon'' (Mammalia, Mesonychia) and evidence of sexual dimorphism in mesonychians |journal=Journal of Vertebrate Paleontology |last1=O'Leary |first1=Maureen A. |last2=Lucas |first2=Spencer G. |last3=Williamson |first3=Thomas E. |s2cid=86542114 }}</ref><ref>[http://www.paleocene-mammals.de/predators.htm Paleocene mammals of the world] "Carnivores, creodonts and carnivorous ungulates: Mammals become predators"</ref> Another large mesonychian is ''[[Harpagolestes]]'' with a skull length of a half a meter in some species.<ref name=Kramarenko/>
=== Bats (Chiroptera) === Found in Quaternary deposits of South and Central Americas, ''[[Desmodus draculae]]'' had a wingspan of {{convert|0.5|m|in|abbr=on}} and a body mass of up to {{convert|60|g|oz|abbr=on}}. Such proportions make it the largest [[vampire bat]] that ever evolved.<ref>{{cite news |last1=de Lazaro |first1=Enrico |title=100,000-Year-Old Fossil of Giant Vampire Bat Found in Argentina {{!}} Sci.News |url=https://www.sci.news/paleontology/desmodus-draculae-fossil-09898.html |work=Sci.News |date=26 July 2021 }}</ref>
=== Hedgehogs, gymnures, shrews, and moles (Eulipotyphla) === [[File:Deinogalerix koenigswaldi Naturalis.JPG|thumb|''[[Deinogalerix]]'' skeleton]] The largest known animal of the group [[Eulipotyphla]] was ''[[Deinogalerix]]'',<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Villier |first1=Boris |title=Deinogalerix: a giant hedgehog from the Miocene |journal=Sezione di Museologia Scientifica e Naturalistica |date=18 January 2013 |volume=6 |pages=93–102 |doi=10.15160/1824-2707/417 }}</ref> measuring up to {{cvt|60|cm}} in total length, with a skull up to {{cvt|21|cm}} long.<ref>{{cite journal | author = Freudenthal, M. | year = 1972 | title = ''Deinogalerix koenigswaldi'' nov. gen., nov. spec., a giant insectivore from the Neogene of Italy | journal = [[Scripta Geologica]] | volume = 14 | pages = 1–19}}</ref>
=== Rodents (Rodentia) === [[File:Giant Pleistocene Beaver, Castoroides ohioensis.jpg|thumb|The [[Castoroides|giant beaver]] (''Castoroides ohioensis'')]] * Several of the extinct South American [[Dinomyidae|dinomyids]] were much bigger than modern rodents. ''[[Josephoartigasia monesi]]'' was the largest-known [[rodent]] of all time, approximately weighing an estimated {{cvt|480–500|kg|lb}}.<ref name=Engelman>{{cite journal |last1=Engelman |first1=Russell K. |title=Resizing the largest known extinct rodents (Caviomorpha: Dinomyidae, Neoepiblemidae) using occipital condyle width |journal=Royal Society Open Science |date=June 2022 |volume=9 |issue=6 |article-number=220370 |doi=10.1098/rsos.220370|pmid=35719882 |pmc=9198521 |bibcode=2022RSOS....920370E }}</ref> ''[[Phoberomys pattersoni]]'' weighed {{cvt|125|-|150|kg|lb}}.<ref name=Engelman/> Both ''Josephoartigasia'' and ''Phoberomys'' reached about {{cvt|1.5|m}} tall at the shoulder.<ref name=Defler2018>{{cite book | url = https://books.google.com/books?id=-HWADwAAQBAJ&dq=Josephoartigasia&pg=PA153 | title = History of Terrestrial Mammals in South America | publisher = Springer International Publishing | date = 2018 | access-date = 27 August 2022 | pages = 151–154 | first = Thomas | last = Defler | isbn = 978-3-319-98449-0 }}</ref> Another huge dinomyid, ''[[Telicomys|Telicomys gigantissimus]]'' had a minimal weight of {{cvt|200|kg|lb}}.<ref name=Defler2018/> * ''[[Amblyrhiza inundata]]'' from the family [[Heptaxodontidae]] was a massive animal, it weighed {{cvt|50|-|200|kg|lb}}.<ref>{{Cite journal | last1= Biknevicus |first1= A. R. | last2 = McFarlane |first2=D. A. | last3 = MacPhee |first3=R. D. E. | title = Body size in ''Amblyrhiza inundata'' (Rodentia: Caviomorpha), an extinct megafaunal rodent from the Anguilla Bank, West Indies: Estimates and implications | journal = American Museum Novitates | issue = 3079 | pages = 1–25 | publisher = [[American Museum of Natural History]] | location = New York | date = 1993 | hdl = 2246/4976 }}</ref><ref name=Defler2018/> * The largest beaver was the [[Castoroides|giant beaver]] (''[[Castoroides]]'') of North America. It grew over 2 m in length and weighed roughly {{cvt|90|to|125|kg}}, also making it one of the largest rodents to ever exist.<ref name="R&S">{{cite journal |last1=Swinehart |first1=Anthony L. |last2=Richards |first2=Ronald L. |title=Palaeoecology of a Northeast Indiana Wetland harboring remains of the pleistocene giant beaver (Castoroides ohioenis) |journal=Proceedings of the Indiana Academy of Science |date=22 December 2001 |pages=151–167 |id={{Gale|A83518043}} |s2cid=130809143 }}</ref> * The largest old world porcupine are the ''[[Hystrix refossa]]'' was larger than living porcupines. It was approximately 20% larger than its closest relative, the living [[Indian porcupine]] (''H. indica''), reaching lengths of over {{cvt|115|cm}}.
=== Rabbits, hares, and pikas (Lagomorpha) === The biggest known prehistoric [[lagomorph]] is the [[Menorca|Minorcan]] giant rabbit ''[[Nuralagus]] rex'' at {{cvt|12|kg}}.<ref name="Milius 2011">{{cite journal |first= Susan |last= Milius |year=2011 |title=''The Bunny That Ruled Minorca'' |journal=[[Science News]] |volume=179 |issue=9 |page=18 |doi=10.1002/scin.5591790921}}</ref>
=== Pangolins (Pholidota) === The largest pangolin was the extinct ''[[Manis palaeojavanica]]''<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Gaudin |first1=Timothy J. |last2=Emry |first2=Robert J. |last3=Wible |first3=John R. |title=The Phylogeny of Living and Extinct Pangolins (Mammalia, Pholidota) and Associated Taxa: A Morphology Based Analysis |journal=Journal of Mammalian Evolution |date=December 2009 |volume=16 |issue=4 |pages=235–305 |doi=10.1007/s10914-009-9119-9 }}</ref> Its total length is measured up to {{convert|2.5|m|ft|abbr=on}}.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Hooijer |first1=D.A. |date=1947 |title=A femur of ''Manis palaeojavanica'' Dubois from Western Java |journal=Proceedings of the Koninklijke Nederlandsche Akademie van Wetenschappen |volume=50 |issue=4 |pages=423–418 }}</ref>
=== Primates (Primates) === [[File:Gigantopithecus.png|thumb|Reconstruction of ''[[Gigantopithecus blacki]]'']] * The largest known non-hominid [[primate]] is ''[[Gigantopithecus blacki]]''. Studies estimate heights around {{cvt|2.74-3.66|m}} tall, weighing {{cvt|225-300|kg}}.<ref name="ApeThatWas">{{cite web|last=Ciochon |first=Russell L |title=The Ape that Was – Asian fossils reveal humanity's giant cousin |url=http://www.uiowa.edu/%7Ebioanth/giganto.html |publisher=University of Iowa |access-date=7 September 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150525202625/http://www.uiowa.edu/~bioanth/giganto.html |archive-date=25 May 2015 }}</ref><ref name="Pettifor">{{cite book | last=Pettifor | first=Eric | title=Selected Readings in Physical Anthropology | chapter=From the Teeth of the Dragon: ''Gigantopithecus Blacki''| pages=143–149 | chapter-url=http://www.wynja.com/arch/gigantopithecus.html | year=2000 | orig-date=1995 | publisher=Kendall/Hunt Publishing Company | isbn=978-0-7872-7155-8 | access-date=7 September 2022 | archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160305124349/http://www.wynja.com/arch/gigantopithecus.html |archive-date=5 March 2016 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Zhang |first1=Yingqi |last2=Harrison |first2=Terry |title=Gigantopithecus blacki: a giant ape from the Pleistocene of Asia revisited |journal=American Journal of Physical Anthropology |date=January 2017 |volume=162 |issue=S63 |pages=153–177 |doi=10.1002/ajpa.23150 |pmid=28105715 |bibcode=2017AJPA..162S.153Z |s2cid=46838584 |doi-access=free }}</ref> Some suggested that it would not exceed {{cvt|2.3|m}} tall with a bipedal posture.<ref name=":6">{{Cite journal |last1=Gayford |first1=Joel H. |last2=Engelman |first2=Russell K. |last3=Sternes |first3=Phillip C. |last4=Itano |first4=Wayne M. |last5=Bazzi |first5=Mohamad |last6=Collareta |first6=Alberto |last7=Salas-Gismondi |first7=Rodolfo |last8=Shimada |first8=Kenshu |date=September 2024 |title=Cautionary tales on the use of proxies to estimate body size and form of extinct animals |journal=Ecology and Evolution |language=en |volume=14 |issue=9 |article-number=e70218 |doi=10.1002/ece3.70218 |pmid=39224151 |issn=2045-7758 |pmc=11368419|bibcode=2024EcoEv..1470218G }}</ref> Another giant ape was ''[[Meganthropus palaeojavanicus]]'' at {{cvt|2.4|m}} in body height,<ref>{{cite report |type=Preprint |doi=10.20944/preprints202011.0504.v1 |doi-access=free |title=Steps of the Asian Giants: Modeling the Body Size Related Foraging Ecology of ''Meganthropus palaeojavanicus'', a 8 Feet Hominid in Central Java |date=2020 |last1=Wibowo |first1=Andri }}</ref> although it is known from very poor remains.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Zanolli |first1=Clément |last2=Kullmer |first2=Ottmar |last3=Kelley |first3=Jay |last4=Bacon |first4=Anne-Marie |last5=Demeter |first5=Fabrice |last6=Dumoncel |first6=Jean |last7=Fiorenza |first7=Luca |last8=Grine |first8=Frederick E. |last9=Hublin |first9=Jean-Jacques |last10=Nguyen |first10=Anh Tuan |last11=Nguyen |first11=Thi Mai Huong |last12=Pan |first12=Lei |last13=Schillinger |first13=Burkhard |last14=Schrenk |first14=Friedemann |last15=Skinner |first15=Matthew M. |last16=Ji |first16=Xueping |last17=Macchiarelli |first17=Roberto |title=Evidence for increased hominid diversity in the Early to Middle Pleistocene of Indonesia |journal=Nature Ecology & Evolution |date=8 April 2019 |volume=3 |issue=5 |pages=755–764 |doi=10.1038/s41559-019-0860-z |pmid=30962558 |bibcode=2019NatEE...3..755Z |url=https://kar.kent.ac.uk/72814/1/01-Indonesian_hominid_paleobiodiversity_v2.pdf }}</ref> * During the [[Pleistocene]], some [[archaic humans]] were close in sizes or even larger than [[early modern humans]]. [[Neanderthals]] (''[[Homo neanderthalensis]]'') reached {{cvt|77.6|kg|lb}} and {{cvt|66.4|kg|lb}} in average weight for males and females, respectively, larger than the parameters of modern humans (''[[Homo sapiens]]'') ({{cvt|68.5|kg|lb}} and {{cvt|59.2|kg|lb}} for males and females, respectively).<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Froehle AW, Churchill SE |year=2009 |title=Energetic Competition Between Neandertals and Anatomically Modern Humans |journal=PaleoAnthropology |pages=96–116|url=http://www.paleoanthro.org/journal/content/PA20090096.pdf |access-date=12 September 2022}}</ref> A [[tibia]] from [[Kabwe]] ([[Zambia]]) indicates an indeterminate ''Homo'' individual of possibly {{cvt|181.2|cm}} in height. It was one of the tallest humans of the [[Middle Pleistocene]] and noticeably large even compared to recent humans.<ref name=EarlyHumans>{{cite journal |doi=10.1016/j.jhevol.2011.11.004 |title=Stature estimation from complete long bones in the Middle Pleistocene humans from the Sima de los Huesos, Sierra de Atapuerca (Spain) |year=2012 |last1=Carretero |first1=José-Miguel |last2=Rodríguez |first2=Laura |last3=García-González |first3=Rebeca |last4=Arsuaga |first4=Juan-Luis |last5=Gómez-Olivencia |first5=Asier |last6=Lorenzo |first6=Carlos |last7=Bonmatí |first7=Alejandro |last8=Gracia |first8=Ana |last9=Martínez |first9=Ignacio |journal=Journal of Human Evolution |volume=62 |issue=2 |pages=242–255 |pmid=22196156 |bibcode=2012JHumE..62..242C |url=https://eprints.ucm.es/26998/1/1-s2.0-S0047248411002193-_01.pdf}}</ref> The tallest ''Homo sapiens'' individuals from the Middle Pleistocene of [[Spain]] reached {{cvt|194|cm}} and {{cvt|174|cm}} for males and females, respectively.<ref name=EarlyHumans/> Some ''[[Homo erectus]]'' could be as large as {{cvt|185|cm}} tall and {{cvt|68|kg|lb}} in weight.<ref>{{cite book | isbn = 978-0-674-60075-1 | url = https://books.google.com/books?id=RGGVsCPuv1cC&dq=homo+erectus+tall&pg=PA412 | title = The Nariokotome Homo erectus skeleton | publisher = Harvard University Press | date = 1993 | access-date = 2 October 2022 | page = 412 | author = Alan Walker, Richard Leakey }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Migliano |first1=Andrea Bamberg |last2=Guillon |first2=Myrtille |title=The |doi=10.1086/667694 }}</ref> * The heaviest known [[Old World monkey]] is the prehistoric baboon ''[[Theropithecus oswaldi]]'' which have weighed {{cvt|72|kg|lb}},<ref>{{cite book |author1=Jablonski, N.G. |author2=Leakey, M.G. |author3=Anton, M. |year=2008 |chapter=Systematic Paleontology of the Cercopithecines |editor1=Jablonski, N.G. |editor2=Leakey, M.G. |series=Koobi Fora Research Project |volume=6 |title=The Fossil Monkeys|pages=103–300 |publisher=California Academy of Sciences |location=San Francisco |url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/236259998|access-date=10 September 2022}}</ref> some even suggested to reach {{cvt|128|kg|lb}}.<ref name="BM">{{Cite journal |last1=Delson |first1=Eric |last2=Terranova |first2=Carl J. |last3=Jungers |first3=William J. |last4=Sargis |first4=Sargis |last5=Jablonski |first5=Nina G. |last6=Dechow |first6=Paul C. |date=2000 |title=Body mass in Cercopithecidae (Primates, Mammalia): estimation and scaling in extinct and extant taxa |journal=Anthropological Papers of the American Museum of Natural History |volume=83 |pages=1–159}}</ref> A male specimen of ''[[Dinopithecus]]'' projected to weigh an average of {{cvt|46|kg|lb}} and up to {{cvt|57|kg|lb}}, exceeds the maximum weight record of the [[chacma baboon]], the largest extant baboon.<ref name="BM" /> * The largest known [[New World monkey]] was ''[[Cartelles]]'', which is studied as specimen of ''[[Protopithecus]]'', weighing up to {{cvt|34.27|kg|lb|order=}}. ''[[Caipora bambuiorum]]'' is another large species, weighing up to {{cvt|27.74|kg|lb|order=}}.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Perry |first1=Jonathan M. G. |last2=Cooke |first2=Siobhán B. |last3=Runestad Connour |first3=Jacqueline A. |last4=Burgess |first4=M. Loring |last5=Ruff |first5=Christopher B. |date=2018 |title=Articular scaling and body mass estimation in platyrrhines and catarrhines: Modern variation and application to fossil anthropoids |journal=Journal of Human Evolution |volume=115 |pages=20–35 |doi=10.1016/j.jhevol.2017.10.008 |pmid=29150186 |doi-access=free |bibcode=2018JHumE.115...20P }}</ref> * The largest [[Omomyidae|omomyid]]s were ''[[Macrotarsius]]'' and ''[[Ourayia]]'' from the [[Middle Eocene]]. Both reached {{cvt|1.5|-|2|kg|lb}} in weight.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Dunn |first1=Rachel H. |title=Additional postcranial remains of omomyid primates from the Uinta Formation, Utah and implications for the locomotor behavior of large-bodied omomyids |journal=Journal of Human Evolution |date=May 2010 |volume=58 |issue=5 |pages=406–417 |doi=10.1016/j.jhevol.2010.02.010 |pmid=20381124 |bibcode=2010JHumE..58..406D }}</ref> * Some prehistoric [[Lemuriformes|lemuriform]] primates grew to huge sizes as well. ''[[Archaeoindris]]'' was a {{convert|1.5|m|ft|adj=mid|-long}} [[sloth lemur]] that lived in [[Madagascar]] and weighed {{convert|150|–|187.8|kg|lb|abbr=on}},<ref>{{Cite book | last1 = Jungers | first1 = W. L. | last2 = Demes | first2 = B. | last3 = Godfrey | first3 = L. R. | chapter = How Big were the "Giant" Extinct Lemurs of Madagascar? | doi = 10.1007/978-0-387-73896-3_23 | title = Elwyn Simons: A Search for Origins | editor1-last = Fleagle | editor1-first = J. G. | editor2-last = Gilbert | editor2-first = C. C. | series = Developments in Primatology: Progress and Prospects | page = 350 | year = 2008 | isbn = 978-0-387-73895-6 }}</ref> as large as an adult male [[gorilla]].<ref>{{cite book | editor1-last = Hartwig | editor1-first = W. C | title = The Primate Fossil Record | publisher = [[Cambridge University Press]] | year = 2002 | isbn = 978-0-521-66315-1 | contribution = Quaternary fossil lemurs | last1 = Godfrey | first1 = L. R. | last2 = Jungers | first2 = W. L. | page = 101 }}</ref> ''[[Palaeopropithecus]]'' from the same family was also heavier than most modern [[lemur]]s, at {{cvt|25.8|-|45.8|kg}}.<ref name=Crowley2019>{{cite journal |last1=Crowley |first1=Brooke E. |last2=Godfrey |first2=Laurie R. |last3=Irwin |first3=Mitchell T. |title=A glance to the past: subfossils, stable isotopes, seed dispersal, and lemur species loss in Southern Madagascar |journal=American Journal of Primatology |date=January 2011 |volume=73 |issue=1 |pages=25–37 |doi=10.1002/ajp.20817 |pmid=20205184 }}</ref> ''[[Megaladapis]]'' is another large extinct lemur at {{cvt|1.3|to|1.5|m}} in length{{citation needed|date=July 2021}} and an average body mass of around {{convert|140|kg|lb|abbr=on}}.<ref>[http://members.tripod.com/cacajao/megaladapis_edwardsi.html ''Megaladapis edwardsi'': Scientific information]. [https://web.archive.org/web/20210120215938/https://cacajao.tripod.com/megaladapis_edwardsi.html Archived copy] from 20 January 2021.{{self-published inline|date=August 2024}}</ref> Other estimates suggest {{cvt|46.5|-|85.1|kg}} but its still much larger than any extant lemur.<ref name=Crowley2019/>
=== Elephants, mammoths and mastodons (Proboscidea) === [[File:Mammuthus sungari skeleton.jpg|thumb|left|[[Steppe mammoth]] skeletal mount]] [[File:Azov. History, Archaeology and Paleontology Museum-Reserve. Deinotherium P4300150 2350.jpg|thumb|Mounted ''[[Deinotherium]]'' skeleton]] * The elephant ''[[Palaeoloxodon namadicus]]'' has been suggested to have been the largest land mammal ever, based on a particularly large partial femur which was estimated to have belonged to an individual {{cvt|22|t|ST|1}} in weight and about {{cvt|5.2|m|1}} tall at the shoulder, though the author of the estimate said that this was speculative and should be treated with caution.<ref name="probos_mass" /> In 2023, a publication by Gregory S. Paul and Larramendi estimated that another specimen identified as cf. P. namadicus, also only known from a partial femur, would have weighed 18–19 tonnes (40,000–42,000 lb). Other authors have noted that weight estimates for proboscideans based on single bones can lead to estimates that are "highly improbable" compared to accurate estimates from complete skeletons.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Paul |first1=Gregory S. |last2=Larramendi |first2=Asier |date=11 April 2023 |title=Body mass estimate of Bruhathkayosaurus and other fragmentary sauropod remains suggest the largest land animals were about as big as the greatest whales |journal=Lethaia |language=en |volume=56 |issue=2 |pages=1–11 |doi=10.18261/let.56.2.5 |bibcode=2023Letha..56..2.5P |issn=0024-1164|doi-access=free }}</ref> In 2024, Biswas, Chang and Tsai estimated a maximum shoulder height of over 4.5 metres (15 ft) and suggested that the body mass for 5 measured specimens ranged from 13.2 to 18.5 tonnes (29,000 to 41,000 lb) from specimens from [[Taiwan]].<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Romano |first1=Marco |last2=Bellucci |first2=Luca |last3=Antonelli |first3=Matteo |last4=Manucci |first4=Fabio |last5=Palombo |first5=Maria Rita |date=2023 |title=Body mass estimate of Anancus arvernensis (Croizet and Jobert 1828): comparison of the regression and volumetric methods |url=https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/jqs.3549 |journal=Journal of Quaternary Science |language=en |volume=38 |issue=8 |pages=1357–1381 |doi=10.1002/jqs.3549 |bibcode=2023JQS....38.1357R |issn=0267-8179|url-access=subscription }}</ref> The largest individual reported individual of the [[steppe mammoth]] of [[Eurasia]] (''Mammuthus trogontherii'') was estimated to reach {{cvt|4.5|m|1}} at the shoulders and {{cvt|14.3|t|ST}} in weight.<ref name="probos_mass"/><ref>{{cite book|last=Osborn|first=H. F.|title=Proboscidea, Vol. II|year=1942|publisher=The American Museum Press.|location=New York}}</ref> ''Stegodon zdanskyi'', the biggest species of ''[[Stegodon]]'', was {{cvt|13|t|ST|1}} in body mass.<ref name="probos_mass" /> Another enormous proboscidean is ''[[Stegotetrabelodon|Stegotetrabelodon syrticus]]'', over {{cvt|4|m}} in height and {{cvt|11|to|12|t|ST|1}} in weight.<ref name="probos_mass" /> The [[Columbian mammoth]] (''[[Mammuthus columbi]]'') was about {{cvt|4|m|1}} tall at the shoulder but didn't weigh as much as other huge proboscideans. Its average mass was {{cvt|9.5|t|ST|1}} with one unusually large specimen about {{cvt|12.5|t|ST|1}}.<ref name="probos_mass" /> * The [[Mammutidae|mammutid]] ''[["Mammut" borsoni]]'' is one of the largest known proboscideans and land mammals. The average fully-grown male is estimated to have been {{cvt|4.1|m}} tall and weighed about {{cvt|16|t|ST|1}}, with very large males possibly rivalling the estimated size of the largest ''Palaeoloxodon namadicus''.<ref name="probos_mass" /> This species also had the longest tusks of any animals with the largest recorded specimen being {{cvt|5.02|m}} long from basis to tip along the curve.<ref>Mol, D. and van Logchem, W. 2009. The mastodon of Milia: the longest tusks in the world. Deposits 19: 28–32.</ref> * ''[[Deinotherium]]'' was the largest proboscidean in [[Deinotheriidae]] family. Bones retrieved in [[Crete]] confirm the existence of specimen {{cvt|4.1|m}} tall at the shoulders and more than {{cvt|14|t|ST|1}} in weight.<ref name="probos_mass" />
=== Sea cows (Sirenia) === [[File:Hydrodamalis gigas skeleton - Finnish Museum of Natural History - DSC04529.JPG|thumb|[[Steller's sea cow]] was the largest [[Sirenia|Sirenian]] of all time.]] According to reports, [[Steller's sea cow]]s have grown to {{convert|8|to|9|m|ft|abbr=on|sp=us}} long as adults, much larger than any [[extant species|extant]] [[sirenia]]ns.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Marsh |first1=Helene |author-link1=Helene Marsh |last2=O'Shea |first2=Thomas J. |last3=Reynolds III |first3=John E. |year=2011 |title=Ecology and Conservation of the Sirenia: Dugongs and Manatees |chapter=Steller's sea cow: discovery, biology and exploitation of a relict giant sirenian |publisher=[[Cambridge University Press]] |location=New York, New York |isbn=978-0-521-88828-8 |oclc=778803577 |pages=18–35 |chapter-url={{Google books|plainurl=yes|id=7pQgAwAAQBAJ|page=front}}}}</ref> The weight of Steller's sea cows is estimated to be {{convert|8|-|10|MT|abbr=on|ST}}.<ref>{{cite journal |last=Scheffer |first=Victor B. |author-link=Victor Blanchard Scheffer |date=November 1972 |title=The Weight of the Steller Sea Cow |journal=[[Journal of Mammalogy]] |doi=10.2307/1379236 |jstor=1379236 |volume=53 |number=4 |pages=912–914 }}</ref>
Its direct ancestor, the [[Cuesta sea cow]] was around 9 m (30 ft) long and possibly 10 tonnes (11 short tonnes) in weight.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Mawatari |first=Shunsuke F. |date=1986 |title=A new genus and species of celleporid bryozoan with ancestrula triad from Hokkaido, Japan |journal=Journal of Natural History |volume=20 |issue=1 |pages=193–202 |doi=10.1080/00222938600770161 |bibcode=1986JNatH..20..193M |issn=0022-2933}}</ref>
=== Arsinoitheres (Arsinoitheriidae) === [[File:Arsinoitherium MNH 2022.png|thumb|Skeleton of ''[[Arsinoitherium]]'']] The largest known [[Arsinoitheriidae|arsinoitheriid]] was ''[[Arsinoitherium]]''. ''A. zitteli'' would have been {{cvt|1.75|m}} tall at the shoulders, and {{cvt|3|m}} long.<ref name="Andrews1906">{{cite book |last=Andrews |first=C.W. |url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/bibliography/55134#/summary |title=A descriptive catalogue of the Tertiary Vertebrata of the Fayûm. British Museum, London |year=1906 |publisher=Taylor and Francis |page=324}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |author=Mondéjar-Fernández |display-authors=etal |date=2008 |title=El género ''Arsinoitherium'': catálogo de la colección inédita del Muséum d'Histoire Naturelle de París y el problema del número de especies |url=https://www.academia.edu/1470135 |journal=Palaeontologica Nova |language=es |volume=8 |pages=291–304}}</ref> ''A. giganteum'' reached even larger size than ''A. zitteli''.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Sanders |first1=William J. |last2=Kappelman |first2=John |last3=Rasmussen |first3=D. Tab |title=New large-bodied mammals from the late Oligocene site of Chilga, Ethiopia - Acta Palaeontologica Polonica |journal=Acta Palaeontologica Polonica |date=2004 |volume=49 |issue=3 |pages=365–392 |url=https://www.app.pan.pl/article/item/app49-365.html }}</ref>
=== Hyraxes (Hyracoidea) === Some of the prehistoric [[hyraxes]] were extremely large compared to modern small relatives. The largest hyracoid ever evolved is ''[[Titanohyrax|Titanohyrax ultimus]]''.{{sfn|Rose|2006|p=260}} With the mass estimation in rage of {{cvt|600|kg|lb}} to over {{cvt|1300|kg|lb}} it was close in size to [[Sumatran rhinoceros]].<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Tabuce |first1=Rodolphe |title=A mandible of the hyracoid mammal Titanohyrax andrewsi in the collections of the Muséum National d'Histoire Naturelle, Paris (France) with a reassessment of the species |journal=Palaeovertebrata |date=18 April 2016 |volume=40 |issue=1 |article-number=e4 |doi=10.18563/pv.40.1.e4 |url=https://hal.archives-ouvertes.fr/hal-03100188/file/2016_Tabuce_354.pdf }}</ref> Another enormous hyrax is ''[[Megalohyrax]]'' which had skull of {{cvt|391|mm}} in length<ref>{{Cite journal |author=J. G. M. Thewissen |author2=E. L. Simons | title = Skull of Megalohyrax eocaenus (Hyracoidea, Mammalia) from the Oligocene of Egypt | doi = 10.1671/0272-4634(2001)021[0098:SOMEHM]2.0.CO;2 | journal = Journal of Vertebrate Paleontology | pages = 98–106 | year = 2001 | volume = 21 | issue = 1 | jstor=4524175 | s2cid=86063305 }}</ref> and reached the size of tapir.{{sfn|Rose|2006|p=260}}<ref>{{cite book |last1=Prothero |first1=Donald R. |last2=Schoch |first2=Robert M. |title=The Evolution of Perissodactyls |date=1989 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0-19-506039-3 |page=65 }}</ref> More recent ''[[Gigantohyrax]]'' was three times as large as the extant relative ''[[Procavia capensis]]'',<ref>{{cite book | isbn = 978-1-107-39405-6 | url = https://books.google.com/books?id=F23lAgAAQBAJ&dq=Gigantohyrax+size&pg=PA41 | title = The Mammals of the Southern African Sub-region | publisher = Cambridge University Press | date = 2005 | access-date = 20 September 2022 | page = 41 | first1 = J. D. |last1=Skinner |first2=Christian T. |last2=Chimimba }}</ref> although it is noticeably smaller than earlier ''Megalohyrax'' and ''Titanohyrax''.<ref>{{cite book | isbn = 978-0-520-25721-4 | url = https://books.google.com/books?id=6c8lDQAAQBAJ&dq=Gigantohyrax+size&pg=PA143 | title = Cenozoic Mammals of Africa | publisher = University of California Press | date = 2010 | access-date = 20 September 2022 | page = 143 | first1= Lars |last1=Werdelin |first2=William Joseph |last2=Sanders }}</ref>
=== Desmostylians (Desmostylia) === [[File:Desmostylus skeletal diagram.jpg|thumb|''[[Desmostylus]]'' skeletal diagram]] The largest known [[desmostylia]]n was a species of ''[[Desmostylus]]'', with skull length of {{cvt|81.8|cm}} and comparable in size to the [[Steller's sea cow]].<ref>{{cite journal | doi=10.1098/rsbl.2016.0186 | title=The rise of ocean giants: Maximum body size in Cenozoic marine mammals as an indicator for productivity in the Pacific and Atlantic Oceans | year=2016 | last1=Pyenson | first1=Nicholas D. | last2=Vermeij | first2=Geerat J. | journal=Biology Letters | volume=12 | issue=7 | pmid=27381883 | pmc=4971165 | s2cid=1652462 }}</ref>
''[[Paleoparadoxia]]'' is also known as one of the largest desmostylians, with body length of {{cvt|3.03|m}}.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Hayashi |first1=Shoji |last2=Houssaye |first2=Alexandra |last3=Nakajima |first3=Yasuhisa |last4=Chiba |first4=Kentaro |last5=Ando |first5=Tatsuro |last6=Sawamura |first6=Hiroshi |last7=Inuzuka |first7=Norihisa |last8=Kaneko |first8=Naotomo |last9=Osaki |first9=Tomohiro |title=Bone Inner Structure Suggests Increasing Aquatic Adaptations in Desmostylia (Mammalia, Afrotheria) |journal=PLOS ONE |date=2 April 2013 |volume=8 |issue=4 |article-number=e59146 |doi=10.1371/journal.pone.0059146 |pmc=3615000 |pmid=23565143 |bibcode=2013PLoSO...859146H |doi-access=free }}</ref>
=== Glyptodonts, armadillos and pampatheres (Cingulata) === The largest [[Cingulata|cingulate]] known is ''[[Doedicurus]]'', at {{cvt|4|m}} long, {{cvt|1.5|m}} high<ref name=Iceagemammals>{{cite web |url=http://www.enchantedlearning.com/subjects/mammals/Iceagemammals.shtml |title = Ice Age Mammals - EnchantedLearning.com}}</ref> and reaching a mass of approximately {{cvt|1910|to|2370|kg|ST}}.{{citation needed|date=May 2021}} The largest species of ''[[Glyptodon]]'', ''Glyptodon clavipes'', reached {{cvt|3|-|3.3|m}} in length<ref>{{cite book | url = https://books.google.com/books?id=cEtTDwAAQBAJ&dq=glyptodon&pg=PA57 | title = Darwin's Fossils. The Collection That Shaped the Theory of Evolution | publisher = Smithsonian | date = 24 April 2018 | access-date = 4 September 2022 | page = 57 | first = Adrian | last = Lister | isbn = 978-1-58834-617-9 }}</ref><ref name=Iceagemammals/> and {{cvt|2|tonne|ST}} in weight.{{citation needed|date=May 2021}}
=== Anteaters and sloths (Pilosa) === {{stack| {{multiple image|total_width=330|direction=horisontal |image1=Megatherium (Giant Ground Sloth) Skeleton (35662312784).jpg |image2=Megatherum DB.jpg |footer=Skeleton and illustration of ''[[Megatherium]]''}} }} *The largest known [[pilosa]]n, as well as the largest sloth, was the [[Megatheriidae|megatheriid]] ''[[Eremotherium|Eremotherium laurillardi]]'', a ground sloth that was initially estimated to weigh up to {{cvt|6.55|tonne|ST}} and a length of up to {{cvt|6|m}},<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Larmon |first1=Jean T. |last2=McDonald |first2=H. Gregory |last3=Ambrose |first3=Stanley |last4=DeSantis |first4=Larisa R. G. |last5=Lucero |first5=Lisa J. |date=27 February 2019 |title=A year in the life of a giant ground sloth during the Last Glacial Maximum in Belize |journal=Science Advances |volume=5 |issue=2 |article-number=eaau1200 |doi=10.1126/sciadv.aau1200|pmid=30820449 |pmc=6392778 |bibcode=2019SciA....5.1200L}}</ref> which is as big as a bull [[African bush elephant]]. However, many studies have gotten lower estimates, with one study suggesting that {{cvt|4.49|tonne|ST}} was the most accurate size estimate for an adult.<ref name=":5">{{Cite journal |last1=Barbosa |first1=Fernando H. De S. |last2=Alves-Silva |first2=Laís |last3=Liparini |first3=Alexandre |last4=Porpino |first4=Kleberson De Oliveira |date=August 2023 |title=Reviewing the body size of some extinct Brazilian Quaternary Xenarthrans |url=https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/jqs.3560 |journal=Journal of Quaternary Science |language=en |volume=39 |issue=8 |pages=1160–1167 |doi=10.1002/jqs.3560 |issn=0267-8179}}</ref> The closely related ground sloth, ''[[Megatherium|Megatherium americanum]]'', was slightly smaller, measuring {{Convert|6|m|ft}} in length,<ref name=":92">{{Cite journal |last=Naish |first=Darren |date=November 2005 |title=Fossils explained 51: Sloths |url=https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/j.1365-2451.2005.00538.x |journal=Geology Today |language=en |volume=21 |issue=6 |pages=232–238 |doi=10.1111/j.1365-2451.2005.00538.x |issn=0266-6979|url-access=subscription }}</ref> and weighed {{cvt|3.7|tonne|ST}} based on volumetric models.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Brassey |first1=Charlotte A. |last2=Gardiner |first2=James D. |date=August 2015 |title=An advanced shape-fitting algorithm applied to quadrupedal mammals: improving volumetric mass estimates |journal=Royal Society Open Science |language=en |volume=2 |issue=8 |article-number=150302 |bibcode=2015RSOS....250302B |doi=10.1098/rsos.150302 |issn=2054-5703 |pmc=4555864 |pmid=26361559}}</ref> * The largest known [[Mylodontoidea|mylodontoid]] was ''[[Lestodon armatus]]'', estimated to have measured {{Convert|15|ft|m}} in length,<ref name=":2">{{Cite journal |last1=Tomassini |first1=Rodrigo L. |last2=Montalvo |first2=Claudia I. |last3=Garrone |first3=Mariana C. |last4=Domingo |first4=Laura |last5=Ferigolo |first5=Jorge |last6=Cruz |first6=Laura E. |last7=Sanz-Pérez |first7=Dánae |last8=Fernández-Jalvo |first8=Yolanda |last9=Cerda |first9=Ignacio A. |date=2 July 2020 |title=Gregariousness in the giant sloth Lestodon (Xenarthra): multi-proxy approach of a bonebed from the Last Maximum Glacial of Argentine Pampas |journal=[[Scientific Reports]] |language=en |volume=10 |issue=1 |page=10955 |doi=10.1038/s41598-020-67863-0 |issn=2045-2322 |pmc=7331707 |pmid=32616813|bibcode=2020NatSR..1010955T }}</ref> and weighed {{Convert|4100|kg|lb}}.<ref name=":1">{{Cite journal |last1=Bargo |first1=M. Susana |last2=Vizcaíno |first2=Sergio F. |last3=Archuby |first3=Fernando M. |last4=Blanco |first4=R. Ernesto |date=25 September 2000 |title=Limb bone proportions, strength and digging in some Lujanian (Late Pleistocene-Early Holocene) mylodontid ground sloths (Mammalia, Xenarthra) |url=http://www.tandfonline.com/doi/abs/10.1671/0272-4634%282000%29020%5B0601%3ALBPSAD%5D2.0.CO%3B2 |journal=Journal of Vertebrate Paleontology |language=en |volume=20 |issue=3 |pages=601–610 |doi=10.1671/0272-4634(2000)020[0601:LBPSAD]2.0.CO;2 |issn=0272-4634|url-access=subscription}}</ref> * The largest extinct [[anteater]] was ''[[Neotamandua borealis]]'', which is estimated to have weighed between {{convert|10|and|100|kg|lb}}.<ref name=miocene>{{Cite FTP |title=Mammals and rainfall: paleoecology of the middle Miocene at La Venta (Colombia, South America)|author1=Kay, R.F.|author2=Madden, R.H.|name-list-style=amp|pages=170–171|year=1997|volume=32|issue=2–3 |url=ftp://asp.tu-dresden.de/pub/Alex/Litheratur/A%20B%20C/Kay%201995.pdf|access-date=23 July 2012|doi=10.1006/jhev.1996.0104|pmid=9061556|server=Journal of Human Evolution|url-status=dead|bibcode=1997JHumE..32..161K }}</ref>
=== Astrapotherians (Astrapotheria) === The largest [[astrapotheria]]n was ''[[Hilarcotherium|Hilarcotherium miyou]]'', with an estimated weight of {{cvt|6.4|tonne|ST}}.<ref name=Carrilloetalungulates>{{cite journal |last1=Carrillo |first1=Juan D. |last2=Amson |first2=Eli |last3=Jaramillo |first3=Carlos |last4=Sánchez |first4=Rodolfo |last5=Quiroz |first5=Luis |last6=Cuartas |first6=Carlos |last7=Rincón |first7=Aldo F. |last8=Sánchez-Villagra |first8=Marcelo R. |title=The Neogene Record of Northern South American Native Ungulates |journal=Smithsonian Contributions to Paleobiology |date=27 July 2018 |issue=101 |pages=iv–67 |doi=10.5479/si.1943-6688.101 |s2cid=135113342 |doi-access=free }}</ref> Other large astrapotherians that weighed weighed about {{cvt|3–4|tonne|ST}}, such as the genus ''[[Granastrapotherium]]''<ref>Johnson, Steven C. and Madden, Richard H.. Uruguaytheriinae Astrapotheres of Tropical South America. Chapter 22 in "Vertebrate Paleontology in the Neotropics. The Miocene Fauna of La Venta, Colombia". Edited by Richard F. Kay, Richard H. Madden, Richard L. Cifelli, and John J. Flynn. Smithsonian Institution Press. Washington and London.</ref> and some species of ''[[Parastrapotherium]]'' (''P. martiale'').<ref>{{Cite journal | last1 = Kramarz | first1 = Alejandro G. | last2 = Bond | first2 = Mariano | title = Revision of ''Parastrapotherium'' (Mammalia, Astrapotheria) and other Deseadan astrapotheres of Patagonia | year = 2008 | journal = Ameghiniana | volume = 45 | issue = 3 | url = http://www.scielo.org.ar/scielo.php?pid=S0002-70142008000300003&script=sci_arttext | access-date = 1 March 2013 }}</ref>
=== Litopterns (Litopterna) === The largest known [[Litopterna|litoptern]] was ''[[Macrauchenia]]'', which had three hoofs per foot. It was a relatively large animal, with a body length of around {{cvt|3|m}}.<ref name="Gingerich 1998">{{Cite book | last = Gingerich | first = Philip D. | author-link = Philip D. Gingerich | year = 1998 | contribution = Paleobiological Perspectives on Mesonychia, Archaeoceti, and the Origin of Whales | editor-last = Thewissen | editor-first = J.G.M. | title = The emergence of whales: evolutionary patterns in the origin of Cetacea | pages=423–450 | publisher = Plenum Press | place=New York | isbn=978-0-306-45853-8 }}</ref>
=== Notoungulates (Notoungulata) === [[File:Em - Toxodon platensis - 1.jpg|thumb|''Toxodon'' was the largest of the [[Notoungulata|notoungulate]]s]] The largest [[Notoungulata|notoungulate]] known of complete remains is ''[[Toxodon]]''. It was about {{cvt|2.7|m}} in body length, and about {{cvt|1.5|m}} high at the shoulder and resembled a heavy rhinoceros. Although incomplete, the preserved fossils suggest that ''[[Mixotoxodon]]'' were the most massive member of the group, with a weight about {{cvt|3.8|tonne|ST}}.<ref>{{cite journal| last1 = Elissamburu | first1 = A | year = 2012 | title = Estimación de la masa corporal en géneros del Orden Notoungulata | journal = Estudios Geológicos | volume = 68 | issue = 1 | doi = 10.3989/egeol.40336.133 | pages=91–111| doi-access = free| hdl = 11336/197170 | hdl-access = free }}</ref>
=== Pyrotherians (Pyrotheria) === The largest mammal of the South American order [[Pyrotheria]] was ''[[Pyrotherium]]'' at {{cvt|2.9|–|3.6|m}} in length and {{cvt|1.8|–|3.5|t|lb}} in weight.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Croft |first1=Darin A. |last2=Gelfo |first2=Javier N. |last3=López |first3=Guillermo M. |title=Splendid Innovation: The Extinct South American Native Ungulates |journal=Annual Review of Earth and Planetary Sciences |date=30 May 2020 |volume=48 |issue=1 |pages=259–290 |doi=10.1146/annurev-earth-072619-060126 |bibcode=2020AREPS..48..259C }}</ref>
== Reptiles (Reptilia) == {{further|Reptile}}
=== Lizards and snakes (Squamata) === {{further|List of largest extinct lizards}} [[File:Varanus priscus Melbourne Museum.jpg|thumb|right|[[Megalania]] skeletal reconstruction on Melbourne Museum steps]] * [[Mosasaur]]s are the largest-known [[Squamata|squamates]]. The largest-known mosasaur is likely ''[[Mosasaurus|Mosasaurus hoffmanni]]'', estimated at more than {{cvt|17|m|0}} in length,<ref>{{Cite journal| last1 = Dortangs| first1 = Rudi W.|first2=Anne S.|last2=Schulp|author3=Eric W. A. Mulder |author4=John W.M. Jagt | title = A large new mosasaur from the Upper Cretaceous of The Netherlands| journal = Netherlands Journal of Geosciences| volume = 81| issue = 1| pages = 1–8| year = 2002| doi = 10.1017/S0016774600020515| doi-access = free| bibcode = 2002NJGeo..81....1D}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal|first=Dimitry V.|last=Grigoriev|title=Giant ''Mosasaurus hoffmanni'' (Squamata, Mosasauridae) from the Late Cretaceous (Maastrichtian) of Penza, Russia|journal=Proceedings of the Zoological Institute RAS|volume=318|issue=2|pages=148–167|year=2014 |doi=10.31610/trudyzin/2014.318.2.148 |s2cid=53574339 |url=https://www.zin.ru/journals/trudyzin/doc/vol_318_2/TZ_318_2_Grigoriev.pdf}}</ref> however these estimations are based on heads and total body length ratio 1:10, which is unlikely for ''Mosasaurus'', and probably that ratio is about 1:7.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Fanti |first1=Federico |last2=Cau |first2=Andrea |last3=Negri |first3=Alessandra |title=A giant mosasaur (Reptilia, Squamata) with an unusually twisted dentition from the Argille Scagliose Complex (late Campanian) of Northern Italy |journal=Cretaceous Research |date=May 2014 |volume=49 |pages=91–104 |doi=10.1016/j.cretres.2014.01.003 |bibcode=2014CrRes..49...91F }}</ref> Another giant mosasaur is ''[[Tylosaurus]]'', estimated at {{cvt|10|-|14|m|0}} in length.<ref>{{cite web| last = Everhart| first = Mike| title = Research: Tylosaurus proriger – A new record of a large mosasaur from the Smoky Hill Chalk| url=http://www.oceansofkansas.com/page10.html| website=[[Oceans of Kansas]]| access-date = 12 May 2010}}</ref><ref>{{cite web| title = Fact File: Tylosaurus Proriger from National Geographic| url = http://animals.nationalgeographic.com/animals/prehistoric/tylosaurus/| archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20100207180043/http://animals.nationalgeographic.com/animals/prehistoric/tylosaurus/| archive-date = 7 February 2010| access-date = 12 May 2010}}</ref> Another mosasaur, ''[[Prognathodon]]'' reached similar sizes. * The largest known prehistoric snake is ''[[Titanoboa|Titanoboa cerrejonensis]]'', estimated at {{cvt|12.8|m}} or even {{cvt|14.3|m}}<ref name="Head13">{{cite conference |first1=Jason |last1=Head |first2=Jonathan |last2=Bloch |date=October 2013 |title=Cranial osteology, body size, systematics, and ecology of the giant Paleocene snake Titanoboa cerrejonensis |url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/280610583 |access-date=22 May 2017 |publisher=[[Journal of Vertebrate Paleontology|Society of Vertebrate Paleontology]] |pages=140–141 |last3=Moreno Bernal |first3=Jorge |last4=Rincón Burbano |first4=Aldo Fernando |last5=Bourque |first5=Jason |conference=73nd Annual Meeting of the Society of vertebrate Paleontology |location=Los Angeles, California}}</ref> in length and {{cvt|730-1135|kg}}.<ref name="Titan">{{cite journal |last1=Head |first1=Jason J. |last2=Bloch |first2=Jonathan I. |last3=Hastings |first3=Alexander K. |last4=Bourque |first4=Jason R. |last5=Cadena |first5=Edwin A. |last6=Herrera |first6=Fabiany A. |last7=Polly |first7=P. David |last8=Jaramillo |first8=Carlos A. |title=Giant boid snake from the Palaeocene neotropics reveals hotter past equatorial temperatures |journal=Nature |volume=457 |issue=7230 |pages=715–717 |date=5 February 2009 |pmid=19194448 |doi=10.1038/nature07671 |bibcode=2009Natur.457..715H }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Feldman |first1=A. |last2=Sabath |first2=N. |last3=Pyron |first3=R.A. |last4=Mayrose |first4=I. |last5=Meiri |first5=S. |year=2016 |title=Body sizes and diversification rates of lizards, snakes, amphisbaenians and the tuatara |journal=Global Ecology and Biogeography |volume=25 |issue=2 |pages=187–197 |bibcode=2016GloEB..25..187F |doi=10.1111/geb.12398 |s2cid=25049185}}</ref>The [[madtsoiid]] ''[[Vasuki indicus]]'' may have rivaled or surpassed ''Titanoboa'' in length, however had smaller vertebral dimensions compared to it.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Datta |first1=Debajit |last2=Bajpai |first2=Sunil |date=18 April 2024 |title=Largest known madtsoiid snake from warm Eocene period of India suggests intercontinental Gondwana dispersal |journal=Scientific Reports |volume=14 |issue=1 |page=8054 |doi=10.1038/s41598-024-58377-0 |pmid=38637509 |doi-access=free |bibcode=2024NatSR..14.8054D |pmc=11549349 }}</ref> A close rival in size to those snakes is [[Palaeophiidae|palaeophiid]] marine snake ''[[Palaeophis|Palaeophis colossaeus]]'', which may have been around {{cvt|9|m|0}} in length<ref name="Titan" /><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Rage |first1=Jean-Claude |last2=Bajpai |first2=Sunil |last3=Thewissen |first3=Johannes G. M. |last4=Tiwari |first4=Brahma N. |title=Early eocene snakes from Kutch, Western India, with a review of the Palaeophiidae |journal=Geodiversitas |date=2003 |volume=25 |issue=4 |pages=695–716 |url=https://sciencepress.mnhn.fr/sites/default/files/articles/pdf/g2003n4a6.pdf}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last=Rage |first=J.-C. |title=''Palaeophis colossaeus'' nov. sp. (le plus grand Seprent connu?) de l'Eocène du Mali et le problème du genre chez les Palaeopheinae |journal=Comptes Rendus des Séances de l'Académie des Sciences |date=1983 |volume=3 |issue=296 |pages=1741–1744}}</ref> or even up to {{cvt|12.3|m|ft}}.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=McCartney |first1=Jacob |last2=Roberts |first2=Eric |last3=Tapanila |first3=Leif |last4=O'Leary |first4=Maureen |title=Large palaeophiid and nigerophiid snakes from Paleogene Trans-Saharan Seaway deposits of Mali |journal=Acta Palaeontologica Polonica |date=2018 |volume=63 |doi=10.4202/app.00442.2017 }}</ref> Another known very large fossil snake is ''[[Gigantophis|Gigantophis garstini]]'', estimated at {{cvt|9.3|–|10.7|m|0}} in length,<ref>Head, J. & Polly, D. 2004. They might be giants: morphometric methods for reconstructing body size in the world's largest snakes. ''Journal of Vertebrate Paleontology'' 24 (Supp. 3), 68A-69A.</ref><ref>{{cite web |title=A giant among snakes |url=https://www.newscientist.com/article/mg18424732-500-a-giant-among-snakes/ |website=newscientist.com}}</ref> although later study shows smaller estimation about {{cvt|6.6|–|7.2|m|0}}.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Rio |first1=Jonathan P. |last2=Mannion |first2=Philip D. |date=4 July 2017 |title=The osteology of the giant snake Gigantophis garstini from the upper Eocene of North Africa and its bearing on the phylogenetic relationships and biogeography of Madtsoiidae |journal=Journal of Vertebrate Paleontology |volume=37 |issue=4 |article-number=e1347179 |bibcode=2017JVPal..37E7179R |doi=10.1080/02724634.2017.1347179 |s2cid=90335531 |hdl-access=free |hdl=10044/1/48946}}</ref> The largest fossil [[Pythonidae|python]] is ''[[Liasis dubudingala]]'' with length roughly {{cvt|9|m|0}}.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Scanlon |first1=John D. |last2=Mackness |first2=Brian S. |date=1 January 2001 |title=A new giant python from the Pliocene Bluff Downs Local Fauna of northeastern Queensland |journal=Alcheringa: An Australasian Journal of Palaeontology |volume=25 |issue=4 |pages=425–437 |doi=10.1080/03115510108619232 |bibcode=2001Alch...25..425S |s2cid=85185368 }}</ref> The largest [[viper]] as well as the largest venomous snake ever recorded is ''[[Laophis|Laophis crotaloides]]'' from the Early [[Pliocene]] of [[Greece]]. This snake reached over {{cvt|3|m|ft|}} in length and {{cvt|26|kg|lb}} in weight.<ref>{{cite journal |title=On the Fossil Vertebrae of a Serpent (Laophis crotaloides, Ow.) discovered by Capt. Spratt, R.N., in a Tertiary Formation at Salonica|journal=Quarterly Journal of the Geological Society |volume=13|pages=196–199|year=1857 |last1=Owen|first1=R.|issue=1–2 |s2cid=131142130|doi=10.1144/GSL.JGS.1857.013.01-02.28 |bibcode=1857QJGS...13..196O |url=https://zenodo.org/record/2310903}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/274564872|title=Rediscovery of Laophis crotaloides – the worlds largest viper?|author=Benjamin P. Kear|website=researchgate.net|date=2014|access-date=14 September 2022}}</ref> Another huge fossil viper is indeterminate species of ''[[Vipera]]''. With a length of around {{cvt|2|m}} it was one of the biggest predators of [[Mallorca]] during the Early Pliocene.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Bailon |first1=Salvador |last2=Bover |first2=Pere |last3=Quintana |first3=Josep |last4=Alcover |first4=Josep Antoni |title=First fossil record of Vipera Laurenti 1768 'Oriental vipers complex' (Serpentes: Viperidae) from the Early Pliocene of the western Mediterranean islands |journal=Comptes Rendus Palevol |date=June 2010 |volume=9 |issue=4 |pages=147–154 |doi=10.1016/j.crpv.2010.04.001 }}</ref> The largest known [[Scolecophidia|blind snake]] is ''[[Boipeba tayasuensis]]'' with estimated total length of {{cvt|1.1|m}}.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Fachini |first1=Thiago Schineider |last2=Onary |first2=Silvio |last3=Palci |first3=Alessandro |last4=Lee |first4=Michael S. Y. |last5=Bronzati |first5=Mario |last6=Hsiou |first6=Annie Schmaltz |date=18 December 2020 |title=Cretaceous Blind Snake from Brazil Fills Major Gap in Snake Evolution |journal=iScience |volume=23 |issue=12 |article-number=101834 |doi=10.1016/j.isci.2020.101834 |pmid=33305189 |pmc=7718481 |bibcode=2020iSci...23j1834F }}</ref> * The largest known land lizard is probably [[megalania]] (''[[Varanus priscus]]'') at {{cvt|7|m|0}} in length.<ref name="Molnar2004">{{cite book |last1=Molnar |first1=Ralph E. |title=Dragons in the Dust: The Paleobiology of the Giant Monitor Lizard Megalania |date=2004 |publisher=Indiana University Press |isbn=978-0-253-34374-1 |pages=174–175 |oclc=52775128 }}</ref> As extant relatives, megalania could have been venomous and in that case this lizard was also the largest venomous vertebrate ever evolved.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Fry |first1=Bryan G. |last2=Wroe |first2=Stephen |last3=Teeuwisse |first3=Wouter |last4=van Osch |first4=Matthias J. P. |last5=Moreno |first5=Karen |last6=Ingle |first6=Janette |last7=McHenry |first7=Colin |last8=Ferrara |first8=Toni |last9=Clausen |first9=Phillip |last10=Scheib |first10=Holger |last11=Winter |first11=Kelly L. |last12=Greisman |first12=Laura |last13=Roelants |first13=Kim |last14=van der Weerd |first14=Louise |last15=Clemente |first15=Christofer J. |last16=Giannakis |first16=Eleni |last17=Hodgson |first17=Wayne C. |last18=Luz |first18=Sonja |last19=Martelli |first19=Paolo |last20=Krishnasamy |first20=Karthiyani |last21=Kochva |first21=Elazar |last22=Kwok |first22=Hang Fai |last23=Scanlon |first23=Denis |last24=Karas |first24=John |last25=Citron |first25=Diane M. |last26=Goldstein |first26=Ellie J. C. |last27=Mcnaughtan |first27=Judith E. |last28=Norman |first28=Janette A. |title=A central role for venom in predation by Varanus komodoensis (Komodo Dragon) and the extinct giant Varanus ( Megalania ) priscus |journal=Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences |date=2 June 2009 |volume=106 |issue=22 |pages=8969–8974 |doi=10.1073/pnas.0810883106 |doi-access=free |pmid=19451641 |pmc=2690028 |bibcode=2009PNAS..106.8969F }}</ref> However, maximum size of this animal is subject to debate.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Irmis |first1=Randall B. |title=Dragons in the Dust: the Paleobiology of the Giant Monitor Lizard Megalania |journal=Journal of Vertebrate Paleontology |date=27 June 2005 |volume=25 |issue=2 |page=479 |doi=10.1671/0272-4634(2005)025[0479:R]2.0.CO;2 }}</ref> Recent studies have estimated it at {{cvt|5.5|m}} long.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Fry |first1=Bryan G. |last2=Wroe |first2=Stephen |last3=Teeuwisse |first3=Wouter |last4=van Osch |first4=Matthias J. P. |last5=Moreno |first5=Karen |last6=Ingle |first6=Janette |last7=McHenry |first7=Colin |last8=Ferrara |first8=Toni |last9=Clausen |first9=Phillip |last10=Scheib |first10=Holger |last11=Winter |first11=Kelly L. |last12=Greisman |first12=Laura |last13=Roelants |first13=Kim |last14=van der Weerd |first14=Louise |last15=Clemente |first15=Christofer J. |date=2 June 2009 |title=A central role for venom in predation by ''Varanus komodoensis'' (Komodo Dragon) and the extinct giant ''Varanus'' (''Megalania'') ''priscus'' |journal=Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences |volume=106 |issue=22 |pages=8969–8974 |doi=10.1073/pnas.0810883106 |doi-access=free |pmid=19451641 |issn=0027-8424|pmc=2690028 |bibcode=2009PNAS..106.8969F }}</ref>
=== Turtles, tortoises and close relatives (Pantestudines) === {{further|Pantestudines}}
==== Cryptodira ==== * The largest known turtle ever was ''[[Archelon ischyros]]'' at {{cvt|5|m|ft}} long and {{cvt|2200|kg}}.<ref name="maximum">{{cite journal |author=Colston, T. J. |author2=Kulkarni, P. |author3=Jetz, W. |author4=Pyron, R. A. |url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/342848355 |title=Phylogenetic and spatial distribution of evolutionary diversification, isolation, and threat in turtles and crocodilians (non-avian archosauromorphs)|journal=BMC Evolutionary Biology|year=2020 |volume=20|issue=1|page=81 |doi=10.1186/s12862-020-01642-3|pmid=32650718 |pmc=7350713 |doi-access=free |bibcode=2020BMCEE..20...81C }}</ref> Possible second-largest sea turtle was ''[[Protostega]]'' at {{cvt|3.9|m}} in total body length.<ref name=KansasTurtles>{{cite web|author=Mike Everhart |url=http://oceansofkansas.com/turtles.html|title=Marine turtles from the Western Interior Sea|website=[[Oceans of Kansas]]|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220407005422/http://oceansofkansas.com/turtles.html|archive-date=7 April 2022|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Danilov |first1=Igor G. |last2=Obraztsova |first2=Ekaterina M. |last3=Arkhangelsky |first3=Maxim S. |last4=Ivanov |first4=Alexey V. |last5=Averianov |first5=Alexander O. |title=Protostega gigas and other sea turtles from the Campanian of Eastern Europe, Russia |journal=Cretaceous Research |date=July 2022 |volume=135 |article-number=105196 |doi=10.1016/j.cretres.2022.105196 |bibcode=2022CrRes.13505196D }}</ref> There is even a larger specimen of this genus from Texas estimated at {{cvt|4.2|m|ft}} in total length.<ref>{{cite journal |author=Derstler, K. |author2=Leitch, A. D. |author3=Larson, P. L. |author4=Finsley, C. |author5=Hill, L.|date=1993|title=The World's Largest Turtles – The Vienna Archelon (4.6 m) and the Dallas Protostega (4.2 m), Upper Cretaceous of South Dakota and Texas|journal=Journal of Vertebrate Paleontology|volume=13 |issue=Supplement to No. 3 |url=https://www.academia.edu/2394297}}</ref><ref name=KansasTurtles/> Partially known ''[[Cratochelone]]'' is estimated to reach {{cvt|4|m|ft}} in total length.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Kear |first1=Benjamin P. |title=Reassessment of Cratochelone Berneyi Longman, 1915, a giant sea turtle from the Early Cretaceous of Australia |journal=Journal of Vertebrate Paleontology |date=11 September 2006 |volume=26 |issue=3 |pages=779–783 |doi=10.1671/0272-4634(2006)26[779:ROCBLA]2.0.CO;2 }}</ref> Another huge prehistoric sea turtle is the Late Cretaceous ''[[Gigantatypus]]'', estimated at over {{cvt|3.5|m|ft}} in length.<ref>{{cite journal |author=H. F. Kaddumi |year=2006 |title=A new genus and species of gigantic marine turtles (Chelonioidea: Cheloniidae) from the Maastrichtian of the Harrana Fauna-Jordan |journal=PalArch's Journal of Vertebrate Palaeontology |volume=3 |issue=1 |pages=1–14 |url=http://www.palarch.nl/wp-content/kaddumi_hf_a_new_genus_and_species_of_gigantic_marine_turtles_chelonioidea_cheloniidae_from_the_maastrichtian_of_the_harrana_fauna_jordan_palarchs_journal_of_vertebrate_palaeontology_3_1_20061.pdf |access-date=4 February 2010 |archive-date=24 February 2012 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120224011546/http://www.palarch.nl/wp-content/kaddumi_hf_a_new_genus_and_species_of_gigantic_marine_turtles_chelonioidea_cheloniidae_from_the_maastrichtian_of_the_harrana_fauna_jordan_palarchs_journal_of_vertebrate_palaeontology_3_1_20061.pdf }}</ref> ''[[Psephophorus terrypratchetti]]'' from the Eocene attained {{cvt|2.3|-|2.5|m|ft}} in body length.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Kohler |first1=R. |title=A new species of the fossil turtle Psephophorus (Order Testudines) from the Eocene of the South Island, New Zealand |journal=Journal of the Royal Society of New Zealand |date=September 1995 |volume=25 |issue=3 |pages=371–384 |doi=10.1080/03014223.1995.9517495 |bibcode=1995JRSNZ..25..371K}}</ref> * The largest [[tortoise]] was ''[[Megalochelys|Megalochelys atlas]]'' at up to {{cvt|2|m|ft|order=}} in shell length<ref>{{Cite web |last=Naish |first=Darren |date=31 January 2024 |title=Megalochelys, Truly a Giant Tortoise |url=https://tetzoo.com/blog/2024/1/31/megalochelys-truly-a-giant-tortoise |access-date=30 May 2024 |website=Tetrapod Zoology |language=en-GB}}</ref> and weighing {{cvt|0.8|-|1.0|t|lb}}.<ref name=Leahy1994>{{cite journal |author1=Gregory S. Paul |author2=Guy D. Leahy|date=1994 |title=Terramegathermy in the time of the titans: Restoring the metabolics of colossal dinosaurs |journal=The Paleontological Society Special Publications|volume=7 |issue=Dino Fest|pages=177–198 |doi=10.1017/S2475262200009515 |url=http://gspauldino.com/TerraTimeofTitans.pdf|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220920172524/http://gspauldino.com/TerraTimeofTitans.pdf|archive-date=20 September 2022}}</ref> ''M. margae'' had carapace of {{cvt|1.4|-|2|m|ft}} long; an unnamed species from [[Java]] reached at least {{cvt|1.75|m|ft}} in carapace length.<ref name=Turtles2015/> The [[Cenozoic]] ''[[Titanochelon]]'' were also larger than extant [[giant tortoise]]s, with a shell length of up to {{cvt|2|m}}.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Pérez-García|first1=Adán|last2=Vlachos |first2=Evangelos|date=1 November 2014|title=New generic proposal for the European Neogene large testudinids (Cryptodira) and the first phylogenetic hypothesis for the medium and large representatives of the European Cenozoic record|journal=Zoological Journal of the Linnean Society |volume=172|issue=3|pages=653–719|doi=10.1111/zoj12183 |doi-access=free}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Pérez-García |first1=Adán |last2=Vlachos |first2=Evangelos |last3=Arribas |first3=Alfonso |title=The last giant continental tortoise of Europe: A survivor in the Spanish Pleistocene site of Fonelas P-1 |journal=Palaeogeography, Palaeoclimatology, Palaeoecology |date=March 2017 |volume=470 |pages=30–39 |doi=10.1016/j.palaeo.2017.01.011 |bibcode=2017PPP...470...30P |hdl=11336/53105 |hdl-access=free }}</ref> Other giant tortoises include ''[[Centrochelys|Centrochelys marocana]]'' at {{cvt|1.8|-|2|m|ft}} in carapace length and Mesoamerican ''[[Hesperotestudo]] sp.'' at {{cvt|1.5|m|ft}} in carapace length.<ref name=Turtles2015/> * The largest [[trionychidae|trionychid]] ever recorded is indeterminate specimen GSP-UM 3019 from the [[Middle Eocene]] of [[Pakistan]]. Bony carapace of GSP-UM 3019 is {{cvt|120|cm|ft}} long and {{cvt|110|cm|ft}} wide indicates the total carapace diameter (with soft margin) about {{cvt|2|m|ft}}.<ref name=Head1999/> ''[[Drazinderetes|Drazinderetes tethyensis]]'' from the same formation had a bony carapace {{cvt|80|cm|ft}} long and {{cvt|70|cm|ft}} wide.<ref name=Head1999>{{cite journal |last1=Head |first1=J. J. |last2=Raza |first2=S. M. |last3=Gingerich |first3=Philip D. |title=Drazinderetes tethyensis, a new large trionychid (Reptilia: Testudines) from the marine Eocene Drazinda Formation of the Sulaiman Range, Punjab (Pakistan) |journal=Contributions from the Museum of Paleontology, University of Michigan |date=1999 |volume=30 |issue=7 |pages=199–214 |hdl=2027.42/48657 |hdl-access=free }}</ref> Another huge trionychid is North American ''[[Axestemys|Axestemys byssinus]]'' at over {{cvt|2|m|ft}} in total length.<ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=TTBvdx3IJIMC&pg=PA200 |title=The Lost World of Fossil Lake: Snapshots from Deep Time |isbn=978-0-226-92296-6 |last1=Grande |first1=Lance |date=14 June 2013 |page=200 |publisher=University of Chicago Press}}</ref>
==== Side-necked turtles (Pleurodira) ==== [[File:The fossil of carapace of Stupendemys Geographicus.jpg|thumb|left|The fossil of carapace of ''[[Stupendemys geographicus]]'']] The largest freshwater turtle of all time was the [[Miocene]] [[Podocnemididae|podocnemid]] ''[[Stupendemys]]'', with an estimated parasagittal carapace length of {{cvt|2.86|m}} and weight of up to {{cvt|1145|kg|lb}}.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Cadena |first1=E.-A. |last2=Scheyer |first2=T.M. |last3=Carrillo-Briceño |first3=J.D. |last4=Sánchez |first4=R. |last5=Aguilera-Socorro |first5=O.A. |last6=Vanegas |first6=A. |last7=Pardo |first7=M. |last8=Hansen |first8=D.M. |last9=Sánchez-Villagra |first9=M.R. |date=12 February 2020 |title=The anatomy, paleobiology, and evolutionary relationships of the largest extinct side-necked turtle|journal=Science Advances |volume=6 |issue=7 |article-number=eaay4593 |doi=10.1126/sciadv.aay4593 |pmid=32095528 |pmc=7015691 |bibcode=2020SciA....6.4593C |doi-access=free}}</ref> ''[[Carbonemys cofrinii]]'' from the same family had a shell that measured about {{cvt|1.72|m}},<ref>{{cite web|date=17 May 2012 |url=http://phys.org/news/2012-05-reveal-ancient-giant-turtle-fossil.html |title=Researchers reveal ancient giant turtle fossil |website=Phys.org |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220922083645/https://phys.org/news/2012-05-reveal-ancient-giant-turtle-fossil.html |archive-date=22 September 2022 |url-status=live|access-date=13 October 2022}}</ref><ref>{{cite news|last=Maugh II|first=Thomas H. |title=Researchers find fossil of a turtle that was size of a Smart car |url=https://www.latimes.com/science/la-xpm-2012-may-18-la-sci-sn-largest-turtle-20120518-story.html |newspaper=Los Angeles Times |date=18 May 2012|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220923015234/https://www.latimes.com/science/la-xpm-2012-may-18-la-sci-sn-largest-turtle-20120518-story.html |archive-date=23 September 2022|url-status=live|access-date=13 October 2022}}</ref><ref>{{cite news| url=https://www.huffpost.com/entry/turtle-fossil-colombia-carbonems-confrinii-car_n_1524960| work=Huffington Post| first=David| last=Freeman| title=Car-Sized Reptile Lived Alongside Titanoboa, Scientists Say| date=17 May 2012|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220923014603/https://www.huffpost.com/entry/turtle-fossil-colombia-carbonems-confrinii-car_n_1524960 |archive-date=23 September 2022|url-status=live |access-date=13 October 2022}}</ref> complete shell was estimated at {{cvt|1.8|m|ft}}.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Cadena |first1=Edwin A. |last2=Ksepka |first2=Daniel T. |last3=Jaramillo |first3=Carlos A. |last4=Bloch |first4=Jonathan I. |title=New pelomedusoid turtles from the late Palaeocene Cerrejón Formation of Colombia and their implications for phylogeny and body size evolution |journal=Journal of Systematic Palaeontology |date=June 2012 |volume=10 |issue=2 |pages=313–331 |doi=10.1080/14772019.2011.569031 |bibcode=2012JSPal..10..313C}}</ref>
==== Macrobaenids (Macrobaenidae) ==== The largest [[Macrobaenidae|macrobaenids]] were the [[Early Cretaceous]] ''[[Yakemys]]'', [[Late Cretaceous]] ''[[Anatolemys]]'', and [[Paleocene]] ''[[Judithemys]]'' and ''[[Carbonemys]]''. All reached {{cvt|70|cm|ft}} in carapace length.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Tong |first1=Haiyan |last2=Chanthasit |first2=Phornphen |last3=Naksri |first3=Wilailuck |last4=Ditbanjong |first4=Pitaksit |last5=Suteethorn |first5=Suravech |last6=Buffetaut |first6=Eric |last7=Suteethorn |first7=Varavudh |last8=Wongko |first8=Kamonlak |last9=Deesri |first9=Uthumporn |last10=Claude |first10=Julien |title=Yakemys multiporcata n. g. n. sp., a Large Macrobaenid Turtle from the Basal Cretaceous of Thailand, with a Review of the Turtle Fauna from the Phu Kradung Formation and Its Stratigraphical Implications |journal=Diversity |date=30 November 2021 |volume=13 |issue=12 |page=630 |doi=10.3390/d13120630 |doi-access=free |bibcode=2021Diver..13..630T }}</ref>
==== Meiolaniformes ==== [[File:Meiolania.jpg|thumb|Skeleton of ''[[Meiolania platyceps]]'']] The largest [[Meiolaniidae|meiolaniid]] was ''[[Meiolania]]''. ''Meiolania platyceps'' had a carapace {{cvt|100|cm|ft}} long<ref name=Turtles2015>{{cite book |doi=10.3854/crm.5.000e.fossil.checklist.v1.2015 |chapter=Turtles and Tortoises of the World During the Rise and Global Spread of Humanity: First Checklist and Review of Extinct Pleistocene and Holocene Chelonians |title=Conservation Biology of Freshwater Turtles and Tortoises |series=Chelonian Research Monographs |date=2015 |last1=Rhodin |first1=Anders |last2=Thomson |first2=Scott |last3=Georgalis |first3=Georgios |last4=Karl |first4=Hans-Volker |last5=Danilov |first5=Igor |last6=Takahashi |first6=Akio |last7=de la Fuente |first7=Marcelo |last8=Bourque |first8=Jason |last9=Delfino |first9=Massimo |last10=Bour |first10=Roger |last11=Iverson |first11=John |last12=Shaffer |first12=Bradley |last13=Van Dijk |first13=Peter Paul |volume=5 |isbn=978-0-9653540-9-7 }}</ref> and probably reached over {{cvt|3|m|ft}} in total body length.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Brown |first1=Lauren |title=The enigmatic palaeoecology and palaeobiogeography of the giant, horned, fossil turtles of Australasia: a review and reanalysis of the data |journal=Herpetological Journal |date=October 2019 |volume=29 |issue=4 |pages=252–263 |doi=10.33256/hj29.4.252263 }}</ref> An unnamed Late Pleistocene species from [[Queensland]] was even larger, up to {{cvt|200|cm|ft}} in carapace length.<ref name=Turtles2015/> ''[[Ninjemys|Ninjemys oweni]]'' reached {{cvt|100|cm|ft}} in carapace length<ref name=Turtles2015/> and {{cvt|200|kg|lb}} in weight.<ref>{{cite book|author=Ross D.E. MacPhee, Hans-Dieter Sues|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=UZLuF1YXYTcC&dq=%22Ninjemys+oweni%22&pg=PA251|title=Extinctions in near time: causes, contexts, and consequences|date=1999|publisher=Kluwer Academic/Plenum Publishers|page=251|isbn=978-0-306-46092-0}}</ref>
=== Sauropterygians (Sauropterygia) === ==== Placodonts and close relatives (Placodontiformes) ==== ''[[Placodus]]'' was among the largest [[placodonts]], with a length of up to {{cvt|3|m}}.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://oceansofkansas.com/placodnt.html|title=PLACODONTS: The bizarre "walrus-turtles" of the Triassic|website=[[Oceans of Kansas]]|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220130172524/http://oceansofkansas.com/placodnt.html|archive-date=30 January 2022|url-status=live}}</ref>
==== Nothosaurs and close relatives (Nothosauroidea) ==== The largest [[nothosaur]] as well as the largest [[Triassic]] [[sauropterygia]]n was ''[[Nothosaurus giganteus]]'' at {{cvt|7|m}} in length.<ref>{{cite journal|last1=Liu|first1=Jun|title=A gigantic nothosaur (Reptilia: Sauropterygia) from the Middle Triassic of SW China and its implications for the Triassic biotic recovery|journal=Scientific Reports|date=27 November 2014|pmc=4245812|pmid=25429609|doi=10.1038/srep07142|volume=4|article-number=7142|bibcode=2014NatSR...4.7142L }}</ref>
==== Plesiosaurs (Plesiosauria) ==== {{Main|Plesiosaur size}}
* The largest known [[Plesiosauroidea|plesiosauroid]] was an indeterminate specimen possibly belonging to ''[[Aristonectes]]'' (identified as cf. ''Aristonectes'' sp.), with a body length of {{convert|11|-|11.9|m|ft}} and body mass of {{convert|10.7|-|13.5|MT|ST}}.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=O'Gorman |first1=J.P. |last2=Santillana |first2=S. |last3=Otero |first3=R. |last4=Reguero |first4=M. |title=A giant elasmosaurid (Sauropterygia; Plesiosauria) from Antarctica: New information on elasmosaurid body size diversity and aristonectine evolutionary scenarios |journal=Cretaceous Research |date=October 2019 |volume=102 |pages=37–58 |doi=10.1016/j.cretres.2019.05.004 |bibcode=2019CrRes.102...37O |s2cid=181725020 |url=https://repositorio.uchile.cl/handle/2250/173073 }}</ref> Another long plesiosauroid was ''[[Albertonectes]]'' at {{convert|11.2-11.6|m|ft}}.<ref>{{Cite journal | last1 = Kubo | first1 = T. | last2 = Mitchell | first2 = M. T. | last3 = Henderson | first3 = D. M. | doi = 10.1080/02724634.2012.658124 | title = ''Albertonectes vanderveldei'', a new elasmosaur (Reptilia, Sauropterygia) from the Upper Cretaceous of Alberta | journal = Journal of Vertebrate Paleontology | volume = 32 | issue = 3 | pages = 557–572| year = 2012 | bibcode = 2012JVPal..32..557K | s2cid = 129500470 }}</ref> ''[[Thalassomedon]]'' rivaled it in size, with its length at {{cvt|10.86–11.6|m|ft}}.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Smith |first1=Elliott |title=Revision of the Genus Styxosaurus and Relationships of the Late Cretaceous Elasmosaurids (Sauropterygia: Plesiosauria) of the Western Interior Seaway |journal=Theses, Dissertations and Capstones |date=1 January 2020 |url=https://mds.marshall.edu/etd/1335/}}</ref> Other large plesiosauroids are ''[[Styxosaurus]]'' and ''[[Elasmosaurus]]''. Both reached some more than {{cvt|10|m|ft}} in length.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Mike Everhart|title=Bite marks on an elasmosaur (Sauropterygia; Plesiosauria) paddle from the Niobrara Chalk (Upper Cretaceous) as probable evidence of feeding by the lamniform shark, ''Cretoxyrhina mantelli''|volume=2|issue=2|url=http://www.palarch.nl/wp-content/everhart_mj_bite_marks_on_an_elasmosaur_sauropterygia_plesiosauria_paddle_from_the_niobrara_chalk_upper_cretaceous_as_probable_evidence_of_feeding_by_the_lamniform_shark_cretoxyrhina_mantelli_palarchs.pdf|year=2005|journal=PalArch's Journal of Vertebrate Paleontology|access-date=2 October 2009|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110716064113/http://www.palarch.nl/wp-content/everhart_mj_bite_marks_on_an_elasmosaur_sauropterygia_plesiosauria_paddle_from_the_niobrara_chalk_upper_cretaceous_as_probable_evidence_of_feeding_by_the_lamniform_shark_cretoxyrhina_mantelli_palarchs.pdf|archive-date=16 July 2011|url-status=live}}</ref><ref name=Gorman2016>{{cite journal|last1=O'Gorman |first1=J. P. |s2cid=133139689 |year=2016 |title=A Small Body Sized Non-Aristonectine Elasmosaurid (Sauropterygia, Plesiosauria) from the Late Cretaceous of Patagonia with Comments on the Relationships of the Patagonian and Antarctic Elasmosaurids |journal=Ameghiniana |volume=53 |issue=3 |pages=245–268 |doi=10.5710/AMGH.29.11.2015.2928|bibcode=2016Amegh..53..245O |url=http://sedici.unlp.edu.ar/handle/10915/108247 |hdl=11336/54311 |hdl-access=free }}</ref> ''[[Hydralmosaurus]]'' (previously synonymized with ''Elasmosaurus'' and ''Styxosaurus'') reached {{cvt|9.44|m|ft}} in total body length.<ref name=Gorman2016/> In past, ''[[Mauisaurus]]'' was considered to be more than {{cvt|8|m|ft}} in length,<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Hiller |first1=Norton |last2=Mannering |first2=Al A. |last3=Jones |first3=Craig M. |last4=Cruickshank |first4=Arthur R. I. |title=The nature of Mauisaurus haasti Hector, 1874 (Reptilia: Plesiosauria) |journal=Journal of Vertebrate Paleontology |date=30 September 2005 |volume=25 |issue=3 |pages=588–601 |doi=10.1671/0272-4634(2005)025[0588:TNOMHH]2.0.CO;2 }}</ref><ref name=Gorman2016/> but later it was determined as ''[[nomen dubium]]''.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Hiller |first1=Norton |last2=O'Gorman |first2=José P. |last3=Otero |first3=Rodrigo A. |last4=Mannering |first4=Al A. |title=A reappraisal of the Late Cretaceous Weddellian plesiosaur genus Mauisaurus Hector, 1874 |journal=New Zealand Journal of Geology and Geophysics |date=3 April 2017 |volume=60 |issue=2 |pages=112–128 |doi=10.1080/00288306.2017.1281317 |bibcode=2017NZJGG..60..112H }}</ref> [[File:Pliosaurus size chart.png|thumb|Size estimation of three species of ''[[Pliosaurus]]''.]] * There is much controversy over the largest-known of the [[Pliosauroidea]]. ''[[Pliosaurus|Pliosaurus funkei]]'' (also known as "[[Predator X]]") is a species of large pliosaur, known from remains discovered in [[Norway]] in 2008. This pliosaur has been estimated at {{cvt|10|-|13|m|ft}} in length.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Knutsen |first1=Espen M. |last2=Druckenmiller |first2=Patric S. |last3=Hurum |first3=Jørn H. |date=2012 |title=A new species of Pliosaurus (Sauropterygia: Plesiosauria) from the Middle Volgian of central Spitsbergen, Norway |journal=Norwegian Journal of Geology |volume=92 |pages=235–258 |url=https://njg.geologi.no/publications/a-new-species-of-pliosaurus-sauropterygia-plesiosauria-from-the-middle-volgian-of-central-spitsbergen-norway/ }}</ref> However, in 2002, a team of paleontologists in [[Mexico]] discovered the remains of a pliosaur nicknamed as "[[Monster of Aramberri]]", which is also estimated at {{cvt|15|m|0}} in length,<ref name="GPP">{{cite journal| doi=10.2113/174.3.271| last1=Buchy |first1=M.-C. |last2=Frey |first2=E. |last3=Stinnesbeck |first3=W. |last4=López-Oliva |first4=J.G. |year=2003 |title=First occurrence of a gigantic pliosaurid plesiosaur in the late Jurassic (Kimmeridgian) of Mexico |journal=Bulletin de la Société Géologique de France |volume=174 |issue=3| pages=271–278| hdl=2042/260
