|url=http://doc.rero.ch/record/14729/files/PAL_E1855.pdf |hdl-access=free}}</ref> with shorter estimation about {{cvt|11.5|m|0}}.<ref name="henry">{{cite thesis |last1=McHenry |first1=Colin Richard |title=Devourer of Gods: the palaeoecology of the Cretaceous pliosaur Kronosaurus queenslandicus |date=2009 |hdl=1959.13/935911 }}{{page needed|date=August 2024}}</ref> This species is, however, claimed to be a juvenile and has been attacked by a larger pliosaur.<ref name="MA">{{cite web|title=Monster von Arramberri|url=http://www.smnk.de/SMNK/02-Forsch-I/02-03-Geo-Pal/2-3-2-1/2-3-2-1-Inhalt.html|access-date=10 September 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120303062113/https://www.smnk.de/SMNK/02-Forsch-I/02-03-Geo-Pal/2-3-2-1/2-3-2-1-Inhalt.html|archive-date=3 March 2012}}</ref> Some media sources claimed that Monster of Aramberri was a ''[[Liopleurodon]]'' but its species is unconfirmed thus far.<ref name="GPP" /> Another very large pliosaur was ''[[Pliosaurus|Pliosaurus macromerus]]'', known from a single {{convert|2.8|m|ft|adj=mid|-long}} incomplete mandible.<ref>{{cite web|title=The Cumnor monster mandible |url=http://scienceblogs.com/tetrapodzoology/2007/12/cumnor_monster_mandible.php|access-date=10 September 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20111011174114/http://scienceblogs.com/tetrapodzoology/2007/12/cumnor_monster_mandible.php|archive-date=11 October 2011}}</ref> The [[Early Cretaceous]] ''[[Kronosaurus queenslandicus]]'' is estimated at {{cvt|9|-|10.9|m|ft}} in length and {{cvt|10.6|-|12.1|t|ST}} in weight.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Kear |first1=Benjamin P. |title=Cretaceous marine reptiles of Australia: a review of taxonomy and distribution |journal=Cretaceous Research |date=June 2003 |volume=24 |issue=3 |pages=277–303 |doi=10.1016/S0195-6671(03)00046-6 |bibcode=2003CrRes..24..277K }}</ref><ref name="henry"/> The [[Late Jurassic]] ''[[Megalneusaurus rex]]'' could reach lengths of {{convert|25|-|30|ft|m|order=flip}}.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.paleonature.org/lets-go-scientific/91-the-largest-pliosaurid-from-north-america|title=The Largest Pliosaurid from North America|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160801041320/http://paleonature.org/lets-go-scientific/91-the-largest-pliosaurid-from-north-america|archive-date=1 August 2016|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Massare |first1=Judy A. |last2=Wahl |first2=William R. |last3=Ross |first3=Mike |last4=Connely |first4=Melissa V. |title=Palaeoecology of the marine reptiles of the Redwater Shale Member of the Sundance Formation (Jurassic) of central Wyoming, USA |journal=Geological Magazine |date=January 2014 |volume=151 |issue=1 |pages=167–182 |doi=10.1017/S0016756813000472 |bibcode=2014GeoM..151..167M }}</ref> Close contender in size was the [[Late Cretaceous]] ''[[Megacephalosaurus eulerti]]'' with a length in range of {{cvt|6|-|9|m}}.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Schumacher |first1=Bruce A. |last2=Carpenter |first2=Kenneth |last3=Everhart |first3=Michael J. |title=A new Cretaceous Pliosaurid (Reptilia, Plesiosauria) from the Carlile Shale (middle Turonian) of Russell County, Kansas |journal=Journal of Vertebrate Paleontology |date=May 2013 |volume=33 |issue=3 |pages=613–628 |doi=10.1080/02724634.2013.722576 |bibcode=2013JVPal..33..613S }}</ref>
=== Proterosuchids (Proterosuchidae) === ''[[Proterosuchus fergusi]]'' is the largest known [[Proterosuchidae|proterosuchid]] with a skull length of {{cvt|47.7|cm}} and a possible body length of {{cvt|3.5|-|4|m}}.{{sfn|Desojo|2013|p=20}}
=== Erythrosuchids (Erythrosuchidae) === [[File:Erythrosuchus NT small.jpg|thumb|Life reconstruction of ''[[Erythrosuchus africanus]]'']] The largest [[Erythrosuchidae|erythrosuchid]] was ''[[Erythrosuchus africanus]]'' with a maximum length of {{cvt|4.75|-|5|m}}.{{sfn|Desojo|2013|p=22}}
=== Phytosaurs (Phytosauria) === Some of the largest known [[Phytosauria|phytosaurs]] include ''[[Redondasaurus]]'' with a length of {{cvt|6.4|m}}<ref name=NMMNHS>{{cite web |title=Redondasaurus |website=New Mexico Museum of Natural History and Science |access-date=15 October 2022 |url=http://www.nmnaturalhistory.org/redondasaurus1.html |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20151220151328/http://www.nmnaturalhistory.org/redondasaurus1.html |archive-date=20 December 2015 }}</ref> and ''[[Smilosuchus]]'' with a length of more than {{cvt|7|m}}.<ref>{{cite book | url = https://books.google.com/books?id=qmycDwAAQBAJ | title = The Rise of Reptiles: 320 Million Years of Evolution | publisher = Johns Hopkins University Press | date = 2019 | access-date = 28 August 2022 | page = 176 | author = Hans-Dieter Sues | isbn = 978-1-4214-2867-3 }}</ref>
=== Non-crocodylomorph pseudosuchians (Pseudosuchia) === {{further|Pseudosuchia}} [[File:Sillosuchus size.png|thumb|right|Size comparison of ''[[Sillosuchus]]'' to a human]] * The largest [[Shuvosauridae|shuvosaurid]] and one of the largest pseudosuchian from the [[Triassic]] period was ''[[Sillosuchus]]''. Biggest specimens could have reached {{cvt|9|-|10|m}} in length.{{sfn|Desojo|2013|p=260}}<ref name=Nesbitt11>{{cite journal|author=Sterling J. Nesbitt |year=2011|title=The Early Evolution of Archosaurs: Relationships and the Origin of Major Clades|journal=Bulletin of the American Museum of Natural History|volume=352 |pages=1–292|doi=10.1206/352.1 |hdl=2246/6112|s2cid=83493714|doi-access=free}}</ref> * The largest known carnivorous pseudosuchian of the Triassic is [[loricata]]n ''[[Fasolasuchus|Fasolasuchus tenax]]'', which measured an estimated of {{cvt|8|to|10|m}}.{{sfn|Desojo|2013|p=260}}<ref name=Nesbitt11/><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Nesbitt |first1=Sterling J. |last2=Brusatte |first2=Stephen L. |last3=Desojo |first3=Julia B. |last4=Liparini |first4=Alexandre |last5=De França |first5=Marco A. G. |last6=Weinbaum |first6=Jonathan C. |last7=Gower |first7=David J. |title=Rauisuchia |journal=Geological Society, London, Special Publications |date=January 2013 |volume=379 |issue=1 |pages=241–274 |doi=10.1144/SP379.1 |bibcode=2013GSLSP.379..241N}}</ref> It is both the largest "[[rauisuchian]]" known to science, and the largest non-dinosaurian terrestrial predator ever discovered.{{citation needed|date=May 2021}} Biggest individuals of ''[[Postosuchus]]''{{sfn|Desojo|2013|p=527}} and ''[[Saurosuchus]]''<ref>{{cite book |last1=Benton |first1=Michael J. |title=Vertebrate Palaeontology |date=2014 |publisher=John Wiley & Sons |isbn=978-1-118-40755-4 |page=158}}</ref> had a body length of around {{cvt|7|m}}. A specimen of ''[[Prestosuchus]]'' discovered in 2010 suggest that this animal also reached lengths of nearly {{cvt|7|m}} making it one of the largest Triassic pseudosuchians.<ref name="LS132">{{cite journal |last1=Liparini |first1=Alexandre |last2=Schultz |first2=Cesar L. |title=A reconstruction of the thigh musculature of the extinct pseudosuchian Prestosuchus chiniquensis from the Dinodontosaurus Assemblage Zone (Middle Triassic Epoch), Santa Maria 1 Sequence, southern Brazil |journal=Geological Society, London, Special Publications |date=January 2013 |volume=379 |issue=1 |pages=441–468 |doi=10.1144/SP379.20 |bibcode=2013GSLSP.379..441L}}</ref> * ''[[Desmatosuchus]]'' was likely one of the largest known [[aetosaur]]s, about {{cvt|4|-|6|m}} in length and {{cvt|280|kg|lb}} in weight.<ref name=PWG05>{{cite journal |last=Parker |first=W.G. |year=2005 |title=A new species of the Late Triassic aetosaur ''Desmatosuchus'' (Archosauria: Pseudosuchia) |journal=Comptes Rendus Palevol |volume=4 |issue=4 |pages=327–340 |doi=10.1016/j.crpv.2005.03.002|bibcode=2005CRPal...4..327P }}</ref><ref name=Detal13>{{cite journal |last1=Desojo |first1=Julia B. |last2=Heckert |first2=Andrew B. |last3=Martz |first3=Jeffrey W. |last4=Parker |first4=William G. |last5=Schoch |first5=Rainer R. |last6=Small |first6=Bryan J. |last7=Sulej |first7=Tomasz |title=Aetosauria: a clade of armoured pseudosuchians from the Upper Triassic continental beds |journal=Geological Society, London, Special Publications |date=January 2013 |volume=379 |issue=1 |pages=203–239 |doi=10.1144/SP379.17 |bibcode=2013GSLSP.379..203D }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=von Baczko |first1=M. Belén |last2=Desojo |first2=Julia. B. |last3=Gower |first3=David J. |last4=Ridgely |first4=Ryan |last5=Bona |first5=Paula |last6=Witmer |first6=Lawrence M. |title=New digital braincase endocasts of two species of Desmatosuchus and neurocranial diversity within Aetosauria (Archosauria: Pseudosuchia) |journal=The Anatomical Record |date=October 2022 |volume=305 |issue=10 |pages=2415–2434 |doi=10.1002/ar.24798 |pmid=34662509 |hdl=11336/152847 |hdl-access=free }}</ref>
=== Crocodiles and close relatives (Crocodylomorpha) === {{further|Crocodylomorpha}} [[File:Large crocodyliformes.svg|thumb|Large crocodylomorphs (†''[[Deinosuchus]]'', †''[[Purussaurus]]'', †''[[Gryposuchus]]'', †''[[Euthecodon]]'', †''[[Sarcosuchus]]'', and modern ''[[Crocodylus porosus]]'') compared to a human]] [[File:Razanandrongobe-fig9-recon.jpg|thumb|Skull size comparison of large crocodylomorphs (Left to right, †''[[Razanandrongobe]]'', †''[[Machimosaurus]]'', †''[[Barinasuchus]]'', †''[[Sarcosuchus]]'', modern ''[[Crocodylus porosus]]'', and †''[[Purussaurus]]'')]]
==== Aegyptosuchids (Aegyptosuchidae) ==== The [[Late Cretaceous]] ''[[Aegisuchus]]'' was originally estimated to reach {{cvt|15|m}} in length by the lower estimate and as much as {{cvt|22|m}} by the upper estimate although a length of over 15 m is likely a significant overestimate.<ref name="Holliday2012">{{cite journal |author=Casey M. Holliday and Nicholas M. Gardner |year=2012 |title=A New Eusuchian Crocodyliform with Novel Cranial Integument and Its Significance for the Origin and Evolution of Crocodylia |journal=PLOS ONE |volume=7 |issue=1 |article-number=e30471 |bibcode=2012PLoSO...730471H |doi=10.1371/journal.pone.0030471 |pmc=3269432 |pmid=22303441 |doi-access=free}}</ref> However, this estimation is likely to be a result of miscalculation, and its length would be only around {{cvt|3.9|m}}.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Gayford |first1=Joel H. |last2=Engelman |first2=Russell K. |last3=Sternes |first3=Phillip C. |last4=Itano |first4=Wayne M. |last5=Bazzi |first5=Mohamad |last6=Collareta |first6=Alberto |last7=Salas-Gismondi |first7=Rodolfo |last8=Shimada |first8=Kenshu |date=2 September 2024 |title=Cautionary tales on the use of proxies to estimate body size and form of extinct animals |journal=Ecology and Evolution |volume=14 |issue=9 |article-number=e70218 |doi=10.1002/ece3.70218 |issn=2045-7758 |pmid=39224151|pmc=11368419 |bibcode=2024EcoEv..1470218G }}</ref>
==== Crocodylians (Crocodylia) ==== * The largest [[caiman]] and likely one of the largest [[crocodylian]]s was ''[[Purussaurus|Purussaurus brasiliensis]]'' estimated at {{cvt|11|-|13|m|0}}.<ref>{{Cite journal| doi = 10.4072/rbp.2006.2.02| last1 = Bocquentin| first1 = Jean| last2 = Melo| first2 = Janira| title = ''Stupendemys souzai'' sp. nov (Pleurodira, Podocnemididae) from the Miocene-Pliocene of the Solimões Formation, Brazil| journal = Revista Brasileira de Paleontologia| volume = 9| issue = 2| pages = 187–192| year = 2006| doi-access = free| bibcode = 2006RvBrP...9..187B}}</ref> According to another information, maximum estimate measure {{cvt|11.4|m|0}} and almost {{cvt|7.8|tonne|ST}} in length and in weight respectively.<ref name="Largest crocodylomorphs">{{cite web |title=Крупнейшие крокодиломорфы |url=https://vk.com/@pseudosuchia-largest-crocodilymorphs |access-date=6 September 2022 |website=vk.com |language=ru}}</ref> However, a 2022 study estimated a length of 7.6–9.2 metres (25–30 ft) and a mass of 2–6.2 metric tons (2.2–6.8 short tons) using a phylogenetic approach; and a length of 9.2–10 metres (30–33 ft) and mass of 3.9–4.9 metric tons (4.3–5.4 short tons) using a non-phylogenetic approach.<ref name="Body size estimation of Caimaninae">{{cite journal |last1=Paiva |first1=Ana Laura S. |last2=Godoy |first2=Pedro L. |last3=Souza |first3=Ray B.B. |last4=Klein |first4=Wilfried |last5=Hsiou |first5=Annie S. |title=Body size estimation of Caimaninae specimens from the Miocene of South America |journal=Journal of South American Earth Sciences |date=October 2022 |volume=118 |article-number=103970 |doi=10.1016/j.jsames.2022.103970 |bibcode=2022JSAES.11803970P }}</ref> * Another giant caiman was ''[[Mourasuchus]]''. Various estimates suggest the biggest specimens reached {{cvt|9.47|m}} in length and {{cvt|8.5|tonne|ST}} in weight.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Cidade |first1=Giovanne M. |last2=Rincón |first2=Ascanio D. |last3=Solórzano |first3=Andrés |title=New cranial and postcranial elements of Mourasuchus (Alligatoroidea: Caimaninae) from the late Miocene of Venezuela and their palaeobiological implications |journal=Historical Biology |date=3 October 2021 |volume=33 |issue=10 |pages=2387–2399 |doi=10.1080/08912963.2020.1795844 |bibcode=2021HBio...33.2387C }}</ref> but more recent estimates suggest {{cvt|4.7|–|5.98|m}} in body length.<ref name="Body size estimation of Caimaninae"/> * The largest [[Alligatoroidea|alligatoroid]] is likely ''[[Deinosuchus|Deinosuchus riograndensis]]'' and ''Deinosuchus hatcheri'', both at {{cvt|12|m|0}} long and weighing {{cvt|8.5|tonne|ST}}.<ref name="schwimmer-size">{{cite book |title=King of the Crocodylians: The Paleobiology of Deinosuchus |last=Schwimmer |first=David R. |publisher=[[Indiana University Press]] |pages=42–63 |chapter=The Size of ''Deinosuchus'' |year=2002 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=0OsPJnC4CCwC |isbn=978-0-253-34087-0}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Lucas|first1=Spencer G. |first2=Robert M. |last2=Sullivan |first3=Justin A. |last3=Spielmann |title=The Giant Crocodylian Deinosuchus from the Upper Cretaceous of the San Juan Basin, New Mexico |journal=New Mexico Museum of Natural History and Science Bulletin |volume=35 |year=2006 |url=http://www.nmnaturalhistory.org/science/bulletins/35/sci_bulletin35_29.pdf |access-date=11 May 2010 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090617095747/http://www.nmnaturalhistory.org/science/bulletins/35/sci_bulletin35_29.pdf |archive-date=17 June 2009}}</ref> * The largest extinct species of the genus ''[[Alligator]]'' was the [[Haile alligator]] (''[[Alligator hailensis]]''), which had a skull {{cvt|52.5|cm}} long and was similar in size to the extant [[American alligator]] (''[[Alligator mississippiensis]]'').<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Stout |first1=Jeremy B. |title=New early Pleistocene Alligator (Eusuchia: Crocodylia) from Florida bridges a gap in Alligator evolution |journal=Zootaxa |date=23 October 2020 |volume=4868 |issue=1 |article-number=zootaxa.4868.1.3 |doi=10.11646/zootaxa.4868.1.3 |pmid=33311408 }}</ref> * The largest [[Gavialidae|gavialids]] were Asian ''[[Rhamphosuchus]]'' at {{cvt|8|–|11|m|0}}<ref>{{cite journal|last=Head |first=J. J. |year=2001 |title=Systematics and body size of the gigantic, enigmatic crocodyloid ''Rhamphosuchus crassidens'', and the faunal history of Siwalik Group (Miocene) crocodylians |journal=Journal of Vertebrate Paleontology |volume=21 |issue=Supplement to No. 3 |pages=1–117 |doi=10.1080/02724634.2001.10010852 |s2cid=220414868}}</ref><ref name=Martin2019/><ref name="Largest crocodylomorphs"/> and South American ''[[Gryposuchus]]'' at {{cvt|10.15|m}} in length.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Riff |first1=Douglas |last2=Aguilera |first2=Orangel A. |title=The world's largest gharials Gryposuchus: description of G. croizati n. sp. (Crocodylia, Gavialidae) from the Upper Miocene Urumaco Formation, Venezuela |journal=Paläontologische Zeitschrift |date=June 2008 |volume=82 |issue=2 |pages=178–195 |doi=10.1007/bf02988408 |bibcode=2008PalZ...82..178R }}</ref><ref name=Martin2019>{{cite journal |last1=Martin |first1=Jeremy E. |last2=Antoine |first2=Pierre-Olivier |last3=Perrier |first3=Vincent |last4=Welcomme |first4=Jean-Loup |last5=Metais |first5=Gregoire |last6=Marivaux |first6=Laurent |title=A large crocodyloid from the Oligocene of the Bugti Hills, Pakistan |journal=Journal of Vertebrate Paleontology |date=4 July 2019 |volume=39 |issue=4 |article-number=e1671427 |doi=10.1080/02724634.2019.1671427 |bibcode=2019JVPal..39E1427M |url=https://hal.archives-ouvertes.fr/hal-02376991/file/Martinetal_Bugti-HAL%20version.pdf }}</ref> * The basal [[crocodyloidea]]n ''[[Astorgosuchus bugtiensis]]'' from the [[Oligocene]] was large. It estimated at {{cvt|8|m}} in length.<ref name=Martin2019/> * The largest known [[Crocodylidae|true crocodile]] was ''[[Euthecodon]]'' which estimated to have reached {{cvt|6.4|-|8.6|m}} or even {{cvt|10|m}} long.<ref>Storrs, G. W. (2003). Late Miocene-Early Pliocene crocodilian fauna of Lothagam, southwest Turkana Basin, Kenya. In: [https://books.google.com/books?id=7qerAgAAQBAJ&q=Lothagam ''Lothagam: The Dawn of Humanity in Eastern Africa''] pp. 137–159. New York. Columbia University Press. {{ISBN|0-231-11870-8}}.</ref><ref name="Largest crocodylomorphs"/> The largest species of the modern ''[[Crocodylus]]'' were [[Kenya]]n ''[[Crocodylus thorbjarnarsoni]]'' at {{cvt|7.56|m}} in length,<ref>{{Cite journal | last1 = Brochu | first1 = C. A. | last2 = Storrs | first2 = G. W. | doi = 10.1080/02724634.2012.652324 | url = https://www.researchgate.net/publication/235961083 | title = A giant crocodile from the Plio-Pleistocene of Kenya, the phylogenetic relationships of Neogene African crocodylines, and the antiquity of ''Crocodylus'' in Africa | journal = Journal of Vertebrate Paleontology | volume = 32 | issue = 3 | page = 587 | year = 2012 | bibcode = 2012JVPal..32..587B | s2cid = 85103427 }}</ref><ref name="Largest crocodylomorphs"/> [[Tanzania]]n ''[[Crocodylus anthropophagus]]'' at {{cvt|7.5|m}} in length<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.palaeocritti.com/by-group/crocodylomorpha/eusuchia/crocodylus|title=''Crocodylus anthropophagus''|website=Palaeocritti.com|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160614172806/http://www.palaeocritti.com/by-group/crocodylomorpha/eusuchia/crocodylus|archive-date=14 June 2016}}</ref><ref>{{cite news|author=Ewen Callaway|date=24 February 2010|url=https://www.newscientist.com/article/dn18569-monster-crocodile-was-ancient-human-nightmare/|title=Monster crocodile was ancient human nightmare|work=[[New Scientist]]|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211207012828/https://www.newscientist.com/article/dn18569-monster-crocodile-was-ancient-human-nightmare/|archive-date=7 December 2021|url-status=live}}</ref> and indeterminate species from Kali Gedeh ([[Java]]) at {{cvt|6|-|7|m}} in length.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Delfino |first1=Massimo |last2=De Vos |first2=John |title=A giant crocodile in the Dubois Collection from the Pleistocene of Kali Gedeh (Java) |journal=Integrative Zoology |date=March 2014 |volume=9 |issue=2 |pages=141–147 |doi=10.1111/1749-4877.12065 |pmid=24673759 |hdl=2318/141647 |hdl-access=free }}</ref> * The largest known [[Mekosuchinae|mekosuchian]] is ''[[Paludirex|Paludirex vincenti]]'', which is estimated to reach up to {{convert|5|m|abbr=on}}.<ref name="Crikey">{{Cite web |title=Crikey! Massive prehistoric croc emerges from South East Queensland |url=https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2020/12/201221101144.htm |access-date=9 January 2021 |website=ScienceDaily |language=en}}</ref><ref name="W13">{{Cite journal |last1=Wroe |first1=S. |last2=Field |first2=J.H. |last3=Archer |first3=M. |last4=Grayson |first4=D.K. |last5=Price |first5=G.J. |last6=Louys |first6=J. |last7=Faith |first7=T. |last8=Webb |first8=G.E. |last9=Davidson |first9=I. |last10=Mooney |first10=S.D. |year=2013 |title=Climate change frames debate over the extinction of megafauna in Sahul (Pleistocene Australia-New Guinea). |journal=Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences |volume=110 |issue=22 |pages=8777–8781 |bibcode=2013PNAS..110.8777W |doi=10.1073/pnas.1302698110 |pmc=3670326 |pmid=23650401 |doi-access=free}}</ref><ref name="Molnar2004" /> Partial jaw specimen from [[Pliocene]] that is attributed to ''[[Quinkana]]'' suggest an individual about {{convert|6-7|m|abbr=on}} in length,<ref name="Molnar2004" /> although other species (known from [[Oligocene]] to [[Pleistocene]]) are smaller with length around {{convert|1.5-3|m|abbr=on}}.<ref name="W02">{{Cite journal |last=Wroe |first=Stephen |date=2002 |title=A review of terrestrial mammalian and reptilian carnivore ecology in Australian fossil faunas, and factors influencing their diversity: the myth of reptilian domination and its broader ramifications |journal=Australian Journal of Zoology |volume=50 |issue=1 |page=1 |doi=10.1071/zo01053 }}</ref><ref name="F90">{{Cite journal |last1=Flannery |first1=T. F. |year=1990 |title=Pleistocene faunal loss: implications of the aftershock for Australia's past and future. |journal=Archaeology in Oceania |volume=25 |issue=2 |pages=45–55 |doi=10.1002/j.1834-4453.1990.tb00232.x}}</ref><ref name="SPK13">{{Cite journal |last1=Sobbe |first1=Ian H. |last2=Price |first2=Gilbert J. |last3=Knezour |first3=Robert A. |date=2013 |title=A ziphodont crocodile from the late Pleistocene King Creek catchment, Darling Downs, Queensland |url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/256440898 |journal=[[Memoirs of the Queensland Museum]] |volume=52 |issue=2 |pages=601–606|doi=10.17082/j.2204-1478.56.2.2013-14 }}</ref>
==== Paralligatorids (Paralligatoridae) ==== The largest [[Paralligatoridae|paralligatorid]] was likely ''[[Kansajsuchus]],'' estimated at up to {{cvt|8|m|0}} long.<ref>{{cite book |last=Storrs |first=G. W. |author2=Efimov, M. B. |year=2000 |url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/259842662 |chapter=Mesozoic crocodyliforms of north-central Eurasia |title=The Age of Dinosaurs in Russia and Mongolia |editor1=Michael J. Benton |editor2=Mikhail A. Shishkin |editor3=David M. Unwin |editor4=Evgenii N. Kurochkin |publisher=Cambridge University Press |pages=402–419}}</ref>
==== Tethysuchians (Tethysuchia) ==== * Some extinct [[Pholidosauridae|pholidosaurids]] reached giant sizes. In the past, ''[[Sarcosuchus imperator]]'' was believed to be the largest crocodylomorph, with initial estimates proposing a length of {{cvt|12|m|0}} and a weight of {{cvt|8|tonne|ST}}.<ref name="SuperCrocFactSheet">{{cite web|url=http://www.supercroc.org/about.htm|title=Fact Sheet |access-date=22 September 2007 |last=Lyon |first=Gabrielle |work=SuperCroc |date=9 December 2001 |publisher=Project Exploration}}</ref> However, recent estimates have now shrunk to a length of {{convert|9|to|9.5|m|ft|1|abbr=on}} and a weight of {{convert|3.5|to|4.3|MT|ST}}.<ref name="Haley D O'Brien 2019">{{cite journal |last1=O'Brien |first1=Haley D |last2=Lynch |first2=Leigha M |last3=Vliet |first3=Kent A |last4=Brueggen |first4=John |last5=Erickson |first5=Gregory M |last6=Gignac |first6=Paul M |title=Crocodylian Head Width Allometry and Phylogenetic Prediction of Body Size in Extinct Crocodyliforms |journal=Integrative Organismal Biology |date=January 2019 |volume=1 |issue=1 |article-number=obz006 |doi=10.1093/iob/obz006 |pmid=33791523 |pmc=7671145 |doi-access=free }}</ref> Related to ''Sarcosuchus'', ''[[Chalawan thailandicus]]'' could have reached more than {{cvt|10|m}} in length,<ref>{{Cite journal | last1 = Martin | first1 = J. E. | last2 = Lauprasert | first2 = K. | last3 = Buffetaut | first3 = E. | last4 = Liard | first4 = R. | last5 = Suteethorn | first5 = V. |name-list-style=amp | title = A large pholidosaurid in the Phu Kradung Formation of north-eastern Thailand | doi = 10.1111/pala.12086 | journal = Palaeontology | pages = 757–769| year = 2013 | volume=57| issue = 4 | s2cid = 128482290 | doi-access = free }}</ref> although other estimates suggest {{cvt|7|-|8|m}}.<ref name=Martin2019/> * The largest [[Dyrosauridae|dyrosaurid]] was ''[[Phosphatosaurus gavialoides]],'' estimated at {{cvt|9|m|0}} in length.<ref>{{cite journal |last=Buffetaut |first=E. |year=1978 |title=Les Dyrosauridae (Crocodylia, Mesosuchia) des phosphates de l'Eocène inférieur de Tunisie: ''Dyrosaurus'', ''Rhabdognathus'', ''Phosphatosaurus'' |journal=Géologie Méditerranéenne |volume=5 |issue=2 |pages=237–256|doi=10.3406/geolm.1978.1046 }}</ref><ref name=Martin2019/>
==== Stomatosuchids (Stomatosuchidae) ==== ''[[Stomatosuchus]]'', a [[Stomatosuchidae|stomatosuchid]], was estimated at {{cvt|10|m|0}} in length.<ref>Naish, D. 2002. [https://darrennaish.files.wordpress.com/2013/01/naish-2001-geology-today-crocodilians.pdf Fossils explained 34: Crocodilians]. ''Geology Today'' '''2''': 71–77. [https://web.archive.org/web/20190124041845/https://darrennaish.files.wordpress.com/2013/01/naish-2001-geology-today-crocodilians.pdf Archived copy] from 24 January 2019.</ref>
==== Notosuchians (Notosuchia) ==== * Some of largest terrestrial [[notosuchia]]n crocodylomorphs were the [[Miocene]] [[Sebecidae|sebecid]] ''[[Barinasuchus]]'', with a skull of {{cvt|95|-|110|cm}} long, and [[Eocene]] sebecid ''[[Dentaneosuchus]]'' with estimated mandible length of {{cvt|1|m|ft|}}.<ref name=Barinasuchus2016>{{cite journal| vauthors = Molnar RE, de Vasconcellos FM |date=2016|title=Cenozoic dinosaurs in South America – revisited|journal=Memoirs of Museum Victoria|volume=74|pages=363–377|doi=10.24199/j.mmv.2016.74.25|doi-access=free}}</ref><ref name="AP">{{cite journal |last1=Martin |first1=Jeremy E. |last2=Pochat-Cottilloux |first2=Yohan |last3=Laurent |first3=Yves |last4=Perrier |first4=Vincent |last5=Robert |first5=Emmanuel |last6=Antoine |first6=Pierre-Olivier |title=Anatomy and phylogeny of an exceptionally large sebecid (Crocodylomorpha) from the middle Eocene of southern France |journal=Journal of Vertebrate Paleontology |date=28 October 2022 |volume=42 |issue=4 |article-number=e2193828 |doi=10.1080/02724634.2023.2193828 |bibcode=2022JVPal..42E3828M}}</ref> Various estimates suggest a possible length of these animals between {{cvt|3|-|10|m}}. Using proportion of ''[[Stratiotosuchus]]'' which is also large to have {{cvt|47|cm}} long skull,<ref name="Riff2011">{{cite journal |last=Riff |first=D. |author2=Kellner, A.W.A. |year=2011 |title=Baurusuchid crocodyliforms as theropod mimics: clues from the skull and appendicular morphology of Stratiotosuchus maxhechti (Upper Cretaceous of Brazil) |journal=Zoological Journal of the Linnean Society |volume=163 |issue=s1 |pages=s37–s56 |doi=10.1111/j.1096-3642.2011.00713.x |doi-access=free}}</ref> ''Barinasuchus'' is estimated to have length at least {{cvt|6.3|m|ft|}}.<ref name="Barinasuchus2016" /><ref name="AP" /> * Other huge notosuchian, although only known from fragmentary material, is an early member ''[[Razanandrongobe]]'', which skull size may exceeded that of ''Barinasuchus'' and overall length may be around {{cvt|7|m|0}}.<ref name="dalsasso2017">{{cite journal |last1=Dal Sasso |first1=C. |last2=Pasini |first2=G. |last3=Fleury |first3=G. |last4=Maganuco |first4=S. |year=2017 |title=''Razanandrongobe sakalavae'', a gigantic mesoeucrocodylian from the Middle Jurassic of Madagascar, is the oldest known notosuchian |journal=PeerJ |volume=5 |article-number=e3481 |doi=10.7717/peerj.3481 |pmc=5499610 |pmid=28690926 |doi-access=free }}</ref><ref name="bbc">{{cite news |date=4 July 2017 |title=Giant croc had teeth like a T. rex |work=[[BBC News]] |publisher=[[BBC]] |url=https://www.bbc.com/news/science-environment-40492978 |access-date=22 April 2020}}</ref>
==== Thalattosuchians (Thalattosuchia) ==== [[File:Plesiosuchus restoration.png|thumb|''[[Plesiosuchus]]'' compared to a human]] * The largest [[thalattosuchia]]n as well as the largest [[Teleosauroidea|teleosauroid]] was unnamed fossil remain from [[Paja Formation]], which may belongs to animal with length of {{cvt|9.6|m}},<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Cortés |first1=Dirley |last2=Larsson |first2=Hans C.E. |last3=Maxwell |first3=Erin E. |last4=Parra Ruge |first4=Mary Luz |last5=Patarroyo |first5=Pedro |last6=Wilson |first6=Jeffrey A. |date=6 October 2019 |title=An Early Cretaceous Teleosauroid (Crocodylomorpha: Thalattosuchia) from Colombia |journal=Ameghiniana |volume=56 |issue=5 |page=365 |doi=10.5710/amgh.26.09.2019.3269 |bibcode=2019Amegh..56..365C |s2cid=210110716 }}</ref> which is as large as outdated length estimate of the [[Early Cretaceous]] ''[[Machimosaurus rex]]'', more recently estimated at {{cvt|7.15|m}} in length.<ref name=Young2016>{{cite journal |last1=Young |first1=Mt |last2=Rabi |first2=M |last3=Bell |first3=Ma |last4=Foffa |first4=D |last5=Steel |first5=L |last6=Sachs |first6=S |last7=Peyer |first7=K |title=Big-headed marine crocodyliforms and why we must be cautious when using extant species as body length proxies for long-extinct relatives |journal=Palaeontologia Electronica |date=2016 |doi=10.26879/648 |doi-access=free }}</ref> ''[[Neosteneosaurus edwardsi]]'' (previously known as ''Steneosaurus edwardsi''<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Johnson |first1=Michela M. |last2=Young |first2=Mark T. |last3=Brusatte |first3=Stephen L. |title=The phylogenetics of Teleosauroidea (Crocodylomorpha, Thalattosuchia) and implications for their ecology and evolution |journal=PeerJ |date=2020 |volume=8 |article-number=e9808 |doi=10.7717/peerj.9808|pmid=33083104 |pmc=7548081 |doi-access=free }}</ref>) was the biggest [[Middle Jurassic]] crocodylomorph, it reached {{cvt|6.6|m}} long.<ref name=Young2016/> * ''[[Plesiosuchus]]'' was very large [[Metriorhynchidae|metriorhynchid]]. With the length of {{cvt|6.83|m}} it exceeded even some pliosaurids of the same time and locality such as ''[[Liopleurodon]]''.<ref>{{Cite journal | last1 = Young | first1 = M. T. | last2 = Brusatte | first2 = S. L. | last3 = De Andrade | first3 = M. B. | last4 = Desojo | first4 = J. B. | last5 = Beatty | first5 = B. L. | last6 = Steel | first6 = L. | last7 = Fernández | first7 = M. S. | last8 = Sakamoto | first8 = M. | last9 = Ruiz-Omeñaca | first9 = J. I. | last10 = Schoch | first10 = R. R. | editor1-last = Butler | editor1-first = Richard J | title = The Cranial Osteology and Feeding Ecology of the Metriorhynchid Crocodylomorph Genera ''Dakosaurus'' and ''Plesiosuchus'' from the Late Jurassic of Europe | doi = 10.1371/journal.pone.0044985 | journal = PLOS ONE | volume = 7 | issue = 9 | article-number = e44985 | year = 2012 | pmid = 23028723| pmc =3445579 | bibcode = 2012PLoSO...744985Y | doi-access = free }}</ref> Other huge metriorhynchids include ''[[Tyrannoneustes]]'' at {{cvt|5|m}} in length<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Young |first1=Mark T. |last2=de Andrade |first2=Marco Brandalise |last3=Brusatte |first3=Stephen L. |last4=Sakamoto |first4=Manabu |last5=Liston |first5=Jeff |title=The oldest known metriorhynchid super-predator: a new genus and species from the Middle Jurassic of England, with implications for serration and mandibular evolution in predacious clades |journal=Journal of Systematic Palaeontology |date=May 2013 |volume=11 |issue=4 |pages=475–513 |doi=10.1080/14772019.2012.704948 |bibcode=2013JSPal..11..475Y }}</ref> and ''[[Torvoneustes]]'' at {{cvt|4.7|m}} in length.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Young |first1=Mark T. |last2=Brusatte |first2=Stephen L. |last3=de Andrade |first3=Marco Brandalise |last4=Desojo |first4=Julia B. |last5=Beatty |first5=Brian L. |last6=Steel |first6=Lorna |last7=Fernández |first7=Marta S. |last8=Sakamoto |first8=Manabu |last9=Ruiz-Omeñaca |first9=Jose Ignacio |last10=Schoch |first10=Rainer R. |title=The Cranial Osteology and Feeding Ecology of the Metriorhynchid Crocodylomorph Genera Dakosaurus and Plesiosuchus from the Late Jurassic of Europe |journal=PLOS ONE |date=18 September 2012 |volume=7 |issue=9 |article-number=e44985 |doi=10.1371/journal.pone.0044985 |doi-access=free |pmid=23028723 |pmc=3445579 |bibcode=2012PLoSO...744985Y }}</ref>
==== Basal crocodylomorphs ==== ''[[Redondavenator]]'' was the largest [[Triassic]] crocodylomorph ever recorded,<ref name=Zanno2015>{{cite journal |last1=Zanno |first1=Lindsay E. |last2=Drymala |first2=Susan |last3=Nesbitt |first3=Sterling J. |last4=Schneider |first4=Vincent P. |title=Early crocodylomorph increases top tier predator diversity during rise of dinosaurs |journal=Scientific Reports |date=19 March 2015 |volume=5 |issue=1 |page=9276 |doi=10.1038/srep09276 |pmid=25787306 |pmc=4365386 |bibcode=2015NatSR...5.9276Z }}</ref> with a skull of at least {{cvt|60|cm|ft}} in length.<ref>{{cite journal |last=Nesbitt |first=Sterling J. |author2=Irmis, Randall B. |author3=Lucas, Spencer G. |author4= Hunt, Adrian P. |year=2005 |title=A giant crocodylomorph from the Upper Triassic of New Mexico |journal=Paläontologische Zeitschrift |volume=79 |issue=4 |pages=471–478 |doi=10.1007/bf02988373|bibcode=2005PalZ...79..471N |s2cid=128541365 }}</ref><ref>[https://ucmp.berkeley.edu/taxa/verts/archosaurs/crocodylomorpha.php Crocodylomorpha. Crocodiles and their relatives], [https://web.archive.org/web/20220620232707/https://ucmp.berkeley.edu/taxa/verts/archosaurs/crocodylomorpha.php archive copy] from 20 June 2022.</ref> Another huge basal crocodylomorph was ''[[Carnufex]]''<ref name=Zanno2015/> at {{cvt|3|m}} long even through that is immature.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Zanno |first1=Lindsay E. |last2=Drymala |first2=Susan |last3=Nesbitt |first3=Sterling J. |last4=Schneider |first4=Vincent P. |date=19 March 2015 |title=Early crocodylomorph increases top tier predator diversity during rise of dinosaurs |journal=Scientific Reports |volume=5 |article-number=9276 |doi=10.1038/srep09276 |pmc=4365386 |pmid=25787306 |bibcode=2015NatSR...5.9276Z }}</ref>
=== Pterosaurs (Pterosauria) === {{Main|Pterosaur size}} [[File:Hatzegopteryx-Witton-and-Naish-2017.png|thumb|''[[Hatzegopteryx]]'' (A-B), ''[[Arambourgiania]]'' (C) and ''[[Quetzalcoatlus]] sp.'' (D-E)]] * The largest known [[pterosaur]] was ''[[Quetzalcoatlus]] northropi'', at {{cvt|127|kg}} and with a wingspan of {{cvt|10|-|12|m|ft}}.<ref name=Witton2008>{{Cite journal | last1 = Witton | first1 = M. P. | last2 = Naish | first2 = D. | editor1-last = McClain | editor1-first = Craig R | doi = 10.1371/journal.pone.0002271 | title = A Reappraisal of Azhdarchid Pterosaur Functional Morphology and Paleoecology | journal = PLOS ONE | volume = 3 | issue = 5 | article-number = e2271 | year = 2008 | pmid = 18509539| pmc =2386974 | bibcode = 2008PLoSO...3.2271W | doi-access = free }}</ref> Another close contender is ''[[Hatzegopteryx]]'', also with a wingspan of {{cvt|12|m}} or more.<ref name=Witton2008/> This estimate is based on a skull {{cvt|3|m}} long.<ref name="buffetautetal2002">{{cite journal| doi = 10.1007/s00114-002-0307-1| last1 = Buffetaut | first1 = E. | last2 = Grigorescu | first2 = D. | last3 = Csiki | first3 = Z. | year = 2002 | title = A new giant pterosaur with a robust skull from the latest Cretaceous of Romania | journal = Naturwissenschaften | volume = 89 | issue = 4| pages = 180–184 | pmid = 12061403| bibcode = 2002NW.....89..180B | s2cid = 15423666 | url = http://doc.rero.ch/record/16209/files/PAL_E3417.pdf }}</ref> Yet another possible contender for the title is ''[[Cryodrakon]]'' which had a {{convert|10|m|ft|adj=on|abbr=}} wingspan.<ref name=Paul2022-2/> An unnamed [[Pterodactyloidea|pterodactyloid]] pterosaur from the [[Nemegt Formation]] could reach a wingspan of nearly {{cvt|10|m}}.<ref name=Andres2017>{{cite journal |author=Takanobu Tsuihiji |author2=Brian Andres |author3=Patrick M O'Connor |author4=Mahito Watabe |author5=Khishigjav Tsogtbaatar |author6=Mainbayar Buuvei |date=2017 |url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/320219078 |title=Gigantic pterosaurian remains from the Upper Cretaceous of Mongolia |journal=Journal of Vertebrate Paleontology |volume=37 |issue=5 |article-number=e1361431 |doi=10.1080/02724634.2017.1361431|bibcode=2017JVPal..37E1431T |s2cid=134424023 }}</ref><ref>{{Cite web | url=http://news.nationalgeographic.com/2017/10/new-pterosaur-found-mongolia-largest-fossils-science/ | title=Ancient Winged Terror Was One of the Largest Animals to Fly| date=31 October 2017 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190330060740/https://news.nationalgeographic.com/2017/10/new-pterosaur-found-mongolia-largest-fossils-science/ |archive-date=30 March 2019}}</ref> According to various assumptions, the wingspan of ''[[Arambourgiania philadelphiae]]'' reached from {{cvt|8|m}} to more than {{cvt|10|m}}.<ref name=Andres2017/><ref name=Paul2022-2>{{Cite book|last=Paul|first=Gregory S.|title=The Princeton Field Guide to Pterosaurs|year=2022|publisher=Princeton University Press|pages=155–172|doi=10.1515/9780691232218|isbn=978-0-691-23221-8|s2cid=249332375 }}</ref> South American ''[[Tropeognathus]]'' reached the maximum wingspan of {{cvt|8.7|m}}.<ref name=kellneretal2013>{{Cite journal |last1= Kellner |first1= A. W. A. |last2= Campos |first2= D. A. |last3= Sayão |first3= J. M. |last4= Saraiva |first4= A. N. A. F. |last5= Rodrigues |first5= T. |last6= Oliveira |first6= G. |last7= Cruz |first7= L. A. |last8= Costa |first8= F. R. |last9= Silva |first9= H. P. |last10= Ferreira |first10= J. S. |title= The largest flying reptile from Gondwana: A new specimen of Tropeognathus cf. T. Mesembrinus Wellnhofer, 1987 (Pterodactyloidea, Anhangueridae) and other large pterosaurs from the Romualdo Formation, Lower Cretaceous, Brazil |doi= 10.1590/S0001-37652013000100009 |journal= Anais da Academia Brasileira de Ciências |volume= 85 |issue= 1 |pages= 113–135 |year= 2013 |pmid= 23538956|doi-access= free }}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal | last1 = Rodrigues | first1 = T. | last2 = Kellner | first2 = A. | doi = 10.3897/zookeys.308.5559 | title = Taxonomic review of the ''Ornithocheirus'' complex (Pterosauria) from the Cretaceous of England | journal = ZooKeys | issue = 308 | pages = 1–112 | year = 2013 | pmid = 23794925| pmc = 3689139| doi-access = free | bibcode = 2013ZooK..308....1R }}</ref> * The largest of non-[[Pterodactyloidea|pterodactyloid]] pterosaurs as well as the largest [[Jurassic]] pterosaur<ref>{{cite web |title=Fossil of largest Jurassic pterosaur found on Skye |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/science-environment-60407928 |website=[[BBC News]] |access-date=22 February 2022 |date=22 February 2022}}</ref> was ''[[Dearc]]'', with an estimated wingspan between {{cvt|2.2|m}} and {{cvt|3.8|m}}.<ref>{{Cite Q|Q110984418}}</ref> Only a fragmentary [[Rhamphorhynchidae|rhamphorhynchid]] specimen from [[Germany]] could be larger (184% the size of the biggest ''[[Rhamphorhynchus]]'').<ref name=Spindler2021>{{cite journal |last1=Spindler |first1=Frederik |last2=Ifrim |first2=Christina |title=Die Spur einer Spur – ein möglicher erster Flugsaurier aus Ettling Trace of a trace – a putative first pterosaur from the Ettling locality |journal=Archaeopteryx|volume=37|pages= 75–83|date=2021 |url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/357673254}}</ref> Other large non-pterodactyloid pterosaurs were ''[[Sericipterus]]'', ''[[Campylognathoides]]'' and ''[[Harpactognathus]]'', with the wingspan of {{cvt|1.73|m}},<ref name=Andres2021>{{cite journal |last1=Andres |first1=Brian |last2=Clark |first2=James M. |last3=Xing |first3=Xu |title=A new rhamphorhynchid pterosaur from the Upper Jurassic of Xinjiang, China, and the phylogenetic relationships of basal pterosaurs |journal=Journal of Vertebrate Paleontology |date=29 January 2010 |volume=30 |issue=1 |pages=163–187 |doi=10.1080/02724630903409220 |bibcode=2010JVPal..30..163A }}</ref> {{cvt|1.75|m}},<ref name=Andres2021/> and {{cvt|2.5|m}},<ref name=Spindler2021/> respectively.
=== Choristoderes (Choristodera) === The largest known [[choristodera]]n, ''[[Kosmodraco|Kosmodraco dakotensis]]'' (previously known as ''Simoedosaurus dakotensis''<ref name=Brownstein2022/>) is estimated to have had a total length of around {{cvt|5|m|ft}}.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Matsumoto |first1=R. |last2=Evans |first2=S. E. |title=Choristoderes and the freshwater assemblages of Laurasia |journal=Journal of Iberian Geology |date=2010 |volume=36 |issue=2 |pages=253–274 |doi=10.5209/rev_JIGE.2010.v36.n2.11 |bibcode=2010JIbG...36..253M |doi-access=free }}</ref><ref name=Brownstein2022>{{Cite journal|last1=Brownstein|first1=C.D.|year=2022|title=High morphological disparity in a bizarre Paleocene fauna of predatory freshwater reptiles|journal=BMC Ecol Evol |volume=22|issue=34|article-number=34|doi=10.1186/s12862-022-01985-z|pmid=35313822|pmc=8935759 |doi-access=free }}</ref>
=== Tanystropheids (Tanystropheidae) === [[File:Tanystropheus recon 5.jpg|thumb|Reconstruction of ''[[Tanystropheus]]'', note that anatomical features based on smaller species ''T. longobardicus'', while size is based on ''T. hydroides'']] ''[[Tanystropheus]]'', the largest of all [[Tanystropheidae|tanystropheids]], reached up to {{cvt|5|m}} in length.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=De-Oliveira |first1=Tiane M. |last2=Pinheiro |first2=Felipe L. |last3=Stock Da-Rosa |first3=Átila Augusto |last4=Dias-Da-Silva |first4=Sérgio |last5=Kerber |first5=Leonardo |title=A new archosauromorph from South America provides insights on the early diversification of tanystropheids |journal=PLOS ONE |date=8 April 2020 |volume=15 |issue=4 |article-number=e0230890 |doi=10.1371/journal.pone.0230890 |doi-access=free |pmid=32267850 |pmc=7141609 |bibcode=2020PLoSO..1530890D }}</ref>
=== Thalattosaurs (Thalattosauria) === The largest species of [[Thalattosauria|thalattosaur]], ''[[Miodentosaurus|Miodentosaurus brevis]]'' grew to more than {{cvt|4|m}} in length.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Zhao|first1=Li-Jun|last2=Sato|first2=Tamaki|last3=Liu|first3=Jun|last4=Li|first4=Chun|last5=Wu|first5=Xiao-Chun|date=2010|title=A new skeleton of ''Miodentosaurus brevis'' (Diapsida: Thallatosauria) with a further study of the taxon|url=http://www.ivpp.cas.cn/cbw/gjzdwxb/xbwzxz/201002/P020100202389848150350.pdf|journal=Vertebrata PalAsiatica|volume=48|issue=1|pages=1–10}}</ref> The second largest member of this group is ''[[Concavispina]]'' with a length of {{cvt|3.64|m}}.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Liu |first1=Jun |last2=Zhao |first2=Li-Jun |last3=Li |first3=Chun |last4=He |first4=Tao |title=Osteology of Concavispina biseridens (Reptilia, Thalattosauria) from the Xiaowa Formation (Carnian), Guanling, Guizhou, China |journal=Journal of Paleontology |date=March 2013 |volume=87 |issue=2 |pages=341–350 |doi=10.1666/12-059R1.1 |bibcode=2013JPal...87..341L }}</ref>
=== Ichthyosaurs (Ichthyosauria) === [[File:Ichthyotitan Size Comparison.svg|thumb|right|Estimated size of ''[[Ichthyotitan]]'' compared to a human]] * The largest known [[Shastasauridae|shastasaur]], [[Ichthyosauria|ichthyosaur]] and marine reptile was ''[[Ichthyotitan]]'', which is estimated to have measured {{cvt|25|m|0}}. In addition to the Aust specimen, which has been attributed to ''Ichthyotitan'', could've measured over {{cvt|30|m|0}}.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Lomax |first1=Dean R. |last2=Salle |first2=Paul de la |last3=Perillo |first3=Marcello |last4=Reynolds |first4=Justin |last5=Reynolds |first5=Ruby |last6=Waldron |first6=James F. |date=17 April 2024 |title=The last giants: New evidence for giant Late Triassic (Rhaetian) ichthyosaurs from the UK |journal=PLOS ONE |language=en |volume=19 |issue=4 |article-number=e0300289 |doi=10.1371/journal.pone.0300289 |doi-access=free |pmc=11023487 |pmid=38630678 |bibcode=2024PLoSO..1900289L }}</ref> Another large shastasaur ''[[Shastasaurus sikanniensis]]'' at {{cvt|21|m|0}} in length<ref>{{cite web|title=Triassic Giant|url=http://www.tyrrellmuseum.com/exhibits/triassic_giant.htm|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110716093513/http://www.tyrrellmuseum.com/exhibits/triassic_giant.htm|url-status=live|access-date=13 October 2022|archive-date=16 July 2011}}</ref><ref name="Sander2022">{{cite journal |last1=Martin Sander |first1=P. |last2=Pérez De Villar |first2=Pablo Romero |last3=Furrer |first3=Heinz |last4=Wintrich |first4=Tanja |title=Giant Late Triassic ichthyosaurs from the Kössen Formation of the Swiss Alps and their paleobiological implications |journal=Journal of Vertebrate Paleontology |date=October 2021 |volume=41 |issue=6 |article-number=e2046017 |doi=10.1080/02724634.2021.2046017 |bibcode=2021JVPal..41E6017M |url=https://figshare.com/articles/journal_contribution/19670558 }}</ref> and {{cvt|81.5|t|lb}} in weight.<ref name="Sander2021" /> ''[[Shonisaurus popularis]]'' was another large Ichthyosaur, measuring {{cvt|15|m}} in length and weighing {{cvt|29.7|t|lb}}.<ref name="Sander2022" /> * ''[[Thalattoarchon|Thalattoarchon saurophagis]]'' is also reasonably large, being at {{cvt|8.6|m|ft}} and around {{cvt|4.3|t|lb}}.<ref name="Fea13">{{cite journal |author1=Nadia B. Fröbisch |author2=Jörg Fröbisch |author3=P. Martin Sander |author4=Lars Schmitz |author5=Olivier Rieppel |year=2013 |title=Macropredatory ichthyosaur from the Middle Triassic and the origin of modern trophic networks |journal=[[Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences|Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America]] |volume=110 |issue=4 |pages=1393–1397 |bibcode=2013PNAS..110.1393F |doi=10.1073/pnas.1216750110 |pmc=3557033 |pmid=23297200 |doi-access=free}}</ref> * The largest [[cymbospondylidae]] ichthyosaur as well as the largest animal of the [[Middle Triassic]] was ''[[Cymbospondylus youngorum]]'' at {{cvt|17.65|m|ft}} in length<ref name="Sander2021" /> and {{cvt|44.7|t|lbs}} in weight.<ref name="Sander2021">{{cite journal |last1=Sander |first1=P. Martin |last2=Griebeler |first2=Eva Maria |last3=Klein |first3=Nicole |last4=Juarbe |first4=Jorge Velez |last5=Wintrich |first5=Tanja |last6=Revell |first6=Liam J. |last7=Schmitz |first7=Lars |title=Early giant reveals faster evolution of large body size in ichthyosaurs than in cetaceans |journal=Science |date=24 December 2021 |volume=374 |issue=6575 |article-number=eabf5787 |doi=10.1126/science.abf5787 |pmid=34941418 |url=http://publication.plazi.org/id/FF88FF9B3839FFA665654B42FFAEA418 }}</ref>
=== Pareiasaurs (Pareiasauria) === Largest [[Pareiasauridae|pareiasaur]]s reached up to {{cvt|3|m}} in length. Such sizes had [[Middle Permian]] ''[[Bradysaurus]]'', ''[[Embrithosaurus]]'', and ''[[Nochelesaurus]]'' from [[South Africa]],<ref name=Romano2021>{{cite journal |author=Marco Romano |author2=Fabio Manucci |author3=Bruce Rubidge |author4=Marc J. Van den Brandt|title=Volumetric Body Mass Estimate and in vivo Reconstruction of the Russian Pareiasaur ''Scutosaurus karpinskii''|journal=Frontiers in Ecology and Evolution|volume=9|date=17 June 2021|article-number=692035 |doi=10.3389/fevo.2021.692035|doi-access=free |bibcode=2021FrEEv...992035R |hdl=11573/1634310|hdl-access=free}}</ref> and the [[Late Permian]] ''[[Scutosaurus]]'' from [[Russia]].<ref name=Romano2021/> The most robust ''Scutosaurus'' had {{cvt|1.16|t|lb}} in body mass.<ref name=Romano2021/>
=== Captorhinids (Captorhinidae) === The heavy built ''[[Moradisaurus|Moradisaurus grandis]]'', with a length of {{cvt|2|m}},<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gUpBDwAAQBAJ&q=%22captorhinid+reptile+Moradisaurus+grandis%22&pg=PT139|title=Carboniferous Giants and Mass Extinction: The Late Paleozoic Ice Age World|first=George R. Jr.|last=McGhee|date=7 August 2018|publisher=Columbia University Press|access-date=16 October 2019|via=Google Books|isbn=978-0-231-54338-5}}</ref> is the largest known [[Captorhinidae|captorhinid]].<ref>{{Cite journal|title=The ontogenetic transformation of the mesosaurid tarsus: a contribution to the origin of the primitive amniotic astragalus|first1=Graciela|last1=Piñeiro|first2=Pablo|last2=Núñez Demarco|first3=Melitta D.|last3=Meneghel|date=17 May 2016|journal=PeerJ|volume=4|article-number = e2036|doi=10.7717/peerj.2036|pmid=27231658|pmc=4878385 |doi-access=free }}</ref> The second largest captorhinid was ''[[Labidosaurikos]]'' with the largest adult skull specimen {{cvt|28|cm}} long.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Dodick |first1=J.T. |last2=Modesto |first2=S.P. |date=1995 |title=The cranial anatomy of the captorhinid reptile Labidosaurikos meachami from the Lower Permian of Oklahoma |journal=Palaeontology |volume=38 |issue=3 |page=687 }}</ref>
== Non-avian dinosaurs (Dinosauria) == {{further|Dinosaur}} {{further|Dinosaur size}}
=== Sauropodomorphs (Sauropodomorpha) === The largest of non-sauropod [[Sauropodomorpha|sauropodomorphs]] ("[[prosauropod]]") was ''[[Euskelosaurus]]''. It reached {{cvt|12.2|m}} in length and {{cvt|2|MT|ST}} in weight.<ref>{{cite book |isbn = 978-0-486-83855-7 | url = https://books.google.com/books?id=QCC9DwAAQBAJ | title = The Fossil Book: A Record of Prehistoric Life | publisher = Dover Publications | date = 15 January 2020 | access-date = 13 October 2022 | page = 444 |author=[[Patricia Vickers-Rich|Patricia Vickers Rich]] |author2=Thomas Hewitt Rich |author3=[[Mildred Adams Fenton]] |author4=Carroll Lane }}</ref> Another huge sauropodomorph ''[[Yunnanosaurus youngi]]'' reached {{cvt|13|m}} long.<ref>Lu, J., Li, T., Zhong, S., Azuma, Y., Fujita, M., Dong, Z., and Ji, Q. (2007). "[https://www.semanticscholar.org/paper/NEW-YUNNANOSAURID-DINOSAUR-(DINOSAURIA%2C-FROM-THE-OF-Junchang-Li/a8549bec983b40bcb561a5bc3ff4e1bc0b0f6979 New yunnanosaurid dinosaur (Dinosauria, Prosauropoda) from the Middle Jurassic Zhanghe Formation of Yuanmou, Yunnan Province of China]". Memoir of the Fukui Prefectural Dinosaur Museum, 6: 1–15.</ref>
==== Sauropods (Sauropoda) ==== [[File:Longest dinosaurs2.svg|alt=|thumb|upright=2.0|Size comparison of selected giant [[Sauropoda|sauropod]] dinosaurs (from left to right): ''[[Supersaurus]]'', ''[[Argentinosaurus]]'', ''[[Diplodocus]]'', ''[[Mamenchisaurus]]'', and ''[[Sauroposeidon]]'']] * A mega-[[sauropoda|sauropod]], ''[[Maraapunisaurus|Maraapunisaurus fragillimus]]'' (previously known as ''Amphicoelias fragillimus''), is a contender for the largest-known dinosaur in history. It has been estimated at {{cvt|58|-|60|m|0}} in maximum length and {{cvt|122400|kg}} in weight.<ref name="giant sauropod">{{cite journal|author=Kenneth Carpenter|url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/40662741|title=Biggest of the big: a critical re-evaluation of the mega-sauropod ''Amphicoelias fragillimus''|journal=In "Paleontology and Geology of the Upper Jurassic Morrison Formation". New Mexico Museum of Natural History and Science Bulletin|volume=36|pages=131–138|year=2006}}</ref> Unfortunately, the fossil remains of this dinosaur have been lost.<ref name="giant sauropod" /> More recently, it was estimated at {{Cvt|35|–|40|m|ft}} in length and {{cvt|80|-|120|t|lb}} in weight.<ref name=Determining2019>{{cite journal |last1=Paul |first1=Gregory |title=Determining the Largest Known Land Animal: A Critical Comparison of Differing Methods for Restoring the Volume and Mass of Extinct Animals |journal=Annals of Carnegie Museum |date=31 December 2019 |volume=85 |issue=4 |page=335 |doi=10.2992/007.085.0403 |bibcode=2019AnCM...85..335P }}</ref> * Known from the incomplete and now disintegrated remains, the [[Late Cretaceous]] ''[[Bruhathkayosaurus matleyi]]'' was an anomalously large sauropod.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Galton |first1=Peter M. |last2=Ayyasami |first2=Krishnan |title=Purported latest bone of a plated dinosaur (Ornithischia: Stegosauria), a 'dermal plate' from the Maastrichtian (Upper Cretaceous) of southern India |journal=Neues Jahrbuch für Geologie und Paläontologie - Abhandlungen |date=July 2017 |volume=285 |issue=1 |pages=91–96 |doi=10.1127/njgpa/2017/0671 }}</ref> Informal estimations suggested as huge parameters as {{cvt|45|m|ft}} in length and {{cvt|139|-|220|t|lb}} in weight.{{sfn|Molina-Pérez|Larramendi|2020|p=46}} Some estimates, however, suggest {{cvt|37|m|ft}} and {{cvt|95|t|lb}} but it's still much heavier than most other sauropods.{{sfn|Molina-Pérez|Larramendi|2020|p=46}} More recent estimations by Gregory Paul in 2023 has placed its weight range around {{cvt|110|t|lb}} to a {{cvt|170|t|lb}}. If true, it would make ''Bruhathkayosaurus'' the single largest terrestrial animal to have walked the earth and would have rivalled the largest blue whale recorded.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Paul |first1=Gregory S. |last2=Larramendi |first2=Asier |date=11 April 2023 |title=Body mass estimate of ''Bruhathkayosaurus'' and other fragmentary sauropod remains suggest the largest land animals were about as big as the greatest whales |journal=Lethaia |volume=56 |issue=2 |pages=1–11 |doi=10.18261/let.56.2.5 |bibcode=2023Letha..56..2.5P |s2cid=259782734 |doi-access=free }}</ref> * BYU 9024, a massive [[cervical vertebra]] found in [[Utah]],<ref>{{cite web|author=[[Michael P. Taylor|Mike Taylor]]|date=2019 |title=Supersaurus, Ultrasaurus and Dystylosaurus in 2019, part 2: what we found in Utah|url=https://svpow.com/2019/06/15/supersaurus-ultrasaurus-and-dystylosaurus-in-2019-part-2-what-we-found-in-utah/|website=svpow.com|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220921232450/https://svpow.com/2019/06/15/supersaurus-ultrasaurus-and-dystylosaurus-in-2019-part-2-what-we-found-in-utah/|archive-date=21 September 2022|url-status=live}}</ref> may belong to a ''[[Barosaurus|Barosaurus lentus]]''{{sfn|Molina-Pérez|Larramendi|2020|p=36}}<ref>{{cite web|author=[[Michael P. Taylor|Mike Taylor]]|url=https://svpow.com/2016/09/16/how-horrifying-was-the-neck-of-barosaurus/|title=How horrifying was the neck of Barosaurus?|date=16 September 2016|website=svpow.com|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220922040417/https://svpow.com/2016/09/16/how-horrifying-was-the-neck-of-barosaurus/|archive-date=22 September 2022|url-status=live}}</ref> or ''[[Supersaurus|Supersaurus vivianae]]''<ref name=Brian2021/> of a huge size, possibly {{cvt|45|-|48|m|ft}} in length and {{cvt|60|-|66|t|lb}} in body mass.{{sfn|Molina-Pérez|Larramendi|2020|p=36}}<ref>{{Cite web|author=[[Michael P. Taylor|Mike Taylor]] |url=https://svpow.com/2019/06/16/supersaurus-ultrasaurus-and-dystylosaurus-in-2019-part-2b-the-size-of-the-byu-9024-animal/ |title=The size of the BYU 9024 animal|date=16 June 2019 |website=svpow.com|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220416135446/https://svpow.com/2019/06/16/supersaurus-ultrasaurus-and-dystylosaurus-in-2019-part-2b-the-size-of-the-byu-9024-animal/|archive-date=16 April 2022|url-status=live}}</ref> ''Supersaurus vivianae'' itself may have been the longest dinosaur yet discovered as a study of 3 specimens suggested length of {{cvt|39|m}} or over {{cvt|40|m}}.<ref name=Brian2021>{{cite web|last=Curtice |first=Brian|date=2021 |title=New Dry Mesa Dinosaur Quarry Supersaurus vivianae (Jensen 1985) axial elements provide additional insight into its phylogenetic relationships and size, suggesting an animal that exceeded 39 meters in length|page=92|url=https://vertpaleo.org/wp-content/uploads/2021/10/SVP_2021_VirtualBook_final.pdf |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221008180700/https://vertpaleo.org/wp-content/uploads/2021/10/SVP_2021_VirtualBook_final.pdf |archive-date=8 October 2022|url-status=live}}</ref> [[File:Mamenchisaurus in Japan.jpg|thumb|Mounted skeleton of ''[[Mamenchisaurus sinocanadorum]]'']] * ''[[Mamenchisaurus sinocanadorum]]'' was likely the largest [[Mamenchisauridae|mamenchisaurid]], reaching nearly {{cvt|35|m|ft}} in length and {{cvt|60|-|80|t|lb}} in weight.<ref name=Determining2019/> ''[[Xinjiangtitan shanshanesis]]'' from the same family had {{cvt|15|m|ft}}-long neck, about 55% of its total length that could be at least {{cvt|27|m|ft}}.{{sfn|Molina-Pérez|Larramendi|2020|p=156}} * The [[Middle Jurassic]] ''[[Breviparopus|Breviparopus taghbaloutensis]]'' was mentioned in ''[[The Guinness Book of Records]]'' as the longest dinosaur at {{cvt|48|m|ft}} although this animal is known only from [[fossil track]]s.<ref>{{cite book|author=Peter Matthews|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=oqoOYFaIIigC&q=Breviparopus+48+m|title=The Guinness Book of Records|date=1992|publisher=Guinness Publishing|page=42| isbn=978-0-85112-978-5 }}</ref><ref>{{cite book|author=Tim Footman, Mark C. Young|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Kud_g_0I6_AC&q=Breviparopus|title=Guinness World Records 2001|publisher=Bantam Books|date=May 2001|page=276|isbn=978-0-553-58375-5}}</ref> Originally thought to be a [[brachiosauridae|brachiosaurid]], it was later identified as a huge [[Diplodocoidea|diplodocoid]], possibly {{cvt|33.5|m|ft}} in length and {{cvt|62|t|lb}} in weight.{{sfn|Molina-Pérez|Larramendi|2020|p=32}} * The tallest sauropod was ''[[Sauroposeidon proteles]]'' with estimated height at {{cvt|16.5|–|18|m|ft}}.{{sfn|Molina-Pérez|Larramendi|2020 |p=55}}<ref>{{cite journal |last=Wedel |first=Mathew J. |author2=Cifelli, R. L. |author3=Sanders, R. K. |s2cid=59141243 |year=2000 |title=Osteology, paleobiology, and relationships of the sauropod dinosaur ''Sauroposeidon'' |url=https://pdfs.semanticscholar.org/9efe/8db6eb32247f90a760110d5a9695977fb761.pdf |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200626075134/https://pdfs.semanticscholar.org/9efe/8db6eb32247f90a760110d5a9695977fb761.pdf |archive-date=26 June 2020 |journal=Acta Palaeontologica Polonica |volume=45 |pages=343–388}}</ref><ref>{{cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=zs9aAAAAYAAJ&q=Sauroposeidon |title=Guinness World Records |publisher=Bantam Books |page=110 |date=2004 |isbn=978-0-553-58712-8}}</ref> ''[[Asiatosaurus]]'' could potentially reach {{cvt|17.5|m|ft}} in height, but this animal is known only from teeth.{{sfn|Molina-Pérez|Larramendi|2020|p=55}} ''[[Giraffatitan]]'' was estimated at {{cvt|16|m|ft}} in height.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Paul |first1=G.S. |year=1988 |title=The brachiosaur giants of the Morrison and Tendaguru with a description of a new subgenus, ''Giraffatitan'', and a comparison of the world's largest dinosaurs |journal=Hunteria |volume=2 |issue=3| pages=1–14 |url=http://gspauldino.com/HunteriaBrachio.pdf |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220627172333/http://gspauldino.com/HunteriaBrachio.pdf |archive-date=27 June 2022 |url-status=live}}</ref>
[[File:Argentinosaurus in Fernbank Museum of Natural History.jpg|thumb|Reconstructed skeleton of ''[[Argentinosaurus]]'']] Other huge sauropods include ''[[Argentinosaurus]]'', ''[[Alamosaurus]]'', and ''[[Puertasaurus]]'' with estimated lengths of {{cvt|30|-|33|m}} and weights of {{cvt|50|-|80|tonne|ST|0}}.<ref name="Paul, G.S. 2010">Paul, G.S., 2010, The Princeton Field Guide to Dinosaurs, Princeton University Press.</ref> ''[[Patagotitan]]'' was estimated at {{Cvt|37|m|ft}} in length<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.bbc.com/news/science-environment-40889321|title=Giant dinosaur slims down... a bit|date=10 August 2017|work=[[BBC News]] |access-date=17 October 2022|language=en-GB |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220921060918/https://www.bbc.com/news/science-environment-40889321|archive-date=21 September 2022 |url-status=live}}</ref> and {{cvt|57|tonne|ST}} in average weight,<ref name=Otero2020>{{cite journal|last1=Otero|first1=Alejandro |last2=Carballido|first2=José L.|last3=Pérez Moreno|first3=Agustín |year=2020|url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/344346605 |title=The appendicular osteology of ''Patagotitan mayorum'' (Dinosauria, Sauropoda)|journal=Journal of Vertebrate Paleontology |volume=40|issue=4 |at=e1793158|doi=10.1080/02724634.2020.1793158 |bibcode=2020JVPal..40E3158O |s2cid=225012747}}</ref> and was similar in size to ''Argentinosaurus'' and ''Puertasaurus''.<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://svpow.com/2017/08/09/dont-believe-the-hype-patagotitan-was-not-bigger-than-argentinosaurus/|title=Don't believe the hype: Patagotitan was not bigger than Argentinosaurus |date=9 August 2017|work=Sauropod Vertebra Picture of the Week |access-date=18 November 2018|language=en-US}}</ref> Giant sauropods like ''[[Supersaurus]]'', ''[[Sauroposeidon]]'', and ''[[Diplodocus]]'' probably rivaled them in length but not in weight.<ref name="giant sauropod" /> ''[[Dreadnoughtus]]'' was estimated at {{cvt|49|t|lb}} in weight<ref name=Otero2020/> and {{cvt|26|m|ft}} in length, but the most complete individual was immature when it died.<ref>{{Cite journal |title=A Gigantic, Exceptionally Complete Titanosaurian Sauropod Dinosaur from Southern Patagonia, Argentina |journal=Scientific Reports |date=4 September 2014 |volume=4 |issue=1 |page=6196 |doi=10.1038/srep06196 |last1=Lacovara |first1=Kenneth J. |last2=Lamanna |first2=M. C. |last3=Ibiricu |first3=L. M. |last4=Poole |first4=J. C. |last5=Schroeter |first5=E. R. |last6=Ullmann |first6=P. V. |last7=Voegele |first7=K. K. |last8=Boles |first8=Z. M. |last9=Carter |first9=A. M. |last10=Fowler |first10=E. K. |last11=Egerton |first11=V. M. |last12=Moyer |first12=A. E. |last13=Coughenour |first13=C. L. |last14=Schein |first14=J. P. |last15=Harris |first15=J. D. |last16=Martínez |first16=R. D. |last17=Novas |first17=F. E. |pmid=25186586 |pmc=5385829 |bibcode=2014NatSR...4.6196L }}</ref> ''[[Turiasaurus]]'' is considered the largest dinosaur from Europe,<ref>{{cite journal |author=Rafael Royo-Torres |author2=Alberto Cobos |author3=Luis Alcalá|date=2006|title=A Giant European Dinosaur and a New Sauropod Clade|journal=Science|volume=314 |issue=5807|pages=1925–1927|doi=10.1126/science.1132885 |pmid=17185599 |bibcode=2006Sci...314.1925R |s2cid=9343711 |url=http://doc.rero.ch/record/14800/files/PAL_E1949.pdf }}</ref><ref name="Winter 2011 Appendix"/> with an estimated length of {{cvt|30|m}} and a weight of {{cvt|50|t|ST}}.<ref name="Paul, G.S. 2010"/><ref name="Winter 2011 Appendix"/> However, lower estimates at {{cvt|21|m|ft}} and {{cvt|30|t|lb}} would make it smaller than the Portuguese ''[[Lusotitan]]'', which reached {{cvt|24|m|ft}} in length and {{cvt|34|t|lb}} in weight.{{sfn|Molina-Pérez|Larramendi|2020|p=28}}
Many large sauropods are still unnamed and may rival the current record holders: * The "[[List of informally named dinosaurs#Archbishop|Archbishop]]", a large brachiosaur that was discovered in 1930. {{As of|October 2023}}, a scientific paper on the specimen is still in progress.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://github.com/MikeTaylor/palaeo-archbishop|title=Paper describing NHMUK PV R5937, a Tendaguru brachiosaurid known as "The Archbishop"|date=25 August 2022|website=GitHub}}</ref> * ''[[Brachiosaurus nougaredi]]'' is yet another large brachiosaur from Early Cretaceous North Africa. The remains have been lost, but the sacrum drawing remains. It suggests a [[sacrum]] of almost {{cvt|1.3|m|ft}} long,<ref>{{cite journal|last=de Lapparent|first=A. F.|year=1960|title=Les dinosauriens du "continental intercalaire" du Sahara central"|trans-title=The dinosaurs of the "continental intercalaire" of the central Sahara|journal=Mémoires de la Société Géologique de France |series=Nouvelle Séries |volume=39|issue=1–6|pages=1–57|url=http://www.paleoglot.org/files/Lapparent_60.pdf|translator-last=Carrano|translator-first=Matthew|language=fr}}</ref> making it the largest dinosaur sacrum discovered so far, except those of ''Argentinosaurus'' and ''[[Apatosaurus]]''.<ref>{{cite journal |doi=10.1111/zoj.12029 |title=Osteology of the Late Jurassic Portuguese sauropod dinosaur ''Lusotitan atalaiensis'' (Macronaria) and the evolutionary history of basal titanosauriforms |journal=Zoological Journal of the Linnean Society|volume=168 |pages=98–206|year=2013|last1=Mannion|first1=P. D.|last2=Upchurch |first2=Paul|last3=Barnes|first3=Rosie N.|last4=Mateus|first4=Octávio |url=http://docentes.fct.unl.pt/sites/default/files/omateus/files/mannion_et_al_2013_sauropod_lusotitan_portugal.pdf}}</ref> * In 2010, the femur of a large sauropod was discovered in France. The femur suggests an animal that grew to immense sizes.<ref>{{cite web |url=http://paleoking.blogspot.com/2014/07/is-french-monster-euhelopodid.html|title=Is the French Monster a Euhelopodid?|last=Nima |website=paleoking.blogspot.com}}</ref>
=== Non-avian theropods (Theropoda) === [[File:Longest theropods.svg|alt=|thumb|upright=2.0|Size comparison of selected giant [[Theropoda|theropod]] dinosaurs (from left to right): ''[[Spinosaurus]]'', ''[[Giganotosaurus]]'', ''[[Mapusaurus]]'', ''[[Tyrannosaurus]]'', and ''[[Carcharodontosaurus]]'']]
* The largest [[Theropoda|theropod]] as well as the largest terrestrial predator yet known is ''[[Tyrannosaurus|Tyrannosaurus rex]]'', with the largest specimen known nicknamed Scotty (RSM P2523.8), located at the Royal Saskatchewan Museum, is reported to measure 13 m (43 ft) in length. Using a mass estimation technique that extrapolates from the circumference of the femur, Scotty was estimated as the largest known specimen at 8.87 metric tons (9.78 short tons) in body mass<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Persons |first1=W. Scott |last2=Currie |first2=Philip J. |last3=Erickson |first3=Gregory M. |title=An Older and Exceptionally Large Adult Specimen of Tyrannosaurus rex |journal=The Anatomical Record |date=April 2020 |volume=303 |issue=4 |pages=656–672 |doi=10.1002/ar.24118 |pmid=30897281 }}</ref> * Other large theropods were ''[[Giganotosaurus carolinii]]'', and ''[[Spinosaurus aegyptiacus]]'', whose largest specimens known estimated at {{cvt|13.2|m|0}}<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Calvo |first1=Jorge O. |last2=Coria |first2=Rodolfo| title=New specimen of Giganotosaurus Carolinii| journal=GAIA |pages=117–122 |year=1998 |url=http://www.mnhn.ul.pt/geologia/gaia/7.pdf |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080216074438/http://www.mnhn.ul.pt/geologia/gaia/7.pdf |archive-date=16 February 2008}}</ref> and {{cvt|12.3|m|0}}<ref name="henderson1999">{{cite journal |author1=Henderson DM |date=1 January 1999|title=Estimating the masses and centers of mass of extinct animals by 3-D mathematical slicing |journal=Paleobiology |volume=25 |issue=1 |pages=88–106 |url=http://paleobiol.geoscienceworld.org/cgi/content/abstract/25/1/88}}</ref> in length, and weigh between {{convert|4.2|to|13.8|MT|ST|abbr=on}}<ref name=coria&currie2002>{{cite journal|last1=Coria|first1=R. A.|last2=Currie|first2=P. J. |title=The braincase of ''Giganotosaurus carolinii'' (Dinosauria: Theropoda) from the Upper Cretaceous of Argentina|journal=Journal of Vertebrate Paleontology|year=2002|volume=22|issue=4|pages=802–811|doi=10.1671/0272-4634(2002)022[0802:TBOGCD]2.0.CO;2 |s2cid=85678725}}</ref><ref name="Coria1995">{{cite journal |last1=Coria |first1=R. A. |last2=Salgado |first2=L. |year=1995 |title=A new giant carnivorous dinosaur from the Cretaceous of Patagonia |journal=Nature |volume=377 |issue=6546| pages=225–226 |doi=10.1038/377224a0 |bibcode=1995Natur.377..224C |s2cid=30701725}}</ref><ref name="calvo&coria1998">{{cite journal |last1=Calvo |first1=J.O. |last2=Coria |first2=R.A. |year=1998 |title=New specimen of ''Giganotosaurus carolinii'' (CORIA & SALGADO, 1995), supports it as the largest theropod ever found |url=http://www.mnhn.ul.pt/geologia/gaia/7.pdf |journal=Gaia |volume=15 |pages=117–122 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080216074438/http://www.mnhn.ul.pt/geologia/gaia/7.pdf |archive-date=16 February 2008}}</ref><ref name=TH07>{{cite journal |last=Therrien |first=F. |author2=Henderson, D.M. |year=2007 |title=My theropod is bigger than yours...or not: estimating body size from skull length in theropods |journal=Journal of Vertebrate Paleontology |volume=27 |issue=1 |pages=108–115 |doi=10.1671/0272-4634(2007)27[108:MTIBTY]2.0.CO;2 |s2cid=86025320 }}</ref> and 14m (46 ft) in length and 7.4 metric tonnes (8.2 short tons) <ref>{{cite journal |last1=Sereno |first1=Paul C |last2=Myhrvold |first2=Nathan |last3=Henderson |first3=Donald M |last4=Fish |first4=Frank E |last5=Vidal |first5=Daniel |last6=Baumgart |first6=Stephanie L |last7=Keillor |first7=Tyler M |last8=Formoso |first8=Kiersten K |last9=Conroy |first9=Lauren L |title=Spinosaurus is not an aquatic dinosaur |journal=eLife |date=30 November 2022 |volume=11 |article-number=e80092 |doi=10.7554/eLife.80092|doi-access=free |pmid=36448670|pmc=9711522}}</ref> respectively (which makes ''[[Spinosaurus]] ''the longest terrestrial carnivore). Some other notable giant theropods (e.g. ''[[Carcharodontosaurus]]'','' [[Acrocanthosaurus]]'', and ''[[Mapusaurus]]'') may also have rivaled them in size. * ''[[Macroelongatoolithus]]'', ranging from {{cvt|34|-|61|cm|ft}} in length,<ref>{{cite thesis|author=Simon, D. J.|date=2014 |url=https://scholarworks.montana.edu/xmlui/bitstream/handle/1/8693/SimonD0814.pdf|title=Giant Dinosaur (theropod) Eggs of the Oogenus Macroelongatoolithus (Elongatoolithidae) from Southeastern Idaho: Taxonomic, Paleobiogeographic, and Reproductive Implications|type=Doctoral Dissertation |publisher=Montana State University |location=Bozeman|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210811064423/https://scholarworks.montana.edu/xmlui/bitstream/handle/1/8693/SimonD0814.pdf|archive-date=11 August 2021|url-status=live}}</ref> is the largest known type of [[egg fossil|dinosaur egg]].<ref name=Beibeilong/> It is assigned to [[Oviraptorosauria|oviraptorosaurs]] like ''[[Beibeilong]]''.<ref name=Beibeilong>{{cite journal |author=Pu, H. |author2=Zelenitsky, D.K. |author3=Lü, J. |author4=Currie, P.J. |author5=Carpenter, K. |author6=Xu, L. |author7=Koppelhus, E.B. |author8=Jia, S. |author9=Xiao, L. |author10=Chuang, H. |author11=Li, T. |author12=Kundrát, M. |author13=Shen, C. |title=Perinate and eggs of a giant caenagnathid dinosaur from the Late Cretaceous of central China|journal=Nature Communications |date=2017|volume=8|article-number=14952|doi=10.1038/ncomms14952 |pmid=28486442|pmc=5477524 |bibcode=2017NatCo...814952P }}</ref>
=== Armoured dinosaurs (Thyreophora) === The largest-known [[thyreophoran]] was ''[[Ankylosaurus]]'' at {{cvt|9|m|0}} in length and {{convert|6|tonne|ST}} in weight.<ref name="vickaryousetal2004">Vickaryous, M.K., [[Teresa Maryańska|Maryanska, T.]], & Weishampel, D.B. 2004. Ankylosauria. In: Weishampel, D.B., Dodson, P., & Osmólska, H. (Eds.). ''The Dinosauria'' (2nd edition). Berkeley: University of California Press. Pp. 363–392.</ref><ref name="coombs">{{cite journal|last=Coombs |first=Walter P.|title=Theoretical Aspects of Cursorial Adaptations in Dinosaurs |journal=[[The Quarterly Review of Biology]] |volume=53 |date=December 1978 |pages=393–418 |doi=10.1086/410790 |issue=4|s2cid=84505681}}</ref> ''[[Stegosaurus]]'' was also {{cvt|9|m}} long<ref name="Winter 2011 Appendix">{{cite book |last=Holtz |first=Thomas R. Jr. |year=2012 |title=Dinosaurs: The Most Complete, Up-to-Date Encyclopedia for Dinosaur Lovers of All Ages |url=http://www.geol.umd.edu/~tholtz/dinoappendix/HoltzappendixWinter2011.pdf |chapter=Winter 2011 Appendix |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221002170312/https://www.geol.umd.edu/~tholtz/dinoappendix/HoltzappendixWinter2011.pdf |archive-date=2 October 2022|url-status=live}}</ref> but around {{convert|5|tonne|ST}} tonnes in weight.{{citation needed|date=September 2014}}
=== Marginocephalians (Marginocephalia) === ==== Pachycephalosaurs (Pachycephalosauria) ==== The largest [[pachycephalosaur]] was the eponymous ''[[Pachycephalosaurus]]''. Previously claimed to be at {{cvt|7|m|ft}} in length,<ref name="Winter 2011 Appendix"/> it was later estimated about {{convert|4.5|m|1}} long and a weight of about {{convert|450|kg}}.<ref>{{cite book |last=Paul |first=Gregory S. |year=2010 |title=The Princeton Field Guide to Dinosaurs |url=https://archive.org/details/princetonfieldgu0000paul |url-access=registration |publisher=Princeton University Press |location=Princeton, NJ |page=[https://archive.org/details/princetonfieldgu0000paul/page/244 244] |isbn=978-0-691-13720-9}}</ref>
==== Ceratopsians (Ceratopsia) ==== [[File:Ceratopsidae Scale.svg|alt=|thumb|upright=2.0|Size comparison of several members of [[Ceratopsidae]] (from left to right): ''[[Nasutoceratops]]'', ''[[Styracosaurus]]'', ''[[Centrosaurus]]'', ''[[Pachyrhinosaurus]]'', ''[[Triceratops]],'' ''[[Pentaceratops]]'', ''[[Chasmosaurus]],'' and ''[[Anchiceratops]]'']] The largest [[ceratopsian]] known is ''[[Triceratops|Triceratops horridus]]'', along with the closely related ''[[Eotriceratops|Eotriceratops xerinsularis]]'' both with estimated lengths of {{cvt|9|m|0}}. ''[[Pentaceratops]]'' and several other ceratopsians rival them in size.<ref>{{cite book| last = Holtz| first = Thomas R. Jr.| title = Dinosaurs: The Most Complete, Up-to-Date Encyclopedia for Dinosaur Lovers of All Ages| publisher = Random House| year = 2008| location = New York| pages = [https://archive.org/details/dinosaursmostcom00holt/page/ updated appendix]| isbn = 978-0-375-82419-7| url = http://www.geol.umd.edu/~tholtz/dinoappendix/DinoappendixSummer2008.pdf}}</ref> ''[[Titanoceratops]]'' had one of the longest skull of any land animal, at {{convert|2.65|m|ft|abbr=on}} long.<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Longrich|first=N.R.|year=2011 |title=''Titanoceratops ouranos'', a giant horned dinosaur from the Late Campanian of New Mexico |journal=Cretaceous Research |volume=32 |issue=3 |pages=264–276 |doi=10.1016/j.cretres.2010.12.007|bibcode=2011CrRes..32..264L }}{{cbignore|bot=medic}}</ref> A recently discovered ''[[Torosaurus]]'' (nicknamed "Adam") may exceed this size with a skull length of {{convert|3|m|ft|abbr=on}} meters.<ref>{{cite journal |title=This Torosaurus skeleton is the largest horned dinosaur ever discovered in the world. |journal=Knuthenborg Museum of Evolution |url=https://www.museumofevolution.com/dinosaurs/torosaurus |archive-date=6 October 2024 |access-date=8 March 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20241006132235/https://www.museumofevolution.com/dinosaurs/torosaurus }}</ref>
=== Ornithopods (Ornithopoda) === [[File:Giant Ornithopod Scale.svg|thumb|upright=2.0|From left to right: ''[[Shantungosaurus giganteus]]'', ''[[Magnapaulia laticaudus]]'', ''[[Edmontosaurus annectens]]'', ''[[Saurolophus angustirostris]]'', ''[[Hypsibema missouriensis]]'', ''[[Charonosaurus jiayinensis]]'', ''[[Iguanodon bernissartensis]]'']] * The very largest known [[ornithopod]]s, like ''[[Shantungosaurus]]'' were as heavy as medium-sized sauropods at up to {{cvt|23|tonne|ST}},<ref name="ZM" /><ref name="LL">{{cite journal|last=Morris|first=William J.|year=1981 |title=A new species of hadrosaurian dinosaur from the Upper Cretaceous of Baja California: ?''Lambeosaurus laticaudus''|journal=Journal of Paleontology|volume=55|issue=2|pages=453–462}}</ref> and {{cvt|16.6|m|0}} in length.<ref name="ZM">{{cite journal|last=Zhao|first=X.|author2=Li, D. |author3=Han, G. |author4=Hao, H. |author5=Liu, F. |author6=Li, L. |author7= Fang, X. |year=2007 |title=''Zhuchengosaurus maximus'' from Shandong Province|journal=Acta Geoscientia Sinica|volume=28|issue=2 |pages=111–122 |doi=10.1007/s10114-005-0808-x|s2cid=119700784}}</ref> ''[[Magnapaulia]]'' reached {{cvt|12.5|m}} in length,<ref>{{Cite journal | last1 = Prieto-Márquez | first1 = A. | last2 = Chiappe | first2 = L. M. | last3 = Joshi | first3 = S. H. | editor1-last = Dodson | editor1-first = Peter | title = The lambeosaurine dinosaur ''Magnapaulia laticaudus'' from the Late Cretaceous of Baja California, Northwestern Mexico | doi = 10.1371/journal.pone.0038207 | journal = PLOS ONE | volume = 7 | issue = 6 | article-number = e38207 | year = 2012 | pmid = 22719869| pmc =3373519 | bibcode = 2012PLoSO...738207P | doi-access = free }}</ref> or, according to original description, even {{cvt|15|m}}.<ref>{{cite journal |last=Morris |first=William J. |year=1981 |title=A new species of hadrosaurian dinosaur from the Upper Cretaceous of Baja California: ?''Lambeosaurus laticaudus'' |journal=Journal of Paleontology |volume=55 |issue=2 |pages=453–462 }}</ref><ref name="Winter 2011 Appendix"/> The [[Mongolia]]n ''[[Saurolophus]]'', ''S. angustirostris'', reached {{cvt|13|m}} long and possibly more.<ref name=Saurolophus>Paul, Greg (2010). "The Princeton Field Guide to Dinosaurs". New Jersey: Princeton University Press. p. 335.</ref> Such animal could weighed up to {{cvt|11|t|ST}}.<ref name=Saurolophus/> The largest ''[[Edmontosaurus]]'' reached {{cvt|12|m}} in length and around {{cvt|6|t|ST}} in body mass.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Wosik |first1=M. |last2=Evans |first2=D.C. |year=2022 |title=Osteohistological and taphonomic life-history assessment of ''Edmontosaurus annectens'' (Ornithischia: Hadrosauridae) from the Late Cretaceous (Maastrichtian) Ruth Mason dinosaur quarry, South Dakota, United States, with implication for ontogenetic segregation between juvenile and adult hadrosaurids |journal=Journal of Anatomy |volume=241 |issue=2 |pages=272–296 |doi=10.1111/joa.13679 |pmid=35801524 |pmc=9296034}}</ref> An estimated maximum length of ''[[Brachylophosaurus]]'' is {{cvt|11|m}} resulting in weight of {{cvt|7|t|ST}}.<ref>Paul, G.S., 2010, The Princeton Field Guide to Dinosaurs, Princeton University Press p. 304</ref> PASAC-1, informally named ''"[[List of informally named dinosaurs#Sabinosaurus|Sabinosaurus]]"'', is the largest well-known North American saurolophine,<ref>{{cite journal |author=Conti, S. |author2=Vila, B. |author3=Sellés, A. G. |author4=Galobart, À. |author5=Benton, M. J. |author6=Prieto- Márquez, A. |date=2020 |url=https://research-information.bris.ac.uk/ws/files/239817750/Conti_et_al.pdf |title=The oldest lambeosaurine dinosaur from Europe: Insights into the
