C. |last7=Panzarin |first7=L. |date=2010 |title=New Basal Iguanodonts from the Cedar Mountain Formation of Utah and the Evolution of Thumb-Spiked Dinosaurs |journal=PLOS ONE |volume=5 |issue=11 |article-number=e14075 |pmid=21124919 |pmc=2989904 |doi=10.1371/journal.pone.0014075 |doi-access=free |bibcode=2010PLoSO...514075M}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |title=The Princeton Field Guide to Dinosaurs 2nd edition |last=Paul |first=Gregory S.|publisher=Princeton University Press |year=2016 |location=New Jersey |page=319}}</ref> * The largest [[Rhabdodontidae|rhabdodontid]] was ''[[Matheronodon]]'', estimated at {{cvt|4.8|m}} in length.<ref>{{cite web |url=http://www.alphr.com/science/1007498/this-ugly-dinosaur-had-giant-scissor-like-teeth |last1=Beall |first1=A. |title=This "ugly" dinosaur had giant scissor-like teeth |publisher=Dennis Publishing |website=Alphr |date=2017 |quote=It was around five metres long, I guess. |access-date=29 August 2022 |archive-date=27 October 2017 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20171027233148/http://www.alphr.com/science/1007498/this-ugly-dinosaur-had-giant-scissor-like-teeth }}</ref> ''[[Rhabdodon]]'' reached approximately {{cvt|4|m}} and {{cvt|250|kg}} according to 2016 estimates.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Paul |first=Gregory S. |title=The Princeton Field Guide to Dinosaurs |year=2016 |publisher=Princeton University Press |isbn=978-1-78684-190-2 |oclc=985402380 |page=312}}</ref>
== Birds (Aves) == [[File:Aepyornis, Dinornis, Struthio, Rhea, Pachyornis, Casuarius, Euryapteryx, Megalapteryx & Anomalopteryx.jpg|thumb|upright=3.0|center|From left to right: a human, †''[[Anomalopteryx|Anomalopteryx didiformus]]'', †''[[Megalapteryx|Megalapteryx didinus]]'', †''[[Euryapteryx|Euryapteryx curtus]]'', ''[[Casuarius casuarius]]'', †''[[Pachyornis elephantopus]]'', ''[[Rhea americana]]'', ''[[Struthio camelus]]'', †''[[Dinornis novaezealandiae]]'', †''[[Aepyornis maximus]]'']] {{clear}}
The largest [[bird]] in the fossil record may be the extinct [[Aepyornis|elephant bird]] species ''Aepyornis maximus'' of [[Madagascar]], whose closest living relative is the [[Kiwi (bird)|kiwi]]. Giant elephant birds exceeded {{convert|2.3|m|ft|sigfig=2}} in height, and average a mass of {{convert|850|kg|lb|abbr=on}}<ref name="Molecular exploration of fossil eggshell uncovers hidden lineage of giant extinct bird" />
The largest [[fowl]] was the [[Dromornithidae|mihirung]] ''[[Dromornis|Dromornis stirtoni]]'' of [[Australia]]. It exceeded {{convert|2.7|m|ft|abbr=on}} in height, and average a mass of {{convert|500|kg|lb|sigfig=2}}<ref name="Murray2004" />
Another contender is ''[[Brontornis|Brontornis burmeisteri]]'', an extinct flightless bird from [[South America]] which reached a weight of {{convert|319|kg|lb|abbr=on}} and a height of approximately {{convert|2.8|m|ft|sigfig=2}}.<ref name="Degrange">Degrange, F. J., Noriega, J. I., & Areta, J. I. (2012). Diversity and paleobiology of the Santacrucian birds. ''Early Miocene paleobiology in Patagonia: high-latitude paleocommunities of the Santa Cruz Formation'', 138–155.</ref>
The tallest recorded bird was ''[[Pachystruthio|Pachystruthio dmanisensis]],'' a relative of the [[ostrich]]. This particular species of bird stood at {{convert|3.5|m|ft|sigfig=3}} tall and average a mass of {{convert|450|kg|lb|abbr=on}}<ref name="Giant Birds Roamed Europe Two Million Years Ago" />
The largest known flightless [[Neoaves|neoave]] was the [[Phorusrhacidae|terror bird]] ''[[Paraphysornis|Paraphysornis brasiliensis]]'' of South America, the [[Brazil]]ian terror bird exceeded {{convert|240|kg|lb|abbr=on}} in mass,{{sfn|Molina-Pérez|Larramendi|2019|p=179}}

(1987, 2001)</ref> |- |35 |[[Mantell's moa]] |''[[Pachyornis geranoides]]'' |{{convert|27|kg|lb|abbr=on}}<ref name="Meat Weights and Nutritional Yield Values for New Zealand Archaeofauna" /> |- |36 | |''[[Patagornis marshi]]'' |{{convert|23|kg|lb|abbr=on}}<ref name="EM" /> |- |37 | |''[[Teratornis merriami]]'' |{{convert|22.5|kg|lb|abbr=on}}<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Fisher |first1=Harvey I. |title=Locomotion in the Fossil Vulture Teratornis |journal=The American Midland Naturalist |date=1945 |volume=33 |issue=3 |pages=725–742 |doi=10.2307/2421186 |jstor=2421186 }}</ref> |- |38 | |''[[Pelagornis|Pelagornis sandersi]]'' |{{convert|21.7|kg|lb|abbr=on}}<ref name="PNAS-20140707">{{cite journal |last=Ksepka |first=Daniel T. |title=Flight performance of the largest volant bird |date=7 July 2014 |journal=[[PNAS]] |doi=10.1073/pnas.1320297111 |volume=111 |issue=29 |pages=10624–10629 |pmid=25002475 |pmc=4115518|bibcode=2014PNAS..11110624K |doi-access=free }}</ref> |- |39 | |''[[Llallawavis scagliai]]'' |{{convert|18|kg|lb|abbr=on}}<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.science.org/content/article/new-species-terror-bird-discovered|title=New species of 'terror bird' discovered|journal=[[Science (journal)|Science]]|first=Sid|last=Perkins |date=9 April 2015 |access-date=14 April 2015|doi=10.1126/science.aab2465}}</ref> |- |40 |[[Giganhinga|Giant darter]] |''[[Giganhinga|Giganhinga kiyuensis]]'' |{{convert|17.7|kg|lb|abbr=on}}<ref name="A07">{{Cite journal |last1=Areta |first1=J. I. |last2=Noriega |first2=J. I. |last3=Agnolin |first3=F. |year=2007 |title=A giant darter (Pelecaniformes: Anhingidae) from the Upper Miocene of Argentina and weight calculation of fossil Anhingidae |journal=Neues Jahrbuch für Geologie und Paläontologie – Abhandlungen |volume=243 |issue=3 |pages=343–350 |doi=10.1127/0077-7749/2007/0243-0343|bibcode=2007NJGPA.243..343A |hdl=11336/80812 |hdl-access=free }}</ref> |- |41 | [[Cygnus falconeri|Giant swan]] |''[[Cygnus falconeri]]'' |{{convert|16|kg|lb|abbr=on}}<ref name="SIZE, FORM AND HABIT OF THE EXTINCT">{{cite journal |last1=Northcote |first1=E. Marjorie |title=SIZE, FORM AND HABIT OF THE EXTINCT MALTESE SWAN ''CYGNUS FALCONERI'' |journal=Ibis |date=April 1982 |volume=124 |issue=2 |pages=148–158 |doi=10.1111/j.1474-919x.1982.tb03753.x }}</ref> |- |42 | |''[[Leptoptilos robustus]]'' |{{convert|16|kg|lb|abbr=on}}<ref name="A new species of giant marabou stor">{{cite journal|title=A new species of giant marabou stork (Aves: Ciconiiformes) from the Pleistocene of Liang Bua, Flores (Indonesia)|vauthors=Meijer HJ, ((R ADue))|journal= Zoological Journal of the Linnean Society| year=2010|volume=160|issue=4| pages=707–724| doi=10.1111/j.1096-3642.2010.00616.x|doi-access=free}}</ref> |- |43 |[[Haast's eagle]] |''[[Hieraaetus moorei]]'' |{{convert|12|kg|lb|abbr=on}}<ref name="Meat Weights and Nutritional Yield Values for New Zealand Archaeofauna" /> |- |44 |[[Dodo]] |''[[Dodo|Raphus cucullatus]]'' |{{convert|10.2|kg|lb|abbr=on}}<ref name=Angst2011>{{cite journal| doi= 10.1007/s00114-010-0759-7| last1= Angst | first1= D.| last2= Buffetaut | first2= E.| last3= Abourachid | first3= A.| date=March 2011| title= The end of the fat dodo? A new mass estimate for ''Raphus cucullatus''| journal= Naturwissenschaften| volume= 98| issue= 3| pages= 233–236| pmid= 21240603| ref= {{sfnRef|Angst|Buffetaut|Abourachid March 2011}} |bibcode= 2011NW.....98..233A| s2cid= 29215473 }}</ref> |- |45 |[[South Island adzebill]] |''[[Adzebill|Aptornis defossor]]'' |{{convert|10|kg|lb|abbr=on}}<ref name="Meat Weights and Nutritional Yield Values for New Zealand Archaeofauna" /> |- |46 |[[New Zealand goose|South Island goose]] |''[[New Zealand goose|Cnemiornis calcitrans]]'' |{{convert|10|kg|lb|abbr=on}}<ref name="Meat Weights and Nutritional Yield Values for New Zealand Archaeofauna" /> |- |47 |[[North Island adzebill]] |''[[Adzebill|Aptornis otidiformis]]'' |{{convert|8|kg|lb|abbr=on}}<ref name="Meat Weights and Nutritional Yield Values for New Zealand Archaeofauna" /> |- |48 |[[New Zealand goose|North Island goose]] |''[[New Zealand goose|Cnemiornis gracilis]]'' |{{convert|8|kg|lb|abbr=on}}<ref name="Meat Weights and Nutritional Yield Values for New Zealand Archaeofauna" /> |- |49 |[[Heracles inexpectatus|Hercules parrot]] |''[[Heracles inexpectatus]]'' |{{convert|7|kg|lb|abbr=on}}<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Worthy |first1=Trevor H. |last2=Hand |first2=Suzanne J. |last3=Archer |first3=Michael |last4=Scofield |first4=R. Paul |last5=De Pietri |first5=Vanesa L. |date=August 2019 |title=Evidence for a giant parrot from the Early Miocene of New Zealand |journal=Biology Letters |volume=15 |issue=8 |article-number=20190467 |doi=10.1098/rsbl.2019.0467 |pmid=31387471 |pmc=6731479 |doi-access=free }}</ref> |- |50 |[[Spectacled cormorant]] |''[[Spectacled cormorant|Phalacrocorax perspicillatus]]'' |{{convert|6.4|kg|lb|abbr=on}}<ref name="calacademy.org">[http://www.calacademy.org/exhibits/science_under_sail/biodiversity.html California Academy of Sciences – Science Under Sail] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120607150621/http://www.calacademy.org/exhibits/science_under_sail/biodiversity.html |date=7 June 2012}}. Calacademy.org</ref> |- |}
=== Enantiornitheans (Enantiornithes) ===
One of the largest [[Enantiornithes|enantiornitheans]] was ''[[Enantiornis]]'',<ref name=Tudge/> with a length in life of around {{cvt|78.5|cm}}, hip height of {{cvt|34|cm}}, weight of {{cvt|6.75|kg|lb}},<ref name=Molina2022>{{cite book | isbn = 978-0-691-19059-4 | url = https://books.google.com/books?id=WnZyDwAAQBAJ&dq=avisaurus+size&pg=PA281 | title = Dinosaur Facts and Figures: The Theropods and Other Dinosauriformes | publisher = Princeton University Press | date = 25 June 2019 | access-date = 29 August 2022 | page = 281 |author=Rubén Molina-Pérez |author2=Asier Larramendi |author3=David Connolly |author4=Gonzalo Ángel Ramírez Cruz |author5=Andrey Atuchin }}</ref> and wingspan comparable to some of the modern gulls, around {{cvt|1.2|m}}.<ref name=Tudge>{{cite book | isbn = 978-0-307-45976-3 | url = https://books.google.com/books?id=dYUxzjxs-ckC&dq=avisaurus+size&pg=PA64 | title = The Bird: A Natural History of Who Birds Are, Where They Came From, and How They Live | publisher = Crown | date = 20 October 2009 | access-date = 29 August 2022 | page = 64 | author = Colin Tudge }}</ref> ''[[Gurilynia]]'' was the largest [[Mesozoic]] bird from [[Mongolia]], with a length of {{cvt|53|cm}}, hip height of {{cvt|23.2|cm}}, and weight of {{cvt|2.1|kg|lb}}.<ref name=Molina2022/>
==== Avisauridae ==== The [[Late Cretaceous]] ''[[Avisaurus]]'' was almost as large as ''Enantiornis''. It had a wingspan around {{cvt|1.2|m}},<ref name=Tudge/> a book estimate weight of {{cvt|5.1|kg|lb}}<ref name=Molina2022/> but a paper later estimated its weight up to {{convert|1.7|kg}} instead.<ref name="darwini">{{Cite journal |last1=Clark |first1=Alexander D. |last2=Atterholt |first2=Jessie |last3=Scannella |first3=John B. |last4=Carroll |first4=Nathan |last5=O'Connor |first5=Jingmai K. |date=9 October 2024 |title=New enantiornithine diversity in the Hell Creek Formation and the functional morphology of the avisaurid tarsometatarsus |journal=PLOS ONE |language=en |volume=19 |issue=10 |article-number=e0310686 |doi=10.1371/journal.pone.0310686 |issn=1932-6203 |pmc=11463745 |pmid=39383133 |bibcode=2024PLoSO..1910686C |doi-access=free}}</ref>
==== Pengornithidae ==== One of the biggest [[Early Cretaceous]] enantiornithine bird was ''[[Pengornis]]'' at {{cvt|50|cm|ft}} in length<ref name="Winter 2011 Appendix"/> and skull length of {{cvt|54.7|mm}}.<ref>{{cite journal|last1=Zhou|first1=Zhonghe|last2=Clarke|first2=Julia|last3=Zhang|first3=Fucheng|date=May 2008|title=Insight into diversity, body size and morphological evolution from the largest Early Cretaceous enantiornithine bird|journal=Journal of Anatomy|volume=212|issue=5|pages=565–77|doi=10.1111/j.1469-7580.2008.00880.x|pmc=2409080|pmid=18397240}}</ref>
=== Gargantuaviidae === ''[[Gargantuavis]]'' is the largest known bird of the [[Mesozoic]], a size ranging between the [[cassowary]] and the [[Common ostrich|ostrich]], and a mass of {{convert|140|kg|lb|abbr=on}} like modern ostriches.<ref>{{cite journal|last=Buffetaut|first=E.|author2=Le Loeuff, J.|title=A new giant ground bird from the Upper Cretaceous of southern France|journal=Journal of the Geological Society, London|volume=155|year=1998|issue=155|pages=1–4|doi=10.1144/gsjgs.155.1.0001|bibcode=1998JGSoc.155....1B|s2cid=128496095 }}</ref> In 2019 specimens MDE A-08 and IVPP-V12325 were measured at {{cvt|1.8|m}} in length, {{cvt|1.3|m}} in hip height, and {{cvt|120|kg|lb}} in weight.<ref name="Rubén Molina-Pérez, Asier Larramendi, David Connolly, Gonzalo Ángel Ramírez Cruz, Andrey Atuchin 282"/>
=== Dromornithiformes === [[File:Dromornis stirtoni 01.jpg|thumb|left|A cast of ''Dromornis stirtoni'' from Australia]] The largest [[Dromornithidae|dromornithid]] was ''[[Dromornis stirtoni]]'' over {{cvt|3|m}} tall<ref>{{cite web |last1=Musser |first1=Anne |title=Stirton's Thunder Bird |url=https://australian.museum/learn/australia-over-time/extinct-animals/dromornis-stirtoni/ |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220831060415/https://australian.museum/learn/australia-over-time/extinct-animals/dromornis-stirtoni/ |archive-date=31 August 2022 |website=The Australian Museum |access-date=10 September 2022 |language=en}}</ref> and {{cvt|528|-|584|kg|lb}} in mass for males.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Handley |first1=Warren D. |last2=Chinsamy |first2=Anusuya |last3=Yates |first3=Adam M. |last4=Worthy |first4=Trevor H. |title=Sexual dimorphism in the late Miocene mihirung Dromornis stirtoni (Aves: Dromornithidae) from the Alcoota Local Fauna of central Australia |journal=Journal of Vertebrate Paleontology |date=2 September 2016 |volume=36 |issue=5 |article-number=e1180298 |doi=10.1080/02724634.2016.1180298 |bibcode=2016JVPal..36E0298H }}</ref>
=== Gastornid (Gastornithiformes) === Large individuals of ''[[Gastornis]]'' reached up to {{cvt|2|m}} in height.<ref>{{cite journal|author1=Witmer, Lawrence |author2=Rose, Kenneth |year=1991|title= Biomechanics of the jaw apparatus of the gigantic Eocene bird ''Diatryma'': Implications for diet and mode of life|journal= [[Paleobiology (journal)|Paleobiology]]|volume=17|issue=2|pages= 95–120|doi=10.1017/S0094837300010435 |bibcode=1991Pbio...17...95W |s2cid=18212799 |url=http://www.oucom.ohiou.edu/dbms-witmer/Downloads/1991_Witmer_%26_Rose_Diatryma.pdf|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100728040104/http://www.oucom.ohiou.edu/dbms-witmer/Downloads/1991_Witmer_%26_Rose_Diatryma.pdf|archive-date=28 July 2010}}</ref> Weight of ''Gastornis'' ranges from {{cvt|100|kg}} to {{cvt|156|kg}} and sometimes to {{cvt|180|kg}} for European specimens and from {{cvt|160|kg}} to {{cvt|229|kg}} for North American.<ref>Gerald Mayr: ''Gastornithidae.'' In: Gerald Mayr: ''Paleogene Fossil Birds.'' Springer-Verlag, Berlin und Heidelberg, 2009, S. 44–47.</ref><ref name="Delphine Angst 2014"/><ref>Delphine Angst und Eric Buffetaut: ''Palaeobiology of Giant Flightless Birds.'' Oxford, 2017, S. 1–282 (S. 173–214).</ref>
=== Waterfowl (Anseriformes) === [[File:Garganornis ballmanni (reconstruction by Stefano Maugeri).jpg|thumb|Reconstruction of ''[[Garganornis ballmanni]]'']] Possibly flightless, the [[Miocene]] ''[[Garganornis ballmanni]]'' was larger than any extant members of [[Anseriformes]], with {{cvt|15.3|-|22.3|kg|lb}} in body mass.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Meijer |first1=Hanneke Johanna Maria |title=A peculiar anseriform (Aves: Anseriformes) from the Miocene of Gargano (Italy) |journal=Comptes Rendus Palevol |date=January 2014 |volume=13 |issue=1 |pages=19–26 |doi=10.1016/j.crpv.2013.08.001 |bibcode=2014CRPal..13...19M }}</ref> Another huge anseriform was the flightless [[New Zealand goose]] (''[[Cnemiornis]]''). It reached {{cvt|15|-|18|kg}}, approaching in size to small species of moa.<ref>{{cite book | isbn = 978-0-253-34034-4 | url = https://books.google.com/books?id=NtVV7ZSlsY4C&dq=Cnemiornis+kg&pg=PA232 | title = The Lost World of the Moa. Prehistoric Life of New Zealand | publisher = Indiana University Press | date = 2002 | access-date = 1 October 2022 | page = 232 | author = [[Trevor Worthy|T. H. Worthy]], [[Richard Holdaway (biologist)|Richard N. Holdaway]] }}</ref>
==== Swans (Cygnini) ==== The largest known [[swan]] was the [[Pleistocene]] [[giant swan]] (''[[Cygnus falconeri]]''), which reached a bill-to-tail length of about {{cvt|190|-|210|cm}},<ref>{{cite book |last1=Snow |first1=D. W. |last2=Perrins |first2=C. M. |year=1998 |title=The Birds of the Western Palearctic |edition=Concise |publisher=OUP |isbn=0-19-854099-X}}</ref> a weight of around {{cvt|16|kg|lbs}}, and a wingspan of {{cvt|3|m|ft}}.<ref name="SIZE, FORM AND HABIT OF THE EXTINCT"/><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Watanabe |first1=Junya |last2=Matsuoka |first2=Hiroshige |title=Flightless diving duck (Aves, Anatidae) from the Pleistocene of Shiriya, northeast Japan |journal=Journal of Vertebrate Paleontology |date=2 November 2015 |volume=35 |issue=6 |article-number=e994745 |doi=10.1080/02724634.2014.994745 |bibcode=2015JVPal..35E4745W }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Watanabe |first1=Junya |title=Quantitative discrimination of flightlessness in fossil Anatidae from skeletal proportions |journal=The Auk |date=July 2017 |volume=134 |issue=3 |pages=672–695 |doi=10.1642/auk-17-23.1 |hdl=2433/227150 |hdl-access=free }}</ref> The [[New Zealand swan]] (''[[Cygnus sumnerensis]]'') weighed up to {{cvt|10|kg|lb}}, compared to the related extant [[black swan]] at only {{cvt|6|kg|lb}}.<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.odt.co.nz/news/dunedin/last-nzs-own-swan|title=At last, NZ's own swan|last=Gibb|first=John|date=27 July 2017|work=[[Otago Daily Times]] |access-date=1 October 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170817081150/https://www.odt.co.nz/news/dunedin/last-nzs-own-swan|archive-date=17 August 2017}}</ref> The large marine swan ''[[Annakacygna|Annakacygna yoshiiensis]]'' from the [[Miocene]] of [[Japan]] far exceeded the extant [[mute swan]] in both size and weight.<ref>{{cite journal| vauthors = Matsuoka H, Hasegawa Y |title=''Annakacygna'', a new genus for two remarkable flightless swans（Aves, Anatidae, Cygnini）from the Miocene of Gunma, central Japan: With a note on the birds' food niche shift and specialization of wings for parental care action |journal=Bulletin of Gunma Museum of Natural History |year=2022 |volume=26 |pages=1–30 |url=http://www.gmnh.pref.gunma.jp/wp-content/uploads/bulletin26_1.pdf }}</ref>
==== Anatinae ==== [[Finsch's duck]] (''[[Chenonetta finschi]]'') reached {{cvt|1|-|2|kg|lb}} in weight, surpassing related modern [[Australian wood duck]] ({{cvt|800|g|lb}}).<ref>{{cite journal |author=Worthy, Trevor H. |author2=Olson, Storrs L.|date=2002|url=http://www.notornis.org.nz/free_issues/Notornis_49-2002/Notornis_49_1_1.pdf|title=Relationships, adaptations, and habits of the extinct duck "Euryanas" finschi|journal=Notornis|volume=49|issue=1|pages=1–17|doi=10.63172/518588mxbxcx |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20071019004140/http://www.notornis.org.nz/free_issues/Notornis_49-2002/Notornis_49_1_1.pdf|archive-date=19 October 2007 }}</ref>
=== Pelicans, ibises and allies (Pelecaniformes) === *The [[Early Pliocene]] ''[[Pelecanus schreiberi]]'' was larger than most extant pelicans. ''[[Pelecanus odessanus]]'' from the [[Late Miocene]] was probably the same size as ''P. schreiberi'', its [[tarsometatarsus]] is {{cvt|150|mm|in}} long.<ref>{{cite journal |author=Olson, Storrs L. |year= 1999|title= A new species of pelican (Aves: Pelecanidae) from the Lower Pliocene of North Carolina and Florida |journal= Proceedings of the Biological Society of Washington |volume=112 |issue=3|pages= 503–509|url=https://repository.si.edu/bitstream/handle/10088/6492/VZ_293_Pelecanus_schreiberi.pdf?sequence=1&isAllowed=y |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221010173815/https://repository.si.edu/bitstream/handle/10088/6492/VZ_293_Pelecanus_schreiberi.pdf?sequence=1&isAllowed=y |archive-date=10 October 2022 }}</ref> *The largest heron was the [[Bennu heron]] (''Ardea bennuides'').{{Dubious|date=October 2024}} Based on remains discovered, it was approximately {{cvt|2|m|ft}} tall and had a wingspan up to {{cvt|2.7|m|ft}}, thus surpassing the size of the largest living species in the heron family, the [[goliath heron]].<ref>{{cite book | author=Callahan, D. | year=2014 | title=A History of Birdwatching in 100 Objects | page=15 | publisher=Bloomsbury Publishing Plc | isbn=978-1408-1-8618-3 }}</ref> *The [[Jamaican ibis]] (''Xenicibis xympithecus'') was a large ibis, weighing about 2 kg (70 oz).
=== Storks and allies (Ciconiiformes) === [[File:L. robustus compared in size to a human..jpg|thumb|''[[Leptoptilos robustus]]'' compared in size to a human]] The largest known of [[Ciconiiformes]] was ''[[Leptoptilos robustus]]'', standing {{cvt|1.8|m}} tall and weighing an estimated {{cvt|16|kg}}.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/earth/hi/earth_news/newsid_9261000/9261713.stm|title=Giant bird found on hobbit island|date=7 December 2010|via=[[BBC News]] }}</ref><ref name="A new species of giant marabou stor"/> ''[[Ciconia maltha]]'' is a relatively large species of ''[[Ciconia]]'', with a height of over {{convert|5|ft|m|abbr=off|sp=us}} and a wingspan up to {{convert|10|ft|m|abbr=off|sp=us}} across.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.flmnh.ufl.edu/florida-vertebrate-fossils/species/ciconia-maltha |title=Florida Vertebrate Fossils: ''Ciconia maltha'' |publisher=[[Florida Museum of Natural History]] |first1=Richard C. |last1=Hulbert Jr. |first2=Natali |last2=Valdes |name-list-style=amp |year=2013 |access-date=30 September 2015}}</ref>
=== Cranes (Gruiformes) === A large true crane ([[Gruinae]]) from the late Miocene ([[Tortonian]]) of [[Germany]] was equal in size to the biggest extant cranes and resembled the long-beaked [[Siberian crane]] (''[[Leucogeranus leucogeranus]]'').<ref>{{cite journal |author=G. Mayr |author2=Thomas Lechner |author3=M. Böhme|date=14 July 2020|title=A skull of a very large crane from the late Miocene of Southern Germany, with notes on the phylogenetic interrelationships of extant Gruinae|journal=Journal of Ornithology|volume=161|issue=4 |pages=923–933|doi=10.1007/s10336-020-01799-0|s2cid=220505689 |doi-access=free|bibcode=2020JOrni.161..923M }}</ref>
=== Shorebirds (Charadriiformes) === ''[[Mancallinae|Miomancalla howardi]]'' was the largest known charadriiform of all time, weighing approximately 0.6 kg (1.3 lb) more than the second-largest member, the [[great auk]] (''[[Pinguinus impennis]]'').<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Smith |first1=N. Adam |title=Evolution of body mass in the Pan-Alcidae (Aves, Charadriiformes): the effects of combining neontological and paleontological data |journal=Paleobiology |date=February 2016 |volume=42 |issue=1 |pages=8–26 |doi=10.1017/pab.2015.24 |bibcode=2016Pbio...42....8S }}</ref>
=== Hesperornithines (Hesperornithes) === The largest known of the [[Hesperornithes|hesperornithines]] was ''[[Canadaga arctica]]'' at {{cvt|2.2|m}} long.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Wilson |first1=Laura |last2=Chin |first2=Karen |last3=Cumbaa |first3=Stephen |last4=Dyke |first4=Gareth |date=1 March 2011 |title=A high latitude hesperornithiform (Aves) from Devon Island: palaeobiogeography and size distribution of North American hesperornithiforms |url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/233446737 |journal=Journal of Systematic Palaeontology |volume=9 |issue=1 |pages=9–23 |doi=10.1080/14772019.2010.502910|bibcode=2011JSPal...9....9W |s2cid=83749554 }}</ref>
=== New World vultures (Cathartiformes) === [[File:Teratornis merriami.jpg|thumb|A skeleton of ''[[Teratornis]]'']] One of the heaviest flying birds of all time was ''[[Argentavis]]'', a Miocene [[Teratornithidae|teratornithid]]. The immense bird had a wingspan estimated up to {{cvt|5.09|-|6.5|m}}<ref name=Alexander2007/><ref name=Ksepka2014>{{cite journal |last1=Ksepka |first1=Daniel T. |title=Flight performance of the largest volant bird |journal=Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America |date=22 July 2014 |volume=111 |issue=29 |pages=10624–10629 |doi=10.1073/pnas.1320297111 |pmid=25002475 |pmc=4115518 |bibcode=2014PNAS..11110624K |doi-access=free }}</ref> and a weight up to {{cvt|70|to|72|kg}}.<ref>{{cite journal|title=The aerodynamics of ''Argentavis'', the world's largest flying bird from the Miocene of Argentina|volume=104|issue=30|pages=12398–12403|date=24 July 2007|journal=Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America |doi=10.1073/pnas.0702040104|last1=Chatterjee|first1=S.|last2=Templin|first2=R. J.|last3=Campbell|first3=K. E.|pmid=17609382|pmc=1906724|bibcode=2007PNAS..10412398C|doi-access=free}}</ref><ref name="Alexander2007"/> ''Argentavis''{{'s}} [[humerus]] was only slightly shorter than an entire human arm.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Campbell |first1=Kenneth E. Jr. |last2=Tonni |first2=E. P. |year=1983 |title=Size and locomotion in teratorns |journal=[[The Auk|Auk]] |volume=100 |issue=2 |pages=390–403 |url=http://sora.unm.edu/sites/default/files/journals/auk/v100n02/p0390-p0403.pdf|doi=10.1093/auk/100.2.390 }}</ref> Another huge teratorn was ''[[Aiolornis]]'', with a wingspan of around {{cvt|5|m}}.<ref>{{Cite journal |author=Campbell, Kenneth |author2=Scott, Eric |author3=Springer, Kathleen|date=1999|title=A new genus for the incredible teratorn.|url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/265948975|journal=Smithsonian Contributions to Paleobiology|volume=89|pages=169–175|via=ResearchGate}}</ref> The [[Pleistocene]] ''[[Teratornis|Teratornis merriami]]'' reached {{cvt|13.7|kg}} and {{cvt|2.94|–|3.38|m|ft}} in wingspan, with lower size estimates still exceeding the largest specimens of [[California condor]] (''[[Gymnogyps californianus]]'').<ref name="Campbell1983">{{cite journal |last1=Campbell |first1=Kenneth E. |last2=Tonni |first2=Eduardo P. |title=Size and Locomotion in Teratorns (Aves: Teratornithidae) |journal=The Auk |date=April 1983 |volume=100 |issue=2 |pages=390–403 |doi=10.1093/auk/100.2.390 }}</ref>
=== Seriemas and allies (Cariamiformes) === [[File:Kelenken_size_comparison.jpg|thumb|right|Size comparison of ''[[Kelenken]]'' and a human]] The largest known-ever [[Cariamiformes|Cariamiforme]] and largest [[Phorusrhacidae|phorusrhacid]] or "terror bird" (highly predatory, flightless birds of America) was ''[[Brontornis]]'', which was about {{cvt|175|cm}} tall at the shoulder, could raise its head {{cvt|2.8| m}} above the ground and could have weighed as much as {{cvt|400|kg}}.<ref>{{cite journal|title=Systematic revision of the Phorusrhacidae (Aves: Ralliformes) |doi=10.1590/S0031-10492003000400001 |journal=Papéis Avulsos de Zoologia |volume=43 |issue=4 |year=2003 |first1=Herculano M.F. |last1=Alvarenga |first2=Elizabeth |last2=Höfling|doi-access=free }}</ref> The immense phorusrhacid ''[[Kelenken]]'' stood {{cvt|3|m}} tall<ref name=KsepkaKelenken>{{cite journal |last1=Ksepka |first1=Daniel T. |title=Flights of fancy in avian evolution |journal=American Scientist |date=2014 |volume=102 |issue=1 |page=39 |doi=10.1511/2014.106.36 |jstor=43707746 }}</ref><ref>{{cite book |last1=Tambussi |first1=Claudia P. |last2=Degrange |first2=Federico J. |chapter=Neogene Birds of South America |title=South American and Antarctic Continental Cenozoic Birds |series=Springer Briefs in Earth System Sciences |date=2013 |pages=59–86 |doi=10.1007/978-94-007-5467-6_7 |isbn=978-94-007-5466-9 |publisher=Springer}}</ref> with a skull {{cvt|716|mm}} long ({{cvt|460|mm}} of which was beak), had the largest head of any known bird.<ref name=KsepkaKelenken/> South American ''[[Phorusrhacos]]'' stood 2.4-2.7 m (7.9-8.8 ft) tall, and weighed nearly {{convert|130|kg|lbs}}, as much as a male [[ostrich]].<ref>{{cite book |last1=Croft |first1=Darin A. |title=Horned Armadillos and Rafting Monkeys: The Fascinating Fossil Mammals of South America |date=2016 |publisher=Indiana University Press |isbn=978-0-253-02084-0 |page=120 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=NOBpDAAAQBAJ&q=Phorusrhacos+skull+65+cm&pg=PA120 |access-date=30 March 2020}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |author=Alvarenga, Herculano M. F. |author2=Höfling, Elizabeth |title=Systematic revision of the Phorusrhacidae (Aves: Ralliformes) |journal=Papéis Avulsos de Zoologia |date=2003 |volume=43 |issue=4 |pages=55–91 |doi=10.1590/S0031-10492003000400001 |url=http://www.scielo.br/scielo.php?script=sci_pdf&pid=S0031-10492003000400001&lng=en&nrm=iso&tlng=en|doi-access=free }}</ref> The largest North American phorusrhacid was ''[[Titanis]]'', which reached a height of approximately 2.5 m (8.2 ft),<ref>{{cite book | url = https://books.google.com/books?id=yD9-BAAAQBAJ | title = Historical Geology | publisher = Cengage Learning | date = 2015 | access-date = 27 August 2022 | page = 368 | author = Reed Wicander, James S. Monroe | isbn = 978-1-305-48076-6 }}</ref> slightly taller than an [[African forest elephant]].
=== Accipitriforms (Accipitriformes) === [[File:Giant Haasts eagle attacking New Zealand moa.jpg|thumb|right|[[Haast's eagle]], the largest bird of prey, attacking [[moa]]]] The largest known [[Accipitriformes|bird of prey]] ever was the enormous [[Haast's eagle]] (''Hieraaetus moorei''), with a wingspan of {{cvt|2.6|to|3|m}}, relatively short for their size.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.petermaas.nl/extinct/speciesinfo/haastseagle.htm |title=Recently Extinct Animals – Species Info – Haast's Eagle |author=Maas, P. |publisher=The Sixth Extinction |access-date=19 April 2013 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130120225443/http://www.petermaas.nl/extinct/speciesinfo/haastseagle.htm |archive-date=20 January 2013 }}</ref><ref>{{cite web |url=http://palaeo.gly.bris.ac.uk/macro/islandrule/haast'seagle.html |title=Haast's Eagle |publisher=Paleobiology and Biodiversity Research Group |access-date=19 April 2013 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140505231658/http://palaeo.gly.bris.ac.uk/macro/islandrule/Haast'sEagle.html |archive-date=5 May 2014 }}</ref> Total length was probably up to {{cvt|1.4|m}} in female<ref>Worthy, T. & Holdaway, R., ''The Lost World of the Moa: Prehistoric Life of New Zealand''. Indiana University Press (2003), {{ISBN|978-0-253-34034-4}}</ref> and they weighed about {{cvt|10|to|15|kg}}.<ref>{{cite journal |last=Brathwaite |first=D. H. |title=Notes on the weight, flying ability, habitat, and prey of Haast's Eagle (''Harpagornis moorei'') |journal=Notornis |date=December 1992 |volume=39 |issue=4 |pages=239–247 |publisher=[[Ornithological Society of New Zealand]] |doi=10.63172/063287mnyudi |url=http://notornis.osnz.org.nz/system/files/Notornis_39_4_239.pdf |access-date=26 January 2014 |archive-date=19 January 2012 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120119162446/http://notornis.osnz.org.nz/system/files/Notornis_39_4_239.pdf }}</ref> Another giant extinct hawk was ''[[Titanohierax]]'' about {{cvt|7.3|kg|lb}} that lived in the [[Antilles]] and The [[Bahamas]], where it was among the top predators.<ref>{{cite web |url=http://scienceblogs.com/tetrapodzoology/2008/01/28/titan-hawks-super-raptors/ |title=Titan-hawks and other super-raptors |date= 27 January 2008 |author=Darren Naish |publisher=Science Blogs |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220925105102/https://scienceblogs.com/tetrapodzoology/2008/01/28/titan-hawks-super-raptors |archive-date=25 September 2022 |access-date=30 September 2022}}</ref> An unnamed late Quaternary eagle from [[Hispaniola]] could be 15–30% larger than the modern [[golden eagle]] (''[[Aquila chrysaetos]]'').<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Steadman |first1=David W. |last2=Almonte Milan |first2=Juan N. |last3=Mychajliw |first3=Alexis M. |title=An Extinct Eagle (Aves: Accipitridae) from the Quaternary of Hispaniola |journal=Journal of Raptor Research |date=13 August 2019 |volume=53 |issue=3 |page=319 |doi=10.3356/JRR-18-769 }}</ref> Some extinct species of ''[[Buteogallus]]'' surpassed their extant relatives in size. ''[[Buteogallus borrasi]]'' was about 33% larger than the modern [[great black hawk]] (''[[Buteogallus urubitinga|B. urubitinga]]'').<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Suárez |first1=William |last2=Olson |first2=Storrs L. |title=The Cuban Fossil Eagle Aquila Borrasi Arredondo: A Scaled-Up Version of the Great Black-Hawk Buteogallus Urubitinga (Gmelin) |journal=Journal of Raptor Research |date=December 2007 |volume=41 |issue=4 |pages=288–298 |doi=10.3356/0892-1016(2007)41[288:TCFEAB]2.0.CO;2 }}</ref> ''[[Buteogallus daggetti|B. daggetti]]'', also known as "walking eagle", was around 40% larger than the [[savanna hawk]] (''[[Buteogallus meridionalis|B. meridionalis]]'').<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Olson |first1=Storrs L. |title=The 'Walking Eagle' Wetmoregyps Daggetti Miller: A Scaled-up Version of the Savanna Hawk (Buteogallus meridionalis) |journal=Ornithological Monographs |date=2007 |issue=63 |pages=110–114 |doi=10.2307/40166902 |jstor=40166902 }}</ref> [[Eyles's harrier]] (''Circus eylesi'') from the [[Pleistocene]]-[[Holocene]] of [[New Zealand]] was more than twice heavier than the extant ''[[Circus approximans|C. approximans]]''.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Holdaway |first1=R. N. |last2=Worthy |first2=T. H. |title=A reappraisal of the late Quaternary fossil vertebrates of Pyramid Valley Swamp, North Canterbury, New Zealand |journal=New Zealand Journal of Zoology |date=January 1997 |volume=24 |issue=1 |pages=69–121 |doi=10.1080/03014223.1997.9518107 |doi-access=free }}</ref>
=== Moa (Dinornithiformes) === [[File:Dinornis robustus, South Island Giant Moa - three quarter view on black YORYM 2004 20.jpg|thumb|The [[South Island giant moa]] (''Dinornis robustus'') was the tallest known bird.]] The tallest known bird was the [[South Island giant moa]] (''Dinornis robustus''), part of the moa family of [[New Zealand]] that went extinct about 500 years ago. It stood up to {{convert|3.7|m|ft|abbr=on}} tall,<ref name="topten">{{Cite web|url=https://www.alltoptens.com/top-10-largest-birds-in-the-world/|title=Top 10 Largest Birds in The World (Living) » AllTopTens.com|first=Sadekur|last=Rahaman|date=8 November 2014}}{{dead link|date=March 2025|bot=medic}}{{cbignore|bot=medic}}</ref> and weighed approximately half as much as a large elephant bird due to its comparatively slender frame.<ref name="Wood" />
=== Tinamous (Tinamiformes) === MPLK-03, a [[tinamou]] specimen that existed during the [[Late Pleistocene]] in [[Argentina]], possibly belongs to the modern genus ''[[Eudromia]]'' and surpacces extant ''[[Eudromia elegans|E. elegans]]'' and ''[[Eudromia formosa|E. formosa]]'' in size by 2.2–8% and 6–14%, respectively.<ref>{{cite journal |author=Marcos Cenizo |author2=Jorge Noriega |author3=Juan Diederle |author4=Esteban Soibelzon |author5=Leopoldo Soibelzon |author6=Sergio Rodriguez |author7=Elisa Beilinson|date=2018|url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/326272032|title=An unexpected large Crested Tinamou (Eudromia, Tinamidae, Aves) near to Last Glacial Maximum (MIS 2, late Pleistocene) of the Argentine Pampas|journal=Historical Biology |volume=32|issue=3|pages=330–338 |doi=10.1080/08912963.2018.1491568|s2cid=91851921 |hdl=11336/84901 |hdl-access=free }}</ref>
=== Elephant birds (Aepyornithiformes) === The largest bird in the fossil record may be the extinct [[elephant bird]]s (''[[Vorombe]]'', ''[[Aepyornis]]'') of Madagascar, which were related to the ostrich. They exceeded {{convert|3|m|ft|abbr=on}} in height and {{convert|500|kg|lb}} in weight.<ref name = "Wood">Wood, Gerald ''The Guinness Book of Animal Facts and Feats'' (1983) {{ISBN|978-0-85112-235-9}}</ref>
=== Ostriches (Struthioniformes) === With {{cvt|450|kg|lb}} in body mass, ''[[Pachystruthio dmanisensis]]'' from the lower [[Pleistocene]] of [[Crimea]] was the largest bird ever recorded in Europe. Despite its giant size, it was a good runner.<ref>{{cite journal|last1= Zelenkov|first1=N. V.|last2= Lavrov|first2=A. V.|last3= Startsev|first3=D. B.|last4= Vislobokova|first4=I. A.|last5= Lopatin|first5=A. V.|title= A giant early Pleistocene bird from eastern Europe: unexpected component of terrestrial faunas at the time of early Homo arrival|journal=Journal of Vertebrate Paleontology|year= 2019|volume=39|issue=2|article-number= e1605521|doi= 10.1080/02724634.2019.1605521|bibcode=2019JVPal..39E5521Z |s2cid=198384367}}</ref> A possible specimen of ''Pachystruthio'' from the lower Pleistocene of [[Hebei Province]] ([[China]]) was about {{cvt|300|kg}} in weight, twice heavier than the [[common ostrich]] (''[[Struthio camelus]]'').<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Buffetaut |first1=Eric |last2=Angst |first2=Delphine |title=A Giant Ostrich from the Lower Pleistocene Nihewan Formation of North China, with a Review of the Fossil Ostriches of China |journal=Diversity |date=26 January 2021 |volume=13 |issue=2 |page=47 |doi=10.3390/d13020047 |doi-access=free |bibcode=2021Diver..13...47B |hdl=1983/0d3c1bba-d496-47b6-a61c-e141a472612a |hdl-access=free }}</ref> Remains of the massive [[Asian ostrich]] (''[[Struthio asiaticus]]'') from the [[Pliocene]]<ref>{{cite book | isbn = 978-1-119-02076-9 | url = https://books.google.com/books?id=UNqTDgAAQBAJ&dq=struthio+asiaticus+large&pg=PA98 | title = Avian Evolution. The Fossil Record of Birds and Its Paleobiological Significance | publisher = Wiley | date = 31 October 2016 | access-date = 30 September 2022 | page = 98 | author = [[Gerald Mayr]] }}</ref> indicate a size 20% bigger than adult male of the extant ''Struthio camelus''.<ref>{{cite journal|author= Cecile Mourer-Chauvire, Denis Geraads|date=January 2008|url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/32226428|title=The Struthionidae and Pelagornithidae (Aves: Struthioniformes, Odontopterygiformes) from the late Pliocene of Ahl Al Oughlam, Morocco|journal=Oryctos|volume=7|pages=169–194}}</ref>
=== Pigeons and doves (Columbiformes) === [[File:DodoMansur cutted.png|thumb|A painting of a live [[Dodo]] from the early 1600s.]] The largest pigeon relative known was the [[dodo]] (''Raphus cucullatus''), possibly exceeding {{convert|1|m|ft|abbr=on}} in height and weighing as much as {{convert|28|kg|lb|abbr=on}}, although recent estimates have indicated that an average wild dodo weighed much less at approximately {{convert|10.2|kg|lb|abbr=on}}.<ref>Vermeij, Geerat J. (2004). ''Nature: An Economic History''. Princeton University Press, {{ISBN|0-691-11527-3}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | doi = 10.1007/s00114-010-0759-7 | last1 = Angst | first1 = D. | last2 = Buffetaut | first2 = E. | last3 = Abourachid | first3 = A. | year = 2011 | title = The end of the fat dodo? A new mass estimate for Raphus cucullatus | journal = Naturwissenschaften | volume = 98 | issue = 3 | pages = 233–236 | pmid = 21240603 | ref = {{sfnRef|Angst|Buffetaut|Abourachid|March 2011}} |bibcode = 2011NW.....98..233A | s2cid = 29215473 }}</ref>
=== Pheasants, turkeys, gamebirds and allies (Galliformes) ===
The largest known of the [[Galliformes]] was likely the [[giant malleefowl]], which could reach {{cvt|7|kg|lb}} in weight.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Vickers-Rich |first1=Patricia |last2=Knight |first2=Frank |title=Kadimakara – Extinct Vertebrates of Australia |date=1985 |publisher=Princeton University Press |isbn=978-0-909674-26-7}}</ref>
=== Songbirds (Passeriformes) === The largest known [[Passeriformes|songbird]] is the extinct [[giant grosbeak]] (''Chloridops regiskongi'') at {{cvt|11|in|order=flip}} long.{{citation needed|date=May 2021}}
=== Cormorants and allies (Suliformes) === [[File:Phalacrocorax perspicillatus.png|thumb|The [[spectacled cormorant]] or Pallas's Cormorant (''[[Phalacrocorax perspicillatus]]'')]]
* The largest known [[cormorant]] was the [[spectacled cormorant]] of the North Pacific (''Phalacrocorax perspicillatus''), which became extinct around 1850 and averaged around {{cvt|6.4|kg}} and {{cvt|1.15|m}}.<ref name="calacademy.org"/> * The largest known darter was ''[[Giganhinga]]'' with estimated weight about {{convert|17.7|kg|abbr=on}},<ref name="A07"/> earlier study even claims {{convert|25.7|kg|abbr=on}}.<ref name="R02">{{Cite journal |last1=Rinderknecht |first1=A. |last2=Noriega |first2=J.I. |year=2002 |title=Un nuevo género de Anhingidae (Aves: Pelecaniformes) del Plioceno–Pleistoceno del Uruguay (Formación San José). |url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/287726682 |url-status=live |journal=Ameghiniana |volume=39 |issue=2 |pages=183–191 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220509200926/https://www.researchgate.net/profile/Jorge-Noriega-5/publication/287726682_A_new_genus_of_Anhingidae_Aves_Pelecaniformes_from_the_Pliocene-Pleistocene_of_Uruguay_San_Jose_Formation/links/56bb439008ae2d6f2013a219/A-new-genus-of-Anhingidae-Aves-Pelecaniformes-from-the-Pliocene-Pleistocene-of-Uruguay-San-Jose-Formation.pdf |archive-date=9 May 2022 |access-date=5 May 2022}}</ref> * The largest known [[Plotopteridae|plotopterid]], penguin-like flightless bird was ''[[Copepteryx|Copepteryx titan]]'' that is known from {{cvt|22|cm}} long femur, almost twice as long as that of [[emperor penguin]].<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Mori |first1=Hirotsugu |last2=Miyata |first2=Kazunori |title=Early Plotopteridae Specimens (Aves) from the Itanoura and Kakinoura Formations (Latest Eocene to Early Oligocene), Saikai, Nagasaki Prefecture, Western Japan |journal=Paleontological Research |date=April 2021 |volume=25 |issue=2 |doi=10.2517/2020PR018 |bibcode=2021PalRe..25.R018M }}</ref>
=== Grebes (Podicipediformes) === The largest known grebe, the [[Atitlán grebe]] (''Podylimbus gigas''), reached a length of about {{convert|46|–|50|cm|in}}.<ref name=desc>{{cite journal| title= Studies from the Dwight collection of Guatemala birds. 1. |journal=American Museum Novitates |issue=379 | year=1929| author=Griscom, Ludlow |hdl=2246/5418 }}</ref>
=== Bony-toothed birds (Odontopterygiformes) === The largest known of the [[Odontopterygiformes]]— a group which has been variously allied with Procellariiformes, Pelecaniformes and Anseriformes and the largest flying birds of all time other than ''Argentavis'' were the huge ''[[Pelagornis]]'', ''[[Cyphornis]]'', ''[[Dasornis]]'', ''[[Gigantornis]]'' and ''[[Osteodontornis]]''.{{citation needed|date=May 2021}} They had a wingspan of {{cvt|5.5|–|6|m}} and stood about {{cvt|1.2|m}} tall.{{citation needed|date=May 2021}} Exact size estimates and judging which one was largest are not yet possible for these birds, as their bones were extremely thin-walled, light and fragile, and thus most are only known from very incomplete remains.{{citation needed|date=May 2021}}
=== Woodpeckers and allies (Piciformes) === The largest known [[Piciformes|woodpecker]] is the possibly extinct [[imperial woodpecker]] (''Campephilus imperialis'') with a total length of about {{cvt|56|–|60|cm|in}}.<ref>{{cite web | url = http://www.birdlife.org/datazone/speciesfactsheet.php?id=718 | title = Imperial Woodpecker ''Campephilus imperialis '' | publisher = BirdLife International | access-date = 28 February 2011 | archive-date = 4 March 2016 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20160304080712/http://www.birdlife.org/datazone/speciesfactsheet.php?id=718 | url-status = live }}</ref>
=== Parrots (Psittaciformes) === The largest known [[Psittaciformes|parrot]] is the extinct ''[[Heracles inexpectatus]]'' with a length of about 1 meter (3.3 feet).<ref name="Worthyetal2019">{{cite journal |last1=Worthy |first1=Trevor H. |last2=Hand |first2=Suzanne J. |last3=Archer |first3=Michael |last4=Scofield |first4=R. Paul |last5=De Pietri |first5=Vanesa L. |date=2019 |title=Evidence for a giant parrot from the early Miocene of New Zealand |journal=Biology Letters |volume=15 |issue=8 |doi=10.1098/rsbl.2019.0467|pmid=31387471 |pmc=6731479 |doi-access=free }}</ref>
=== Penguins (Sphenisciformes) === [[File:Anthropornis Scale.svg|thumb|Size comparison of the giant penguin ''[[Anthropornis nordenskjoeldi]]'']] One of the heaviest penguins ever known is ''[[Kumimanu fordycei]]'', with a body mass estimate of {{cvt|148 to 159.7|kg||abbr=}}, derived from humerus measurements.<ref name="Ksepka-2023">{{cite journal |last1=Ksepka |first1=Daniel T. |last2=Field |first2=Daniel J. |last3=Heath |first3=Tracy A. |last4=Pett |first4=Walker |last5=Thomas |first5=Daniel B. |last6=Giovanardi |first6=Simone |last7=Tennyson |first7=Alan J.D. |title=Largest-known fossil penguin provides insight into the early evolution of sphenisciform body size and flipper anatomy |journal=Journal of Paleontology |date=March 2023 |volume=97 |issue=2 |pages=434–453 |doi=10.1017/jpa.2022.88 |bibcode=2023JPal...97..434K |url=https://www.repository.cam.ac.uk/handle/1810/341076 }}</ref> Another example is ''[[Palaeeudyptes klekowskii]]'' of [[Antarctica]], with a bill-to-tail length estimated at {{cvt|2.02|m|ftin|abbr=}} and an estimated body weight of {{cvt|84.2|kg||abbr=}}, slightly smaller than previous estimates.<ref name="Ksepka-2023" /><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Acosta Hospitaleche |first1=Carolina |date=1 October 2014 |title=New giant penguin bones from Antarctica: Systematic and paleobiological significance |journal=Comptes Rendus Palevol |volume=13 |issue=7 |pages=555–560 |bibcode=2014CRPal..13..555A |doi=10.1016/j.crpv.2014.03.008 |doi-access=free |hdl-access=free |hdl=11336/32571 }}</ref> The Eocene ''[[Anthropornis nordenskjoeldi]]'' is comparable in size, and was once estimated to reach lengths of {{cvt|2.05|m|ftin|abbr=}} and a weight of {{cvt|108|kg||abbr=}}.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Jadwiszczak |first1=Piotr |title=Body size of Eocene Antarctic penguins |journal=Polish Polar Research |date=2001 |volume=22 |issue=2 |pages=147–158 |url=https://journals.pan.pl/dlibra/publication/126905/edition/110730/content }}</ref> However, recent estimation from humerus measurements put ''A. nordenskjoeldi'' more in the range of {{cvt|67|kg||abbr=}} in weight.<ref name="Ksepka-2023" /> Other large penguins include the [[New Zealand giant penguin]] (''Pachydyptes pondeorsus'') weighing around {{cvt|65.4 to 94.6|kg||}}, and ''[[Icadyptes salasi]]'' at {{cvt|52.8 to 73.0|kg||}}.<ref>{{Cite news| url = https://www.washingtonpost.com/wp-dyn/content/article/2007/06/25/AR2007062500652.html?nav=hcmodule | title = Giant Penguins May Have Roamed Peru | work = [[Associated Press]] via [[Washington Post]] | date = 25 June 2007 | access-date = 29 June 2007 | first=Randolph E. | last=Schmid}}</ref><ref name="Ksepka-2023" />
=== Owls (Strigiformes) === The largest known [[Strigiformes|owl]] of all time was the [[Cuba]]n ''[[Ornimegalonyx]]'' at {{cvt|43.3|in|order=flip}} tall probably exceeding {{cvt|9|kg}}.<ref>Arredondo, Oscar (1976) translated Olson, Storrs L. ''The Great Predatory Birds of the Pleistocene of Cuba'' pp. 169–187 in "Smithsonian Contributions to Paleobiology number 27; Collected Papers in Avian Paleontology Honoring the 90th Birthday of Alexander Wetmore"</ref>
== Amphibians (Amphibia) == The largest known [[amphibian]] of all time was the {{cvt|30|ft|order=flip}} long [[temnospondyli|temnospondyl]] ''[[Prionosuchus]]''.<ref name=evidence>{{cite journal|author=Daniel L. Levy, Rebecca Heald|date=August 2015|url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/280967577|title=Biological Scaling Problems and Solutions in Amphibians|journal=Cold Spring Harbor Perspectives in Biology|volume=8|issue=1|article-number=a019166 |doi=10.1101/cshperspect.a019166 |pmid=26261280 |pmc=4691792 }}</ref>
=== Lissamphibians (Lissamphibia) === ==== Frogs and toads (Anura) ==== [[File:Beelzebufo Scale V2.svg|thumb|Size comparison of ''[[Beelzebufo]]'']] The largest known [[frog]] ever was an as yet unnamed [[Eocene]] species that was about {{convert|58|-|59|cm|in|1|adj=mid|-long}}.<ref>{{Cite journal |author=Rodrigo A. Otero |author2=Paulina Jimenez-Huidobrob |author3=Sergio Soto-Acuna |author4=Roberto E. Yury-Yanez |url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/263766635 |title=Evidence of a giant helmeted frog (Australobatrachia, Calyptocephalellidae) from Eocene levels of the Magallanes Basin, southernmost Chile|journal=Journal of South American Earth Sciences|volume=55|date=2014|pages=133–140 |doi=10.1016/j.jsames.2014.06.010|bibcode=2014JSAES..55..133O}}</ref> The [[Late Cretaceous]] ''[[Beelzebufo]]'' grew to at least {{cvt|23.2|cm|in|1}} (snout-vent length), which is around the size of a modern [[African bullfrog]].<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Evans |first1=Susan E. |last2=Groenke |first2=Joseph R. |last3=Jones |first3=Marc E. H. |last4=Turner |first4=Alan H. |last5=Krause |first5=David W. |title=New Material of ''Beelzebufo'', a Hyperossified Frog (Amphibia: Anura) from the Late Cretaceous of Madagascar |doi=10.1371/journal.pone.0087236 |journal=PLOS ONE |volume=9 |issue=1 |at=e87236 |year=2014 |pmid=24489877 |pmc=3905036 |bibcode=2014PLoSO...987236E |doi-access=free}}</ref>
==== Salamanders, newts and allies (Urodela) ==== [[File:Andrias matthewi size comparison.jpg|thumb|upright|left |''[[Andrias matthewi]]'' size comparison]] * ''[[Andrias matthewi]]'' was the largest [[lissamphibia]]n ever known, with total length up to {{cvt|2.3|m|ft}}.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Naylor |first=Bruce G. |date=1981 |title=Cryptobranchid Salamanders from the Paleocene and Miocene of Saskatchewan |journal=Copeia |volume=1981 |issue=1 |pages=76–86 |doi=10.2307/1444042 |jstor=1444042}}</ref> * ''[[Habrosaurus]]'' was the largest [[Sirenidae|sirenid]]. It reached {{cvt|1.6|m|ft}} long.<ref>{{Cite book|last1=Wilson |first1=Gregory P. |title=Through the End of the Cretaceous in the Type Locality of the Hell Creek Formation in Montana and Adjacent Areas|last2=Clemens |first2=William A.|last3=Horner|first3=John R. |last4=Hartman|first4=Joseph H.|date=21 January 2014 |publisher=Geological Society of America |isbn=978-0-8137-2503-1 |language=en}}</ref>
=== Diadectomorphs (Diadectomorpha) === [[File:Diadectes Scale.svg|thumb|Size comparison of ''[[Diadectes]]'']] The largest known diacectid, herbivorous ''[[Diadectes]]'', was a heavily built animal, up to {{cvt|3|m}} long, with thick vertebrae and ribs.<ref>{{cite book | isbn = 978-0-12-386920-3 | url = https://books.google.com/books?id=Gay9N_ry79kC&dq=diadectes+3+m&pg=PA96 | title = Herpetology. An Introductory Biology of Amphibians and Reptiles | publisher = Elsevier Science | date = 25 March 2013 | access-date = 25 September 2022 | page = 96 | author = Laurie J. Vitt, Janalee P. Caldwell }}</ref><ref>{{cite book | isbn = 978-0-19-512568-9 | url = https://books.google.com/books?id=UtrBDwAAQBAJ&dq=diadectes+3+m&pg=PA276 | title = Brains Through Time. A Natural History of Vertebrates | publisher = Oxford University Press | date = 2020 | access-date = 25 September 2022 | page = 276 | author = [[Georg F. Striedter]], [[Glenn Northcutt|R. Glenn Northcutt]] }}</ref>
=== Anthracosauria === The largest known anthracosaur was ''[[Anthracosaurus]]'', with skull about {{cvt|40|cm}} in length.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Panchen |first=A. L. |date=1977 |title=On Anthracosaurus russelli Huxley (Amphibia: Labyrinthodontia) and the Family Anthracosauridae |journal=Philosophical Transactions of the Royal Society of London. Series B, Biological Sciences |volume=279 |issue=968 |pages=447–512 |doi=10.1098/rstb.1977.0096 |jstor=2417840 |bibcode=1977RSPTB.279..447P }}</ref>
=== Embolomeri === [[File:Pholiderpeton NT.jpg|thumb|Restoration of ''[[Pholiderpeton]]'']] The longest member of this group was ''Eogyrinus attheyi'', species sometimes placed under genus ''[[Pholiderpeton]]''.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Marjanović |first1=David |last2=Laurin |first2=Michel |date=4 January 2019 |title=Phylogeny of Paleozoic limbed vertebrates reassessed through revision and expansion of the largest published relevant data matrix |journal=PeerJ |volume=6 |article-number=e5565 |doi=10.7717/peerj.5565 |pmid=30631641 |pmc=6322490 |doi-access=free }}</ref> Its skull had length about {{cvt|41|cm}}.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Panchen |first=A. L. |date=1972 |title=The Skull and Skeleton of Eogyrinus attheyi Watson (Amphibia: Labyrinthodontia) |journal=Philosophical Transactions of the Royal Society of London. Series B, Biological Sciences |volume=263 |issue=851 |pages=279–326 |doi=10.1098/rstb.1972.0002 |jstor=2417176 |bibcode=1972RSPTB.263..279P }}</ref>
=== Temnospondyls (Temnospondyli) === [[File:Prionosuchus scale.svg|thumb|Scale diagram of small and large specimens of ''[[Prionosuchus]]'']] The largest known temnospondyl amphibian is ''[[Prionosuchus]]'', which grew to lengths of {{cvt|9|m}}.<ref name=evidence /> Another huge temnospondyl was ''[[Mastodonsaurus|Mastodonsaurus giganteus]]'' at {{cvt|6|m|ft}} long.<ref name="SRR99">{{cite journal|last=Schoch|first=R.R.|year=1999|title=Comparative osteology of ''Mastodonsaurus giganteus'' (Jaeger, 1828) from the Middle Triassic (Lettenkeuper: Longobardian) of Germany (Baden-Württemberg, Bayern, Thüringen)|url=https://www.zobodat.at/pdf/Stuttgarter-Beitraege-Naturkunde_278_B_0001-0175.pdf|journal=Stuttgarter Beiträge zur Naturkunde Serie B|volume=278|pages=1–175}}</ref> Unnamed species of temnospondyl from [[Lesotho]] is partial, but possible body length estimation is {{cvt|7|m|ft}}.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Steyer|first1=J. Sébastien|last2=Damiani|first2=Ross|date=May 2005|title=A giant brachyopoid temnospondyl from the Upper Triassic or Lower Jurassic of Lesotho|journal=Bulletin de la Société Géologique de France|volume=176|issue=3|pages=243–248|doi=10.2113/176.3.243 }}</ref>
== Fishes (Pisces) == [[Fish]]es are a [[paraphyletic]] group of non-[[tetrapod]] vertebrates.
=== Jawless fish (Agnatha) === {{further|Agnatha}}
==== Conodonts (Conodonta) ==== ''[[Iowagnathus|Iowagnathus grandis]]'' is estimated to have length over {{convert|50|cm|ft|1|abbr=on}}.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Liu |first1=Huaibao P. |last2=Bergström |first2=Stig M. |last3=Witzke |first3=Brian J. |last4=Briggs |first4=Derek E. G. |last5=McKay |first5=Robert M. |last6=Ferretti |first6=Annalisa |date=2017 |title=Exceptionally preserved conodont apparatuses with giant elements from the Middle Ordovician Winneshiek Konservat-Lagerstätte, Iowa, USA |journal=Journal of Paleontology |volume=91 |issue=3 |pages=493–511 |doi=10.1017/jpa.2016.155 |bibcode=2017JPal...91..493L |s2cid=132698401 |doi-access=free }}</ref>
==== Heterostracans (Heterostraci) ==== Some members of [[Psammosteidae]] such as ''[[Obruchevia]]'' and ''[[Tartuosteus]]'' are estimated to reached up to {{convert|2|m|ft|1|abbr=on}}.<ref>{{cite web |title=Heterostraci systematics, phylogenetics and macroevolution: investigating evolutionary patterns of extinct jawless vertebrates {{!}} Research Explorer {{!}} The University of Manchester |url=https://www.research.manchester.ac.uk/portal/en/theses/heterostraci-systematics-phylogenetics-and-macroevolution-investigating-evolutionary-patterns-of-extinct-jawless-vertebrates(13982d14-613f-489e-9b77-a1a57876fa77).html |access-date=24 August 2022 |website=www.research.manchester.ac.uk |language=en}}</ref>
==== Thelodonts (Thelodonti) ==== Although known from partial materials, ''[[Thelodus|Thelodus parvidens]]'' (=''T. macintoshi'') is estimated to reached up to {{convert|1|m|ft|1|abbr=on}}.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Turner |first=Susan |date=1986 |title=Thelodus macintoshi Stetson 1928, the largest known thelodont (Agnatha: Thelodonti) |url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/part/28067 |journal=Breviora |volume=486 |pages=1–18 }}</ref>
==== Cephalaspidomorphs (Cephalaspidomorphi) ==== A species of ''[[Parameteoraspis]]'' reached up to {{convert|1|m|ft|1|abbr=on}}.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Albert |first1=James S. |last2=Johnson |first2=Derek M. |last3=Knouft |first3=Jason H. |date=2009 |title=Fossils provide better estimates of ancestral body size than do extant taxa in fishes |journal=Acta Zoologica |volume=90 |pages=357–384 |doi=10.1111/j.1463-6395.2008.00364.x }}</ref>
=== Spiny sharks (Acanthodii) === The largest of the now-extinct [[Acanthodii]] was ''[[Xylacanthus (fish)|Xylacanthus grandis]]'', an [[Ischnacanthiformes|ischnacanthiform]] based on a ~{{cvt|35|cm}} long jaw bone. Based on the proportions of its relative ''[[Ischnacanthus]]'', ''X. grandis'' had an estimated total length of {{cvt|2.5|m}}.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Ørvig |first=Tor |date=1967 |title=Some new acanthodian material from the Lower Devonian of Europe |journal=Journal of the Linnean Society of London, Zoology |volume=47 |issue=311 |pages=131–153 |doi=10.1111/j.1096-3642.1967.tb01400.x }}</ref>
=== Placoderms (Placodermi) === [[File:Dunkleosteus skull QM email.jpg|thumb|Cast of a ''[[Dunkleosteus]]'' skull]] The largest known [[placoderm]] was the giant predatory ''[[Dunkleosteus]]''. The largest and most well known species was ''D. terrelli'', was initially estimated to be {{convert|6-10|m|ft|1|abbr=on}}<ref name="Anderson&Westneat2007">{{Cite journal |last=Anderson |first=P.S.L. |author2=Westneat, M. |date=2007 |title=Feeding mechanics and bite force modelling of the skull of Dunkleosteus terrelli, an ancient apex predator |journal=Biology Letters |volume=3 |issue=1 |pages=76–79 |doi=10.1098/rsbl.2006.0569 |pmc=2373817 |pmid=17443970}}</ref><ref name="Anderson&Westneat2009">{{cite journal |last=Anderson |first=P.S.L. |author2=Westneat, M. |date=2009 |title=A biomechanical model of feeding kinematics for ''Dunkleosteus terrelli'' (Arthrodira, Placodermi) |url=http://projects.fieldmuseum.org/sites/default/files/DunkPaleoBio.pdf |journal=Paleobiology |volume=35 |issue=2 |pages=251–269 |bibcode=2009Pbio...35..251A |doi=10.1666/08011.1 |s2cid=86203770 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20171107014026/http://projects.fieldmuseum.org/sites/default/files/DunkPaleoBio.pdf |archive-date=7 November 2017 |access-date=29 October 2017}}</ref><ref name="Ferron2017">{{Cite journal |last1=Ferrón |first1=Humberto G. |last2=Martínez-Pérez |first2=Carlos |last3=Botella |first3=Héctor |date=2017 |title=Ecomorphological inferences in early vertebrates: reconstructing Dunkleosteus terrelli (Arthrodira, Placodermi) caudal fin from palaeoecological data |journal=PeerJ |language=en |volume=5 |article-number=e4081 |doi=10.7717/peerj.4081 |issn=2167-8359 |pmc=5723140 |pmid=29230354 |doi-access=free}}</ref> in length and {{convert|1-4|MT|ST|abbr=on}} in weight.<ref name="Anderson&Westneat20072">{{Cite journal |last=Anderson |first=P.S.L. |author2=Westneat, M. |date=2007 |title=Feeding mechanics and bite force modelling of the skull of Dunkleosteus terrelli, an ancient apex predator |journal=Biology Letters |volume=3 |issue=1 |pages=76–79 |doi=10.1098/rsbl.2006.0569 |pmc=2373817 |pmid=17443970}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last=Williams |first=Nigel |year=2007 |title=Force feeding |journal=Current Biology |volume=17 |issue=1 |pages=R3 |bibcode=2007CBio...17...R3W |doi=10.1016/j.cub.2006.11.057 |s2cid=36585467 |doi-access=free}}</ref> However, more recent reconstructions suggest estimates over {{convert|5|m|ft|abbr=on}} is poorly supported. Instead the largest ''D. terrelli'' probably measured {{convert|4.1|m|ft}} in length and weighed {{convert|1494|–|1764|kg|lb}}.<ref name="DT">{{Cite journal |last=Engelman |first=Russell K. |date=2023 |title=A Devonian Fish Tale: A New Method of Body Length Estimation Suggests Much Smaller Sizes for Dunkleosteus terrelli (Placodermi: Arthrodira) |journal=Diversity |volume=15 |issue=3 |page=318 |bibcode=2023Diver..15..318E |doi=10.3390/d15030318 |doi-access=free}}</ref><ref name=":42">{{Cite journal |last=Engelman |first=Russell K. |date=2024 |title=Reconstructing Dunkleosteus terrelli (Placodermi: Arthrodira): A new look for an iconic Devonian predator |url=https://palaeo-electronica.org/content/2024/5307-dunkleosteus-reconstruction |journal=Palaeontologia Electronica |doi=10.26879/1343 |doi-access=free}}</ref> Another large placoderm, ''[[Titanichthys|Titanichthys clarki]]'', may have rivaled it in size.<ref>{{cite book | isbn = 978-0-19-857718-8 | url = https://books.google.com/books?id=B-ASAQAAIAAJ&q=Titanichthys+length | title = Major Evolutionary Radiations | publisher = Systematics Association | date = 1990 | access-date = 28 August 2022 | page = 310 | author = Gilbert Powell Larwood, Paul D. Taylor }}</ref> Recent reconstructions suggest ''T. clarki'' was estimated to have a length around {{cvt|4.15|m|ft}}.<ref name="DT" />
=== Cartilaginous fish (Chondrichthyes) === {{further|Cartilaginous fish}}
==== Mackerel sharks (Lamniformes) ==== [[File:Megalodon-Carcharodon-Scale-Chart-SVG.svg|thumb|How estimates for the size of [[Megalodon]] using different assumptions (brown) compare with the [[whale shark]] (blue), [[great white shark]] (yellow), and [[human]] (black) for scale]] * Species in the extinct genus ''[[Otodus]]'' were huge. A giant [[shark]], ''[[Megalodon|Otodus megalodon]]''<ref name="Shimada2016">{{cite journal|last1= Shimada|first1=K.|last2= Chandler|first2=R. E.|last3= Lam|first3=O. L. T.|last4= Tanaka|first4=T.|last5= Ward|first5=D. J.|title=A new elusive otodontid shark (Lamniformes: Otodontidae) from the lower Miocene, and comments on the taxonomy of otodontid genera, including the "megatoothed" clade|journal= Historical Biology|volume=29|issue=5|year=2016|pages= 1–11|doi= 10.1080/08912963.2016.1236795|s2cid=89080495}}</ref><ref name="Shimada2019">{{Cite journal|last=Shimada|first=Kenshu|year=2019|title=The size of the megatooth shark, ''Otodus megalodon'' (Lamniformes: Otodontidae), revisited|journal=Historical Biology|volume=33|issue=7|pages=1–8|doi=10.1080/08912963.2019.1666840|s2cid=208570844 }}</ref><ref name="Cooper2020">{{cite journal|last1=Cooper|first1=J. A.|last2=Pimiento|first2=C.|last3=Ferrón|first3=H. G.|last4=Benton|first4=M. J.|year=2020|title=Body dimensions of the extinct giant shark ''Otodus megalodon'': a 2D reconstruction|journal=Scientific Reports|volume=10|issue=14596|page=14596|doi=10.1038/s41598-020-71387-y|pmc=7471939|pmid=32883981|bibcode=2020NatSR..1014596C|doi-access=free}}</ref> is by far the biggest [[Lamniformes|mackerel shark]] as well as [[Fish|non-tetropod fish]] ever known.<ref>{{cite video|people=deGruy, Michael|title=Perfect Shark|medium=TV-Series|publisher=BBC (UK)|date=2006}}</ref> Most estimates of megalodon's size extrapolate from teeth, with maximum length estimates up to {{convert|10.6|-|20|m|ft|abbr=on}}<ref name="Shimada2019"/><ref name="Cooper2020"/><ref name="Perezetal2021">{{Cite journal|last1=Perez|first1=Victor|last2=Leder|first2=Ronny|last3=Badaut|first3=Teddy|date=2021|title=Body length estimation of Neogene macrophagous lamniform sharks (Carcharodon and Otodus) derived from associated fossil dentitions|url=https://palaeo-electronica.org/content/2021/3284-estimating-lamniform-body-size|journal=Palaeontologia Electronica|volume=24|issue=1|pages=1–28|doi=10.26879/1140|doi-access=free}}</ref> with a 2025 study estimating a maximum length estimate of {{Convert|24.3|m|ft}}<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal |last1=Shimada |first1=Kenshu |last2=Motani |first2=Ryosuke |last3=Wood |first3=Jake J. |last4=Sternes |first4=Phillip C. |last5=Tomita |first5=Taketeru |last6=Bazzi |first6=Mohamad |last7=Collareta |first7=Alberto |last8=Gayford |first8=Joel H. |last9=Türtscher |first9=Julia |last10=Jambura |first10=Patrick L. |last11=Kriwet |first11=Jürgen |last12=Vullo |first12=Romain |last13=Long |first13=Douglas J. |last14=Summers |first14=Adam P. |last15=Maisey |first15=John G. |date=2025 |title=Reassessment of the possible size, form, weight, cruising speed, and growth parameters of the extinct megatooth shark, Otodus megalodon (Lamniformes: Otodontidae), and new evolutionary insights into its gigantism, life history strategies, ecology, and extinction |journal=Palaeontologia Electronica |volume=28 |issue=1 |page=1502 |doi=10.26879/1502 |doi-access=free |pmid=40105087 |pmc=7617484}}</ref> and [[Mode (statistics)|modal]] length estimates based on individuals from all [[Ontogeny|ontogenetic stages]] (life stages) of {{convert|10.5|m|ft|abbr=on}}.<ref name="Pimiento201622">{{cite journal|last1=Pimiento|first1=C.|last2=MacFadden|first2=B. J.|last3=Clements|first3=C. F.|last4=Varela|first4=S.|last5=Jaramillo|first5=C.|last6=Velez-Juarbe|first6=J.|last7=Silliman|first7=B. R.|year=2016|title=Geographical distribution patterns of ''Carcharocles megalodon'' over time reveal clues about extinction mechanisms|journal=Journal of Biogeography|volume=43|issue=8|pages=1645–1655|doi=10.1111/jbi.12754|bibcode=2016JBiog..43.1645P |s2cid=55776834}}</ref><ref name="Pimiento&Balk2015">{{cite journal|last1=Pimiento|first1=C.|last2=Balk|first2=M. A.|year=2015|title=Body-size trends of the extinct giant shark ''Carcharocles megalodon'': a deep-time perspective on marine apex predators|journal=Paleobiology|volume=41|issue=3|pages=479–490|doi=10.1017/pab.2015.16|pmc=4541548|pmid=26321775|bibcode=2015Pbio...41..479P }}</ref> Due to fragmentary remains, there have been many contradictory size estimates for megalodon, as they can only be drawn from fossil teeth and vertebrae.<ref name="H2">{{Cite book|last=Renz|first=Mark|url={{google books|plainurl=yes|id=cMRe5GmDXmUC}}|title=Megalodon: Hunting the Hunter|publisher=PaleoPress|year=2002|isbn=978-0-9719477-0-2|location=Lehigh Acres, Florida|pages=1–159|oclc=52125833}}</ref>{{rp|87}}<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Portell |first1=Roger W. |last2=Hubbell |first2=Gordon |last3=Donovan |first3=Stephen K. |last4=Green |first4=Jeremy L. |last5=Harper |first5=David A. T. |last6=Pickerill |first6=Ron |title=Miocene sharks in the Kendeace and Grand Bay formations of Carriacou, The Grenadines, Lesser Antilles |journal=Caribbean Journal of Science |date=January 2008 |volume=44 |issue=3 |pages=279–286 |doi=10.18475/cjos.v44i3.a2 }}</ref> With the 2025 study suggesting that the animal had a more elongated slender appearance.<ref name=":0" /> Mature male megalodon may have had a body mass of {{convert|12.6|to|33.9|MT|ST}}, and mature females may have been {{convert|27.4|to|59.4|MT|ST}}, assuming that males could range in length from {{convert|10.5|to|14.3|m|ft|abbr=on}} and females {{convert|13.3|to|17|m|ft|abbr=on}}.<ref name="Gottfried1996">{{cite book|last1=Gottfried|first1=MD|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=2My8M5tL-KIC&q=Gottfried|title=Great White Sharks: The Biology of ''Carcharodon carcharias''|last2=Compagno|first2=LJV|last3=Bowman|first3=SC|publisher=Academic Press|year=1996|isbn=978-0-12-415031-7|editor1-last=Klimley|location=San Diego, California|pages=55–89|chapter=Size and skeletal anatomy of the giant megatooth shark ''Carcharodon megalodon''|editor2-last=Ainley}}</ref> Related to megalodon, ''[[Otodus angustidens]]'' and ''[[Otodus chubutensis|O. chubutensis]]'' reached the large sizes too. Each was estimated at {{cvt|9.3|m}}<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Gottfried M.D., Fordyce R.E|year=2001|title=An associated specimen of ''Carcharodon angustidens'' (Chondrichthyes, Lamnidae) from the Late Oligocene of New Zealand, with comments on ''Carcharodon'' interrelationships|journal=Journal of Vertebrate Paleontology|volume=21|issue=4|pages=730–739|doi=10.1671/0272-4634(2001)021[0730:AASOCA]2.0.CO;2|s2cid=86092645 }}</ref> and {{cvt|12.2|m}},<ref>{{cite web|last=Andres|first=Lutz|title=Megatooth Fossils Found at the Calvert Cliffs of Maryland|url=http://www.fossilguy.com/sites/calvert/calv_meg.htm|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220826104122/http://www.fossilguy.com/sites/calvert/calv_meg.htm|archive-date=26 August 2022|access-date=10 September 2022}}</ref> respectively. * Other giant mackerel sharks were [[Pseudoscapanorhynchidae]] from the [[Cretaceous]] period. ''[[Cretodus]]'' had a size range of {{cvt|9|-|11|m}} (for ''C. crassidens''),<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Amalfitano |first1=Jacopo |last2=Dalla Vecchia |first2=Fabio Marco |last3=Carnevale |first3=Giorgio |last4=Fornaciari |first4=Eliana |last5=Roghi |first5=Guido |last6=Giusberti |first6=Luca |title=Morphology and paleobiology of the Late Cretaceous large-sized shark Cretodus crassidens (Dixon, 1850) (Neoselachii; Lamniformes) |journal=Journal of Paleontology |date=September 2022 |volume=96 |issue=5 |pages=1166–1188 |doi=10.1017/jpa.2022.23 |bibcode=2022JPal...96.1166A |doi-access=free }}</ref> ''[[Leptostyrax]]'' reached lengths of {{cvt|6.3|-|8.3|m}}.<ref>{{cite journal|last1=Frederickson |first1=J.A. |last2=Schaefer |first2=S.N. |last3=Doucette-Frederickson |first3=J.A. |year=2015 |title=A gigantic shark from the Lower Cretaceous Duck Creek Formation of Texas |journal=PLOS ONE |volume=10 |issue=6 |article-number=e0127162 |doi=10.1371/journal.pone.0127162 |pmid=26039066 |pmc=4454486 |bibcode=2015PLoSO..1027162F |doi-access=free }}</ref> * The [[Cenozoic]] ''[[Parotodus]]'' reached up to {{convert|7.6|m|ft|abbr=on}} in length.<ref>{{cite journal|author=Kent, B.W.|title=Speculations on the Size and Morphology of the Extinct Lamnoid Shark, ''Parotodus benedeni'' (le Hon)|year=1999|journal=The Mosasaur|volume=6|pages=11–15|url=https://www.petit-fichier.fr/2013/01/27/kent-b-w-1999-taille-parotodus-benedenii/kent-b-w-1999-taille-parotodus-benedenii.pdf}}</ref> * The heaviest [[thresher shark]] was likely ''[[Alopias grandis]]''. It was similar in size or even larger than the extant [[great white shark]] and probably did not have an elongated dorsal tail, characteristic of modern relatives.<ref>{{cite report|url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/303680226|last1=Ward|first1=D. J.|last2=Kent|first2=B. W.|title=A new giant species of thresher shark from the Miocene of the United States|publisher=Natural History Museum|doi=10.13140/RG.2.1.1723.0969|year=2015}}</ref>
==== Ground sharks (Carcharhiniformes) ==== The [[Cenozoic]] ''[[Hemipristis serra]]'' was considerably larger than its modern-day relatives and had much larger teeth. Its total length is estimated to be at {{convert|6|m|ft|abbr=}} long.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Pimiento |first1=Catalina |last2=Cantalapiedra |first2=Juan L. |last3=Shimada |first3=Kenshu |last4=Field |first4=Daniel J. |last5=Smaers |first5=Jeroen B. |title=Evolutionary pathways toward gigantism in sharks and rays |journal=Evolution |date=March 2019 |volume=73 |issue=3 |pages=588–599 |doi=10.1111/evo.13680 |pmid=30675721 |url=https://cronfa.swan.ac.uk/Record/cronfa48696/Download/0048696-05022019162842.pdf }}</ref>
==== Hybodonts (Hybodontiformes) ==== One of the largest [[Hybodontiformes|hybodontiforms]] was the Jurassic ''[[Asteracanthus]]'' with body length of up to {{cvt|3|m}}.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Stumpf |first1=Sebastian |last2=López-Romero |first2=Faviel A. |last3=Kindlimann |first3=René |last4=Lacombat |first4=Frederic |last5=Pohl |first5=Burkhard |last6=Kriwet |first6=Jürgen |title=A unique hybodontiform skeleton provides novel insights into Mesozoic chondrichthyan life |journal=Papers in Palaeontology |date=August 2021 |volume=7 |issue=3 |pages=1479–1505 |doi=10.1002/spp2.1350 |bibcode=2021PPal....7.1479S |doi-access=free }}</ref> ''[[Crassodus|Crassodus reifi]]'' is known from less materials, however it is estimated that reached over {{cvt|3|m}}.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Maisch |first1=Michael W. |last2=Matzke |first2=Andreas T. |title=A new hybodontid shark (Chondrichthyes, Hybodontiformes) from the Lower Jurassic Posidonienschiefer Formation of Dotternhausen, SW Germany |journal=Neues Jahrbuch für Geologie und Paläontologie - Abhandlungen |date=June 2016 |volume=280 |issue=3 |pages=241–257 |doi=10.1127/njgpa/2016/0577 |bibcode=2016NJGPA.280..241M }}</ref>
==== Ctenacanthiformes ==== The largest member of [[ctenacanthiformes]] is ''[[Saivodus]] striatus'' with estimated length around {{cvt|6-9|m}}.<ref>{{cite news |last1=Katz |first1=B. |date=4 February 2020 |title=Jaw of 330-Million-Year-Old Shark Discovered in Kentucky Cave |url=https://www.smithsonianmag.com/smart-news/jaw-330-million-year-old-shark-found-kentucky-cave-180974115/}}</ref><ref name="DT" />
==== Skates and allies (Rajiformes) ==== The giant [[Sclerorhynchidae|sclerorhynchid]] ''[[Onchopristis]]'' reached about {{cvt|4.25|m}} in length.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Villalobos-Segura |first1=E. |last2=Kriwet |first2=J. |last3=Vullo |first3=R. |last4=Stumpf |first4=S. |last5=Ward |first5=D.J. |last6=Underwood |first6=C.J. |date=2021 |title=The skeletal remains of the euryhaline sclerorhynchoid †''Onchopristis'' (Elasmobranchii) from the 'Mid'-Cretaceous and their palaeontological implications |journal=Zoological Journal of the Linnean Society |volume=193 |issue=2 |pages=746–771 |doi=10.1093/zoolinnean/zlaa166}}</ref>
==== Eugeneodonts (Eugeneodontida) ==== [[File:Helicoprion_skeletal.jpg|thumb|Size comparation of ''[[Helicoprion]]'']] The largest known [[Eugeneodontida|eugeneodont]] is an as-yet unnamed species of ''[[Helicoprion]]'' discovered in Idaho. The specimens suggest an animal that possibly exceeded {{cvt|12|m}} in length.<ref name=Leviathans>{{cite web|url=http://www.eartharchives.org/articles/buzzsaw-toothed-leviathans-cruised-the-ancient-seas/|title=Buzzsaw-toothed leviathans cruised the ancient seas|website=eartharchives.org|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210822060933/http://www.eartharchives.org/articles/buzzsaw-toothed-leviathans-cruised-the-ancient-seas/|archive-date=22 August 2021}}</ref> Another fairly large eugeneodont is ''[[Parahelicoprion]]''. Being slimmer than ''Helicoprion'', it reached nearly the same size,<ref name=Leviathans/> possibly up to {{cvt|12|m}} in length,<ref name=Drobyshevsky>{{cite book | isbn = 978-5-04-209523-8 | url = https://books.google.com/books?id=JUXEDwAAQBAJ | title = Палеонтология антрополога. Книга 1. Докембрий и палеозой (Paleontology of anthropologist. Volume 1. Precambrian and Paleozoic) | publisher = [[Eksmo]] | date = 2022 | access-date = 31 August 2022 | pages = | author = [[Stanislav Drobyshevsky]] }}</ref> although these numbers originate from non-academic [[Citizen science|amateur researchers]] and are not supported by [[scientific literature]].<ref name=":6" /> Despite being known from limited remains, it is possible that ''[[Campyloprion]]'' is larger, making it the largest Carboniferous helicoprionid eugeneodont ever known, with a length of {{cvt|9|m|ft}} based on its tooth size. <ref name=":1">{{Cite web |last=Itano |first=Wayne |title=''Campyloprion'', a little-known Helicoprion-like shark: The story behind the article |url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/330970918_Campyloprion_a_little-known_Helicoprion-like_shark_The_story_behind_the_article |access-date=27 August 2023 |website=researchgate.net}}</ref>
=== Lobe-finned fish (Sarcopterygii) === {{further|Sarcopterygii}}
==== Coelacanths (Actinistia) ==== [[File:Mawsonia scaling.png|thumb|Size estimation of ''Mawsonia gigas'']] The largest [[coelacanth]] is Cretaceous ''[[Mawsonia (fish)|Mawsonia gigas]]'' with estimated total length up to {{cvt|5.3|m}}. Jurassic ''[[Trachymetopon]]'' may have reached size close to that, about {{cvt|5|m}}.<ref name=Coelacanths>{{Cite journal |last1=Cavin |first1=Lionel |last2=Piuz |first2=André |last3=Ferrante |first3=Christophe |last4=Guinot |first4=Guillaume |date=3 June 2021 |title=Giant Mesozoic coelacanths (Osteichthyes, Actinistia) reveal high body size disparity decoupled from taxic diversity |journal=Scientific Reports |volume=11 |issue=1 |page=11812 |doi=10.1038/s41598-021-90962-5 |pmc=8175595 |pmid=34083600|bibcode=2021NatSR..1111812C }}</ref> An undetermined [[Mawsoniidae|mawsoniid]] from the [[Maastrichtian]] deposits of [[Morocco]] probably reached {{cvt|3.65|-|5.52|m}} in length.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Brito |first1=Paulo M. |last2=Martill |first2=David M. |last3=Eaves |first3=Ian |last4=Smith |first4=Roy E. |last5=Cooper |first5=Samuel L.A. |title=A marine Late Cretaceous (Maastrichtian) coelacanth from North Africa |journal=Cretaceous Research |date=June 2021 |volume=122 |article-number=104768 |doi=10.1016/j.cretres.2021.104768 |bibcode=2021CrRes.12204768B |url=https://researchportal.port.ac.uk/portal/en/publications/a-marine-late-cretaceous-maastrichtian-coelacanth-from-north-africa(c8521ebb-5597-4a15-9320-961113ee06d9).html |url-access=subscription }}</ref><ref name=Coelacanths/>
==== Lungfish (Dipnoi) ==== Cretaceous ''[[Ceratodus]]'' sp. from [[Western Interior Seaway|Western Interior]] is estimated to have had a length of around {{cvt|4|m}}.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Shimada |first1=Kenshu |last2=Kirkland |first2=James I. |date=2011 |title=A mysterious king-sized Mesozoic lungfish from North America |journal=Transactions of the Kansas Academy of Science |volume=114 |issue=1/2 |pages=135–141 |doi=10.1660/062.114.0114 |jstor=41309634 |s2cid=84698238}}</ref>
==== Stem-tetrapods (Tetrapodomorpha) ==== [[File:Rhizodus.jpg|thumb|Reconstruction of ''[[Rhizodus]]'']] [[File:Hyneria (cropped).jpg|thumb|Reconstruction of ''[[Hyneria]]'']] * Not only the largest known [[Rhizodontida|rhizodont]], but also the largest lobe-finned fish was the {{cvt|5.63|-|7|m}} long ''[[Rhizodus]]''.<ref>{{cite journal |last=Jeffery |first=J.E. |year=2003 |title=Mandibles of rhizodontids: anatomy, function andevolution within the tetrapod stem-group |journal=Transactions of the Royal Society of Edinburgh: Earth Sciences |volume=93 |issue=3 |pages=255–276 |doi=10.1017/S0263593300000432 |s2cid=129517553}}</ref><ref name="DT" /> Another large rhizodonts were ''[[Strepsodus]]'' with estimated length around {{cvt|3|-|5|m}} and ''[[Barameda]]'' estimated at {{cvt|3|-|4|m}} in length.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Jeffery |first=Jonathan E. |date=1 September 2012 |title=Cranial morphology of the Carboniferous rhizodontid Screbinodus ornatus (Osteichthyes: Sarcopterygii) |journal=Journal of Systematic Palaeontology |volume=10 |issue=3 |pages=475–519 |doi=10.1080/14772019.2011.595961 |bibcode=2012JSPal..10..475J |s2cid=84810001 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Holland |first1=Timothy |title=Upper Devonian osteichthyan remains from the Genoa River, Victoria, Australia |journal=Memoirs of Museum Victoria |date=2010 |volume=67 |pages=35–44 |doi=10.24199/j.mmv.2010.67.04 |doi-access=free}}</ref> * [[Tristichopteridae|Tristichopterid]] ''[[Hyneria]]'' reached length up to {{cvt|3.5|m}}.<ref name="DT" />
=== Ray-finned fish (Actinopterygii) === {{further|Actinopterygii}}
==== Acipenseriformes ====
* ''[[Gyrosteus]]'', which belongs to extinct [[acipenseriform]] family [[Chondrosteidae]], is estimated to have standard length about {{cvt|6|-|7|m}}.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Hornung |first1=Jahn J. |last2=Sachs |first2=Sven |date=22 January 2020 |title=First record of Gyrosteus mirabilis (Actinopterygii, Chondrosteidae) from the Toarcian (Lower Jurassic) of the Baltic region |journal=PeerJ |volume=8 |article-number=e8400 |doi=10.7717/peerj.8400 |doi-access=free |pmid=32002331 |pmc=6982408 }}</ref> * The largest known fossil [[sturgeon]] is "''[[Acipenser]]''" ''gigantissimus'' known from fragmentary remains, which is estimated to reach up to {{cvt|5.8|m}}.<ref name="nessov1997">Nessov, L.A. (1997). ''Cretaceous nonmarine vertebrates of northern Eurasia.'' Saint Petersburg: University of Saint Petersburg Institute of Earth Crust, 218 pp. [in Russian].</ref> * The largest known fossil [[paddlefish]] is unnamed remain from [[Judith River Formation]], it may exceeded {{cvt|2|m}}, known remains exceeded size of recently extinct [[Chinese paddlefish]], which scientifically reported to exceed {{cvt|3|m}}.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Grande |first1=Lance |last2=Bemis |first2=William E. |date=1991 |title=Osteology and Phylogenetic Relationships of Fossil and Recent Paddlefishes (Polyodontidae) with Comments on the Interrelationships of Acipenseriformes |journal=Memoir (Society of Vertebrate Paleontology) |volume=1 |pages=ii–121 |doi=10.2307/3889328 |jstor=3889328 }}</ref>
