==== Pachycormiformes ==== [[File:Pachycormidae assortment.png|thumb|right|Largest specimen of ''Leedsichthys'' compared to human and other [[pachycormid]] fish]] The largest known [[Actinopterygii|ray-finned fish]] and largest bony fish of all time was the [[pachycormid]], ''[[Leedsichthys problematicus]]'', at around {{cvt|16.5|m}} long.<ref name="Liston2013">Liston, J., Newbrey, M., Challands, T., and Adams, C., 2013, "Growth, age and size of the Jurassic pachycormid ''Leedsichthys problematicus'' (Osteichthyes: Actinopterygii) in: Arratia, G., Schultze, H. and Wilson, M. (eds.) ''Mesozoic Fishes 5 – Global Diversity and Evolution''. Verlag Dr. Friedrich Pfeil, München, Germany, pp. 145–175</ref> Earlier estimates have had claims of larger individuals with lengths over {{cvt|27|m}}.<ref>Martill, D.M., 1988, "''Leedsichthys problematicus'', a giant filter-feeding teleost from the Jurassic of England and France", ''Neues Jahrbuch für Geologie und Paläontologie'' Monatshefte '''1988''' (11): 670–680</ref><ref>[Ed.] "Catch the 100ft fish", ''The Mirror'', London, England, 18 September 2003</ref>
==== Ichthyodectiformes ==== [[File:Ichthyodectidae1.jpg|thumb|Comparation of some ichthyodectiforms: ''[[Xiphactinus]]'' (1), ''[[Ichthyodectes]]'' (2), ''[[Cladocyclus]]'' (3), ''[[Chirocentrites]]'' (4)]] The largest known of [[ichthyodectiform]] fish was ''[[Xiphactinus]]'', which measured up to {{cvt|6.1|m}} long.<ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20160304030243/http://discoverfossils.com/documents/press-release-fossil-find-july-2010.pdf "Major Fossil Discovery Underway in Morden"] (PDF). Canadian Fossil Discovery Centre. 16 July 2010. Press release.</ref> ''[[Ichthyodectes]]'' reached {{cvt|3|m}} long, twice lesser than ''Xiphactinus''.<ref>{{cite book | url = https://books.google.com/books?id=Ej4rDwAAQBAJ&dq=%22ichthyodectes%22+long&pg=PA110 | title = Oceans of Kansas: A Natural History of the Western Interior Sea | publisher = Indiana University Press | date = 11 September 2017 | access-date = 26 August 2022 | page = 110 | first = Michael J. | last = Everhart | isbn = 978-0-253-02715-3 }}</ref>
==== Pycnodontiformes ==== The largest known [[pycnodontiform]] was ''[[Gyrodus|Gyrodus circularis]]'', with length up to {{cvt|2|m}}.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Kriwet |first1=Jürgen |title=The dentition of the enigmatic pycnodont fish, Athrodon wittei (Fricke, 1876) (Neopterygii, Pycnodontiformes; Late Jurassic; NW Germany) |journal=Fossil Record |date=August 2008 |volume=11 |issue=2 |pages=61–66 |doi=10.1002/mmng.200800002 |bibcode=2008FossR..11...61K |doi-access=free }}</ref>
==== Bichirs (Polypteriformes) ==== The [[Late Cretaceous]] ''[[Bawitius]]'' was likely the largest [[bichir]] of all time. It reached up to {{cvt|3|m}} in length.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Grandstaff, B. S|author2=Smith, J. B.|author3=Lamanna, M. C.|author4=Lacovara, K. J.|author5=Abdel-Ghani, M. S. |year=2012 |title=''Bawitius'', gen. nov., a giant polypterid (Osteichthyes, Actinopterygii) from the Upper Cretaceous Bahariya Formation of Egypt |journal=Journal of Vertebrate Paleontology |volume=32 |issue=1 |pages=17–26 |doi=10.1080/02724634.2012.626823|bibcode=2012JVPal..32...17G |s2cid=140547157}}</ref>
==== Opahes, ribbonfishes, oarfishes and allies (Lampriformes) ==== ''[[Megalampris]]'' was likely the largest fossil [[opah]]. This fish was around {{cvt|4|m}} in length when alive, which is twice the length of the largest living opah species, ''[[Lampris guttatus]]''.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Gottfried |first1=Michael D. |last2=Fordyce |first2=R. Ewan |last3=Rust |first3=Seabourne |date=2006 |journal=Journal of Vertebrate Paleontology |title=''Megalampris keyesi'', a Giant Moonfish (Teleostei, Lampridiformes) from the Late Oligocene of New Zealand |volume=6 |issue=3 |pages=544–551 |doi=10.1671/0272-4634(2006)26[544:MKAGMT]2.0.CO;2 |s2cid=85849900 }}</ref>
==== Salmon and trout (Salmoniformes) ==== The largest [[salmon]] was ''[[Oncorhynchus rastrosus]]'', varying in size from {{cvt|1.9|m}} and {{cvt|177|kg}}<ref name=Sankey2016>{{cite journal|first1=Julia |last1=Sankey |first2=Jacob |last2=Biewer |first3=Janis |last3=Basuga |first4=Francisco |last4=Palacios |first5=Hugh |last5=Wagner |title=The giant, spike-toothed salmon, ''Oncorhynchus rastrosus'' and the "Proto-Tuolumne River" (early Pliocene) of Central California. |journal=PaleoBios |volume=33 |year=2016 |pages=1–16 |doi=10.5070/P9331033123 | url=https://escholarship.org/uc/item/84g0595b|doi-access=free }}</ref> to {{cvt|2.4|m}} and {{cvt|200|kg}}.<ref>Stearley, R.F. and G.R. Smith. 2016. Salmonid fishes from Mio-Pliocene lake sediments in the Western Snake River Plain and the Great Basin. in [https://deepblue.lib.umich.edu/bitstream/handle/2027.42/134040/MP%20204.pdf W.L. Fink and N. Carpenter (eds.). Fishes of the Mio-Pliocene Western Snake River Plain and Vicinity]. Misc. Pub. Museum Zoology, University of Michigan 204:1–45</ref><ref name=Sankey2016/>
==== Pufferfishes, boxfishes, triggerfishes, ocean sunfishes and allies (Tetraodontiformes) ====
* ''[[Austromola angerhoferi]]'' had total body length about {{cvt|3.2|m}}, and total height {{cvt|4|m}}, comparable with largest [[ocean sunfish]].<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Gregorova |first1=Ruzena |last2=Schultz |first2=Ortwin |last3=Harzhauser |first3=Mathias |last4=Kroh |first4=Andreas |last5=Ćorić |first5=Stjepan |date=12 June 2009 |title=A giant early Miocene sunfish from the North Alpine Foreland Basin (Austria) and its implication for molid phylogeny |journal=Journal of Vertebrate Paleontology |volume=29 |issue=2 |pages=359–371 |doi=10.1671/039.029.0201 |bibcode=2009JVPal..29..359G |s2cid=54774567 }}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Troyer |first1=Emily M. |last2=Betancur-R |first2=Ricardo |last3=Hughes |first3=Lily C. |last4=Westneat |first4=Mark |last5=Carnevale |first5=Giorgio |last6=White |first6=William T. |last7=Pogonoski |first7=John J. |last8=Tyler |first8=James C. |last9=Baldwin |first9=Carole C. |last10=Ortí |first10=Guillermo |last11=Brinkworth |first11=Andrew |last12=Clavel |first12=Julien |last13=Arcila |first13=Dahiana |date=11 July 2022 |title=The impact of paleoclimatic changes on body size evolution in marine fishes |journal=Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences |volume=119 |issue=29 |article-number=e2122486119 |doi=10.1073/pnas.2122486119 |doi-access=free |pmc=9308125 |pmid=35858316 |bibcode=2022PNAS..11922486T }}</ref> * Some extinct species of ''[[Balistes]]'' like ''B. vegai'' and ''B. crassidens'' are estimated to have total length up to {{cvt|1.8|m}}.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Viñola Lopez |first1=Lazaro W. |last2=Carr |first2=Richard |last3=Lorenzo |first3=Logel |date=20 October 2020 |title=First occurrence of fossil Balistes (Tetradontiformes: Balistidae) from the Miocene of Cuba with the description of a new species and a revision of fossil Balistes |journal=Historical Biology |volume=32 |issue=9 |pages=1290–1299 |doi=10.1080/08912963.2019.1580278 |bibcode=2020HBio...32.1290V |s2cid=92003143 }}</ref>
==== Lizardfishes (Aulopiformes) ==== The largest [[Aulopiformes|lizardfish]] was ''[[Stratodus]]'' which could reach length of {{cvt|5|m}}.<ref>{{cite report |type=Preprint |last1=Michaut |first1=Marc |title=Stratodontidae, Maastrichtien du Niger, partie 1 |date=February 2012 |url=https://hal.science/hal-00734124 }}</ref>
== Echinoderms (Echinodermata) ==
=== Crinozoa === [[File:Seirocrinus subangularis 2.JPG|thumb|Fossil of ''[[Seirocrinus|Seirocrinus subangularis]]'']]
==== Sea lilies (Crinoidea) ==== The longest stem of ''[[Seirocrinus|Seirocrinus subangularis]]'' reached over {{cvt|26|m}}.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Hess |first1=Hans |title=Treatise Online, no. 16, Part T, Revised, Volume 1, Chapter 19: Paleoecology of pelagic crinoids |journal=Treatise Online |date=18 January 2011 |doi=10.17161/to.v0i0.4086 |doi-access=free }}</ref>
=== Asterozoa ===
==== Starfish (Asteroidea) ==== ''[[Helianthaster]]'' from [[Hunsrück Slate]] had a radius of about {{cvt|25|cm}}.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Blake |first=Daniel B. |date=1 June 2009 |title=Re-evaluation of the Devonian family Helianthasteridae Gregory, 1899 (Asteroidea: Echinodermata) |journal=Paläontologische Zeitschrift |volume=83 |issue=2 |pages=293–308 |doi=10.1007/s12542-009-0020-x |bibcode=2009PalZ...83..293B |s2cid=131480746 }}</ref>
== Graptolites (Graptolithina) == The longest known graptoloid [[graptolite]] is ''[[Stimulograptus|Stimulograptus halli]]'' at {{cvt|1.45|m|ft}}. It was found in [[Silurian]] deposits of the [[United Kingdom]].<ref>{{cite journal|author= David K. Loydell, Robert F. Loveridge|date=23 April 2001|title= The world's longest graptolite?|journal=Geological Journal|volume=36|issue=1|pages=55–57|doi=10.1002/gj.875|bibcode=2001GeolJ..36...55L |s2cid=129566263 }}</ref>
== Kinorhynchs (Kinorhyncha) == [[Cambrian]] [[Kinorhyncha|kinorhynchs]] from [[Qingjiang biota]], also known as "mud dragons", reached {{cvt|4|cm|in}} in length, much larger than extant relatives that grow only a few millimeters in length.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Fu |first1=Dongjing |last2=Tong |first2=Guanghui |last3=Dai |first3=Tao |last4=Liu |first4=Wei |last5=Yang |first5=Yuning |last6=Zhang |first6=Yuan |last7=Cui |first7=Linhao |last8=Li |first8=Luoyang |last9=Yun |first9=Hao |last10=Wu |first10=Yu |last11=Sun |first11=Ao |last12=Liu |first12=Cong |last13=Pei |first13=Wenrui |last14=Gaines |first14=Robert R. |last15=Zhang |first15=Xingliang |date=22 March 2019 |title=The Qingjiang biota—A Burgess Shale–type fossil Lagerstätte from the early Cambrian of South China |journal=Science |volume=363 |issue=6433 |pages=1338–1342 |doi=10.1126/science.aau8800 |pmid=30898931 |bibcode=2019Sci...363.1338F |s2cid=85448914 |doi-access=free }}</ref><ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.theguardian.com/science/2019/mar/21/mindblowing-haul-of-fossils-over-500m-years-old-unearthed-in-china|title='Mindblowing' haul of fossils over 500m years old unearthed in China|work=[[The Guardian]] |date=21 March 2019}}</ref>
== Arthropods (Arthropoda) ==
=== Dinocaridida ===
==== Gilled lobopodians ==== [[File:20210225 Omnidens size.png|thumb|Size estimation of ''[[Omnidens]]''.]] Based on the findings of mouthparts, the [[Cambrian]] gilled [[lobopodia]]n ''[[Omnidens|Omnidens amplus]]'' is estimated to have been {{convert|1.5|m|ft}}.<ref name="Fisher-1945" /> It is also known as the largest Cambrian animal known to exist.<ref name="Fisher-1945">{{cite journal |last1=Vinther |first1=Jakob |last2=Porras |first2=Luis |last3=Young |first3=Fletcher J. |last4=Budd |first4=Graham E. |last5=Edgecombe |first5=Gregory D. |title=The mouth apparatus of the Cambrian gilled lobopodian Pambdelurion whittingtoni |journal=Palaeontology |date=November 2016 |volume=59 |issue=6 |pages=841–849 |doi=10.1111/pala.12256 |bibcode=2016Palgy..59..841V |url=https://research-information.bris.ac.uk/en/publications/16da11f1-5231-4d6c-9968-69ddc5633a8a }}</ref>
==== Radiodont (Radiodonta) ==== [[File:20210221 Aegirocassis size.png|thumb|right|Scaled diagram of ''Aegirocassis'']] The largest known [[Radiodonta|radiodont]] is ''[[Aegirocassis]] benmoulai'', estimated to have been at least {{cvt|2|m}} long.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Van Roy |first1=Peter |last2=Daley |first2=Allison C. |last3=Briggs |first3=Derek E. G. |title=Anomalocaridid trunk limb homology revealed by a giant filter-feeder with paired flaps |journal=Nature |date=June 2015 |volume=522 |issue=7554 |pages=77–80 |doi=10.1038/nature14256 |pmid=25762145 |bibcode=2015Natur.522...77V }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Lerosey-Aubril |first1=Rudy |last2=Pates |first2=Stephen |title=New suspension-feeding radiodont suggests evolution of microplanktivory in Cambrian macronekton |journal=Nature Communications |date=14 September 2018 |volume=9 |issue=1 |page=3774 |doi=10.1038/s41467-018-06229-7 |pmid=30218075 |pmc=6138677 |bibcode=2018NatCo...9.3774L }}</ref>
=== Chelicerata ===
==== Sea spiders (Pycnogonida) ==== The largest fossil [[sea spider]] is ''[[Palaeoisopus|Palaeoisopus problematicus]]'' with legspan about {{cvt|32|cm}}.<ref name="Palaeoisopus1980">{{Cite journal |last1=Bergström |first1=Jan |last2=Stürmer |first2=Wilhelm |last3=Winter |first3=Gerhard |date=1 June 1980 |title=Palaeoisopus, Palaeopantopus and Palaeothea, pycnogonid arthropods from the Lower Devonian Hunsrück Slate, West Germany. |url=https://www.academia.edu/5146832 |journal=Paläontologische Zeitschrift |language=en |volume=54 |issue=1–2 |pages=7–54 |doi=10.1007/BF02985882 |bibcode=1980PalZ...54....7B |s2cid=86746066}}</ref>
==== Horseshoe crabs and allies (Xiphosura) ====
* ''[[Willwerathia]]'' reached {{cvt|9|cm}} in carapace width and was the largest species of basal ("[[Synziphosurina|synziphosurine]]") [[xiphosura]]ns.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Anderson |first1=Lyall I. |last2=Poschmann |first2=Markus |last3=Brauckmann |first3=Carsten |title=On the Emsian (Lower Devonian) arthropods of the Rhenish Slate Mountains: 2. The synziphosurineWillwerathia |journal=Paläontologische Zeitschrift |date=November 1998 |volume=72 |issue=3–4 |pages=325–336 |doi=10.1007/BF02988363 |bibcode=1998PalZ...72..325A }}</ref><ref name="Lamsdell2013">{{cite journal |last1=Lamsdell |first1=James C. |title=Revised systematics of Palaeozoic 'horseshoe crabs' and the myth of monophyletic Xiphosura: Re-evaluating the Monophyly of Xiphosura |journal=Zoological Journal of the Linnean Society |date=January 2013 |volume=167 |issue=1 |pages=1–27 |doi=10.1111/j.1096-3642.2012.00874.x }}</ref> However, the [[Devonian]] ''[[Maldybulakia]]'' reached nearly {{cvt|11.5|cm}}<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Edgecombe |first1=Gregory D. |title=Early myriapodous arthropods from Australia: Maldybulakia from the Devonian of New South Wales |journal=Records of the Australian Museum |date=25 November 1998 |volume=50 |issue=3 |pages=293–313 |doi=10.3853/j.0067-1975.50.1998.1288 |doi-access=free }}</ref> and was assigned to xiphosurans in 2013.<ref name="Lamsdell2013" /> * Horseshoe crab trackway [[Trace fossil|icnofossil]] ''[[Kouphichnium|Kouphichnium lithographicum]]'' from Cerin in [[Ain]] indicates length of animal {{cvt|77.4–85.1|cm}}.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Gaillard |first=Christian |date=1 December 2011 |title=A giant limulid trackway (Kouphichnium lithographicum) from the lithographic limestones of Cerin (Late Kimmeridgian, France): ethological and environmental implications |journal=[[Swiss Journal of Geosciences]] |volume=104 |issue=1 |pages=57–72 |doi=10.1007/s00015-010-0032-2 |s2cid=140543244 |doi-access=free |bibcode=2011SwJG..104...57G }}</ref>
==== Chasmataspidids (Chasmataspidida) ==== [[File:20210202 Chasmataspidida size comparison.png|thumb|Size comparison of the chasmataspidids]] The largest [[Chasmataspidida|chasmataspidids]] were the [[Ordovician]] ''[[Hoplitaspis]]'' at {{cvt|29|cm}} in length and similar in size range ''[[Chasmataspis]]''.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Lamsdell |first1=James C. |last2=Gunderson |first2=Gerald O. |last3=Meyer |first3=Ronald C. |title=A common arthropod from the Late Ordovician Big Hill Lagerstätte (Michigan) reveals an unexpected ecological diversity within Chasmataspidida |journal=BMC Evolutionary Biology |date=December 2019 |volume=19 |issue=1 |page=8 |doi=10.1186/s12862-018-1329-4 |doi-access=free |pmid=30621579 |pmc=6325806 |bibcode=2019BMCEE..19....8L }}</ref>
==== Eurypterids (Eurypterida) ==== [[File:Mega-Eurypterids.svg|thumb|Size comparison of the largest known [[eurypterid]]s]] * The largest known [[Eurypterida|eurypterid]] was ''[[Jaekelopterus rhenaniae]]'' at {{cvt|2.5|m}} in length, which is also the largest arthropod known to exist.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Braddy|first1=Simon J|last2=Poschmann|first2=Markus|last3=Tetlie|first3=O. Erik|date=23 February 2008|title=Giant claw reveals the largest ever arthropod|journal=Biology Letters|volume=4|issue=1|pages=106–109|doi=10.1098/rsbl.2007.0491|pmc=2412931|pmid=18029297}}</ref> ''[[Erettopterus|Erettopterus grandis]]'' possibly reached this same length but this is based on an incomplete [[telson]] only. A close contender was ''[[Acutiramus|Acutiramus bohemicus]]'' at {{cvt|2.1|m}} in length.<ref name=Lamsdell2009>{{cite journal|last1= Lamsdell|first1= James C.|last2= Braddy|first2= Simon J.|date= 2009|title= Cope's Rule and Romer's theory: patterns of diversity and gigantism in eurypterids and Palaeozoic vertebrates|journal= Biology Letters|volume= 6|issue= 2|pages= 265–9|doi= 10.1098/rsbl.2009.0700|pmc= 2865068|pmid= 19828493|id= [http://rsbl.royalsocietypublishing.org/content/suppl/2009/10/09/rsbl.2009.0700.DC1/rsbl20090700supp1.xls Supplementary information]|url= http://rsbl.royalsocietypublishing.org/content/suppl/2009/10/09/rsbl.2009.0700.DC1/rsbl20090700supp1.xls|access-date= 5 September 2022|archive-date= 28 February 2018|archive-url= https://web.archive.org/web/20180228041351/http://rsbl.royalsocietypublishing.org/content/suppl/2009/10/09/rsbl.2009.0700.DC1/rsbl20090700supp1.xls}}</ref> The largest [[Megalograptidae|megalograptid]] as well as the largest [[Ordovician]] eurypterid was ''[[Pentecopterus]]''. It reached up to {{cvt|1.7|m}} in length.<ref name=Winneshiek2015>{{cite journal|last1=Lamsdell|first1=James C.|last2=Briggs|first2=Derek E. G.|last3=Liu|first3=Huaibao|last4=Witzke|first4=Brian J.|last5=McKay|first5=Robert M.|date=1 September 2015|title=The oldest described eurypterid: a giant Middle Ordovician (Darriwilian) megalograptid from the Winneshiek Lagerstätte of Iowa|journal=[[BMC Evolutionary Biology]]|volume=15|issue=1 |page=169|doi=10.1186/s12862-015-0443-9|pmc=4556007|pmid=26324341 |doi-access=free |bibcode=2015BMCEE..15..169L }}</ref> All these were [[Eurypterina|eurypterine]] eurypterids.<ref name=Lamsdell2009/><ref name=Winneshiek2015/> * The largest [[Stylonurina|stylonurine]] eurypterid was ''[[Hibbertopterus]]'', with {{cvt|1.8|m}} in length.<ref name=Lamsdell2009/>
==== Arachnids (Arachnida) ==== * There are three contenders for largest-known [[arachnid]] as well as the largest [[scorpions]] of all time: ''[[Pulmonoscorpius kirktonensis]]'', ''[[Brontoscorpio|Brontoscorpio anglicus]]'' and ''[[Praearcturus|Praearcturus gigas]]''. Each was estimated to have been {{cvt|70|cm}},<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Jeram |first1=Andrew J. |title=Scorpions from the Viséan of East Kirkton, West Lothian, Scotland, with a revision of the infraorder Mesoscorpionina |journal=Earth and Environmental Science Transactions of the Royal Society of Edinburgh |date=1993 |volume=84 |issue=3–4 |pages=283–299 |doi=10.1017/S0263593300006106 |bibcode=1993EESTR..84..283J }}</ref> {{cvt|90|cm}}<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Kjellesvig-Waering|first=Erik N.|date=1972|title=Brontoscorpio anglicus: A Gigantic Lower Paleozoic Scorpion from Central England|journal=Journal of Paleontology|volume=46|issue=1|pages=39–42|jstor=1302906 }}</ref> and up to {{cvt|100|cm}},<ref name="KW1986">{{cite journal |last=Kjellesvig-Waering |first=E. N. |year=1986 |title=A restudy of the fossil Scorpionida of the world |url=https://archive.org/details/mobot31753003645626 |journal=Palaeontographica Americana |volume=55 |pages=1–287}}</ref> respectively. * ''[[Mongolarachne jurassica]]'' is the largest described fossil [[spider]], with the total body length of female is approximately {{cvt|24.6|mm|in}} while the front legs reach about {{cvt|56.5|mm|in}} in length.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Selden |first1=P. A. |last2=Shih |first2=C.K. |last3=Ren |first3=D. |title=A golden orb-weaver spider (Araneae: Nephilidae: ''Nephila'') from the Middle Jurassic of China |journal= Biology Letters |year=2011 |doi=10.1098/rsbl.2011.0228 |pmid=21508021 |pmc=3169061 |volume=7 |issue=5 |pages=775–8}}</ref> ''[[Dinodiplura|Dinodiplura ambulacra]]'' had larger body length, combined length of carapace and opisthosoma reaches {{cvt|26.15|mm}}.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Selden |first1=Paul A. |last2=Da Costa Casado |first2=Fabio |last3=Vianna Mesquita |first3=Marisa |title=Mygalomorph spiders (Araneae: Dipluridae) from the Lower Cretaceous Crato lagerstätte, Araripe Basin, north-east Brazil |journal=Palaeontology |date=July 2006 |volume=49 |issue=4 |pages=817–826 |doi=10.1111/j.1475-4983.2006.00561.x |bibcode=2006Palgy..49..817S |hdl=1808/8355 |hdl-access=free }}</ref> * The largest of prehistoric [[whipscorpion]]s and possibly the largest-known whipscorpion ever discovered<ref>{{cite book | url = https://books.google.com/books?id=5ZBh_-QvX2MC | title = The Crato Fossil Beds of Brazil: Window into an Ancient World | publisher = Cambridge University Press | date = 2007 | access-date = 22 August 2022 | page = 124 |author=David M. Martill |author2=Günter Bechly |author3=Robert F. Loveridge | isbn = 978-1-139-46776-6 }}</ref> was ''[[Mesoproctus|Mesoproctus rayoli]]''. The type specimen has body length reaching {{cvt|65.9|mm|in}} with a carapace of {{cvt|25.7|mm}} in length, while another specimen has a carapace of {{cvt|32.5|mm}} in length and {{cvt|16|mm}} in width, comparable or even larger than the extant ''[[Mastigoproctus]]''.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Santana |first1=William |last2=Pinheiro |first2=Allysson P. |last3=Silva |first3=Thiago Andrade |last4=Lima |first4=Daniel |date=3 January 2024 |title=Description of a new fossil Thelyphonida (Arachnida, Uropygi) and further record of Cratosolpuga wunderlichi Selden, in Selden and Shear, 1996 (Arachnida, Solifugae) from Crato Formation (Aptian/Albian), Araripe Basin, Brazil |journal=PeerJ |volume=12 |article-number=e16670 |doi=10.7717/peerj.16670 |doi-access=free |pmid=38188171 |pmc=10771091 }}</ref><ref>Jason A. Dunlop, David M. Martill. [https://d-nb.info/1216876800/34 The first whipspider (Arachnida: Amblypygi) and three new whipscorpions (Arachnida: Thelyphonida) from the Lower Cretaceous Crato Formation of Brazil]. Transactions of the Royal Society of Edinburgh: Earth Sciences, 92, 325–334, 2002.</ref> * The largest [[Ricinulei]] to ever exist was ''[[Curculioides|Curculioides bohemondi]]'' with a body length of {{cvt|21.77|mm}}.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Whalen |first1=Niall |last2=Selden |first2=Paul |title=A new, giant ricinuleid (Arachnida, Ricinulei), from the Pennsylvanian of Illinois, and the identification of a new, ontogenetically stable, diagnostic character |journal=Journal of Paleontology |date=May 2021 |volume=95 |issue=3 |pages=601–612 |doi=10.1017/jpa.2020.104 |bibcode=2021JPal...95..601W }}</ref> * The largest fossil [[Acariformes|acariform]] [[mite]] and also the largest [[Erythraeoidea|erythraeoid]] mite ever recorded was ''[[Immensmaris|Immensmaris chewbaccei]]'' with idiosoma of more than {{cvt|8|mm}} in length.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Dunlop |first1=Jason A. |last2=Frahnert |first2=Konrad |last3=Mąkol |first3=Joanna |title=A giant mite in Cretaceous Burmese amber |journal=Fossil Record |date=23 October 2018 |volume=21 |issue=2 |pages=285–290 |doi=10.5194/fr-21-285-2018 |doi-access=free |bibcode=2018FossR..21..285D }}</ref> * The largest known [[Trigonotarbida|trigonotarbid]] was ''[[Kreischeria]]'' with a minimal length of {{cvt|51|mm}}.<ref name=Ronny1997>{{cite journal |first1=Ronny |last1=Rößler |first2=Jason A. |last2=Dunlop |name-list-style=amp |year=1997 |title=Redescription of the largest trigonotarbid arachnid – ''Kreischeria wiedei'' Geinitz 1882 from the Upper Carboniferous of Zwickau, Germany |journal=[[Paläontologische Zeitschrift]] |volume=71 |issue=3–4 |pages=237–245 |doi=10.1007/BF02988493 |bibcode=1997PalZ...71..237R |s2cid=129447249 }}</ref> The second largest was ''[[Pleophrynus]]'' at {{cvt|36|mm}} in length.<ref name=Ronny1997/>
=== Artiopods (Artiopoda) === {{Further|Artiopoda}} ''[[Retifacies]]'' probably reached up to {{cvt|55|cm}}.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Zhang |first1=Maoyin |last2=Liu |first2=Yu |last3=Hou |first3=Xianguang |last4=Ortega-Hernández |first4=Javier |last5=Mai |first5=Huijuan |last6=Schmidt |first6=Michel |last7=Melzer |first7=Roland R. |last8=Guo |first8=Jin |date=2022 |title=Ventral Morphology of the Non-Trilobite Artiopod Retifacies abnormalis Hou, Chen & Lu, 1989, from the Early Cambrian Chengjiang Biota, China |journal=Biology |volume=11 |issue=8 |page=1235 |doi=10.3390/biology11081235 |pmid=36009864 |pmc=9405172 |doi-access=free }}</ref> ''[[Tegopelte]]'' is another one example of large non-trilobite artiopod, reached {{cvt|280|mm}} long<ref name=Minter2011/> and was the largest of the [[Burgess Shale]] bilaterians, surpassing all other benthic organisms by at least twice.<ref name=Minter2011>{{Cite journal |author=Nicholas J. Minter, M. Gabriela Mángano & Jean-Bernard Caron |year=2011 |title=Skimming the surface with Burgess Shale arthropod locomotion |journal=[[Proceedings of the Royal Society B: Biological Sciences]] |volume= 279 |issue=1733 |pages= 1613–1620|doi=10.1098/rspb.2011.1986 |pmid=22072605 |pmc=3282348}}</ref>
==== Trilobites (Trilobita) ==== Some of [[trilobite]]s exceeded {{cvt|60|cm}} in length. A nearly complete specimen of ''[[Isotelus|Isotelus rex]]'' from [[Manitoba]] attained a length over {{cvt|70|cm}}, and an ''[[Ogyginus|Ogyginus forteyi]]'' from Portugal was almost as long. Fragments of [[trilobite]]s suggest even larger record sizes. An isolated [[pygidium]] of ''[[Hungioides|Hungioides bohemicus]]'' implies that the full animal was {{cvt|90|cm}} long.<ref name="Hungioides">{{Cite journal| last1 = Gutiérrez-Marco| first1 = Juan C. | last2 = Sá | first2 = Artur A. | last3 = Garcia-Bellido | first3 = Diego C. | last4 = Rabano | first4 = Isabel | last5 = Valério | first5 = Manuel | title = Giant Trilobites and Trilobite Clusters from the Ordovician of Portugal | journal = Geology | volume = 37 | issue = 5 | pages = 443–446 | year = 2009 | doi = 10.1130/G25513A.1| bibcode = 2009Geo....37..443G }}</ref>
=== Myriapods (Myriapoda) === [[File:Arthropleura Rekonstruktion 2 W. Kraus.jpg|thumb|A life-size reconstruction of ''[[Arthropleura]]'']] The largest known [[Myriapoda|myriapod]] by far was ''[[Arthropleura]]''. Measuring {{convert|2.5|m}} long<ref>{{Cite book | url=https://books.google.com/books?id=wFqrAgAAQBAJ&q=some+individuals+of+arthropleura+armata&pg=PA238 |title = When the Invasion of Land Failed: The Legacy of the Devonian Extinctions|isbn = 978-0-231-16057-5|last1 = George r. Mcghee|first1 = Jr.|date = 12 November 2013| publisher=Columbia University Press }}</ref> and {{convert|50|cm}} wide.<ref>{{cite web|title=The Excitement of Discovery|url=http://museum.gov.ns.ca/mnh/nature/tracefossils/english/sections/whodunnit/traces/diplichnites.html|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120204055638/http://museum.gov.ns.ca/mnh/nature/tracefossils/english/sections/whodunnit/traces/diplichnites.html|archive-date=4 February 2012|access-date=17 April 2006|work=Virtual Museum of Canada}}</ref> Some specimens could have been even larger, up to {{convert|2.63|m}} in length and {{convert|50|kg|lbs}} in weight.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/science-environment-59734747 |title=Largest-ever millipede fossil found on Northumberland beach |work=[[BBC News]] |date=21 December 2021 |access-date=21 December 2021}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |display-authors=etal|last1=Neil Davies |title=The largest arthropod in Earth history: insights from newly discovered Arthropleura remains (Serpukhovian Stainmore Formation, Northumberland, England) |journal=Journal of the Geological Society |date=21 December 2021 |volume=179 |issue=3 |article-number=jgs2021-115 |doi=10.1144/jgs2021-115 |s2cid=245401499 |url=https://jgs.lyellcollection.org/content/early/2021/11/19/jgs2021-115}}</ref>
=== Non-hexapod crustaceans (Crustacea) === ==== Cycloids (Cyclida) ==== The largest [[Cyclida|cyclid]] was ''Opolanka decorosa'', the [[Late Triassic]] ''[[Halicyne]]''-like cycloid which reached over {{cvt|6|cm}} across the [[carapace]].<ref>{{cite journal |first=Jerzy |last=Dzik |year=2008 |title=Gill structure and relationships of the Triassic cycloid crustaceans |journal=[[Journal of Morphology]] |volume=269 |issue=12 |pages=1501–1519 |pmid=18690662 |doi=10.1002/jmor.10663 |bibcode=2008JMorp.269.1501D |s2cid=17617567 |url=http://www.paleo.pan.pl/people/Dzik/Publications/Opolanka.pdf |doi-access=free }}</ref>
==== Remipedes (Remipedia) ==== ''[[Tesnusocaris]]'' had body length at least {{cvt|9.5|cm}},<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Emerson |first1=M.J. |last2=Schram |first2=F.R. |date=1991 |title=Remipedia, part 2: paleontology. |url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/4373576 |journal=Proceedings of the San Diego Society of Natural History |volume=7 |pages=1–52}}</ref> larger than all living [[Remipedia|remipedes]] which can reach up to {{cvt|4.5|cm}}.<ref>[http://www.crustacea.net/crustace/remipedia/www/robustus.htm Remipedia: Species – robustus, Godzillius]. Crustacea.net (2 October 2002)</ref>
=== Insects (Insecta) === {{Further|List of largest insects}}
==== Sawflies, wasps, bees, ants and allies (Hymenoptera) ==== [[File:Titanomyrma lubei DMNH9041 02.jpg|thumb|''[[Titanomyrma]]'' with rufous hummingbird for scale]] * The largest known of this group was the giant ant ''[[Titanomyrma|Titanomyrma giganteum]]'' with [[Eusociality|queens]] growing to {{cvt|6|cm}}. It had a wingspan of {{cvt|15|cm}}.<ref name="Messel">{{cite book |doi=10.1038/npg.els.0004143 |chapter=Messel |title=Encyclopedia of Life Sciences |date=2006 |last1=Schaal |first1=Stephan |isbn=978-0-470-01617-6 }}</ref> * ''[[Apis lithohermaea]]'' is one of the largest honey bees ever found, comparable in size to the modern ''[[Apis dorsata]]''.<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Engel|first=M. S.|date=2006|title=The giant honey bee, Apis lithohermaea Engel, from the Miocene of Japan and the geological history of Apis (Hymenoptera: Apidae)|url=https://www.cabidigitallibrary.org/doi/full/10.5555/20063220072 |journal=Honeybee Science }}</ref> * The giant [[horntail]] ''[[Ypresiosirex|Ypresiosirex orthosemos]]'' reached {{convert|67.9|mm|in|abbr=on}} in length including the incomplete [[ovipositor]].<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Archibald |first1=S.B. |last2=Rasnitsyn |first2=A.P. |year=2015 |title=New early Eocene Siricomorpha (Hymenoptera: Symphyta: Pamphiliidae, Siricidae, Cephidae) from the Okanagan Highlands, western North America |journal=The Canadian Entomologist |volume=148 |issue=2 |pages=209–228 |doi=10.4039/tce.2015.55 |s2cid=85743832 }}</ref> Another example of a giant [[sawfly]] is ''[[Hoplitolyda duolunica]]'', with wingspan over {{convert|92|mm|in|abbr=on}}.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Gao |first1=Taiping |last2=Shih |first2=Chungkun |last3=Rasnitsyn |first3=Alexandr P. |last4=Ren |first4=Dong |date=3 May 2013 |title=Hoplitolyda duolunica gen. et sp. nov. (Insecta, Hymenoptera, Praesiricidae), the Hitherto Largest Sawfly from the Mesozoic of China |journal=PLOS ONE |volume=8 |issue=5 |article-number=e62420 |doi=10.1371/journal.pone.0062420 |pmc=3643952 |pmid=23671596 |bibcode=2013PLoSO...862420G |doi-access=free }}</ref>
==== Fleas (Siphonaptera) ==== The largest known in [[Siphonaptera]] was probably ''[[Pseudopulex|Pseudopulex magnus]]'', growing to {{cvt|22.8|mm|order=flip||abbr=}} in length.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Zhang |first1=Yanjie |last2=Shih |first2=Chungkun |last3=Rasnitsyn |first3=Alexandr |last4=Ren |first4=Dong |last5=Gao |first5=Taiping |date=2020 |title=A new flea from the Early Cretaceous of China |journal=Acta Palaeontologica Polonica |volume=65 |doi=10.4202/app.00680.2019 |s2cid=213622677 |doi-access=free }}</ref>
==== Earwigs (Dermaptera) ==== [[File:Labidura herculeana (cropped).jpg|thumb|''[[Labidura herculeana]]'' (St. Helena earwig) specimen]] Extinct as recently as after 1967<ref name=GiantLabidura>{{cite web |title=Labidura |work=St Helena and Ascension Island Natural History |url=http://www.kidstonmill.org.uk/Labidura.htm |access-date=13 October 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110717014815/http://www.kidstonmill.org.uk/Labidura.htm |archive-date=17 July 2011 }}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.bbc.com/earth/story/20141117-giant-earwig-declared-extinct|title=World's largest earwig is declared extinct|first=Matt|last=Walker|website=BBC Earth|date=17 November 2014|language=en|access-date=13 October 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170501144702/http://www.bbc.com/earth/story/20141117-giant-earwig-declared-extinct|archive-date=1 May 2017}}</ref> and also submitted as the [[Holocene]] [[subfossil]]s,<ref>F. E. Zeuner. 1962. A subfossil giant dermapteron from St. Helena. Proceedings of the Zoological Society of London 138:651–653</ref> the [[Saint Helena earwig|Saint Helena giant earwig]] (''[[Labidura herculeana]]'', with synonym ''Labidura loveridgei'') reached {{cvt|84|mm}} in length including forceps {{cvt|34|mm}} long.<ref name=GiantLabidura/>
==== Chresmodidae ==== [[Chresmodidae]] had long specialized legs like of the modern [[Gerridae]] family. One of the Chresmodidae, ''[[Chresmoda|Chresmoda obscura]]'', could have reached a size of about {{convert|19|cm}}.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.solnhofen-fossilienatlas.de/fossil.php?fossilid=1780|title=Fossil mit der ID: 1780 – Chresmoda obscura GERMAR, 1839 – Die Fossilien von Solnhofen|work=solnhofen-fossilienatlas.de|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220820121925/https://www.solnhofen-fossilienatlas.de/fossil.php?fossilid=1780|archive-date=20 August 2022|access-date=13 October 2022|url-status=live}}</ref>
==== Beetles (Coleoptera) ==== One of the largest known fossil [[beetle]]s in the [[taxonomic rank|superfamily]] [[Scarabaeoidea]] is ''[[Protognathinus|Protognathinus spielbergi]]''. It had total length including mandibles about {{convert|5.5|cm}}.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Chalumeau |first1=F. |last2=Brochier |first2=B. |date=2001 |title=Une forme fossile nouvelle de Chiasognathinae: ''Protognathinus spielbergi''(Coleoptera, Lucanidae). |url=http://www.bio-nica.info/biblioteca/Chalumeau2991ProtognathinusSpielbergi.pdf |journal=Lambillionea |volume=101 |pages=593–595 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220920173746/http://www.bio-nica.info/biblioteca/Chalumeau2991ProtognathinusSpielbergi.pdf |archive-date=20 September 2022 |url-status=live}}</ref> The largest fossil [[Scarabaeidae|scarabaeid]] was ''[[Oryctoantiquus|Oryctoantiquus borealis]]'' with an estimated body length of {{convert|5|cm}}.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Ratcliffe |first1=Brett C. |last2=Smith |first2=Dena M. |last3=Erwin |first3=Diane |title=Oryctoantiquus borealis, New Genus and Species from the Eocene of Oregon, U.S.A., the World's Oldest Fossil Dynastine and Largest Fossil Scarabaeid (Coleoptera: Scarabaeidae: Dynastinae) |journal=The Coleopterists Bulletin |date=March 2005 |volume=59 |issue=1 |pages=127–135 |doi=10.1649/0010-065X(2005)059[0127:OBNGAS]2.0.CO;2 }}</ref>
==== Titanopterans (Titanoptera) ==== [[File:Gigatitan vulgaris reconstruction.png|thumb|Reconstruction of ''[[Gigatitan|Gigatitan vulgaris]]'']] Related to modern [[orthoptera]]ns, [[titanoptera]]ns from the [[Triassic]] period were much larger. The wingspan of ''[[Gigatitan|Gigatitan vulgaris]]'' was up to {{convert|40|cm}}.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Park |first1=Tae-Yoon S. |last2=Kim |first2=Do-Yoon |last3=Nam |first3=Gi-Soo |last4=Lee |first4=Mirinae |date=6 May 2022 |title=A new titanopteran Magnatitan jongheoni n. gen. n. sp. from southwestern Korean Peninsula |journal=Journal of Paleontology |volume=96 |issue=5 |pages=1111–1118 |doi=10.1017/jpa.2022.30 |bibcode=2022JPal...96.1111P |s2cid=248592776 |doi-access=free }}</ref> ''[[Clatrotitan|Clatrotitan andersoni]]'' also reached a huge size, having a forewing {{convert|13.8|cm}} long.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=McKeown |first=Keith C. |date=15 May 1937 |title=New fossil insect wings (Protohemiptera, family Mesotitanidae) |journal=Records of the Australian Museum |volume=20 |issue=1 |pages=31–37 |doi=10.3853/j.0067-1975.20.1937.565 |doi-access=free }}</ref>
==== Antlions and related net-winged insects (Neuroptera) ==== ''[[Makarkinia adamsi]]'' from the [[Crato Formation]] is estimated to have the longest forewings of any [[neuroptera]]n species, estimated at {{convert|160|mm|in|abbr=on}}.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Bechly |first1=G. |last2=Makarkin |first2=V. N. |year=2016 |title=A new gigantic lacewing species (Insecta: Neuroptera) from the Lower Cretaceous of Brazil confirms the occurrence of Kalligrammatidae in the Americas |journal=Cretaceous Research |volume=58 |pages= 135–140|doi=10.1016/j.cretres.2015.10.014 |bibcode=2016CrRes..58..135B }}</ref>
==== Cockroaches, termites, mantises and allies (Dictyoptera) ==== {{Further|Dictyoptera}} * The largest known [[roachoid]], cockroach-like stem dictyopteran, is ''[[Opsiomylacris]]'' sp., with wing length of {{cvt|75|mm|in}}, which is comparable to a modern ''[[Megaloblatta longipennis]]''.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Schneider |first1=Joerg W. |last2=Rößler |first2=Ronny |date=14 March 2023 |title=The Early History of Giant Cockroaches: Gyroblattids and Necymylacrids (Blattodea) of the Late Carboniferous |journal=Diversity |language=en |volume=15 |issue=3 |page=429 |doi=10.3390/d15030429 |bibcode=2023Diver..15..429S |doi-access=free |issn=1424-2818}}</ref> * Cretaceous [[cockroach]] ''[[Ptiloteuthis|Ptiloteuthis foliatus]]'' had a {{cvt|7.9|cm}} long wing.<ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=KIJiGSeDbqcC&q=ptiloteuthis |title=Genus Ptiloteuthis Gabb: Notulae Naturae of The Academy of Natural Sciences of Phila., No. 9 |publisher=Academy of Natural Sciences |isbn=978-1-60483-009-5 |language=en}}</ref> * Found in the [[Miocene]] of [[Austria]], the giant termite ''[[Gyatermes|Gyatermes styriensis]]'' reached {{cvt|25|mm|in}} in body length and had a wingspan of {{cvt|76|mm|in}}.<ref>{{cite journal|last1=Engel|first1=M.S.|last2=Gross|first2=M.|title=A giant termite from the Late Miocene of Styria, Austria (Isoptera)|journal=Naturwissenschaften|date=2008|volume=96|issue=2|pages=289–295|doi=10.1007/s00114-008-0480-y|pmid=19052720|bibcode=2009NW.....96..289E|s2cid=21795900}}</ref>
==== Dragonflies, damselflies and griffinflies (Odonatoptera) ==== [[File:Meganeura.png|thumb|Reconstruction of ''[[Meganeura]]'']]
* The largest known [[odonatoptera]]n insect was ''[[Meganeuropsis permiana]]'' with a single wing of {{cvt|33|cm}}. ''[[Meganeura]]'' had a {{cvt|32|cm}} long wing.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Gand |first1=G. |last2=Nel |first2=A. N. |last3=Fleck |first3=G. |last4=Garrouste |first4=R. |date=1 January 2008 |title=The Odonatoptera of the Late Permian Lodève Basin (Insecta) |url=https://revistas.ucm.es/index.php/JIGE/article/view/JIGE0808120115A |journal=Journal of Iberian Geology |language=es |volume=34 |issue=1 |pages=115–122 }}</ref> * [[Triadotypidae|Triadotypid]] [[odonata]]n ''[[Reisia|Reisia gelasii]]'' (=''Triadotypus guillaumei'') from Triassic had {{convert|136|mm|in}} long wing, and wingspan can be {{convert|280|mm|in}}.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Zhang |first1=HaiChun |last2=Zheng |first2=DaRan |last3=Wang |first3=Bo |last4=Fang |first4=Yan |last5=Jarzembowski |first5=Edmund A. |date=1 May 2013 |title=The largest known odonate in China: Hsiufua chaoi Zhang et Wang, gen. et sp. nov. from the middle jurassic of inner mongolia |journal=Chinese Science Bulletin |volume=58 |issue=13 |pages=1579–1584 |doi=10.1007/s11434-012-5567-3 |bibcode=2013ChSBu..58.1579Z |s2cid=95712438 |doi-access=free }}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Deregnaucourt |first1=Isabelle |last2=Wappler |first2=Torsten |last3=Anderson |first3=John |last4=Bethoux |first4=Olivier |date=2017 |title=A new triadotypid insect from the Late Triassic of South Africa |journal=Acta Palaeontologica Polonica |volume=62 |doi=10.4202/app.00345.2017 |s2cid=115149987 |doi-access=free }}</ref>
==== Mayflies (Ephemeroptera) ====
* The largest known [[mayfly]] is Permian ''[[Ponalex|Ponalex maximus]]'', with {{convert|55|mm|0|abbr=on}} long hindwing.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Sinitshenkova |first=Nina |date=2012 |title=The biggest fossil mayfly (Insecta: Ephemerida = Ephemeroptera) from the Upper Permian locality of Isady, Northern European Russia |url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/258221843 |journal=Far Eastern Entomologist}}</ref> Cretaceous ''[[Epicharmeropsis|Epicharmeropsis quadrivenulosus]]'' had {{convert|37|mm|0|abbr=on}} long forewing.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Huang |first1=Jiandong |last2=Ren |first2=Dong |last3=Sinitshenkova |first3=Nina D. |last4=Shih |first4=Chungkun |date=2 November 2007 |title=New genus and species of Hexagenitidae (Insecta: Ephemeroptera) from Yixian Formation, China |journal=Zootaxa |volume=1629 |issue=1 |pages=39–50 |doi=10.11646/zootaxa.1629.1.3 }}</ref> * Although ''[[Bojophlebia|Bojophlebia prokopi]]'' from the [[Upper Carboniferous]] of [[Moravia]] ([[Czech Republic]]) with a wingspan of {{convert|45|cm|0|abbr=on}} is described as the largest mayfly,<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Kukalová-Peck |first1=Jarmila |title=Ephemeroid wing venation based upon new gigantic Carboniferous mayflies and basic morphology, phylogeny, and metamorphosis of pterygote insects (Insecta, Ephemerida) |journal=Canadian Journal of Zoology |date=April 1985 |volume=63 |issue=4 |pages=933–955 |doi=10.1139/z85-139 |bibcode=1985CaJZ...63..933K }}</ref> later study shows that this insect is not related to mayflies.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Sroka |first1=Pavel |last2=Staniczek |first2=Arnold H. |last3=Bechly |first3=Günter |date=2 November 2015 |title=Revision of the giant pterygote insect Bojophlebia prokopi Kukalová-Peck, 1985 (Hydropalaeoptera: Bojophlebiidae) from the Carboniferous of the Czech Republic, with the first cladistic analysis of fossil palaeopterous insects |journal=Journal of Systematic Palaeontology |volume=13 |issue=11 |pages=963–982 |doi=10.1080/14772019.2014.987958 |bibcode=2015JSPal..13..963S |s2cid=84037275 }}</ref>
==== Palaeodictyoptera ==== The largest known [[palaeodictyoptera]]n was ''[[Mazothairos]]'', with an estimated wingspan of up to {{cvt|22|in|order=flip}}.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Kukalová-Peck|first1=Jarmila|last2=Richardson|first2=Eugene S.|date=1983|title=New Homoiopteridae (Insecta: Paleodictyoptera) with wing articulation from Upper Carboniferous strata of Mazon Creek, Illinois|journal=Canadian Journal of Zoology|volume=61|issue=7|pages=1670–1687|doi=10.1139/z83-218|bibcode=1983CaJZ...61.1670K }}</ref> If a subcircular wing known from [[Piesberg Quarry]] belongs to a palaeodictyopteran, it possibly had single wing length at least {{cvt|30|cm|order=out}}.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Brauckmann |first1=Carsten |last2=HERD |first2=KARL |date=2007 |title=A subcircular insect wing from the Late Carboniferous of Osnabrück, Germany |url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/242601181 |journal=Clausthaler Geowissenschaften |volume=6 |pages=79–85}}</ref>
==== Archaeognatha (jumping bristletails) and other wingless primitive insects ====
* The largest known [[Machilidae|machilid]] is Triassic ''[[Gigamachilis]]'', with {{convert|40|mm|in}} body length not counting the length of the [[Cercus|filament]], and estimated total length about {{convert|80|mm|in}}.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Montagna |first1=Matteo |last2=Haug |first2=Joachim T. |last3=Strada |first3=Laura |last4=Haug |first4=Carolin |last5=Felber |first5=Markus |last6=Tintori |first6=Andrea |date=7 April 2017 |title=Central nervous system and muscular bundles preserved in a 240 million year old giant bristletail (Archaeognatha: Machilidae) |journal=Scientific Reports |volume=7 |issue=1 |article-number=46016 |doi=10.1038/srep46016 |pmid=28387236 |pmc=5384076 |bibcode=2017NatSR...746016M }}</ref> * The largest specimens of the extinct suborder [[Monura]] reached {{convert|30|mm|in}} or more, not counting the length of the filament.<ref>{{cite book |author= Hoell, H.V. |author2= Doyen, J.T. |author3= Purcell, A.H. |name-list-style=amp |year=1998 |title=Introduction to Insect Biology and Diversity |edition=2nd |publisher= Oxford University Press |page= 321|isbn= 0-19-510033-6}}</ref> * Although ''[[Ramsdelepidion]]'' was once considered as a {{convert|60|mm|in}}-long [[silverfish]],<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Shear |first1=William A. |last2=Kukalová-Peck |first2=Jarmila |title=The ecology of Paleozoic terrestrial arthropods: the fossil evidence |journal=Canadian Journal of Zoology |date=September 1990 |volume=68 |issue=9 |pages=1807–1834 |doi=10.1139/z90-262 |bibcode=1990CaJZ...68.1807S }}</ref> it was later considered that classification is uncertain and just treated as [[Crown group|stem group]] insect.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Stockar |first=Rudolf |title=The first Mesozoic record of the extinct apterygote insect genus Dasyleptus (Insecta: Archaeognatha: Monura: Dasyleptidae) from the Triassic of Monte San Giorgio (Switzerland) |url=https://www.academia.edu/21541788 |journal=Paleodiversity |volume=4}}</ref> * The wingless early insect ''[[Carbotriplura]]'' had body length about {{convert|103|mm|in}} without tail filaments.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Staniczek |first1=Arnold H. |last2=Sroka |first2=Pavel |last3=Bechly |first3=GüNter |title=Neither silverfish nor fowl: the enigmatic C arboniferous Carbotriplura kukalovae K luge, 1996 (Insecta: C arbotriplurida) is the putative fossil sister group of winged insects (Insecta: P terygota) |journal=Systematic Entomology |date=October 2014 |volume=39 |issue=4 |pages=619–632 |doi=10.1111/syen.12076 |bibcode=2014SysEn..39..619S}}</ref>
==Arrow worms (Chaetognatha)== The [[Cambrian]] stem-[[Chaetognatha|chaetognathan]] ''[[Timorebestia]] koprii'' had reached up to {{cvt|20|cm}} in body length and {{cvt|30|cm}} including the antennae.<ref name="SG">{{cite journal |last1=Park |first1=Tae-Yoon S. |last2=Nielsen |first2=Morten Lunde |last3=Parry |first3=Luke A. |last4=Sørensen |first4=Martin Vinther |last5=Lee |first5=Mirinae |last6=Kihm |first6=Ji-Hoon |last7=Ahn |first7=Inhye |last8=Park |first8=Changkun |last9=de Vivo |first9=Giacinto |last10=Smith |first10=M. Paul |last11=Harper |first11=David A. T. |last12=Nielsen |first12=Arne T. |last13=Vinther |first13=Jakob |title=A giant stem-group chaetognath |journal=Science Advances |date=2024 |volume=10 |issue=1 |article-number=eadi6678 |doi=10.1126/sciadv.adi6678 |pmid=38170772 |pmc=10796117 |bibcode=2024SciA...10I6678P}}</ref> ''[[Capinatator]]'' had a third of length, about {{cvt|10|cm}}, but it is not considered as stem member and still had length similar to the largest modern arrow worms.<ref name="SG" /><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Briggs |first1=Derek E.G. |last2=Caron |first2=Jean-Bernard |date=2017 |title=A Large Cambrian Chaetognath with Supernumerary Grasping Spines |url=https://linkinghub.elsevier.com/retrieve/pii/S0960982217308606 |journal=Current Biology |volume=27 |issue=16 |pages=2536–2543.e1 |doi=10.1016/j.cub.2017.07.003 |pmid=28781052 |bibcode=2017CBio...27E2536B |issn=0960-9822}}</ref>
== Ringed worms (Annelida) == ''[[Websteroprion]]'' is the largest known fossil [[eunicida]]n [[Annelida|annelid]], with estimated length ranges {{cvt|0.42|-|8.3|m}}, however comparison with closely related extant taxa indicates length around {{cvt|1|-|2|m}}.<ref name=Erikssonetal2017>{{Cite journal |last1=Eriksson |first1=Mats E. |last2=Parry |first2=Luke A. |last3=Rudkin |first3=David M. |date=February 2017 |title=Earth's oldest "Bobbit worm" – gigantism in a Devonian eunicidan polychaete |journal=Scientific Reports|volume=7 |issue=2 |pages=239–260 |language=en |doi=10.1038/srep43061|pmid=28220886 |pmc=5318920 |bibcode=2017NatSR...743061E |doi-access=free}}</ref> It also had the biggest [[scolecodont]]s of any prehistoric [[polychaete]], up to {{cvt|13.2|mm}} in length and possibly larger.<ref name=Erikssonetal2017/>
== Molluscs (Mollusca) ==
=== Snails and slugs (Gastropoda) === [[File:Campanile giganteum.jpg|thumb|right|''[[Campanile giganteum]]'' shell]]
* The largest known [[Gastropoda|gastropods]] were in the genus ''[[Campanile (gastropod)|Campanile]]'', with the extinct ''[[Campanile giganteum]]'' having shell lengths up to {{cvt|90|cm}}<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Zuerich |first1=ETH-Bibliothek |title=Giant gastropods of the genus Campanile from the Caribbean Eocene |journal=E-Periodica |url=https://www.e-periodica.ch/cntmng?pid=egh-001:1987:80::1248 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220615114321/https://www.e-periodica.ch/cntmng?pid=egh-001:1987:80::1248 |archive-date=15 June 2022 |language=de}}</ref> or even more than {{cvt|120|cm}}.<ref>[http://vertebresfossiles.free.fr/tempsgeol/cerithium_giganteum.html Cerithium giganteum ou Campanile giganteum]. [https://web.archive.org/web/20160728205144/http://vertebresfossiles.free.fr/tempsgeol/cerithium_giganteum.html Archived copy] from 28 July 2016.</ref> * The largest known [[cowrie]] is ''[[Vicetia (gastropod)|Vicetia bizzottoi]]'', with shell length of {{cvt|33.5|cm}}.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Dominici |first1=Stefano |last2=Fornasiero |first2=Mariagabriella |last3=Giusberti |first3=Luca |date=14 December 2020 |title=The largest known cowrie and the iterative evolution of giant cypraeid gastropods |journal=Scientific Reports |volume=10 |issue=1 |page=21893 |doi=10.1038/s41598-020-78940-9 |pmc=7736312 |pmid=33318588 |bibcode=2020NatSR..1021893D }}</ref> * ''[[Pebasiconcha immanis]]'' is the largest land snail ever known, shell height is {{cvt|25.6|cm}} with a partial specimen that may exceed {{cvt|30|cm}} in height.<ref name="Wesselingh 20062">{{cite journal |last1=Wesselingh |first1=Frank |last2=Anderson |first2=L. C. |last3=Kadolsky |first3=D. |title=Molluscs from the Miocene Pebas Formation of Peruvian and Colombian Amazonia |journal=Scripta Geologica |date=January 2006 |volume=133 |pages=19–290 |url=https://repository.naturalis.nl/pub/209728 }}</ref>
=== Bivalves (Bivalvia) ===
* The largest known [[bivalve]] ever as well as the largest [[Inoceramidae|inoceramid]] was ''[[Platyceramus platinus]]'', a giant that usually had an axial length of {{cvt|1|m}}, but some individuals could reach an axial length of up to {{cvt|3|m}}.<ref>Journal of Paleontology, January 2007, Vol. 81, No. 1 (January 2007), pp. 64–81</ref> Another large prehistoric bivalve was ''[[Inoceramus]]''. In 1952, {{cvt|187|cm|ft}}-long specimen of ''Inoceramus steenstrupi'' was found in the [[Late Cretaceous]] deposits of [[Greenland]].<ref>{{cite web|url=https://natur.gl/verdens-stoerste-musling/|title=Verdens største musling|work=Greenland Institute of Natural Resources |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220818085326/https://natur.gl/verdens-stoerste-musling/|archive-date=18 August 2022}}</ref> * Some Permian [[Alatoconchidae|alatoconchid]] genus like ''[[Shikamaia]]'' had shell length about {{cvt|1|m}}.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Asato |first1=Kaito |last2=Kase |first2=Tomoki |last3=Ono |first3=Teruo |last4=Sashida |first4=Katsuo |last5=Agematsu |first5=Sachiko |title=Morphology, Systematics and Paleoecology of Shikamaia , Aberrant Permian Bivalves (Alatoconchidae: Ambonychioidea) from Japan |journal=Paleontological Research |date=October 2017 |volume=21 |issue=4 |pages=358–379 |doi=10.2517/2017PR002 |bibcode=2017PalRe..21..358A }}</ref> Previous estimation reconstructed length of ''Shikamaia'' around {{cvt|1.6|m}}.<ref>{{Cite web |title=化石館だより 5月号 No.25 金生山の大きな貝化石 |url=http://www2.og-bunka.or.jp/lsc/lsc-upfile/columnPdf/00/06/6_1_file.pdf |script-website=ja:[[:ja:金生山化石館|金生山化石館]]}}</ref> * The longest [[Ostreida|ostreid]] is ''[[Konbostrea]]'', with shell height reaching up to {{cvt|1.2|m}}.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=CHINZEI |first=K. |date=1986 |title=Shell structure, growth, and functional morphology of an elongate Cretaceous oyster. |url=https://www.palass.org/publications/palaeontology-journal/archive/29/1/article_pp139-154 |journal=Palaeontology |volume=29 |issue=1 |pages=139–154}}</ref> * [[Rudists|Rudist]] ''[[Titanosarcolites]]'' had overall size around {{cvt|2|m}}.<ref name="Worthy-2019" />
=== Tusk shells (Scaphopoda) ===
* Complete shell length of [[tusk shell]] ''[[Prodentalium|Prodentalium onoi]]'' is estimated to be over {{cvt|30|cm}}.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Asato |first1=Kaito |last2=Kase |first2=Tomoki |title=Gigantic scaphopods (Mollusca) from the Permian Akasaka Limestone, central Japan |journal=Journal of Paleontology |date=July 2021 |volume=95 |issue=4 |pages=748–762 |doi=10.1017/jpa.2021.3 |bibcode=2021JPal...95..748A }}</ref>
=== Cephalopods (Cephalopoda) === {{Main|Cephalopod size}} [[File:Parapuzosia seppenradensis cast.jpg|thumb|''[[Parapuzosia seppenradensis]]'' shell]]
==== Nautiloids (Nautiloidea) ==== The largest and longest known of [[Nautiloidea|nautiloids]] was ''[[Endoceras|Endoceras giganteum]]'' with a shell length of {{cvt|5.73|m}}. There is a record of an individual whose shell length had reached {{cvt|9.14|m}}, but it is doubtful.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Klug |first1=Christian |last2=De Baets |first2=Kenneth |last3=Kröger |first3=Björn |last4=Bell |first4=Mark A. |last5=Korn |first5=Dieter |last6=Payne |first6=Jonathan L. |title=Normal giants? Temporal and latitudinal shifts of Palaeozoic marine invertebrate gigantism and global change |journal=Lethaia |date=April 2015 |volume=48 |issue=2 |pages=267–288 |doi=10.1111/let.12104 |bibcode=2015Letha..48..267K }}</ref>
==== Ammonites (Ammonoidea) ==== The largest known [[ammonite]] was ''[[Parapuzosia seppenradensis]]''.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Payne |first1=Jonathan L. |last2=Boyer |first2=Alison G. |last3=Brown |first3=James H. |last4=Finnegan |first4=Seth |last5=Kowalewski |first5=Michał |last6=Krause |first6=Richard A. |last7=Lyons |first7=S. Kathleen |last8=McClain |first8=Craig R. |last9=McShea |first9=Daniel W. |last10=Novack-Gottshall |first10=Philip M. |last11=Smith |first11=Felisa A. |last12=Stempien |first12=Jennifer A. |last13=Wang |first13=Steve C. |title=Two-phase increase in the maximum size of life over 3.5 billion years reflects biological innovation and environmental opportunity |journal=Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences |date=6 January 2009 |volume=106 |issue=1 |pages=24–27 |doi=10.1073/pnas.0806314106 |pmid=19106296 |pmc=2607246 |bibcode=2009PNAS..106...24P |doi-access=free }}</ref> A partial fossil specimen found in [[Germany]] had a shell diameter of {{cvt|1.95|m}}, but the [[living chamber]] was incomplete, so the estimated shell diameter was probably about {{cvt|3.5|m}} and weighed about {{cvt|705|kg|lb}} when it was alive.<ref>Teichert, C. & B. Kummel 1960. [https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/3199542 Size of endoceroid cephalopods]. ''Breviora Museum of Comparative Zoology'' '''128''': 1–7.</ref> However, a later study estimates shell diameter up to around {{cvt|2|m}}.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Ifrim |first1=Christina |last2=Stinnesbeck |first2=Wolfgang |last3=González |first3=Arturo H. González |last4=Schorndorf |first4=Nils |last5=Gale |first5=Andrew S. |date=10 November 2021 |title=Ontogeny, evolution and palaeogeographic distribution of the world's largest ammonite Parapuzosia (P.) seppenradensis (Landois, 1895) |journal=PLOS ONE |volume=16 |issue=11 |article-number=e0258510 |doi=10.1371/journal.pone.0258510 |pmc=8580234 |pmid=34758037 |bibcode=2021PLoSO..1658510I |doi-access=free }}</ref>
==== Belemnites (Belemnoidea) ==== The largest known [[belemnite]] was ''[[Megateuthis gigantea]]'', reaching about {{cvt|50|and|700|mm}} in maximum diameter and length of rostrum, respectively.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Iba |first1=Yasuhiro |last2=Sano |first2=Shin-Ichi |last3=Goto |first3=Michiharu |title=Large Belemnites were Already Common in the Early Jurassic—New Evidence from Central Japan |journal=Paleontological Research |date=January 2015 |volume=19 |issue=1 |pages=21–25 |doi=10.2517/2014PR025|bibcode=2015PalRe..19...21I |s2cid=55001872 }}</ref>
==== Squids, octopuses, cuttlefishes and allies (Neocoleoidea) ====
* A specimen of [[Octopus|Octopod]], a member of family [[Muensterellidae]] and [[Enchoteuthinae]], ''[[Enchoteuthis|Enchoteuthis melanae]]'' (NDGS 241) which was previously attributed to ''[[Tusoteuthis|Tusoteuthis longa]]'' (another more dubious member), had a mantle length of up to {{convert|2|m}}, comparable to the modern-day [[giant squid]]. Previously, this taxon was considered similar to the giant squid, with total length including arms over {{convert|10|m}}. However, considering other fossil relatives, the total length including arms is estimated to be less than {{convert|3|m}}, to be realistic and reasonable.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Fuchs |first1=Dirk |last2=Iba |first2=Yasuhiro |last3=Heyng |first3=Alexander |last4=Iijima |first4=Masaya |last5=Klug |first5=Christian |last6=Larson |first6=Neal L. |last7=Schweigert |first7=Günter |title=The Muensterelloidea: phylogeny and character evolution of Mesozoic stem octopods |journal=Papers in Palaeontology |date=February 2020 |volume=6 |issue=1 |pages=31–92 |doi=10.1002/spp2.1254 |bibcode=2020PPal....6...31F }}</ref> * Both non-octopod ''[[Yezoteuthis]]'' and [[Squid|teuthid]] ''[[Haboroteuthis]]'' are estimated to be similar in size to the modern-day [[giant squid]].<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Tanabe |first1=Kazushige |last2=Hikida |first2=Yoshinori |last3=Iba |first3=Yasuhiro |title=Two Coleoid Jaws from the Upper Cretaceous of Hokkaido, Japan |journal=Journal of Paleontology |date=January 2006 |volume=80 |issue=1 |pages=138–145 |doi=10.1666/0022-3360(2006)080[0138:TCJFTU]2.0.CO;2 }}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Tanabe |first1=Kazushige |last2=Misaki |first2=Akihiro |last3=Ubukata |first3=Takao |date=2014 |title=Late Cretaceous record of large soft-bodied coleoids based on lower jaw remains from Hokkaido, Japan |journal=Acta Palaeontologica Polonica |language=en |doi=10.4202/app.00052.2013 |doi-access=free }}</ref> Incomplete jaw specimen from [[Yezo Group]] (KMNH IvP 902007) is twice as large as those of ''Yezotuethis''.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Tanabe |first1=Kazushige |last2=Misaki |first2=Akihiro |date=1 November 2023 |title=Upper Cretaceous record of non-belemnitid coleoid jaws from Hokkaido, Japan, and its evolutionary implications |url=https://linkinghub.elsevier.com/retrieve/pii/S0195667123001520 |journal=Cretaceous Research |volume=151 |article-number=105624 |doi=10.1016/j.cretres.2023.105624 |bibcode=2023CrRes.15105624T |issn=0195-6671|url-access=subscription }}</ref>
== Brachiopods (Brachiopoda) == [[File:Gigantoproductus giganteus Carbon GB.jpg|thumb|''[[Gigantoproductus giganteus]]'']] The largest [[brachiopod]] ever evolved was ''[[Striatifera|Striatifera striata]]'' from [[Akkermanovka Quarry]], [[Russia]], with height up to {{convert|0.5|m}}.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Mychko |first1=Eduard V. |last2=Feldmann |first2=Rodney M. |last3=Schweitzer |first3=Carrie E. |last4=Alekseev |first4=Alexander S. |title=New genus of Cyclida (Crustacea) from Lower Carboniferous (Mississippian, Viséan) of Russia and England and new species from Viséan of Russia |journal=Neues Jahrbuch für Geologie und Paläontologie - Abhandlungen |date=25 October 2019 |volume=294 |issue=1 |pages=81–90 |doi=10.1127/njgpa/2019/0847 |bibcode=2019NJGPA.294...81M }}</ref> Another huge brachiopod was the [[Carboniferous]] ''[[Gigantoproductus giganteus]]'', with shell width from {{cvt|30|cm}}<ref>{{cite book | url = https://books.google.com/books?id=bL60DwAAQBAJ&dq=largest+Gigantoproductus+giganteus&pg=PA47 | title = Convergent Evolution on Earth. Lessons for the Search for Extraterrestrial Life | publisher = MIT Press | date = 2019 | access-date = 23 August 2022 | page = 47 | author = George R. McGhee, Jr. | isbn = 978-0-262-35418-9 }}</ref> to over {{convert|35|cm}}.<ref name="Worthy-2019">{{Cite journal |last=Vermeij |first=Geerat J. |date=15 January 2016 |title=Gigantism and Its Implications for the History of Life |journal=PLOS ONE |volume=11 |issue=1 |article-number=e0146092 |doi=10.1371/journal.pone.0146092 |pmc=4714876 |pmid=26771527 |bibcode=2016PLoSO..1146092V |doi-access=free }}</ref><ref>{{cite book | url = https://books.google.com/books?id=mGLwAAAAMAAJ&q=Gigantoproductus+35+cm | title = Fossils of the World. A Comprehensive Practical Guide to Collecting and Studying Fossils | publisher = Arch Cape Press | date = 1989 | access-date = 23 August 2022 | page = 146 |author=Vojtěch Turek |author2=J. Marek |author3=Josef Beneš | isbn = 978-0-517-67904-3 }}</ref> ''[[Titanaria|Titanaria costellata]]'' had large and long shell {{cvt|35|-|36|cm}} in width, nearly as large as ''Gigantoproductus''.<ref>{{cite book | url = https://books.google.com/books?id=19TxCc6lglwC&dq=Gigantoproductus+35+cm&pg=PA334 | title = Morphology, Classification and Life Habits of the Productoidea (Brachiopoda) | publisher = Geological Society of America | date = 1960 | access-date = 23 August 2022 | page = 334 | author = Helen Marguerite Muir-Wood |author2=Gustav Arthur Cooper }}</ref>
== Hyoliths (Hyolitha) == The largest [[hyolith]] is ''[[Macrotheca almgreeni]]'', with length about {{convert|50|cm}}.<ref name="Worthy-2019" /><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Peel |first1=John S. |last2=Yochelson |first2=Ellis L. |title=Permian Toxeumorphorida from Greenland: an appraisal of the molluscan class Xenoconchia |journal=Lethaia |date=July 1984 |volume=17 |issue=3 |pages=211–221 |doi=10.1111/j.1502-3931.1984.tb01619.x |bibcode=1984Letha..17..211P }}</ref>
== Cnidarians (Cnidaria) == === Jellyfishes and allies (Medusozoa) === The largest fossil [[jellyfish]] is [[Cambrian]] ''[[Cordubia|Cordubia gigantea]]'', with diameter of {{convert|88|cm}}.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Mayoral |first1=Eduardo |last2=Liñán |first2=Eladio |last3=Vintaned |first3=José Antonio Gámez |last4=Muñiz |first4=Fernando |last5=Gozalo |first5=Rodolfo |date=3 March 2021 |title=Stranded jellyfish in the lowermost Cambrian (Corduban) of Spain |journal=Spanish Journal of Palaeontology |volume=19 |issue=2 |pages=191–198 |doi=10.7203/sjp.19.2.20531 |s2cid=126508499 |doi-access=free }}</ref> Specimens from the Cambrian of [[Wisconsin]] reached {{cvt|70|cm}} in length.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Clarke |first1=Tom |title=Jellies roll back time |journal=Nature |date=30 January 2002 |doi=10.1038/news020128-5 }}</ref>
== Vendobionts (Vendobionta) == {{Further|Vendobionta}}
=== Petalonamids (Petalonamae) === [[File:Trepassia wardae fossil.png|thumb| A large specimen of ''[[Trepassia|Trepassia wardae]]'']] Longest specimens of ''[[Trepassia|Trepassia wardae]]'' (also known as ''Charnia wardi'') reached {{cvt|185|cm}} in length.<ref name=LAFLAMME2016>{{cite journal |last1=Laflamme |first1=M. |last2=Narbonne |first2=G. M. |last3=Greentree |first3=C. |last4=Anderson |first4=M. M. |title=Morphology and taphonomy of an Ediacaran frond: Charnia from the Avalon Peninsula of Newfoundland |journal=Geological Society, London, Special Publications |date=January 2007 |volume=286 |issue=1 |pages=237–257 |doi=10.1144/sp286.17 |bibcode=2007GSLSP.286..237L }}</ref> ''[[Charnia|Charnia masoni]]'' is known from specimens as small as only {{cvt|1|cm}}, up to the largest specimens of {{cvt|66|cm}} in length.<ref>{{cite journal | doi=10.1002/spp2.1234 | title=Anatomy of the Ediacaran rangeomorph ''Charnia masoni'' | year=2019 | last1=Dunn | first1=Frances S. | last2=Wilby | first2=Philip R. | last3=Kenchington | first3=Charlotte G. | last4=Grazhdankin | first4=Dmitriy V. | last5=Donoghue | first5=Philip C. J. | last6=Liu | first6=Alexander G. | journal=Papers in Palaeontology | volume=5 | issue=1 | pages=157–176 | pmid=31007942 | pmc=6472560 | bibcode=2019PPal....5..157D }}</ref>
=== Proarticulata === ''[[Dickinsonia|Dickinsonia tenuis]]'' reached {{cvt|1.4|m|ft}} in length, that makes it one of the largest [[precambrian]] organisms.<ref name=Jenkins1992> {{Cite book | author=Jenkins, R.J.F. | year=1992 | chapter=Functional and ecological aspects of Ediacarian assemblages | editor1=Lipps, J. | editor2=Signor, P.W. | title=Origin and early evolution of the Metazoa | pages=131–176 | location=New York, NY | publisher=Springer | isbn=978-0-306-44067-0 | oclc=231467647 | chapter-url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gUQMKiJOj64C&pg=PA165 }} </ref><ref>{{cite book |author1=Fedonkin M. A. |author2=Gehling J. G. |author3=Grey K. |author4=Narbonne G. M. |author5=Vickers-Rich P. |year=2007 | url=https://books.google.com/books?id=OFKG6SmPNuUC | title=The Rise of Animals. Evolution and Diversification of the Kingdom Animalia | publisher=Johns Hopkins University Press | page=326 | isbn=978-0-8018-8679-9}}</ref>
== Sponges (Porifera) == The largest known [[Permian]] sponge ''[[Gigantospongia]]'' had diameter up to {{convert|2.5|m}}.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Rigby |first1=J. Keith |last2=Senowbari-Daryan |first2=Baba |date=1996 |title=Gigantospongia, New Genus, the Largest Known Permian Sponge, Capitan Limestone, Guadalupe Mountains, New Mexico |journal=Journal of Paleontology |volume=70 |issue=3 |pages=347–355 |doi=10.1017/S0022336000038294 |jstor=1306431 |bibcode=1996JPal...70..347R |s2cid=130222477 }}</ref>

({{IPAc-en|l|ə|ˈ|b|r|ɒ|n}}{{cite web |title=LeBron James #ScoringKing Ceremony In LA 👑 {{!}} February 9, 2023 |url=https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sCpyAMhHhqo&t=11s |website=[[YouTube]] |publisher=[[National Basketball Association]] |access-date=July 29, 2024 |date=February 10, 2023}} {{respelling|lə|BRON}}; born December 30, 1984) is an American professional [[basketball]] player for the [[Los Angeles Lakers]] of the [[National Basketball Association]] (NBA). Nicknamed "'''King James'''", he is the [[List of NBA career scoring leaders|NBA's all-time leading scorer]] and has won four [[List of NBA champions|NBA championships]] from 10 [[NBA Finals]] appearances, having made eight consecutive appearances between 2011 and 2018.{{#invoke:cite|web|title=LeBron James Bio|url=https://www.nba.com/player/2544/lebron-james/bio|website=NBA.com|date=January 1, 2021|access-date=April 15, 2022|archive-date=April 15, 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220415214923/https://www.nba.com/player/2544/lebron-james/bio|url-status=live }} He also won the inaugural [[NBA Cup]] in 2023 with the Lakers and has won three [[Basketball at the Summer Olympics|Olympic gold medals]] as a member of the [[United States men's national basketball team|U.S. national team]]. James is widely considered one of the greatest basketball players of all time.{{Cite news |last=Staff |first=The Athletic NBA |date=2025-06-10 |title=NBA 75: Top 75 NBA players of all time, from MJ and LeBron to Lenny Wilkens |url=https://www.nytimes.com/athletic/3137873/2022/02/23/the-nba-75-the-top-75-nba-players-of-all-time-from-mj-and-lebron-to-lenny-wilkens/ |access-date=2025-06-23 |work=The New York Times |language=en-US |issn=0362-4331}}{{Cite web |url =https://www.si.com/nba/2016/02/08/michael-jordan-lebron-james-stephen-curry-nba-greatest#gid=ci02556958e0022580&pid=50--paul-arizin |title =SI's 50 greatest players in NBA history |author =Jack McCallum |date =February 8, 2016 |publisher =[[Sports illustrated]] |accessdate =2024-03-29 |archiveurl =https://web.archive.org/web/20240329124115/https://www.si.com/nba/2016/02/08/michael-jordan-lebron-james-stephen-curry-nba-greatest#gid=ci02556958e0022580&pid=50--paul-arizin |archivedate =2024-03-29}} In addition to ranking [[List of NBA career assists leaders|fourth in NBA career assists]] and [[List of NBA career steals leaders|sixth in NBA career steals]], James holds several individual honors, including four [[NBA MVP]] awards, four [[NBA Finals Most Valuable Player|Finals MVP]] awards, the [[NBA Rookie of the Year|Rookie of the Year]] award, three [[NBA All-Star Game Kobe Bryant Most Valuable Player Award|All-Star Game MVP]] awards, the inaugural NBA Cup MVP, and the [[FIBA Men's Olympics Most Valuable Player|Olympics MVP]] in the [[Basketball at the 2024 Summer Olympics – Men's tournament|2024 Summer Olympics]]. A record 21-time [[NBA All-Star|All-Star]] and 21-time [[All-NBA Team|All-NBA selection]] (including a record 13 First Team selections), he has also made six [[NBA All-Defensive First Team|All-Defensive Teams]]. The [[List of oldest and youngest NBA players|oldest active player in the NBA]], he is tied with [[Vince Carter]] for the [[List of NBA seasons played leaders|most seasons played]] and holds the record for the [[List of NBA career minutes played leaders|most minutes played in league history]]. Born and raised in [[Akron, Ohio]], James gained national attention at [[St. Vincent–St. Mary High School]] and was heavily touted as a future NBA superstar for his all-around scoring, passing, athleticism and playmaking abilities.{{#invoke:cite|news|title=Ohio High School Junior LeBron James Is So Good That He's Already Being Mentioned as the Heir to Air Jordan|url=https://vault.si.com/vault/2002/02/18/ahead-of-his-class-ohio-high-school-junior-lebron-james-is-so-good-that-hes-already-being-mentioned-as-the-heir-to-air-jordan|newspaper=Sports Illustrated Vault | Si.com|date=February 18, 2002|access-date=July 1, 2023|archive-date=July 2, 2023|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230702103936/https://vault.si.com/vault/2002/02/18/ahead-of-his-class-ohio-high-school-junior-lebron-james-is-so-good-that-hes-already-being-mentioned-as-the-heir-to-air-jordan|url-status=live}} A [[prep-to-pro]], James was selected by the [[Cleveland Cavaliers]] with the [[List of first overall NBA draft picks|first overall pick]] of the [[2003 NBA draft]]. He won Rookie of the Year and quickly established himself as one of the league's premier players, leading Cleveland to its first NBA Finals appearance in [[2007 NBA Finals|2007]] and winning the [[List of National Basketball Association annual scoring leaders|scoring title]] in 2008. After winning back-to-back MVPs in 2009 and 2010, he left the Cavaliers and joined the [[Miami Heat]] as a [[Free agent (NBA)|free agent]] in 2010, a controversial move announced in the nationally televised special titled ''[[The Decision (TV program)|The Decision]]''.{{#invoke:cite|news|first=Tom|last=Withers|title=AP Was There: LeBron James dumps Cleveland for Miami|url=https://apnews.com/article/miami-heat-cleveland-cavaliers-nba-entertainment-sports-c842cbbffbd42a55ed8374fd1e1b4a93|work=AP News|date=April 6, 2022|access-date=April 15, 2022|archive-date=April 15, 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220415214923/https://apnews.com/article/miami-heat-cleveland-cavaliers-nba-entertainment-sports-c842cbbffbd42a55ed8374fd1e1b4a93|url-status=live}} With the Heat, James won his first two NBA championships in [[2012 NBA Finals|2012]] and [[2013 NBA Finals|2013]], earning MVP and Finals MVP honors both years. After four seasons in Miami, he returned to Cleveland in 2014, leading the Cavaliers to their first-ever championship in [[2016 NBA Finals|2016]] by [[List of teams that have overcome 3–1 series deficits#National Basketball Association|overcoming a 3–1 deficit]] against the [[Golden State Warriors]] and ending the [[Cleveland sports curse]]. He signed with the Lakers in 2018, winning another title in [[2020 NBA Finals|2020]] and becoming the first player to win Finals MVP with three different teams. In 2023, he surpassed [[Kareem Abdul-Jabbar]] to become the NBA's all-time leading scorer, and in 2024, he and his son [[Bronny James|Bronny]] became the first father-son teammates in league history. In 2025, James was inducted into the [[Naismith Memorial Basketball Hall of Fame]] as a member of the [[2008 United States men's Olympic basketball team|2008 U.S. Olympic team]] (also known as the "Redeem Team"). He and [[Chris Paul]] became the first NBA players inducted into the Hall of Fame while still active.{{cite web |title=LeBron James and Chris Paul to make Hall of Fame history as active players |url=https://basketnews.com/news-222457-lebron-james-and-chris-paul-to-make-hall-of-fame-history-as-active-players.html |website=BasketNews.com |access-date=31 July 2025 |date=6 April 2025}} Off the court, James has earned further wealth and fame from numerous endorsement contracts. He is the first player in NBA history to accumulate $1 billion in earnings as an active player.{{#invoke:cite|web|first=Jasmyn|last=Wimbish|title=LeBron James becomes first player in NBA history to make $1 billion in earnings while still playing|url=https://www.cbssports.com/nba/news/lebron-james-becomes-first-player-in-nba-history-to-make-1-billion-in-earnings-while-still-playing/|work=CBS Sports|date=July 22, 2021|access-date=July 24, 2021|archive-date=July 25, 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210725082200/https://www.cbssports.com/nba/news/lebron-james-becomes-first-player-in-nba-history-to-make-1-billion-in-earnings-while-still-playing/|url-status=live}} James has been featured in books, documentaries (including winning three [[Sports Emmy Award]]s as an executive producer), and television commercials. He was among ''[[Time (magazine)|Time]]''{{'}}s [[Time 100|100 most influential people in the world]] in 2005, 2013, 2017, and 2019 — the most selections for a professional athlete. James has won 20 [[ESPY Awards]], hosted ''[[Saturday Night Live]]'', and starred in the sports film ''[[Space Jam: A New Legacy]]'' (2021). He has been a part-owner of [[Liverpool F.C.]] since 2011 and leads the LeBron James Family Foundation, which has opened an [[Elementary schools in the United States|elementary school]], housing complex, retail plaza, and medical center in Akron.{{cite magazine |first=Sean |last=Gregory |title=Exclusive: LeBron James to Open New Community Hub in Akron |url=https://time.com/5921157/lebron-james-house-three-thirty-akron/ |magazine=Time |date=December 15, 2020 |access-date=May 19, 2021 |archive-date=October 15, 2021 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211015222042/https://time.com/5921157/lebron-james-house-three-thirty-akron/ |url-status=live}}{{#invoke:cite|news|title=LeBron James foundation to open new medical center|url=https://fox8.com/news/lebron-james-foundation-to-open-new-medical-center/|work=Fox 8.com (WJW-TV)|date=June 7, 2022|access-date=June 7, 2022|archive-date=June 7, 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220607132338/https://fox8.com/news/lebron-james-foundation-to-open-new-medical-center/|url-status=live }} ==Early life== LeBron Raymone James was born on December 30, 1984, in [[Akron, Ohio]], to Gloria Marie James, who was only 16 at the time of his birth.{{#invoke:cite|book|last=Jones|first=Ryan|title=King James: Believe the Hype|year=2003|publisher=St. Martin's Griffin|location=New York|isbn=978-0-312-34992-9|url=https://archive.org/details/kingjames00ryan}}{{rp|22}} His father, Anthony McClelland, has an extensive criminal record that included [[arson]] and [[theft]] and was not involved in his life.{{#invoke:cite|news|last=Donegan|first=Lawrence|date=March 2, 2003|title=America's most wanted|url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2003/mar/02/ussport|url-status=live|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210422224142/https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2003/mar/02/ussport|archive-date=April 22, 2021|access-date=May 29, 2021|newspaper=The Guardian}}{{#invoke:cite|web|first=Tom|last=Friend|title=NEXT: LeBron James|date=December 10, 2002|work=ESPN The Magazine|url=https://www.espn.com/magazine/vol5no26next.html|access-date=March 16, 2023|archive-date=March 31, 2023|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230331011146/https://www.espn.com/magazine/vol5no26next.html|url-status=live}}{{#invoke:cite|web|title=LeBron James Fast Facts|work=CNN|date=July 7, 2014|url=https://www.cnn.com/2014/07/07/sport/lebron-james-fast-facts/index.html|access-date=March 16, 2023|archive-date=April 4, 2023|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230404144528/https://www.cnn.com/2014/07/07/sport/lebron-james-fast-facts/index.html|url-status=live}}{{Cite web |title=Who is LeBron James dad and what happened to him? |url=https://basketnews.com/news-219527-lebron-james-dad.html |access-date=2025-08-04 |website=basketnews.com |language=en}} When James was growing up, life was often a struggle for the family, as they moved from apartment to apartment in the seedier neighborhoods of Akron while Gloria struggled to find steady work.{{#invoke:cite|web|title=LeBron James Biography |url=http://www.jockbio.com/Bios/James/James_bio.html |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120224182236/http://www.jockbio.com/Bios/James/James_bio.html|archive-date=February 24, 2012|access-date=May 23, 2010|website=JockBio.com}} Realizing that her son would be better off in a more stable family environment, Gloria allowed him to move in with the family of Frank Walker, a local youth football coach who introduced James to basketball when he was nine years old.{{rp|23}} James began playing organized basketball in the fifth grade.{{#invoke:cite|web|last=Nichols|first=Rachel|date=July 30, 2018|title=LeBron James opens up on his new school, the Lakers and life's challenges|url=http://www.espn.com/nba/story/_/id/24194051/lebron-james-discusses-opening-public-school-akron-move-los-angeles-lakers-nba|access-date=July 31, 2018|work=ESPN.com|archive-date=December 28, 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211228025705/https://www.espn.com/nba/story/_/id/24194051/lebron-james-discusses-opening-public-school-akron-move-los-angeles-lakers-nba|url-status=live}} He later played [[Amateur Athletic Union]] (AAU) basketball for the Northeast Ohio Shooting Stars. The team enjoyed success on a local and national level, led by James and his friends Sian Cotton, [[Dru Joyce III]], and Willie McGee.{{rp|24}} The group dubbed themselves the "Fab Four" and promised each other that they would attend high school together.{{rp|27}} In a move that stirred local controversy, they chose to attend [[St. Vincent–St. Mary High School]], a private [[Catholic school]] with predominantly white students.{{#invoke:cite|news|last=Covert|first=Colin|title=Movie review: Band of brothers in 'More Than a Game'|url=http://www.startribune.com/entertainment/movies/64387712.html?elr=KArksD:aDyaEP:kD:aUnc5PDiUiD3aPc:_Yyc:aULPQL7PQLanchO7DiUr|newspaper=Star Tribune|access-date=July 1, 2012|date=October 15, 2009|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131224102803/http://www.startribune.com/entertainment/movies/64387712.html?elr=KArksD:aDyaEP:kD:aUnc5PDiUiD3aPc:_Yyc:aULPQL7PQLanchO7DiUr|archive-date=December 24, 2013|url-status=dead}} ==High school== ===Basketball=== [[File:LeBron_James_high_school_2003.jpg|thumb|right|James playing for the St. Vincent–St. Mary Fighting Irish on January 20, 2003.]] As a {{convert|6|ft|2|in|m|adj=on}} tall freshman, James averaged 18 [[point (basketball)|points]] and 6.2 [[rebound (basketball)|rebounds]] per game for the [[St. Vincent–St. Mary High School|St. Vincent–St. Mary]] varsity basketball team.{{#invoke:cite|web|title=Prospect Profile: LeBron James|url=http://www.nba.com/draft2003/profiles/JamesLeBron.html|website=NBA.com|access-date=March 4, 2013|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130709135603/http://www.nba.com/draft2003/profiles/JamesLeBron.html|archive-date=July 9, 2013|url-status=dead}} The Fighting Irish went 27–0 en route to the Division III state title, making them the only boys high school team in Ohio to finish the season undefeated.{{#invoke:cite|news|last1=Gaffney|first1=Tom|last2=Morgan|first2=David Lee|date=March 26, 2000|title=Winning one for Carter|page=D8|work=Akron Beacon Journal|url=https://www.newspapers.com/clip/21535260/lebron_james_hs_freshman/|via=Newspapers.com|access-date=July 3, 2018|archive-date=July 4, 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180704093259/https://www.newspapers.com/clip/21535260/lebron_james_hs_freshman/|url-status=live}} {{free access}} As a sophomore, James averaged 25.3 points and 7.4 rebounds, along with 5.5 [[Assist (basketball)|assists]] and 3.7 [[Steal (basketball)|steals]] per game.
