At 15, he won the [[Norwegian Chess Championship]], and later became the youngest ever player to qualify for the [[Candidates Tournament]] in 2005.<ref name=":3">{{Cite web |last=Svensen |first=Tarjei J. |date=2024-02-16 |title=The 7 Most Mindblowing Magnus Carlsen Records |url=https://www.chess.com/article/view/7-most-mindblowing-magnus-carlsen-records |access-date=2024-02-16 |website=Chess.com |language=en-US |archive-date=17 February 2024 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240217000110/https://www.chess.com/article/view/7-most-mindblowing-magnus-carlsen-records |url-status=live }}</ref> At 17, he finished joint first in the [[Corus 2008 chess tournament|top group of Corus]]. He surpassed a rating of 2800 at 18, the youngest at the time to do so. In 2010, at 19, he reached {{Numero|1}} in the FIDE world rankings, the youngest person ever to do so.
Magnus Carlsen became World Chess Champion in [[World Chess Championship 2013|2013]] by defeating [[Viswanathan Anand]]. He [[World Chess Championship 2014|retained his title]] against Anand the following year and won both the 2014 World Rapid Championship and World Blitz Championship, becoming the first player to hold all three titles simultaneously, a feat which he repeated in 2019 and 2022.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Barden |first=Leonard |date=30 December 2022 |title=Chess: Magnus Carlsen captures double world crown in Rapid and Blitz |url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2022/dec/30/chess-carlsen-triumphs-in-world-rapid-then-sprints-to-avoid-blitz-time-forfeit |access-date=30 December 2022 |website=[[The Guardian]] |archive-date=20 May 2023 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230520052728/https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2022/dec/30/chess-carlsen-triumphs-in-world-rapid-then-sprints-to-avoid-blitz-time-forfeit |url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |date=30 December 2022 |title=World Blitz: Carlsen clinches triple crown, Assaubayeva defends her title |url=https://en.chessbase.com/post/world-rapid-blitz-ch-2022-live-blitz-2 |access-date=31 December 2022 |website=Chess News |language=en |archive-date=30 December 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221230103656/https://en.chessbase.com/post/world-rapid-blitz-ch-2022-live-blitz-2 |url-status=live}}</ref> He defended his classical world title against [[Sergey Karjakin]] in [[World Chess Championship 2016|2016]], [[Fabiano Caruana]] in [[World Chess Championship 2018|2018]], and [[Ian Nepomniachtchi]] in [[World Chess Championship 2021|2021]]. Carlsen declined to defend his title in [[World Chess Championship 2023|2023]], citing a lack of motivation.<ref name=Lourim>{{cite web |last1=Lourim |first1=Jake |title=Magnus Carlsen Is Giving Up The World Title. But The Carlsen Era Lives On. |url=https://fivethirtyeight.com/features/magnus-carlsen-is-giving-up-the-world-title-but-the-carlsen-era-lives-on/ |website=FiveThirtyEight |date=26 July 2022 |access-date=12 September 2022 |archive-date=26 July 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220726145423/https://fivethirtyeight.com/features/magnus-carlsen-is-giving-up-the-world-title-but-the-carlsen-era-lives-on/ |url-status=live}}</ref>
Known for his attacking style as a teenager, Carlsen has since developed into a universal player. He uses a variety of [[chess opening|openings]] to make it harder for opponents to prepare against him and reduce the utility of pre-game [[Chess engine|computer analysis]].<ref>{{Cite web|author=Leonard Barden|title=Magnus Carlsen: the greatest of all time or too much of a grinder?|url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2014/feb/28/magnus-carlsen-record-rating-critics|access-date=20 August 2023|website=The Guardian|date=1 March 2014|archive-date=20 August 2023|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230820164954/https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2014/feb/28/magnus-carlsen-record-rating-critics|url-status=live}}</ref> Carlsen has stated that the [[chess middlegame|middlegame]] is his favourite part of the game as it comes down to "pure chess".<ref name=CNRU/>
{{TOC limit|3}}
== Childhood ==
Carlsen was born in [[Tønsberg]], Norway, on 30 November 1990<ref name=snl>{{cite encyclopedia |title=Magnus Carlsen |first1=Atle |last1=Grønn |first2=Håkon Martol |last2=Briseid |encyclopedia=[[Store norske leksikon]] |editor-last=Bolstad |editor-first=Erik |publisher=Norsk nettleksikon |location=Oslo |url=https://snl.no/Magnus_Carlsen |date=14 February 2009 |language=no |access-date=27 October 2019 |archive-date=26 April 2020 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200426025018/https://snl.no/Magnus_Carlsen |url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.aftenposten.no/sport/i/dOGygj/sjakkekspertens-forklaring-paa-carlsens-sterke-vaar-caruana-spilte-ham-god |title=Sjakkekspertens forklaring på Carlsens sterke vår: – Caruana spilte ham god |newspaper=Aftenposten |date=13 June 2019 |last=Haugli |first=Kurt B.M. |language=no |access-date=27 October 2019 |archive-date=27 October 2019 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20191027161831/https://www.aftenposten.no/sport/i/dOGygj/sjakkekspertens-forklaring-paa-carlsens-sterke-vaar-caruana-spilte-ham-god |url-status=live}}</ref> to Sigrun Øen (1963–2024), a [[chemical engineer]], and Henrik Albert Carlsen, an [[Information technology consulting|IT consultant]].<ref name=NYT2008/> The family spent one year in [[Espoo]], Finland, and then in [[Brussels]], Belgium, before returning to [[Norway]] in 1998, where they lived in [[Lommedalen]], Bærum. They later moved to [[Haslum]].<ref>{{cite book |author=Danielsen, Arne |title=Mesteren: Magnus Carlsen og sjakkspillet |year=2010 |publisher=Cappelen Damm |location=Oslo |page=27 |language=no |isbn=978-82-02-33754-4 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=FdyrtQEACAAJ |access-date=24 July 2018 |archive-date=19 June 2024 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240619052012/https://books.google.com/books?id=FdyrtQEACAAJ |url-status=live }}</ref> Carlsen showed an aptitude for intellectual challenges at a young age. At two years, he could solve 50-piece [[jigsaw puzzle]]s; at four, he enjoyed assembling [[Lego]] sets with instructions intended for children aged 10–14.<ref>Agdestein (2004), p. 10.</ref>
His father, a keen amateur chess player,<ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.ft.com/content/349d6c32-7589-11e4-a1a9-00144feabdc0 |archive-url=https://ghostarchive.org/archive/20221210/https://www.ft.com/content/349d6c32-7589-11e4-a1a9-00144feabdc0 |archive-date=10 December 2022 |url-status=live |title=Magnus Carlsen, an unlikely chess master|author =<!-- no byline -->|website =[[Financial Times]] |date =28 November 2014 |url-access=subscription}}</ref> taught him to play at the age of five, although he initially showed little interest in it.<ref name="New Yorker 2011">{{cite magazine |author=Max, D.T. |url=http://archives.newyorker.com/?i=2011-03-21#folio=040 |title=The prince's gambit: A chess star emerges for the post-computer age |magazine=The New Yorker |date=21 March 2011 |access-date=4 January 2013 |archive-date=2 October 2018 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20181002141900/http://archives.newyorker.com/?i=2011-03-21#folio=040 |url-status=live }}</ref> Magnus has three sisters; he has said his original motivation to study chess seriously was a desire to be able to beat his elder sister.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.telegraph.co.uk/news/celebritynews/my-perfect-weekend/8177372/My-perfect-weekend-Magnus-Carlsen.html |archive-url=https://ghostarchive.org/archive/20220110/https://www.telegraph.co.uk/news/celebritynews/my-perfect-weekend/8177372/My-perfect-weekend-Magnus-Carlsen.html |archive-date=10 January 2022 |url-access=subscription |url-status=live|title=My perfect weekend: Magnus Carlsen|first=Max|last=Davidson|website=The Telegraph|date=2 December 2010}}{{cbignore}}</ref>
[[File:Magnus Carlsen simultan-dscn0443-2.jpeg|thumb|left|Carlsen, aged 13, in [[Molde (town)|Molde]] giving a [[simultaneous exhibition]], July 2004]] Carlsen developed his early chess skills by playing alone. Carlsen had an exceptional memory and could recall the locations, populations, flags and capitals of all the countries in the world by the age of five.<ref>{{cite book |author=Agdestein, Simen |title=Hvordan Magnus Carlsen ble verdensmester | year=2014 |publisher=NKI forlaget |location=Oslo |page=189}}</ref> He participated in his first tournament—the youngest division of the 1999 [[Norwegian Chess Championship]]—at 8 years and 7 months, and scored 6/11.<ref>Agdestein (2004), p. 189.</ref>
Carlsen was coached at the Norwegian College of Elite Sport by the country's top player, [[Grandmaster (chess)|Grandmaster]] (GM) [[Simen Agdestein]],<ref name=NYT2008>{{cite web |author=McClain, Dylan Loeb |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2008/09/01/world/europe/01iht-profile.4.15806138.html?pagewanted=all&_r=0 |title=Magnus Carlsen, chess prodigy from Norway |work=The New York Times |year=2008 |access-date=4 January 2013 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20181125094343/https://www.nytimes.com/2008/09/01/world/europe/01iht-profile.4.15806138.html?pagewanted=all&_r=0 |archive-date=25 November 2018 |url-status=dead }}</ref> who in turn cites Norwegian [[manager (association football)|football manager]] [[Egil Olsen|Egil "Drillo" Olsen]] as a key inspiration for his coaching strategy.<ref name="Agdestein 2014, p. 36">Agdestein (2014), p. 36</ref> In 2000, Agdestein introduced Carlsen to [[Torbjørn Ringdal Hansen]], a former Norwegian junior champion and later [[International Master]] (IM) and GM,<ref name=FideProfileRingdalHansen>{{cite web |title=FIDE Chess Profile 1501720 Hansen, Torbjorn Ringdal |url=http://ratings.fide.com/card.phtml?event=1501720 |publisher=FIDE |access-date=15 September 2016 |archive-date=6 November 2018 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20181106190130/http://ratings.fide.com/card.phtml?event=1501720 |url-status=live }}</ref> as Ringdal served a one-year ''[[:no:Siviltjeneste i Norge|siviltjeneste]]'' (an [[alternative civilian service]] programme) at the college.{{citation needed|date=April 2025}}
Over the course of that year, Carlsen's rating rose from 904 in June 2000 to 1907. His breakthrough occurred in the Norwegian junior teams championship in September 2000, where he scored 3½/5 against the country's top junior players and gained a {{chessgloss| tournament performance rating}} (TPR) of around 2000.<ref>Agdestein (2004), pp. 16–18; 26.</ref> Apart from chess, which he studied about three to four hours a day, his favourite pastimes included playing [[Association football|football]] and reading [[Donald Duck]] comics.<ref>Agdestein (2004), pp. 78–79.</ref> He also practised [[skiing]] until the age of ten.<ref>Agdestein (2014), p. 17</ref>
From autumn 2000 to the end of 2002, Carlsen played almost 300 rated tournament games, as well as in several blitz tournaments, and participated in other minor events.<ref>Agdestein (2004), p. 80.</ref> In October 2002, he placed sixth in the [[European Youth Chess Championship|European Under-12 Championship]] in [[Peñiscola]].<ref name="European U12 Championship">{{cite web|url=http://www.torneionline.com/loto_tornei_d.php?codice=2002ESP06&tipo=1|title=European U12 Championship|publisher=Italian Chess Federation|access-date=1 September 2015|archive-date=16 May 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200516151116/http://www.torneionline.com/loto_tornei_d.php?codice=2002ESP06&tipo=1|url-status=live}}</ref> The following month, he tied for first place in the [[World Youth Chess Championship|2002 World Under-12 Championship]] in [[Heraklion]], placing second to [[Ian Nepomniachtchi]] on tiebreak.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://brasilbase.pro.br/w12b2002.htm|title=17° World Championship u12 (boys)|publisher=BrasilBase|access-date=1 September 2015|archive-date=6 September 2019|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190906052953/http://brasilbase.pro.br/w12b2002.htm|url-status=live}}</ref> He then obtained three [[Norm (chess)|IM norms]] in relatively quick succession: the first at the January 2003 [[Gausdal Municipality|Gausdal]] Troll Masters (score 7/10, 2453 PR); the second at the June 2003 Salongernas IM-tournament in [[Stockholm]] (6/9, 2470 PR); and the third at the July 2003 [[Politiken Cup]] in [[Copenhagen]] (8/11, 2503 PR). He was officially awarded the IM title on 20 August 2003.<ref>Agdestein (2004), p. 190.</ref>
After finishing primary school, Carlsen took a year off to participate in international chess tournaments in Europe during the autumn of 2003, then returned to complete secondary education at a sports school.<ref>Agdestein (2004), p. 104.</ref><ref>{{cite web|title=Sulky 'Mozart of chess' the new Kasparov|url=http://www.smh.com.au/executive-style/culture/sulky-mozart-of-chess--the-new-kasparov-20100222-or5a.html|website=The Sydney Morning Herald|date=22 February 2010|access-date=31 January 2015|archive-date=7 July 2014|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140707223400/http://www.smh.com.au/executive-style/culture/sulky-mozart-of-chess--the-new-kasparov-20100222-or5a.html|url-status=live}}</ref> During the year away from school, he placed joint-third in the European Under-14 Championship<ref name="Chess-Results">{{cite web|title=European Youth Championship Boys – U14|url=http://chess-results.com/tnr1434.aspx?art=4&lan=1&turdet=YES|publisher=Chess-Results|date=22 November 2003|access-date=5 January 2013|archive-date=10 November 2013|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131110154934/http://chess-results.com/tnr1434.aspx?art=4&lan=1&turdet=YES|url-status=live}}</ref> and ninth in the [[World Youth Chess Championship|2003 World Under-14 Championship]].<ref>{{cite web|url=http://chess-results.com/tnr1377.aspx?lan=16&art=1&turdet=YES&wi=984|title=World Youth Chess Championship 2003 – Boys-14|publisher=Chess-Results|access-date=1 September 2015|archive-date=9 March 2016|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160309060742/http://chess-results.com/tnr1377.aspx?lan=16&art=1&turdet=YES&wi=984|url-status=live}}</ref>
== Chess career == === 2004 === {{Chess diagram small | tright | Carlsen vs. Ernst, 2004 | | | |rd| |rd|kd| | pd|pd| | |bd|pd|pd| | | | | |pd|nd| |pd | qd| |pd| |nl| | |pl | | | |pl| |bl| | | | | | | | | | | pl|pl|pl| |ql|pl|pl| | |kl| |rl| | | |rl | Position after 17...c5. The game continued 18.Ng6 fxg6 19.Qxe6+ Kh8 20.hxg6 Ng8 21.Bxh6 gxh6 22.Rxh6+ Nxh6 23.Qxe7 Nf7 24.gxf7 Kg7 25.Rd3 Rd6 26.Rg3+ Rg6 27.Qe5+ Kxf7 28.Qf5+ Rf6 29.Qd7{{chessAN|#}} }} {{AN chess|pos=example}} [[File:ErnstCarlsenWijkaanZee2004.jpg|thumb|Carlsen vs. Ernst, 2004]] Carlsen made headlines after his victory, at the age of 13, in the C group at the 2004 [[Corus chess tournament]] in [[Wijk aan Zee]].<ref>[https://theweekinchess.com/html/twic481.html The Week in Chess 481] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230405101120/https://theweekinchess.com/html/twic481.html |date=5 April 2023 }}, 26 January 2004.</ref> He obtained a score of 10½/13, losing just one game (against the highest-rated player of the C group, [[Duško Pavasovič]]).<ref>{{cite journal|author=Carlsen, Magnus|title=Magnificent Magnus|journal=New in Chess|year=2004|issue=2|pages=48–51}}</ref> As a result, he earned his first [[Norm (chess)#Grandmaster norm|GM norm]], and achieved a {{chessgloss|performance rating|PR}} of 2702. Particularly notable was his win over [[Sipke Ernst]] in the penultimate round, when he sacrificed material to give [[Checkmate|mate]] in just 29 moves.<ref>Kavalek, Lubomir (23 February 2012). [https://www.huffingtonpost.com/lubomir-kavalek/mozarts-of-chess_b_1288929.html "Mozarts of Chess"] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170214143225/http://www.huffingtonpost.com/lubomir-kavalek/mozarts-of-chess_b_1288929.html |date=14 February 2017 }}. Huffpost. Retrieved 6 August 2018.</ref> His victory in the C group qualified him to play in the B group in 2005, and it led [[Lubomir Kavalek]], writing for the ''[[Washington Post]]'', to give him the title "the [[Wolfgang Amadeus Mozart|Mozart]] of chess" (although, as pointed out by [[Edward Winter (chess historian)|Edward Winter]], the nickname had been given to many illustrious predecessors).<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.chesshistory.com/winter/extra/mozart.html|title='The Mozart of Chess' by Edward Winter|website=Chesshistory.com|access-date=7 November 2021|archive-date=28 April 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200428120052/https://www.chesshistory.com/winter/extra/mozart.html|url-status=live}}</ref> Agdestein said that Carlsen had an excellent memory and played an unusually wide range of openings.<ref>{{cite journal |author=Agdestein, Simen |title=On the Road |journal=New in Chess |year=2006 |issue=4 |pages=17–21}}</ref> Carlsen's prowess caught the attention of [[Microsoft]], which became his sponsor.<ref name="The Mozart of Chess">{{cite web |author=Berntsen, Mathias |url=http://chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=1447 |title=The Mozart of Chess: Mega Magnus in Wijk aan Zee |publisher=ChessBase News |date=27 January 2004 |access-date=3 January 2013 |archive-date=14 July 2011 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110714081233/http://chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=1447 |url-status=live }}</ref>
Carlsen obtained his second GM norm at the Moscow [[Aeroflot Open]] in February. On 17 March, in a [[Fast chess|blitz chess]] tournament in [[Reykjavík]], Iceland, he defeated former World Champion [[Anatoly Karpov]]. It was a preliminary event leading up to a [[Fast chess|rapid]] knockout tournament beginning the next day. In that event, Carlsen was paired with [[Garry Kasparov]], then the top-rated player in the world. Carlsen achieved a draw in their first game but lost the second, and was thus knocked out of the tournament.<ref name=h>{{cite web |url=http://www.chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=1536 |title=Boy meets Beast in Reykjavik |publisher=ChessBase News |date=19 March 2004 |access-date=3 January 2013 |archive-date=9 November 2012 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20121109054626/http://chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=1536 |url-status=live }}</ref>
In the sixth [[Dubai]] Open Chess Championship, held 18–28 April, Carlsen obtained his third GM norm, which made him the world's youngest GM at the time, and the [[Chess prodigy#List of youngest grandmasters|second-youngest GM in history at the time]] (after [[Sergey Karjakin]], who earned the title at 12 years and 7 months).<ref>{{cite web |url=http://www.chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=1614 |title=Magnificent Magnus, the world's youngest grandmaster |publisher=ChessBase News |date=30 April 2004 |access-date=3 January 2013 |archive-date=5 March 2011 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110305194814/http://www.chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=1614 |url-status=live }}</ref> Carlsen played in the [[FIDE World Chess Championship 2004|FIDE World Chess Championship]], becoming the youngest player ever to participate in one, but was knocked out in the first round by [[Levon Aronian]].<ref>{{cite web |title=FIDE WCC R1: Youngest and oldest falter |url=http://www.chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=1728 |publisher=ChessBase News |date=20 April 2004 |access-date=3 January 2013 |archive-date=9 November 2012 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20121109205910/http://chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=1728 |url-status=live }}</ref>
In July, Carlsen and [[Berge Østenstad]], then the reigning Norwegian champion, tied for first in the Norwegian Chess Championship, each scoring 7/9. A two-game match between them was arranged to decide the title. Both games were drawn, which left Østenstad the champion because he had superior tiebreaks in the tournament.<ref>{{cite web |url=http://www.aftenposten.no/nyheter/sport/Ostenstad-slo-ut-Magnus-Carlsen-i-sjakk-NM-6316149.html |title=Østenstad slo ut Magnus Carlsen i sjakk-NM |work=Aftenposten |publisher=Aftenposten.com |date=5 September 2004 |language=no |access-date=5 February 2013 |archive-date=10 November 2013 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131110170701/http://www.aftenposten.no/nyheter/sport/Ostenstad-slo-ut-Magnus-Carlsen-i-sjakk-NM-6316149.html |url-status=live }}</ref>
=== 2005 === In the Smartfish Chess Masters event at the [[Drammen]] International Chess Festival 2004–05, Carlsen defeated [[Alexei Shirov]], then ranked {{Numero|10}}<ref>{{cite web |title=Top 100 Players October 2004 – Archive |url=http://ratings.fide.com/toparc.phtml?cod=69 |access-date=3 January 2013 |publisher=FIDE |archive-date=29 April 2020 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200429104112/http://ratings.fide.com/toparc.phtml?cod=69 |url-status=live }}</ref> in the world, as well as the co-winner of the tournament.<ref>{{cite web |url=http://www.chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=2118 |title=Smartfish sensation: Carlsen defeats Shirov |publisher=ChessBase News |date=4 January 2005 |access-date=3 January 2013 |archive-date=9 November 2012 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20121109200942/http://chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=2118 |url-status=live }}</ref> In the semi-finals of the [[León, Spain|Ciudad de León]] rapid chess tournament in June, Carlsen played a four-game match against Viswanathan Anand, who was ranked {{Numero|2}} in the world at the time and had won the 2003 [[Fast chess#World Rapid chess champions|World Rapid Chess Championship]].<ref>{{cite web |url=http://en.chessbase.com/home/TabId/211/PostId/4001276 |title=World Champion Vishy Anand! |publisher=ChessBase News |date=2 November 2003 |access-date=21 August 2013 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131017210329/http://en.chessbase.com/home/TabId/211/PostId/4001276 |archive-date=17 October 2013 }}</ref> Anand won 3–1.<ref>{{cite web |title=Kasimdzhanov and Anand win 3:1, 3:1 |url=http://www.chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=2447 |publisher=ChessBase News |date=11 June 2005 |access-date=3 January 2013 |archive-date=9 November 2012 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20121109205001/http://chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=2447 |url-status=live }}</ref>
In the Norwegian Chess Championship, Carlsen again finished in shared first place, this time with his mentor Simen Agdestein. A playoff between them was played between 7 and 10 November. This time, Carlsen had the better tiebreaks, but the rule giving the title to the player with better tiebreak scores in the event of a 1–1 tie had been revoked. The match was closely fought—Agdestein won the first game, Carlsen the second—so it went into a series of two-game rapid matches until there was a winner. Carlsen won the first rapid game, Agdestein the second. Then followed three draws until Agdestein won the championship title with a victory in the sixth rapid game.<ref>{{cite web |author=Valaker, Ole |title=Deilig å vinne |url=http://pub.nettavisen.no/nettavisen/sjakk/article489452.ece |work=[[Nettavisen]] |date=9 November 2005 |language=no |access-date=4 January 2013 |archive-date=22 July 2012 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120722021745/http://pub.nettavisen.no/nettavisen/sjakk/article489452.ece |url-status=dead }}</ref>
In October, Carlsen took first place at the Arnold Eikrem Memorial in Gausdal with a score of 8/9 and a {{chessgloss|performance rating|PR}} of 2792.<ref>{{cite web |author=Svensen, Tarjei J. |url=http://www.chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=2689 |title=14-year-old Carlsen with 2792 performance |publisher=ChessBase News |date=20 October 2005 |access-date=3 January 2013 |archive-date=9 November 2012 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20121109053732/http://chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=2689 |url-status=live }}</ref> [[File:Carlsen Magnus.jpg|thumb|Carlsen in Warsaw, 2005]] At the end of 2005, Carlsen participated at the [[Chess World Cup 2005|Chess World Cup]] in [[Khanty-Mansiysk]], Russia. In the knockout tournament, he upset 44th-ranked [[Zurab Azmaiparashvili]] in round one, and proceeded to defeat [[Farrukh Amonatov]] and [[Ivan Cheparinov]] to reach the round of 16. There he lost to [[Evgeny Bareev]],<ref>{{cite web |title=Bareev defeats Carlsen, Bacrot KO's Lautier |url=http://www.chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=2785 |publisher=ChessBase News |date=9 December 2005 |access-date=3 January 2013 |archive-date=9 November 2012 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20121109220518/http://chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=2785 |url-status=live }}</ref> but won against [[Joël Lautier]] and [[Vladimir Malakhov (chess player)|Vladimir Malakhov]] before losing again to [[Gata Kamsky]]. Carlsen finished in tenth place and became the youngest player to be an official World Championship [[Candidates Tournament|Candidate]].<ref>{{cite web |title=Khanty-Mansiysk: The prodigies are through |url=http://www.chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=2802 |publisher=ChessBase News |date=15 December 2005 |access-date=3 January 2013 |archive-date=7 May 2020 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200507100109/https://en.chessbase.com/post/khanty-mansiysk-the-prodigies-are-through |url-status=live }}</ref>
=== 2006 === Carlsen qualified for a place in the Corus B group due to his first-place finish in Corus group C in 2004. His shared first place with [[Alexander Motylev]] with 9/13 (+6−1=6) qualified him to play in the Corus group A in 2007.<ref>{{cite journal |author=Geuzendam, Dirk Jan ten |title=Motylev and Magnus move up |journal=New in Chess |year=2006 |issue=2 |pages=42–49}}</ref>
At the 2006 international 'Bosna' tournament in [[Sarajevo]], Carlsen shared first place with [[Liviu-Dieter Nisipeanu]] (who won on tiebreak) and Vladimir Malakhov; this could be regarded as Carlsen's first "A" elite tournament win, although it was not a clear first.<ref>{{cite web |title=Magnus Carlsen: age 15, performance 2700 |url=http://en.chessbase.com/post/magnus-carlsen-age-15-performance-2700 |date=19 May 2006 |access-date=3 June 2014 |archive-date=17 March 2015 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150317164032/http://en.chessbase.com/post/magnus-carlsen-age-15-performance-2700 |url-status=live }}</ref>
Carlsen was close to winning the 2006 Norwegian Chess Championship outright, but a last-round loss to Berge Østenstad dropped him into another tie for first place with Agdestein. It also prevented Carlsen from beating Agdestein's record as the youngest Norwegian champion ever.<ref>{{cite web |title=Carlsen falters, shares victory with Agdestein |url=http://www.chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=3235 |publisher=ChessBase News |date=16 July 2006 |access-date=3 January 2013 |archive-date=9 November 2012 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20121109080102/http://chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=3235 |url-status=live }}</ref> Nonetheless, in the playoff held from 19 to 21 September, Carlsen won 3–1. After two draws at standard time controls, Carlsen won both rapid games in round two, securing his first Norwegian championship win.<ref>{{cite web |title=Magnus Carlsen, 15, Norwegian Chess Champion |url=http://www.chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=3355 |publisher=ChessBase News |date=21 September 2006 |access-date=4 January 2013 |archive-date=9 November 2012 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20121109195928/http://chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=3355 |url-status=live }}</ref>
Carlsen won the Glitnir Blitz Tournament in Iceland.<ref>{{cite web |author=Nafn |url=http://skaksamband.is/index.php?option=content&task=view&id=7&Itemid=32 |title=Skáksamband Íslands – Glitnir blitz 2006 |publisher=Skaksamband.is |access-date=3 January 2010 |archive-date=12 February 2009 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090212122254/http://skaksamband.is/index.php?option=content&task=view&id=7&Itemid=32 |url-status=dead }}</ref> He achieved a 2–0 win over Viswanathan Anand in the semi-finals and achieved the same score in the finals.<ref>{{cite web |author=Nafn |url=http://skaksamband.is/index.php?option=content&task=view&id=23&Itemid=33 |title=Skáksamband Íslands – Results |publisher=Skaksamband.is |access-date=3 January 2010 |archive-date=12 February 2009 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090212122211/http://skaksamband.is/index.php?option=content&task=view&id=23&Itemid=33 |url-status=dead }}</ref> He scored 6/8 in the [[37th Chess Olympiad]] and achieved a PR of 2820.<ref>{{cite web |url=http://schach.wienerzeitung.at/tnr3410.aspx?art=23&lan=1&flag=30&snr=24 |title=37th Chess Olympiad 2006 general. Schachserver der Wiener Zeitung (Austria) |publisher=Schach.wienerzeitung.at |access-date=3 January 2013 |archive-date=12 February 2009 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090212160205/http://schach.wienerzeitung.at/tnr3410.aspx?art=23&lan=1&flag=30&snr=24 |url-status=dead }}</ref>
In the Midnight Sun Chess Tournament in [[Tromsø]], Carlsen finished second to [[Sergei Shipov]].<ref>{{cite web |title=Shipov wins Midnight Sun, Carlsen second |url=http://www.chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=3215 |publisher=ChessBase News |date=3 July 2006 |access-date=3 January 2013 |archive-date=9 November 2012 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20121109183052/http://chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=3215 |url-status=live }}</ref> In the [[Biel Chess Festival|Biel Grandmaster Tournament]], he placed second, beating the tournament winner [[Alexander Morozevich]] twice.<ref>{{cite web |title=The remarkable Alexander Morozevich wins Biel |url=http://www.chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=3271 |publisher=ChessBase News |date=4 August 2006 |access-date=3 January 2013 |archive-date=9 November 2012 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20121109082403/http://chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=3271 |url-status=live }}</ref>
In the NH Chess Tournament held in [[Amsterdam]] in August, Carlsen participated in an "Experience" vs. "Rising Stars" [[Scheveningen system|Scheveningen]] team match. The "Rising Stars" won the match 28–22, with Carlsen achieving the best individual score for the Rising Stars team (6½/10) and a 2700 PR, thus winning the right to participate in the 2007 [[Amber chess tournament|Melody Amber]] tournament.<ref>{{cite web |url=http://nhchess.quinsy.net/ |title=The NH Chess Tournament |publisher=Nhchess.quinsy.net |access-date=3 January 2010 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090901051828/http://www.nhchess.quinsy.net/ |archive-date=1 September 2009 }}</ref>
With a score of 7½/15, Carlsen placed 8th out of 16 participants at the World Blitz Championship in [[Rishon LeZion]], Israel.<ref>{{cite web |author=Liss, Erik |title=Grischuk wins FIDE World Blitz Championship |url=http://www.chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=3334 |publisher=ChessBase News |date=12 September 2006 |access-date=3 January 2013 |archive-date=9 November 2012 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20121109094801/http://chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=3334 |url-status=live }}</ref> In the rapid chess tournament ''Rencontres nationales et internationales d'échecs'' in [[Cap d'Agde]], France, he reached the semi-final, losing there to Sergey Karjakin.<ref>{{cite web |title=Teimour Radjabov wins Cap d'Agde |url=http://www.chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=3466 |publisher=ChessBase News |date=3 November 2006 |access-date=3 January 2013 |archive-date=9 November 2012 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20121109141715/http://chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=3466 |url-status=live }}</ref> In November, Carlsen achieved a shared 8th place of 10 participants in the [[Mikhail Tal Memorial]] in Moscow with two losses and seven draws. He finished ninth in a group of 18 participants in the associated blitz tournament, which was won by Anand.<ref>{{cite web |title=Tal-Mem Blitz: Anand wins with two point lead |url=http://www.chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=3496 |publisher=ChessBase News |date=19 November 2006 |access-date=3 January 2013 |archive-date=9 November 2012 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20121109065817/http://chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=3496 |url-status=live }}</ref>
=== 2007 === [[File:Magnus Øen Carlsen vs. Aronian Linares 2007.jpg|thumb|Carlsen playing [[Levon Aronian]] at Linares 2007]] Playing in the top group of the Corus chess tournament for the first time, Carlsen placed last with nine draws and four losses, scoring 4½/13.<ref>{{cite journal |author=Geuzendam, Dirk Jan ten |title=Three cheers for Aronian, Radjabov and Topalov |journal=New in Chess |year=2007 |issue=2 |pages=10–47}}</ref> In the [[Linares chess tournament]], Carlsen played against top-rated players [[Veselin Topalov]], [[Viswanathan Anand]], [[Peter Svidler]], [[Alexander Morozevich]], [[Levon Aronian]], [[Peter Leko]], and [[Vasyl Ivanchuk]]. Despite being rated significantly lower than any of them, he finished in second place on tiebreaks with 7½/14, having scored four wins, seven draws and three losses, and achieving a PR of 2778.<ref>{{cite web |title=Linares R14: Anand wins Morelia/Linares by a full point |url=http://www.chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=3722 |publisher=ChessBase News |date=10 March 2007 |access-date=3 January 2013 |archive-date=9 November 2012 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20121109063207/http://chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=3722 |url-status=live }}</ref>
Carlsen played for the first time in the [[Melody Amber]] blind and rapid chess tournament in [[Monte Carlo]] in March. In the 11 rounds, he achieved eight draws and three losses in the [[blindfold chess|blindfold]] games, as well as three wins, seven draws and one loss in the rapid games. This resulted in a shared ninth place in the blindfold, shared second place in the rapid (behind Anand), and a shared eighth place overall.<ref>{{cite web |title=Blindfold king Kramnik wins 16th Amber Tournament |url=http://www.chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=3766 |publisher=ChessBase News |date=30 March 2007 |access-date=4 January 2013 |archive-date=20 October 2012 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20121020150928/http://www.chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=3766 |url-status=live }}</ref>
In May and June, he participated in the Candidates Tournament for the [[FIDE World Chess Championship 2007]], facing Levon Aronian in a six-game match at standard time controls, which Carlsen drew (+2−2=2) by coming from behind twice. The four-game rapid playoff was drawn as well (+1−1=2), with Carlsen winning the last game to stay in the match. Eventually, Aronian eliminated Carlsen from the tournament after winning both tiebreak blitz games.<ref>{{cite journal |author=Geuzendam, Dirk Jan ten |title=A Whiff of the Golden Age |journal=New in Chess |year=2007 |issue=5 |pages=22–53}}</ref>
In July and August, Carlsen won the Biel Grandmaster Tournament with a 6/10 record and a PR of 2753. His score was matched by [[Alexander Onischuk]] and they played a match to break the tie. After drawing two rapid and two blitz games, Carlsen won the [[armageddon chess|armageddon]] game.<ref>{{cite web |title=Carlsen: 'This victory is a milestone for my career' |url=http://www.chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=4032 |publisher=ChessBase News |date=5 August 2007 |access-date=3 January 2013 |archive-date=9 November 2012 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20121109163902/http://chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=4032 |url-status=live }}</ref> Immediately after the Biel tournament, Carlsen entered the open Arctic Chess Challenge in Tromsø, but his fourth-place result with +5=4 was a slight underperformance in terms of rating. In the first round, Carlsen conceded a draw to his classmate Brede Hagen (rated 2034)<ref>{{cite web|url=http://en.chessbase.com/post/second-arctic-che-challenge-in-troms|title=Second Arctic Chess Challenge in Tromsø|date=7 August 2007|website=Chess News|access-date=5 April 2016|archive-date=17 April 2016|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160417181548/http://en.chessbase.com/post/second-arctic-che-challenge-in-troms|url-status=live}}</ref> after having a lost position at one point.<ref>{{cite web |url=http://www.chessgames.com/perl/chessgame?gid=1469361 |title=Brede Hagen vs Magnus Carlsen |publisher=Chessgames.com |access-date=3 January 2013 |archive-date=8 March 2013 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130308185242/http://www.chessgames.com/perl/chessgame?gid=1469361 |url-status=live }}</ref> A game which attracted some attention was his sixth-round win over his father, Henrik Carlsen.<ref>{{cite news |url=http://www.chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=4039 |title=Carlsen vs Carlsen – Magnus beats his dad |date=10 August 2007 |publisher=ChessBase News |access-date=3 January 2013 |archive-date=9 November 2012 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20121109014136/http://chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=4039 |url-status=live }}</ref>
Carlsen reached the semi-final round of the [[Chess World Cup 2007|World Chess Cup]] in December, after defeating [[Michael Adams (chess player)|Michael Adams]] in the round of 16 and Ivan Cheparinov in the quarterfinals. In the semi-final, he was eliminated by the eventual winner, Gata Kamsky, scoring ½–1½.<ref>{{cite web |title=Khanty-Mansiysk: Gata Kamsky in the Final |url=http://www.chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=4315 |publisher=ChessBase News |date=10 December 2007 |access-date=3 January 2013 |archive-date=9 November 2012 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20121109042935/http://chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=4315 |url-status=live }}</ref>
=== 2008 === [[File:Magnus Carlsen 08.jpg|thumb|Carlsen in 2008]] In the top group A of the [[Corus 2008 chess tournament|69th Corus chess tournament]], Carlsen scored 8/13, achieving a PR of 2830. Carlsen won five games, lost two and drew six, sharing first place with [[Levon Aronian]].<ref>{{cite web |title=Wijk R13: Aronian, Carlsen win Wijk aan Zee 2008 |url=http://www.chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=4417 |publisher=ChessBase News |date=27 January 2008 |access-date=3 January 2013 |archive-date=9 November 2012 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20121109022724/http://chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=4417 |url-status=live }}</ref> At the Linares chess tournament, Carlsen had another 2800+ PR, scoring 8/14. He finished in sole second place, ½ point behind the winner World Champion Viswanathan Anand.<ref>{{cite web |title=Linares R14: All games drawn, Anand wins Super-GM |url=http://www.chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=4493 |publisher=ChessBase News |date=7 March 2008 |access-date=3 January 2013 |archive-date=9 November 2012 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20121109020904/http://chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=4493 |url-status=live }}</ref>
In March, Carlsen played for the second time in the Melody Amber blind and rapid chess tournament, held in [[Nice]] for the first time. In the 11 rounds he achieved four wins, four draws and two losses in the blindfold, and three wins, two losses, and six draws in the rapid. This resulted in a shared fifth place in the blindfold, shared third place in the rapid and a shared second place in the overall tournament.<ref>{{cite web |title=Melody Amber: Aronian wins with 2½ point lead |url=http://www.chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=4536 |publisher=ChessBase News |date=27 March 2008 |access-date=3 January 2013 |archive-date=9 November 2012 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20121109204319/http://chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=4536 |url-status=live }}</ref>
Carlsen was one of 21 players in the six-tournament [[FIDE Grand Prix 2008–2010]], a qualifier for the [[World Chess Championship 2012]]. In the first tournament, in [[Baku]], Azerbaijan, he finished in a three-way tie for first place, with another 2800 PR. He later withdrew from the Grand Prix cycle despite his initial success, criticising FIDE for "changing the rules dramatically in the middle of a [[World Championship]] cycle".<ref>{{cite web |url=http://www.chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=5053 |title=Chess News – Magnus Carlsen withdraws from Grand Prix |date=5 December 2008 |publisher=ChessBase News |access-date=3 January 2013 |archive-date=9 November 2010 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20101109132451/http://www.chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=5053 |url-status=live }}</ref>
Carlsen won a rapid match against [[Peter Leko]] held in [[Miskolc]], Hungary, scoring 5–3.<ref>{{Cite news |author=Gyimesi, Zoltán |title=Miskolc: Carlsen wins the rapid chess match 5:3 |url=http://www.chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=4672 |publisher=ChessBase News |date=3 June 2008 |access-date=3 January 2013 |archive-date=9 November 2012 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20121109015847/http://chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=4672 |url-status=live }}</ref> In June, Carlsen won the annual [[Aerosvit chess tournament]],<ref>{{cite web |url=http://www.ukrchess.org.ua/aerosvit2008/index_e.htm |title=Aerosvit-2008 official site |publisher=Ukrchess.org.ua |date=20 June 2008 |access-date=31 January 2012 |archive-date=11 March 2020 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200311172335/http://www.ukrchess.org.ua/aerosvit2008/index_e.htm |url-status=live }}</ref> finishing undefeated with 8/11 in a [[Category (chess tournament)|category]] 19 field and achieving a PR of 2877, his best PR at that point in his career.<ref>{{cite web |title=Aerosvit 2008: Carlsen wins by a full point |url=http://www.chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=4712 |publisher=ChessBase News |date=19 June 2008 |access-date=3 January 2013 |archive-date=4 June 2011 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110604211050/http://www.chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=4712 |url-status=live }}</ref> Playing in the category 18 Biel Grandmaster Tournament, Carlsen finished third with 6/10, with a PR of 2740.<ref>{{cite web |author=Friedel, Frederic |url=http://www.chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=4812 |title=Biel 2008: impressions from the final round |publisher=ChessBase News |date=4 August 2008 |access-date=5 February 2013 |archive-date=9 November 2012 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20121109060442/http://chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=4812 |url-status=live }}</ref>
In the [[Mainz]] World Rapid Chess Championship, Carlsen finished in second place after losing the final to defending champion Anand 3–1.<ref>{{cite web |author=Fischer, Johannes |url=http://www.chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=4811 |title=Mainz 2008: Anand punishes Carlsen in Grenkeleasing final |publisher=ChessBase News |date=4 August 2008 |access-date=3 January 2013 |archive-date=9 November 2012 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20121109032631/http://chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=4811 |url-status=live }}</ref> In the qualification round Carlsen scored 1½–½ against [[Judit Polgár]], 1–1 against Anand and 1–1 against Alexander Morozevich.<ref>{{cite web |author=Fischer, Johannes |title=Mainz 2008: Anand to face Carlsen in Grenkeleasing final |url=http://www.chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=4807 |publisher=ChessBase News |date=3 September 2008 |access-date=3 January 2013 |archive-date=9 November 2012 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20121109032714/http://chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=4807 |url-status=live }}</ref> In the category 22 [[Bilbao Chess Masters Final|Bilbao Masters]], Carlsen tied for second with a 2768 PR.<ref>{{cite web |title=Bilbao R10: Topalov wins Bilbao, leads in live rankings |url=http://www.chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=4908 |publisher=ChessBase News |date=16 September 2008 |access-date=3 January 2013 |archive-date=15 September 2008 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080915230303/http://www.chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=4908 |url-status=live }}</ref>
=== 2009 === Playing in Group A of the [[Tata Steel Chess Tournament#2009|71st Corus chess tournament]], Carlsen tied for fifth with a 2739 PR.<ref>{{cite news |url=http://www.chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=5190 |title=Sergey Karjakin wins Wijk aan Zee 2009 |newspaper=Chess News |date=February 2009 |publisher=ChessBase News |access-date=3 January 2013 |archive-date=20 November 2012 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20121120053548/http://www.chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=5190 |url-status=live }}</ref> In the [[Linares International Chess Tournament|Linares chess tournament]], he finished third with a 2777 PR.<ref>{{cite web |url=http://www.chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=5271 |title=Linares R14: All games drawn, Grischuk wins on tiebreak |publisher=ChessBase News |date=7 March 2009 |access-date=4 January 2013 |archive-date=20 November 2012 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20121120013222/http://www.chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=5271 |url-status=live }}</ref> He tied for second place with [[Veselin Topalov]] at the [[M-Tel Masters]] (category 21) tournament in Sofia, Bulgaria. He lost to eventual winner [[Alexei Shirov]] in their final game, dropping him from first.<ref>{{cite web |author=McClain, Dylan Loeb |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2009/05/31/crosswords/chess/31chess.html |title=Highly Ranked Youth Loses Tactical Battle to an Old Pro |work=The New York Times |date=30 May 2009 |access-date=3 January 2013 |archive-date=16 November 2018 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20181116022422/https://www.nytimes.com/2009/05/31/crosswords/chess/31chess.html |url-status=live }}</ref>
Carlsen won the category 21 [[Nanjing Pearl Spring]] tournament, 2½ points ahead of second-place finisher Topalov, the world's highest-rated player at the time. He scored an undefeated 8/10, winning every game as [[White and Black in chess|white]] (against Topalov, [[Wang Yue (chess player)|Wang Yue]], Leko, [[Teimour Radjabov]], and [[Dmitry Jakovenko]]), and also winning as black against Jakovenko. By rating performance, this was one of the greatest results in history, with a PR of 3002.<ref name="ChessBase 10 October 2009">{{cite web |title=Nanjing: Carlsen wins Pearl Spring with 3002 performance |url=http://www.chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=5822 |publisher=ChessBase News |date=10 October 2009 |access-date=3 January 2013 |archive-date=16 February 2013 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130216035441/http://www.chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=5822 |url-status=live }}</ref> Chess statistician [[Jeff Sonas]] has declared it one of the 20 best tournament performances of all time, and the best chess performance of all time by a teenager.<ref>{{cite web |url=http://www.chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=5828 |title=Chess News – Facts and figures: Magnus Carlsen's performance in Nanjing |date=12 October 2009 |publisher=ChessBase News |access-date=3 January 2013 |archive-date=10 May 2011 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110510023149/http://www.chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=5828 |url-status=live }}</ref>
In the [[Mikhail Tal Memorial|Tal Memorial]], played from 5 to 14 November, Carlsen started with seven straight draws, but finished with wins over former FIDE World Champion [[Ruslan Ponomariov]] and Peter Leko. This result put Carlsen in shared second place behind former World Champion [[Vladimir Kramnik]] and equal with Ivanchuk.<ref>{{cite web |author=McClain, Dylan Loeb |url=http://gambit.blogs.nytimes.com/2009/11/15/kramnik-wins-tal-memorial-carlsen-claims-no-1-ranking/ |title=Kramnik Wins Tal Memorial, Carlsen Claims No. 1 Ranking |work=[[The New York Times]] Chess Blog |date=15 November 2009 |access-date=3 January 2013 |archive-date=26 December 2012 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20121226003729/http://gambit.blogs.nytimes.com/2009/11/15/kramnik-wins-tal-memorial-carlsen-claims-no-1-ranking/ |url-status=live }}</ref><ref>{{cite web |author=McClain, Dylan Loeb |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2009/11/15/crosswords/chess/15champion.html?_r=1&ref=world |title=Norwegian, 18, Is Youngest to Be Ranked No. 1 at Chess |work=[[The New York Times]] Chess Blog |date=14 November 2009 |access-date=3 January 2013 |archive-date=16 May 2020 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200516151128/https://www.nytimes.com/2009/11/15/crosswords/chess/15champion.html?_r=1&ref=world |url-status=live }}</ref> After the Tal Memorial, Carlsen won the World Blitz Championship, played from 16 to 18 November in Moscow, Russia. His score of 28 wins, 6 draws and 8 losses left him three points ahead of Anand, who finished in second place.<ref>{{cite web |url=http://www.chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=5923 |title=World Blitz Championship: Carlsen wins by three-point margin |publisher=ChessBase News |date=18 November 2009 |access-date=3 January 2013 |archive-date=9 November 2012 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20121109045551/http://chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=5923 |url-status=live }}</ref> [[File:MagnusCarlsen.jpg|thumb|Carlsen at the World Blitz Championship 2009]]
Carlsen entered the [[London Chess Classic]] as the top seed in a field including Kramnik, [[Hikaru Nakamura]], Michael Adams, [[Nigel Short]], [[Ni Hua]], [[Luke McShane]] and [[David Howell (chess player)|David Howell]]. He defeated Kramnik in round one and went on to win the tournament with 13/21 (three points were awarded for a win, and one for a draw; using classical scoring he finished with 5/7) and a PR of 2844, one point ahead of Kramnik. This victory propelled him to {{Numero|1}} of the FIDE rating list, surpassing Veselin Topalov.<ref>{{cite web |title=London R7: Carlsen wins Chess Classic |url=http://www.chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=5990 |publisher=ChessBase News |date=16 December 2009 |access-date=3 January 2013 |archive-date=9 November 2012 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20121109053206/http://chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=5990 |url-status=live }}</ref><ref>{{cite magazine |last1=Max |first1=D. T. |title=The Prince's Gambit |url=https://www.newyorker.com/magazine/2011/03/21/the-princes-gambit |magazine=The New Yorker |date=13 March 2011 |access-date=2 December 2020 |archive-date=30 November 2020 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201130191202/https://www.newyorker.com/magazine/2011/03/21/the-princes-gambit |url-status=live }}</ref>
Based on his average ranking from the July 2009 and January 2010 FIDE lists, Carlsen qualified for the Candidates Tournament that would determine the challenger to World Champion Viswanathan Anand in the World Chess Championship 2012. In November 2010, however, Carlsen announced he was withdrawing from the Candidates Tournament. Carlsen described the 2008–12 cycle as "[not] sufficiently modern and fair", and wrote that "Reigning champion privileges, the long (five-year) span of the cycle, changes made during the cycle resulting in a new format (Candidates) that no World Champion has had to go through since Kasparov, puzzling ranking criteria as well as the shallow ceaseless match-after-match concept are all less than satisfactory in my opinion."<ref>{{cite web |url=http://www.chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=6789 |title=Magnus Carlsen drops out of World Championship cycle |publisher=ChessBase News |date=5 November 2010 |access-date=3 January 2013 |archive-date=21 January 2013 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130121001258/http://www.chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=6789 |url-status=live }}</ref>
In early 2009 Carlsen engaged former World Champion Garry Kasparov as a personal trainer.<ref>{{cite web |author=Doggers, Peter |url=http://www.chessvibes.com/reports/magnus-carlsen-my-job-is-to-improve-my-chess/#more-15978 |title=Magnus Carlsen: 'My job is to improve my chess' |publisher=ChessVibes |date=7 September 2009 |access-date=3 January 2013 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130128130236/http://www.chessvibes.com/reports/magnus-carlsen-my-job-is-to-improve-my-chess/#more-15978 |archive-date=28 January 2013 |url-status=dead }}</ref> In September their partnership was revealed to the public by Norwegian newspapers.<ref>{{cite web |author=Peterson, Macauley |url=https://webcast.chessclub.com/blog/2009/09/07/carlsen-and-kasparov-a-new-team/ |title=Carlsen, Kasparov Team Up |publisher=The Internet Chess Club |date=7 September 2009 |access-date=30 November 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110718111009/https://webcast.chessclub.com/blog/2009/09/07/carlsen-and-kasparov-a-new-team/|archive-date=18 July 2011}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |url=http://www.chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=5742 |title=Breaking news: Carlsen and Kasparov join forces |publisher=ChessBase News; translated from the Norwegian newspaper VG Nett |date=7 September 2009 |access-date=3 January 2013 |archive-date=14 January 2011 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110114211644/http://www.chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=5742 |url-status=live }}</ref>
Responding to a question in an interview with ''Time'' magazine in December 2009 as to whether he used computers when studying chess, Carlsen explained that he does not use a chess set when studying on his own.<ref name="Time 2009">{{cite magazine |url=http://www.time.com/time/world/article/0,8599,1948809,00.html |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20091227184603/http://www.time.com/time/world/article/0,8599,1948809,00.html |url-status=dead |archive-date=27 December 2009 |magazine=Time |author=Harrell, Eben |title=Magnus Carlsen: The 19-Year-Old King of Chess |date=25 December 2009 |access-date=3 January 2013}}</ref>
=== 2010 === Carlsen won the [[Tata Steel Chess Tournament#2010|72nd Corus chess tournament]] played 16–31 January with 8½ points. His ninth-round loss to Kramnik ended a streak of 36 rated games undefeated.<ref>{{cite news |url=http://www.nettavisen.no/sjakk/article2812643.ece |title=Så tapte Magnus |author=Valaker, Ole |date=26 January 2010 |publisher=Nettavisen |language=no |access-date=3 January 2013 |archive-date=28 January 2010 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100128094942/http://www.nettavisen.no/sjakk/article2812643.ece |url-status=dead }}</ref> Carlsen struggled in the last round against [[Fabiano Caruana]], but saved a draw, leaving him half a point ahead of Kramnik and [[Alexei Shirov|Shirov]].<ref>{{cite news |url=http://www.nettavisen.no/sjakk/article2817756.ece |title=Magnus vant thrillerfinale |author=Valaker, Ole |date=31 January 2010 |publisher=Nettavisen |language=no |access-date=3 January 2013 |archive-date=3 February 2010 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100203155644/http://www.nettavisen.no/sjakk/article2817756.ece |url-status=dead }}</ref>
In March it was announced that Carlsen had split from Kasparov and would no longer use him as a trainer,<ref name="barden">{{cite news |url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2010/mar/13/chess-magnus-carlsen-garry-kasparov |title=World No1 Magnus Carlsen parts company with mentor Garry Kasparov |author=Barden, Leonard |date=13 March 2010 |work=[[The Guardian]] |access-date=3 January 2013}}</ref> although Carlsen later stated that they would remain in contact and he would continue to attend training sessions with Kasparov.<ref>{{cite web |url=http://www.chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=6187 |title=Magnus Carlsen on his chess career |publisher=ChessBase News |date=15 March 2010 |access-date=3 January 2013 |archive-date=3 September 2011 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110903210833/http://www.chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=6187 |url-status=live }}</ref>
Carlsen shared first place alongside Ivanchuk in the Amber blindfold and rapid tournament. Scoring 6½/11 in the blindfold and 8/11 in the rapid, Carlsen accumulated 14½ from a possible 22 points.<ref>{{cite web |title=Amber: Carlsen and Ivanchuk win 19th Amber |url=http://www.chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=6214 |publisher=ChessBase News |date=26 March 2010 |access-date=3 January 2013 |archive-date=23 November 2010 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20101123144055/http://chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=6214 |url-status=live }}</ref> Carlsen helped Anand prepare for the [[World Chess Championship 2010]] against Veselin Topalov, which Anand won 6½–5½ to retain the title. Carlsen had also helped Anand prepare for the World Chess Championships in [[World Chess Championship 2007|2007]] and [[World Chess Championship 2008|2008]].<ref>{{cite web |url=http://www.chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=6346 |title=Chess News – Anand in Playchess – the helpers in Sofia |work=Chess News |date=19 May 2010 |publisher=ChessBase News |access-date=3 January 2013 |archive-date=22 May 2010 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100522043142/http://www.chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=6346 |url-status=live }}</ref>
Carlsen won the Bazna Kings Tournament in Romania in June, two points ahead of Radjabov and [[Boris Gelfand]].<ref>{{cite web |author=Rogozenco, Dorian |title=Medias R10: Magnus Carlsen wins with two-point lead |url=http://www.chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=6457 |publisher=ChessBase News |date=25 June 2010 |access-date=3 January 2013 |archive-date=19 November 2012 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20121119143402/http://www.chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=6457 |url-status=live }}</ref> In August, Carlsen played in the Arctic Securities Chess Stars tournament in Kristiansund, Norway., defeating Anand 1½–½ in the two-game final to win the championship.<ref>{{cite web |url=http://chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=6644 |title=Carlsen beats Anand, wins Arctic Securities Rapid |date=31 August 2010 |publisher=ChessBase News |access-date=3 January 2013 |archive-date=15 July 2011 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110715225524/http://www.chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=6644 |url-status=live }}</ref>
Carlsen suffered setbacks in his next two tournaments. In the [[39th Chess Olympiad]] he scored 4½/8, losing three games, to [[Baadur Jobava]], Michael Adams, and [[Sanan Sjugirov]]; these were his first losses with the black pieces in more than a year.<ref>{{cite web |url=http://www.chess-results.com/tnr36795.aspx?art=20&fed=NOR&lan=1&flag=30&m=-1&wi=1000 |title=Team-Composition for federation NOR – Open |publisher=Chess Results |access-date=3 January 2013 |archive-date=10 May 2011 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110510053930/http://www.chess-results.com/tnr36795.aspx?art=20&fed=NOR&lan=1&flag=30&m=-1&wi=1000 |url-status=live }}</ref> His team, Norway, finished 51st out of 149 teams.<ref>{{cite web |url=http://ugra-chess.com/standings.php |title=Standings; World Chess Olympiad 2010 Khanty-Mansiysk; Всемирная Шахматная Олимпиада Ханты-Мансийск |publisher=Ugra-chess |access-date=3 January 2013 |archive-date=8 January 2014 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140108052048/http://ugra-chess.com/standings.php |url-status=usurped }}</ref>
Carlsen played in the [[Bilbao Chess Masters Final#Third Grand Slam Masters Final (2010)|Grand Slam Masters Final]] in October, which he had qualified for automatically by winning three of the previous year's four Grand Slam chess events.<ref>{{cite web |url=http://www.chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=6567 |title=Final Chess Masters 2010 in Shanghai and Bilbao |date=2 August 2010 |publisher=ChessBase News |access-date=3 January 2013 |archive-date=15 September 2010 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100915000641/http://www.chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=6567 |url-status=live }}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://theweekinchess.com/html/twic827.html#2|title=The Week in Chess 827|website=The Week in Chess|access-date=5 April 2016|archive-date=1 April 2016|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160401005125/http://theweekinchess.com/html/twic827.html#2|url-status=live}}</ref> The average Elo of the participants at the time was 2789, making the Grand Slam Final the strongest chess tournament in history. In the first round, Carlsen lost with black to Kramnik; this was Carlsen's second consecutive loss to Kramnik, and placed his hold on the world {{Numero|1}} ranking in jeopardy. In his second round, Carlsen lost with the white pieces to Anand; this was his first loss as White since January 2010. Carlsen recovered somewhat in the latter part of the tournament, achieving a win over Shirov, and finishing with 2½/6; Kramnik won the event with 4/6.<ref>{{cite news |url=http://www.nettavisen.no/sjakk/article3006142.ece |title=Magnus endte på 3. plass |author=Valaker, Ole |date=15 October 2010 |publisher=Nettavisen |language=no |access-date=3 January 2013 |archive-date=4 November 2010 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20101104045029/http://www.nettavisen.no/sjakk/article3006142.ece |url-status=dead }}</ref>
In October, Carlsen competed at the [[Pearl Spring chess tournament]] in [[Nanjing]], China.<ref>{{cite web |url=http://previews.chessdom.com/2010/nanjing-chess-2010 |title=Nanjing Super Chess Tournament 2010 (updated) |publisher=Previews.chessdom.com |date=30 October 2010 |access-date=3 January 2013 |archive-date=27 September 2017 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170927000107/http://previews.chessdom.com/2010/nanjing-chess-2010 |url-status=dead }}</ref> This was the only tournament in 2010 to feature Anand, Carlsen and Topalov, at the time the top three players in the world, and was the first tournament in history to feature three players rated at least 2800. Carlsen secured first place by defeating Topalov with black. This was his second victory in the tournament over the former world {{Numero|1}}; his final score of 7/10 (with a PR of 2903) was a full point ahead of runner-up Anand.<ref>{{cite web |title=Nanjing R10: Magnus wins with 2900+ performance |url=http://www.chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=6778 |publisher=ChessBase News |date=30 October 2010 |access-date=5 January 2013 |archive-date=3 March 2011 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110303204214/http://www.chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=6778 |url-status=live }}</ref>
[[File:London Chess Classic 2010 Calsen 02.jpg|thumb|Carlsen at the 2010 London Chess Classic]] On 5 November, Carlsen withdrew from the [[World Chess Championship 2012#Candidates tournament|2011 Candidates Tournament]], having qualified as the highest rated challenger, citing dissatisfaction with the World Championship cycle format.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://en.chessbase.com/post/magnus-carlsen-drops-out-of-world-championship-cycle|date=5 November 2010|website=chessbase|title=Magnus Carlsen drops out of World Championship cycle|access-date=25 December 2024|archive-date=4 February 2013|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130204104934/http://www.chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=6789|url-status=live}}</ref>
In the World Blitz Championship, held in Moscow in November, Carlsen finished third with a score of 23½/38, behind Radjabov and winner Levon Aronian.<ref>{{cite web |url=http://www.chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=6816 |title=Aronian wins World Blitz Championship |publisher=ChessBase News |date=18 November 2010 |access-date=3 January 2013 |archive-date=25 December 2010 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20101225004843/http://chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=6816 |url-status=live }}</ref> After the tournament, Carlsen played a private 40-game blitz match against Hikaru Nakamura,<ref>{{cite web |author=Peterson, Macauley |title=Nakamura – Carlsen, Private Blitz Match |url=http://main.uschess.org/content/view/10889/611/ |publisher=United States Chess Federation |date=8 December 2010 |access-date=16 December 2012 |archive-date=8 March 2013 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130308183020/http://main.uschess.org/content/view/10889/611/ |url-status=live }}</ref> winning with a score of 24½–15½.<ref>{{cite journal |title=NIC's Café |page=6 |journal=New in Chess |year=2011 |issue=1}}</ref> Carlsen won the London Chess Classic in December, ahead of Anand and McShane.<ref>{{cite journal |author=Geuzendam, Dirk Jan ten |title=London is Carlsen's call |journal=New in Chess |year=2011 |issue=1 |pages=10–29}}</ref>
=== 2011 === Carlsen competed in the GM-A group of the [[Tata Steel Chess Tournament#2011|73rd Tata Steel Chess Tournament]] (formerly called the Corus chess tournament) on 14–30 January in Wijk aan Zee in an attempt to defend his title; the field included World Champion Viswanathan Anand, Levon Aronian, former World Champion Vladimir Kramnik, [[Alexander Grischuk]], Hikaru Nakamura, Ruslan Ponomariov, among others. Despite losing games with white against [[Anish Giri]] and reigning Russian champion [[Ian Nepomniachtchi]], Carlsen finished with 8/13, including victories over Kramnik and tournament winner Nakamura.<ref>{{cite journal |author=Geuzendam, Dirk Jan ten |title=Class of 2011 |journal=New in Chess |year=2011 |issue=2 |pages=11–37}}</ref> Although Carlsen's performance raised his rating from 2814 to 2815, Anand's 8½/13 score elevated his rating to 2817, making him the world {{Numero|1}} for the March 2011 FIDE rating list.<ref>{{cite web |title=Top 100 Players March 2011 – Archive |url=http://ratings.fide.com/toparc.phtml?cod=185 |publisher=FIDE |access-date=3 January 2013 |archive-date=7 March 2020 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200307224750/http://ratings.fide.com/toparc.phtml?cod=185 |url-status=live }}</ref>
The first tournament victory of the year came in the Bazna Kings tournament, a double round robin played in [[Mediaș]], Romania on 11–21 June. Carlsen finished with 6½/10, equal with Sergey Karjakin but with a better tiebreak score. Carlsen won his White games against Nakamura, Nisipeanu, and Ivanchuk and drew the rest of the games.<ref>{{cite news |url=http://chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=7309 |title=Media Kings Rd10: Carlsen–Karjakin draw, Carlsen wins Medias 2011 |date=21 June 2011 |publisher=ChessBase News |access-date=3 January 2013 |archive-date=25 June 2011 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110625104621/http://www.chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=7309 |url-status=live }}</ref>
Carlsen won the [[Biel Chess Festival|44th Biel Grandmaster tournament]], held from 16 to 29 July. He took clear first place with a score of 19/30 (+5−1=4; three points for a win) in a field comprising Fabiano Caruana, Maxime Vachier-Lagrave, Alexander Morozevich, Alexey Shirov and Yannick Pelletier, two points ahead of Morozevich. This was Carlsen's second title.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://theweekinchess.com/chessnews/events/biel-chess-festival-2011|title=Biel Chess Festival 2011 {{!}} The Week in Chess|website=theweekinchess.com|access-date=31 January 2019|archive-date=31 January 2019|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190131145418/http://theweekinchess.com/chessnews/events/biel-chess-festival-2011|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=https://en.chessbase.com/post/biel-rd-9-caruana-beats-morozevich-carlsen-wins-biel-2011|title=Biel Rd.9: Caruana beats Morozevich, Carlsen wins Biel 2011|date=28 July 2011|website=Chess News|language=en|access-date=31 January 2019|archive-date=31 January 2019|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190131201411/https://en.chessbase.com/post/biel-rd-9-caruana-beats-morozevich-carlsen-wins-biel-2011|url-status=live}}</ref>
The Grand Slam Chess Final was held as a double round robin with six players, in São Paulo (25 September – 1 October) and Bilbao (5–11 October). Although Carlsen had a slow start, including a loss against bottom-ranked [[Francisco Vallejo Pons]], he finished +3−1=6, equal with Ivanchuk (whose +4−3=3 finish was equal due to three points for a win). Carlsen then won the blitz tiebreak against Ivanchuk. The other players were Anand, Aronian, Nakamura, and Vallejo Pons.<ref>{{cite web |url=http://www.chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=7597 |title=Bilbao Masters – Carlsen takes first after blitz tiebreak |publisher=ChessBase News |date=12 October 2011 |access-date=3 January 2013 |archive-date=9 November 2012 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20121109100954/http://chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=7597 |url-status=live }}</ref>
Another tournament victory was achieved in the Tal Memorial in Moscow 16–25 November as a round robin with ten players. Carlsen won two games, against Gelfand and Nakamura, and drew the rest. Although he finished equal on points with Aronian, he placed ahead since the tiebreak was determined by the number of black games; Carlsen had five black games, while Aronian only had four.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.chessvibes.com/reports/carlsen-catches-aronian-in-last-round-tal-memorial |title=Carlsen catches Aronian in last round, wins Tal Memorial on tiebreak |publisher=ChessVibes |date=25 November 2011 |access-date=3 January 2013 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140327183729/http://www.chessvibes.com/reports/carlsen-catches-aronian-in-last-round-tal-memorial |archive-date=27 March 2014 }}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.chessgames.com/perl/chess.pl?tid=75834|title=Tal Memorial (2011)|access-date=2 January 2018|archive-date=29 October 2019|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20191029080438/http://www.chessgames.com/perl/chess.pl?tid=75834|url-status=live}}</ref>
In the London Chess Classic, played 3–12 December, Carlsen's streak of tournament victories ended when he finished third, behind Kramnik and Nakamura. Carlsen won three games and drew five. Although he did not win the tournament, Carlsen gained rating points, rising to a new personal record of 2835.<ref>{{cite news |url=http://www.vg.no/sport/artikkel.php?artid=10040819 |title=Sjakk-Carlsen har aldri vært bedre |date=12 December 2011 |access-date=3 January 2013 |language=no |publisher=Verdens Gang |archive-date=2 December 2013 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131202231927/http://www.vg.no/sport/artikkel.php?artid=10040819 |url-status=live }}</ref>
=== 2012 === [[File:MCarlsen11.jpg|thumb|Carlsen at the [[Tata Steel Chess Tournament#2012|74th Tata Steel Chess Tournament]] in 2012]] At the [[Tata Steel Chess Tournament#2012|74th Tata Steel Chess Tournament]] held on 14–29 January in Wijk aan Zee, Carlsen finished in a shared second place with 8/13, behind Aronian, and equal with Radjabov and Caruana. Carlsen defeated Gashimov, Aronian, Gelfand, and Topalov, but lost against Karjakin.<ref>{{cite web |url=http://www.chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=7879 |title=Wijk aan Zee Rd13: Aronian is sole winner! |date=29 January 2012 |publisher=ChessBase News |access-date=5 February 2013 |archive-date=1 February 2012 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120201170615/http://www.chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=7879 |url-status=live }}</ref> At the blitz chess tournament at Tal Memorial, held in Moscow on 7 June, Carlsen shared first place with Morozevich. In the main event (a category 22 ten-player round robin), he won two games and drew seven. He finished in first place, ahead of Radjabov and Caruana.<ref>{{cite web |title=Tal Memorial Rd9 – Carlsen takes title in record event |url=http://www.chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=8262 |publisher=ChessBase News |date=19 June 2012 |access-date=3 January 2013 |archive-date=9 November 2012 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20121109080810/http://chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=8262 |url-status=live }}</ref>
Carlsen then went on to finish second in the Biel Grandmaster Tournament, with 18 points, just one point behind [[Wang Hao (chess player)|Wang Hao]] using the 3–1–0 scoring system. As in the Tal Memorial earlier in 2012, Carlsen managed to finish the tournament without any losses (+4−0=6). He also defeated the winner Wang in both of their individual games. In the exhibition blitz tournament at Biel before the GM tournament, Carlsen was eliminated (+1−2=0) in the first round by Étienne Bacrot. Bacrot deprived Carlsen of a win in the classical tournament by holding him to a draw in the final round. Carlsen would have won the classical tournament on the traditional 1–½–0 scoring system, with 7/10.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://theweekinchess.com/chessnews/events/45th-biel-chess-festival-2012|title=45th Biel Chess Festival 2012 – The Week in Chess|website=The Week in Chess|access-date=5 April 2016|archive-date=14 April 2016|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160414181610/http://theweekinchess.com/chessnews/events/45th-biel-chess-festival-2012|url-status=live}}</ref>
The Grand Slam Chess Final was again held as a double round robin with six players, in São Paulo and Bilbao. Carlsen started with a loss against Caruana, but after three wins in the second (Bilbao) round, finished +4−1=5, equal first with Caruana, and ahead of Aronian, Karjakin and Anand. Carlsen won the tournament by winning both tiebreak games against Caruana.<ref>{{cite news |url=http://www.chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=8550 |title=Bilbao Rd10: Carlsen takes title in blitz tiebreak |newspaper=Chess News |publisher=ChessBase News |date=13 October 2012 |access-date=3 January 2013 |archive-date=17 October 2012 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20121017231401/http://www.chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=8550 |url-status=live }}</ref>
From 24 to 25 November, Carlsen took part in the chess festival "Segunda Gran Fiesta Internacional de Ajedrez" in [[Mexico City]]. As part of it, Carlsen took on an online audience (dubbed as "The World") with the white pieces and won. He then took part in the knockout exhibition event "Cuadrangular UNAM". Carlsen first beat [[Lázaro Bruzón]] 1½–½, thus qualifying for a final against Judit Polgár (who had in turn beat [[Manuel León Hoyos]] 1½–½). Carlsen lost the first game, but won the second one, and in the tiebreak defeated Polgár 2–0.<ref>{{cite web |url=http://www.chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=8652 |title=Second Gran Fiesta UNAM with Carlsen and Polgar |date=24 November 2012 |access-date=26 November 2012 |publisher=ChessBase News |archive-date=27 November 2012 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20121127223541/http://www.chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=8652 |url-status=live }}</ref><ref>{{cite web |url=http://www.chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=8657 |title=2nd UNAM won by Magnus Carlsen |date=26 November 2012 |publisher=ChessBase News |access-date=3 January 2013 |archive-date=29 November 2012 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20121129025530/http://www.chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=8657 |url-status=live }}</ref>
Carlsen won the London Chess Classic in December with five wins (over McShane, Aronian, [[Gawain Jones]], Adams and Judit Polgár) and three draws (against Kramnik, Nakamura and Anand).<ref name=wic-carlsenwinslondon2012/> This win, the third time Carlsen had won the tournament in the past four years, increased his rating from 2848 to a new record of 2861, breaking Kasparov's 13-year record of 2851.<ref name=wic-carlsenwinslondon2012/><ref name="chessvibesDec2012">{{cite web |url=http://www.chessvibes.com/reports/carlsen-beats-kasparovs-rating-record-in-london |title=Carlsen beats Kasparov's rating record in London |date=8 December 2012 |publisher=Chessvibes |access-date=3 January 2013 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130116094745/http://www.chessvibes.com/reports/carlsen-beats-kasparovs-rating-record-in-london |archive-date=16 January 2013 |url-status=dead }}</ref> By rating performance, this was one of the best results in history, with a PR of 2994.<ref>{{cite web |url=http://www.chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=8700 |title=LCC R9: Carlsen first, Kramnik second, both with big wins |date=10 December 2012 |publisher=ChessBase News |access-date=3 January 2013 |archive-date=13 December 2012 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20121213011039/http://www.chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=8700 |url-status=live }}</ref>
=== 2013 === [[File:Magnus Carlsen Tata Steel 2013.jpg|thumb|Carlsen in play during round seven of the [[Tata Steel Chess Tournament#2013|75th Tata Steel]], 2013]] Carlsen played in the [[Tata Steel Chess Tournament#2013|75th Tata Steel Chess Tournament]] from 11 to 27 January in Wijk aan Zee. In the 13-round tournament, he scored 10 points (+7−0=6), winning clear first 1½ points ahead of second-place finisher Aronian.<ref>{{cite web |title=Tata R13: Karjakin, Wang score, Carlsen wins Wijk by 1½ points |url=http://www.chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=8806 |date=27 January 2013 |publisher=ChessBase News |access-date=27 January 2013 |archive-date=29 January 2013 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130129213220/http://www.chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=8806 |url-status=live }}</ref> On 1 February, Danish GM [[Peter Heine Nielsen]] joined the team of assistants who helped Carlsen prepare for the [[World Chess Championship 2013#Candidates Tournament|Candidates Tournament in March]]. Before this, Nielsen was on Viswanathan Anand's team.<ref>{{cite web |url=http://www.chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=8817 |title=Nielsen switches from Anand to Carlsen |publisher=ChessBase News |date=31 January 2013 |access-date=5 February 2013 |archive-date=2 February 2013 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130202233950/http://www.chessbase.com/newsdetail.asp?newsid=8817 |url-status=live }}</ref>
Carlsen played in the [[World Chess Championship 2013#Candidates Tournament|2013 Candidates Tournament]], which took place in London, from 15 March to 1 April. He finished with +5−2=7, and won the tournament on tiebreak over Vladimir Kramnik. As a result, he earned the right to challenge Anand for the World Championship.<ref>{{cite web |author=Ramírez, Alejandro |url=http://www.chessbase.com/Home/TabId/211/PostId/4009346/candidates-r14--leaders-lose-carlsen-qualifies-020413.aspx |title=Candidates R14 – leaders lose, Carlsen qualifies |publisher=ChessBase News |date=1 April 2013 |access-date=1 April 2013 |archive-date=4 April 2013 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130404075241/http://www.chessbase.com/Home/TabId/211/PostId/4009346/candidates-r14--leaders-lose-carlsen-qualifies-020413.aspx |url-status=live }}</ref>
In May, Carlsen played in the [[Norway Chess#Norway Chess 2013|first edition]] of [[Norway Chess]] tournament. He finished second, scoring 5½/9 (+3−1=5), half a point behind Sergey Karjakin.<ref>{{cite web |url=http://chessbase.com/Home/TabId/211/PostId/4009861/sergey-karjakin-wins-norway-chess-2013-180513.aspx |title=Sergey Karjakin wins Norway Chess 2013 |date=18 May 2013 |publisher=ChessBase News |access-date=18 May 2013 |archive-date=7 June 2013 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130607140558/http://www.chessbase.com/Home/TabId/211/PostId/4009861/sergey-karjakin-wins-norway-chess-2013-180513.aspx |url-status=live }}</ref>
Carlsen played in the Tal Memorial from 12 to 23 June. He finished second, with 5½/9, half a point behind Boris Gelfand. Carlsen ended the tournament with +3−1=5, losing to Caruana but beating Anand, Kramnik and Nakamura.<ref>{{cite web |title=Tal Final: Gelfand wins, Carlsen clear second |url=http://www.chessbase.com/Home/TabId/211/PostId/4010255/tal-final-gelfand-wins-carlsen-clear-second-230613.aspx |date=23 June 2013 |publisher=Chessbase News |access-date=23 June 2013 |archive-date=25 June 2013 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130625005511/http://www.chessbase.com/Home/TabId/211/PostId/4010255/tal-final-gelfand-wins-carlsen-clear-second-230613.aspx |url-status=live }}</ref> Later that month, Carlsen played a four-game friendly rapid match against [[Borki Predojević]], which he won 2½–1½.<ref>{{cite web |author=Doggers, Peter |url=http://www.chessvibes.com/carlsen-beats-predojevic-with-only-25-15 |title=Carlsen beats Predojevic with (only) 2.5–1.5 |publisher=ChessVibes |date=29 June 2013 |access-date=11 July 2013 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130702064143/http://www.chessvibes.com/carlsen-beats-predojevic-with-only-25-15 |archive-date=2 July 2013 |url-status=dead }}</ref>
