In the [[Sinquefield Cup]], held in September, Carlsen finished first, scoring 4½/6 (+3−0=3), a point ahead of Nakamura.<ref>{{cite web |url=http://www.chessbase.com/Home/TabId/211/PostId/4011175/sinquefield-06-magic-magnus-swindles-160913.aspx |title=Sinquefield 06: Magic Magnus Swindles |publisher=ChessBase News |date=15 September 2013 |access-date=16 September 2013 |archive-date=16 September 2013 |archive-url=https://archive.today/20130916055418/http://www.chessbase.com/Home/TabId/211/PostId/4011175/sinquefield-06-magic-magnus-swindles-160913.aspx |url-status=live }}</ref>
==== World Chess Championship 2013 ==== {{Main|World Chess Championship 2013}}
Carlsen faced Anand in the World Chess Championship 2013, at Hyatt Regency in [[Chennai]], India, from 9 to 22 November. Carlsen won the match 6½–3½ by winning games five, six and nine and drawing the remainder, becoming the new World Chess Champion.<ref>{{cite news|url=http://en.chessbase.com/post/chennai-g10-magnus-carlsen-is-the-new-world-champion|title=Chennai G10: Magnus Carlsen is the new World Champion!|date=22 November 2013|publisher=ChessBase News|access-date=22 November 2013|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131124212658/http://en.chessbase.com/post/chennai-g10-magnus-carlsen-is-the-new-world-champion|archive-date=24 November 2013}}</ref> Though he was the challenger, and less experienced than Anand, he handled the pressure with ease. He had his first win in game 5 by taking advantage of a small mistake by Anand, and emerged victorious in games 6 and 9, making him the 16th undisputed World Chess Champion.{{citation needed|date=April 2025}}
:{| class="wikitable" style="text-align:center; background:white; color:black" |+World Chess Championship 2013 |- ! !! Rating !! Rank !! <small>[https://www.chess.com/events/2013-anand-carlsen/01/Magnus_Carlsen-Viswanathan_Anand/ Game 1]<br />9 Nov.</small> !! <small>[https://www.chess.com/events/2013-anand-carlsen/02/Viswanathan_Anand-Magnus_Carlsen/ Game 2]<br />10 Nov.</small> !! <small>[https://www.chess.com/events/2013-anand-carlsen/03/Magnus_Carlsen-Viswanathan_Anand/ Game 3]<br />12 Nov.</small> !! <small>[https://www.chess.com/events/2013-anand-carlsen/04/Viswanathan_Anand-Magnus_Carlsen/ Game 4]<br />13 Nov.</small> !! <small>[https://www.chess.com/events/2013-anand-carlsen/05/Magnus_Carlsen-Viswanathan_Anand/ Game 5]<br />15 Nov.</small> !! <small>[https://www.chess.com/events/2013-anand-carlsen/06/Viswanathan_Anand-Magnus_Carlsen/ Game 6]<br />16 Nov.</small> !! <small>[https://www.chess.com/events/2013-anand-carlsen/07/Viswanathan_Anand-Magnus_Carlsen/ Game 7]<br />18 Nov.</small> !! <small>[https://www.chess.com/events/2013-anand-carlsen/08/Magnus_Carlsen-Viswanathan_Anand/ Game 8]<br />19 Nov.</small> !! <small>[https://www.chess.com/events/2013-anand-carlsen/09/Viswanathan_Anand-Magnus_Carlsen/ Game 9]<br />21 Nov.</small> !! <small>[https://www.chess.com/events/2013-anand-carlsen/10/Magnus_Carlsen-Viswanathan_Anand/ Game 10]<br /> 22 Nov.</small> !! <small>Game 11<br />24 Nov.</small> !! <small>Game 12<br />26 Nov.</small> !! Points |- | align=left | {{flagathlete|[[Viswanathan Anand]]|IND}} || 2775 || 8 | style="background:black; color:white" | ½ || ½ | style="background:black; color:white" | ½ || ½ | style="background:black; color:white" | 0 || 0 | ½ || style="background:black; color:white" | ½ | 0 || style="background:black; color:white" | ½ |colspan="2" rowspan="2" align="center"|Not required | 3½ |- | align=left | '''{{flagathlete|Magnus Carlsen|NOR}}''' || '''2870''' || 1 | ½ || style="background:black; color:white" | ½ | ½ || style="background:black; color:white" | ½ | 1 || style="background:black; color:white" | 1 | style="background:black; color:white" | ½ || ½ | style="background:black; color:white" | 1 || ½ | '''6½''' |}
=== 2014 === From 29 January to 4 February, Carlsen played in the [[Zurich Chess Challenge]], winning the blitz event (+2−1=2) and the classical event (+3−0=2). He fared worse in the rapid event (+1−2=2), which counted towards the overall standings, but retained enough of a lead to win the tournament. The other players in the event were Aronian, Nakamura, Caruana, Gelfand and Anand.<ref>{{cite news |url=http://www.chessdom.com/zurich-chess-challenge-2014/ |title=Zurich Chess Challenge 2014 |date=19 September 2013 |publisher=ChessDom |access-date=23 January 2014 |archive-date=1 October 2018 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20181001013636/http://www.chessdom.com/zurich-chess-challenge-2014/ |url-status=live }}</ref> On 22 March, Carlsen played a game for his club Stavanger in the final team match for promotion to the Norwegian Premier League. His win over [[Vladimir Georgiev (chess player)|Vladimir Georgiev]] helped his team to a 3½–2½ win over Nordstrand.<ref>{{cite news|url=http://www.aftenbladet.no/100Sport/sjakk/Magnus-Carlsen-sjokkerte-med-opprykkskamp-i-norsk-1-divisjon-426932_1.snd#.Uy3sK_nWWQC|title=Magnus Carlsen sjokkerte med opprykkskamp i norsk 1. divisjon|last=Aas|first=Odd Inge|author2=Staveli, Marius|date=22 March 2014|publisher=Stavanger Aftenblad|language=no|access-date=22 March 2014|archive-date=16 May 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200516151152/http://www.aftenbladet.no/100Sport/sjakk/Magnus-Carlsen-sjokkerte-med-opprykkskamp-i-norsk-1-divisjon-426932_1.snd#.Uy3sK_nWWQC|url-status=dead}}</ref>
Carlsen won the [[Shamkir Chess|Vugar Gashimov Memorial]] in [[Shamkir (city)|Şəmkir]], Azerbaijan, played from 20 to 30 April. He played in the A group along with Caruana, Nakamura, Karjakin, [[Shakhriyar Mamedyarov]] and Radjabov. Carlsen started the tournament with 2/2, beating Mamedyarov and Nakamura. He then drew against Karjakin, only to lose two consecutive games for the first time in four years, losing to Caruana with black and then with white to Radjabov. In the second half of the tournament, Carlsen scored 4/5, beating Mamedyarov and Nakamura again, and securing the tournament victory by beating Caruana in the final round, finishing with +5−2=3.<ref>{{cite news |url=http://en.chessbase.com/post/gashimov-r10-from-fool-to-king |title=Gashimov R10: From Fool to King |author=Ramírez, Alejandro |date=30 March 2014 |publisher=ChessBase |access-date=30 March 2014 |archive-date=7 August 2018 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180807115156/https://en.chessbase.com/post/gashimov-r10-from-fool-to-king |url-status=live }}</ref>
On 8 May, Carlsen played an exhibition game at [[Oslo City]] against the people of Norway, assisted by a grandmaster panel consisting of Simen Agdestein, Jon Ludvig Hammer, and [[Leif Erlend Johannessen]]. Each of the panel members proposed a move and the public could then vote over the proposed moves. Each panel member was allowed three chances to let [[chess engine]] [[Houdini (chess)|Houdini]] propose a move during the game. Norway's moves were executed by [[Oddvar Brå]] who was disguised in a red spandex suit for the occasion. The game was drawn when Carlsen forced a [[perpetual check]].<ref>{{cite news|url=http://www.vg.no/sport/sjakk/carlsen-remis-mot-norge-dere-maatte-bruke-houdini-for-aa-ha-en-sjanse/a/10147920/|title=Carlsen-remis mot Norge: – Dere måtte bruke Houdini for å ha en sjanse|last=Bakkehaug|first=Wegard|date=8 May 2014|publisher=VG|language=no|access-date=8 May 2014|archive-date=20 November 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20171120045929/https://www.vg.no/sport/sjakk/carlsen-remis-mot-norge-dere-maatte-bruke-houdini-for-aa-ha-en-sjanse/a/10147920/|url-status=live}}</ref>
From 2–13 June, Carlsen played in the [[Norway Chess#Norway Chess 2014|second edition]] of Norway Chess, a ten-man round robin. He placed second with 5½/9, ½ a point behind the winner Karjakin. Other players in the event were Aronian, Caruana, Topalov, Svidler, Kramnik, Grischuk, Giri and Agdestein.<ref>{{cite news |url=http://www.chessdom.com/norway-chess-2014-six-players-announced/ |title=Norway Chess 2014 – Six Players Announced |date=22 January 2014 |publisher=ChessDom |access-date=27 January 2014 |archive-date=19 February 2019 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190219015758/http://www.chessdom.com/norway-chess-2014-six-players-announced/ |url-status=live }}</ref>
Carlsen won the FIDE World Rapid Championship, which was held in Dubai from 16 to 19 June.<ref>{{cite news|url=http://en.chessbase.com/post/world-rapid-miraculous-carlsen|title=World Rapid: Miraculous Carlsen|access-date=20 June 2014|archive-date=30 November 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20181130113129/https://en.chessbase.com/post/world-rapid-miraculous-carlsen|url-status=live}}</ref> He went on to claim the World Blitz Championship two days later, becoming the first player to simultaneously hold the title in all three FIDE rated time controls.<ref>{{cite news|url=http://en.chessbase.com/post/world-blitz-triple-crown-achieved|title=World Blitz: Triple Crown Achieved!|access-date=20 June 2014|archive-date=11 March 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200311002544/https://en.chessbase.com/post/world-blitz-triple-crown-achieved|url-status=live}}</ref>
Carlsen played nine games for Norway in the [[41st Chess Olympiad]], scoring five wins, two draws, and two losses (against [[Arkadij Naiditsch]] and [[Ivan Šarić (chess player)|Ivan Šarić]]).<ref>{{cite web|url=http://chess-results.com/tnr140380.aspx?lan=1&art=20&flag=30&wi=821&snr=14|title=41st Olympiad Tromso 2014 Open|publisher=chess-results.com|access-date=23 August 2014|archive-date=5 March 2016|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160305071956/http://chess-results.com/tnr140380.aspx?lan=1&art=20&flag=30&wi=821&snr=14|url-status=live}}</ref>
Carlsen placed second to Fabiano Caruana in the [[Sinquefield Cup#Sinquefield Cup 2014|Sinquefield Cup]], a six-player double round robin held from 27 August to 7 September. Billed as the strongest chess tournament ever held, the remaining 4 players in the event were [[Levon Aronian]], [[Hikaru Nakamura]], [[Veselin Topalov]], and [[Maxime Vachier-Lagrave]]. Carlsen lost to Caruana in round 3 and defeated Aronian and Nakamura in rounds 5 and 7, respectively. He finished the tournament with 5½/10 (+2−1=7), three points behind Caruana.<ref>{{cite web |author=Ramirez A |title=Sinquefield 10: Draws end magnificent event |work=Chess News |url=http://en.chessbase.com/post/sinquefield-10-draws-end-magnificent-event |publisher=Chessbase News |date=7 September 2014 |access-date=18 October 2014 |archive-date=7 September 2014 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140907095913/http://en.chessbase.com/post/sinquefield-10-draws-end-magnificent-event |url-status=live }}</ref>
[[File:Speech of the president Putin at the closing ceremony of a world-title match on chess.jpeg|thumb|left|upright=1.2|[[Viswanathan Anand|Anand]] and Carlsen on stage at the closing ceremony of [[2014 World Chess Championship]] in Sochi, Russia ([[Vladimir Putin]] at podium)]]
==== World Chess Championship 2014 ==== {{Main|World Chess Championship 2014}}
Carlsen faced Anand in a match for the title of World Chess Champion in 2014. Anand qualified by winning the 2014 [[Candidates Tournament]]. The rematch was held from 7 to 23 November in [[Sochi]], Russia. After 11 of 12 games, Carlsen led 6½–4½, thereby defending his World Champion title.<ref>{{cite web |author=Silver, Albert |title=Sochi G10: Unrealized opportunities |date=21 November 2014 |url=http://en.chessbase.com/post/sochi-g10-unrealized-opportunities |publisher=ChessBase News |access-date=22 November 2014 |archive-date=25 November 2014 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20141125015310/http://en.chessbase.com/post/sochi-g10-unrealized-opportunities |url-status=live }}</ref>
{| class="wikitable" style="text-align:center; background:white; color:black" |+World Chess Championship 2014 |- ! !! Rating !! Rank !! <small>[http://www.chessbomb.com/arena/2014-wcc/01-Anand_Viswanathan-Carlsen_Magnus Game 1]<br />8 Nov.</small> !! <small>[http://www.chessbomb.com/arena/2014-wcc/02-Carlsen_Magnus-Anand_Viswanathan Game 2]<br />9 Nov.</small> !! <small>[http://www.chessbomb.com/arena/2014-wcc/03-Anand_Viswanathan-Carlsen_Magnus Game 3]<br />11 Nov.</small> !! <small>[http://www.chessbomb.com/arena/2014-wcc/04-Carlsen_Magnus-Anand_Viswanathan Game 4]<br />12 Nov.</small> !! <small>[http://www.chessbomb.com/arena/2014-wcc/05-Anand_Viswanathan-Carlsen_Magnus Game 5]<br />14 Nov.</small> !! <small>[http://www.chessbomb.com/arena/2014-wcc/06-Carlsen_Magnus-Anand_Viswanathan Game 6]<br />15 Nov.</small> !! <small>[http://www.chessbomb.com/arena/2014-wcc/07-Carlsen_Magnus-Anand_Viswanathan Game 7]<br />17 Nov.</small> !! <small>[http://www.chessbomb.com/arena/2014-wcc/08-Anand_Viswanathan-Carlsen_Magnus Game 8]<br />18 Nov.</small> !! <small>[http://www.chessbomb.com/arena/2014-wcc/09-Carlsen_Magnus-Anand_Viswanathan Game 9]<br />20 Nov.</small> !! <small>[http://www.chessbomb.com/arena/2014-wcc/10-Anand_Viswanathan-Carlsen_Magnus Game 10]<br />21 Nov.</small> !! <small>[http://www.chessbomb.com/arena/2014-wcc/11-Carlsen_Magnus-Anand_Viswanathan Game 11]<br />23 Nov.</small> !! <small>Game 12<br />25 Nov.</small>!! Points |- | align=left | '''{{flagathlete|Magnus Carlsen|NOR}}''' || '''2863''' || 1 | style="background:black; color:white" | ½ || 1 | style="background:black; color:white" | 0 || ½ | style="background:black; color:white" | ½ || 1 | ½ || style="background:black; color:white" | ½ | ½ || style="background:black; color:white" | ½ | 1 || rowspan="2" | Not<br />required | '''6½''' |- | align=left | {{flagathlete|[[Viswanathan Anand]]|IND}} || 2792 || 6 | ½ || style="background:black; color:white" | 0 | 1 || style="background:black; color:white" | ½ | ½ || style="background:black; color:white" | 0 | style="background:black; color:white" | ½ || ½ | style="background:black; color:white" | ½ || ½ | style="background:black; color:white" | 0 | 4½ |}
=== 2015 === In January, Carlsen won the [[Tata Steel Chess Tournament#2015|77th Tata Steel Chess Tournament]], which was played mainly in Wijk aan Zee on 9–25 January. Carlsen had a poor start to the tournament with two draws and a loss in the third round to [[Radosław Wojtaszek]], which left him in tenth place among the fourteen players. However, six consecutive wins thrust Carlsen into clear first place. Drawing the final four games was sufficient to win the tournament with 9 points out of 13, half a point ahead of Anish Giri, Maxime Vachier-Lagrave, [[Wesley So]] and [[Ding Liren]].<ref>{{cite news|url=http://www.tv2.no/a/6490158|title=Carlsen vant Tata Steel etter remis mot Saric|last=Hystad|first=Joar|author2=Hansen, Erle Marki|date=25 January 2015|publisher=TV2|language=no|access-date=25 January 2015|archive-date=7 August 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180807121458/https://www.tv2.no/a/6490158/|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.chessgames.com/perl/chess.pl?tid=85130|title=Tata Steel Masters (2015)|website=Chessgames.com|access-date=7 November 2021|archive-date=6 October 2019|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20191006052810/http://www.chessgames.com/perl/chess.pl?tid=85130|url-status=live}}</ref>
In February, Carlsen won the 3rd [[Grenke Chess Classic]] after a five-game tiebreak with Arkadij Naiditsch. Carlsen had finished joint first with Naiditsch on 4½/7, beating Michael Adams, Anand, and [[David Baramidze]], and losing to Naiditsch in their classical encounter.<ref>{{cite web |author=Ramirez, Alejandro |title=GRENKE Final: Carlsen wins in Armageddon! |url=http://en.chessbase.com/post/grenke-final-carlsen-wins-in-armageddon |publisher=Chessbase News |date=10 February 2015 |access-date=10 February 2015 |archive-date=10 February 2015 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150210093410/http://en.chessbase.com/post/grenke-final-carlsen-wins-in-armageddon |url-status=live }}</ref> This tournament victory meant that Carlsen began 2015 by winning two out of two tournaments. Carlsen continued his streak in April, winning [[Shamkir Chess]] with a score of 7/9 (+5−0=4), defeating Mamedyarov, Caruana, Vachier-Lagrave, Kramnik, and [[Rauf Mamedov]]. With a performance rating of 2983, this was Carlsen's third best tournament result ever, behind only Nanjing 2009 (3002 TPR) and London 2012 (2994 TPR).<ref>{{cite web|url=https://chess24.com/en/read/news/shamkir-chess-2015-winners-and-losers|title=Shamkir Chess 2015: Winners and losers|first=Colin|last=McGourty|publisher=Chess24|date=28 April 2015|access-date=3 August 2017|archive-date=10 April 2019|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190410213924/https://chess24.com/en/read/news/shamkir-chess-2015-winners-and-losers|url-status=live}}</ref>
Carlsen had a poor result in the [[Norway Chess#Norway Chess 2015|third edition]] of Norway Chess, held 15–26 June. In the first round he obtained a winning position against Topalov after pressing in a long endgame, only to lose on time when he mistakenly thought that he would receive 15 minutes of extra time at move 60.<ref>{{cite news|url=http://en.chessbase.com/post/norway-01-spectacular-round|title=Norway 01: Spectacular Round!|last=Ramirez|first=Alejandro|date=16 June 2016|publisher=ChessBase|access-date=18 June 2015|archive-date=7 November 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20181107002751/http://en.chessbase.com/post/norway-01-spectacular-round|url-status=live}}</ref> He was then outplayed by Caruana in the second round, missed a win against Anish Giri in round 3, and lost to Anand in round 4. He won against Grischuk in round 5, drew against Nakamura and Vachier-Lagrave in rounds 6 and 7, and defeated Aronian in round 8, but he lost the last round against Jon Ludvig Hammer, leaving him in seventh place and with a performance rating of 2693.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.chessbomb.com/arena/2015-norway|title=Norway Chess 2015|publisher=Chessbomb.com|access-date=25 June 2015|archive-date=6 November 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20181106191114/https://www.chessbomb.com/arena/2015-norway|url-status=live}}</ref> Carlsen said of this result: "It's just extremely frustrating not to be able to show anything close to what I am capable of in my home country."<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.telegraph.co.uk/culture/chess/malcolmpein/11701271/Hammer-blow-for-Magnus-Carlsen-as-Topalov-wins-Norway-Chess.html |archive-url=https://ghostarchive.org/archive/20220110/https://www.telegraph.co.uk/culture/chess/malcolmpein/11701271/Hammer-blow-for-Magnus-Carlsen-as-Topalov-wins-Norway-Chess.html |archive-date=10 January 2022 |url-access=subscription |url-status=live|title=Hammer blow for Magnus Carlsen as Topalov wins Norway Chess|first=Malcolm|last=Pein|website=The Telegraph|date=26 June 2015}}{{cbignore}}</ref>
From 22 August to 3 September, Carlsen played in the [[Sinquefield Cup#Sinquefield Cup 2015|2015 Sinquefield Cup]]. He finished in second place with 5/9 (+3−2=4), one point behind winner Levon Aronian. He defeated the 2014 Sinquefield winner Fabiano Caruana, as well as Maxime Vachier-Lagrave and wild-card Wesley So, but lost to Veselin Topalov and Alexander Grischuk.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://en.chessbase.com/post/sinquefield-09-aronian-wins-tournament|title=Sinquefield 09: Aronian wins tournament|first=Alejandro|last=Ramirez|publisher=ChessBase|date=2 September 2015|access-date=3 August 2017|archive-date=4 September 2015|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150904211417/http://en.chessbase.com/post/sinquefield-09-aronian-wins-tournament|url-status=live}}</ref>
[[File:Schnellschachweltmeister Carlsen bei der Siegerehrung mit den Nächstplatzierten Nepomnjaschtschi (re.) und Radjabov in Berlin 2015.JPG|thumb|upright|Carlsen at the FIDE World Rapid Championship 2015 award ceremony]] In October, Carlsen successfully defended his title in the FIDE World Rapid Championships held in Berlin, as the first World Rapid Champion to do so in history, going +8−0=7.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://worldchess.com/2015/10/12/carlsen-is-2015-world-rapid-champion/|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20181106190943/https://worldchess.com/2015/10/12/carlsen-is-2015-world-rapid-champion/|url-status=dead|archive-date=6 November 2018|title=FIDE World Championships 2015|publisher=WorldChess.com|access-date=12 October 2015}}</ref> He reached the highest live rapid rating in history after the tournament, and was at that point ranked {{Numero|1}} in all three disciplines simultaneously.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.2700chess.com/|title=Chess Live Ratings|publisher=2700chess.com|access-date=12 October 2015|archive-date=11 October 2015|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20151011081700/http://www.2700chess.com/|url-status=live}}</ref>{{Verify source|date=July 2019}} However, Carlsen lost his {{Numero|1}} blitz ranking after he had a weak second day in the World Blitz Championship, and was unable to retain his World Blitz Champion title, losing it to [[Alexander Grischuk]].<ref>{{cite web|url=http://en.chessbase.com/post/world-blitz-2015-grischuk-wins-third-title|title=World Blitz 2015: Grischuk wins third title|first=Albert|last=Silver|publisher=ChessBase|date=14 October 2015|access-date=3 August 2017|archive-date=9 November 2015|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20151109153230/http://en.chessbase.com/post/world-blitz-2015-grischuk-wins-third-title|url-status=live}}</ref>
In November, Carlsen participated in the European Team Chess Championship with the Norwegian team. He started off poorly, scoring ½ points out of 3 games, losing to Levon Aronian, drawing against [[Sune Berg Hansen]], and losing again to [[Yannick Pelletier]] due to a [[Blunder (chess)|blunder]]. However, he finished the tournament strongly, scoring victories against [[Peter Leko]] and [[Radoslaw Wojtaszek]], the latter of whom he had lost to earlier in the year, but his performance was not enough to earn his team a medal, and he lost 16 rating points during the event.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://en.chessbase.com/post/etcc-r09-russia-triumphs-with-double-gold|title=ETCC R09: Russia triumphs with double gold|first=Albert|last=Silver|publisher=ChessBase|date=24 November 2015|access-date=3 August 2017|archive-date=23 November 2015|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20151123182751/http://en.chessbase.com/post/etcc-r09-russia-triumphs-with-double-gold|url-status=live}}</ref>
From 4–13 December, Carlsen participated in the final leg of the [[Grand Chess Tour]], the [[London Chess Classic#2015 Classic: 4–13 December|London Chess Classic]]. He scored 5½/9 (+2−0=7) in the event, defeating Nakamura (thus inflicting Nakamura's 12th classical loss to Carlsen) and Grischuk, and finished joint first with [[Anish Giri]] and [[Maxime Vachier-Lagrave]]. In the 3-way tiebreak, Carlsen was the top seed, meaning he faced the winner of the first tie-break match between Giri and Vachier-Lagrave. Carlsen eventually won the tournament by defeating Vachier-Lagrave, which meant he also won the overall Grand Chess Tour.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://en.chessbase.com/post/huffington-carlsen-wins-grand-chess-tour|title=Carlsen Wins Grand Chess Tour|first=Lubomir|last=Kavalek|publisher=ChessBase|date=19 December 2015|access-date=3 August 2017|archive-date=3 August 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170803090407/http://en.chessbase.com/post/huffington-carlsen-wins-grand-chess-tour|url-status=live}}</ref> Carlsen then played in the second edition of the [[Qatar Masters Open]], which was held from 20 to 29 December. He finished joint first with 7/9 (+5−0=4), and defeated [[Yu Yangyi]] in a tie-break match to win the tournament.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://en.chessbase.com/post/qatar-2015-rd9-carlsen-takes-title-in-thrilling-final-round|title=Qatar 2015 Rd9: Carlsen takes title in thrilling final round|first=Albert|last=Silver|publisher=ChessBase|date=29 December 2015|access-date=3 August 2017|archive-date=1 January 2016|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160101155915/http://en.chessbase.com/post/qatar-2015-rd9-carlsen-takes-title-in-thrilling-final-round|url-status=live}}</ref>
=== 2016 === From 15 to 31 January, Carlsen participated in the [[Tata Steel Chess Tournament#2016|78th Tata Steel Chess Tournament]], held in Wijk Aan Zee. Carlsen won the tournament by scoring 9 points out of 13 (+5−0=8), earning him his 5th Wijk Aan Zee title.<ref>{{cite web |author=Silver A. |title=Tata Steel Rd13: Magnus Carlsen wins fifth title |url=http://en.chessbase.com/post/tata-steel-rd13-magnus-carlsen-wins-fifth-title |publisher=ChessBase News |date=31 January 2016 |access-date=31 January 2016 |archive-date=2 February 2016 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160202171643/http://en.chessbase.com/post/tata-steel-rd13-magnus-carlsen-wins-fifth-title |url-status=live }}</ref>
From 18 to 30 April, Carlsen played in the [[Norway Chess#Norway Chess 2016|fourth edition]] of the Norway Chess Tournament. He finished in first place with 6/9 (+4−1=4), half a point ahead of [[Levon Aronian]], and a full point in front of [[Vladimir Kramnik]], [[Veselin Topalov]] and [[Maxime Vachier-Lagrave]]. This was Carlsen's first Norway Chess victory.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://en.chessbase.com/post/carlsen-wins-norway-chess|title=Carlsen wins Norway Chess|first=André|last=Schulz|publisher=ChessBase|date=29 April 2016|access-date=3 August 2017|archive-date=3 August 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170803100644/http://en.chessbase.com/post/carlsen-wins-norway-chess|url-status=live}}</ref>
From 17 to 20 June, Carlsen played in the Leuven leg of the Grand Chess Tour. He finished first in the rapid portion of the tournament with 12/18 (+5−2=2; two points for a win) and also first in the blitz portion with 11/18 (+7−3=8) to place first overall with a score of 23/36, two and a half points ahead of the runner-up, [[Wesley So]].<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.chess.com/news/view/magnus-carlsen-cruises-to-victory-in-leuven-9816|title=Magnus Carlsen Cruises To Victory In Leuven|last=Doggers (PeterDoggers)|first=Peter|website=Chess.com|date=22 June 2016|language=en-US|access-date=24 March 2019|archive-date=24 March 2019|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190324071750/https://www.chess.com/news/view/magnus-carlsen-cruises-to-victory-in-leuven-9816|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=http://theweekinchess.com/html/twic1128.html#2|title=The Week in Chess 1128|website=theweekinchess.com|access-date=24 March 2019|archive-date=14 March 2019|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190314074948/http://theweekinchess.com/html/twic1128.html#2|url-status=live}}</ref>
In July, Carlsen won the 9th edition of the Bilbao Masters Final, scoring 17 points out of 10 games (+4−1=5; a win was 3 points, a draw was 1 point). His only loss was to [[Hikaru Nakamura]], who had never beaten Carlsen in classical chess before. Also during this tournament, Carlsen recorded his first victory over [[Anish Giri]] in a classical game.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://en.chessbase.com/post/bilbao-rd-9-carlsen-beats-giri-and-wins-tournament|title=Bilbao, Rd. 9: Carlsen beats Giri and wins tournament|publisher=ChessBase|first=André|last=Schulz|work=Chess News |date=22 July 2016|access-date=30 June 2017|archive-date=25 December 2016|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20161225144335/http://en.chessbase.com/post/bilbao-rd-9-carlsen-beats-giri-and-wins-tournament|url-status=live}}</ref>
[[File:Carlsen Magnus (30238051906).jpg|thumb|upright|Carlsen at the 42nd Chess Olympiad, 8 September 2016]]
Carlsen played ten games for Norway in the [[42nd Chess Olympiad]], scoring five wins and five draws, as the Norwegians placed 5th among the 180 teams in the open event.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://chess-results.com/tnr232875.aspx?lan=1&art=20&flag=30&wi=821&snr=12|title=42nd Olympiad Baku 2016 Open|publisher=chess-results.com|access-date=15 September 2016|archive-date=16 May 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200516151240/http://chess-results.com/tnr232875.aspx?lan=1&art=20&flag=30&wi=821&snr=12|url-status=live}}</ref>
Carlsen also featured in Chess.com's Grandmaster Blitz Battle Championship. Magnus defeated [[Tigran L. Petrosian]] 21 to 4 in the first round, and beat [[Alexander Grischuk]] 16 to 8 in the semi-final. On 27 October, he faced Nakamura in the final. Playing both blitz and bullet chess for a total of three hours, Carlsen defeated Nakamura 14½ to 10½ and became the first winner of the Chess.com Grandmaster Blitz Battle Championship.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://en.chessbase.com/post/carlsen-beats-nakamura-in-3-hour-blitz-battle|title=Carlsen beats Nakamura in 3-hour blitz battle|first=Albert|last=Silver|publisher=ChessBase|date=28 October 2016|access-date=3 August 2017|archive-date=3 August 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170803091315/http://en.chessbase.com/post/carlsen-beats-nakamura-in-3-hour-blitz-battle|url-status=live}}</ref>
From 26 to 30 December, Carlsen played in the World Rapid and Blitz Championships held in [[Doha]], Qatar. He scored 11/15 in the rapid tournament, finishing third on tiebreak behind Ivanchuk and Grischuk.<ref>{{cite web|last1=Doggers|first1=Peter|title=Ivanchuk Wins World Rapid Championship|url=https://www.chess.com/news/ivanchuk-wins-world-rapid-championship-2103|website=Chess.com|date=29 December 2016|access-date=10 October 2017|archive-date=16 May 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200516151242/https://www.chess.com/news/ivanchuk-wins-world-rapid-championship-2103|url-status=live}}</ref> In the blitz tournament, he scored 16½/21, finishing second on tiebreak behind Karjakin, and 2 points clear of the rest of the field.<ref>{{cite web|last1=Doggers|first1=Peter|title=Karjakin Wins World Blitz Championship|url=https://www.chess.com/news/karjakin-wins-world-blitz-championship-9527|website=Chess.com|date=31 December 2016|access-date=10 October 2017|archive-date=16 May 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200516151249/https://www.chess.com/news/karjakin-wins-world-blitz-championship-9527|url-status=live}}</ref>
==== World Chess Championship 2016 ==== {{Main|World Chess Championship 2016}}
Carlsen faced [[Sergey Karjakin]] in the 2016 [[World Chess Championship 2016|World Chess Championship]] in New York City. The 12-game standard match, held between 11 and 28 November, concluded with a 6–6 tie. The match began with seven consecutive draws. Karjakin drew first blood by winning game 8, but Carlsen equalised the match in game 10. Games 11 and 12 were both drawn. The tiebreaking games were held on 30 November, Carlsen's 26th birthday. After drawing games 1 and 2, Carlsen won games 3 and 4 to record a 3–1 victory and retain his World Champion title.{{citation needed|date=April 2025}}

=== 2017 === [[File:Tata Steel 2017 Magnus Carlsen (cropped).png|thumb|Carlsen at the [[79th Tata Steel Chess Tournament]], 29 January 2017]] In January, Carlsen participated in the [[79th Tata Steel Chess Tournament]]. He started well, scoring 2 wins and 4 draws in his first 6 games, but missed mate-in-3 versus Giri in round 7, which Giri described as "the most embarrassing moment" of Carlsen's career.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://chess24.com/en/read/news/tata-steel-2017-7-magnus-most-embarrassing-moment|title=Tata Steel 2017, 7: Magnus' most embarrassing moment?|first=Colin|last=McGourty|publisher=Chess24|date=22 January 2017|access-date=30 June 2017|archive-date=1 January 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200101102701/https://chess24.com/en/read/news/tata-steel-2017-7-magnus-most-embarrassing-moment|url-status=live}}</ref> Carlsen then lost in round 8 to [[Richárd Rapport]], and ultimately placed second with 8/13 (+4−1=8), one point behind winner Wesley So.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://en.chessbase.com/post/2017-tata-rd13-deserved-winner|title=2017 Tata Rd13: Deserved Winner|publisher=ChessBase|first=Alejandro|last=Ramirez|date=29 January 2017|access-date=30 June 2017|archive-date=30 June 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170630151928/http://en.chessbase.com/post/2017-tata-rd13-deserved-winner|url-status=live}}</ref>
From 13 to 22 April, Carlsen competed in the [[Grenke Chess Classic#2017|4th Grenke Chess Classic]], finishing in joint second place, though third on tiebreaks, with Fabiano Caruana, with a score of 4/7 (+1−0=6). The clear winner with 5½ points (+4−0=3) was Levon Aronian. As a result, Carlsen's FIDE rating dropped to 2832, his lowest since November 2011 (2826).<ref>{{cite web|url=https://en.chessbase.com/post/grenke-classic-2017-aronian-misses-win-in-exciting-finale|title=Grenke Classic 2017: Aronian misses win in exciting finale|first=Elshan|last=Moradiabadi|publisher=ChessBase|date=23 April 2017|access-date=24 June 2017|archive-date=25 August 2019|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190825071807/https://en.chessbase.com/post/grenke-classic-2017-aronian-misses-win-in-exciting-finale|url-status=live}}</ref>
Carlsen participated in the [[Norway Chess#Norway Chess 2017|fifth edition]] of Norway Chess from 6 to 16 June. He performed poorly and had a {{chessgloss|performance rating}} of 2755, his lowest since 2015 (2670, at the European Team Chess Championship). Ultimately, he placed ninth in the [[round-robin tournament]] with 4/9 (+1−2=6), losing to Aronian and Kramnik and winning against Karjakin. Aronian won the tournament with 6/9 (+3−0=6).<ref>{{cite web|url=http://en.chessbase.com/post/levon-aronian-wins-norway-chess-2017|title=Levon Aronian wins Norway Chess 2017!|publisher=ChessBase|first=Alex|last=Yermolinsky|date=16 June 2017|access-date=24 June 2017|archive-date=20 June 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170620195811/http://en.chessbase.com/post/levon-aronian-wins-norway-chess-2017|url-status=live}}</ref>
Carlsen played in the Paris leg of the [[Grand Chess Tour#Grand Chess Tour 2017|2017 Grand Chess Tour]], which was held from 21 to 25 June. He finished first in the rapid portion of the tournament with 14/18 (+5−0=4; two points for a win) and fifth in the blitz portion with 10/18 (+8−6=4) to place joint first overall with Vachier-Lagrave. Carlsen then defeated Vachier-Lagrave in the playoff to win the tournament.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://en.chessbase.com/post/magnus-carlsen-wins-grand-chess-tour-paris-in-exciting-finish|title=Magnus Carlsen wins Grand Chess Tour – Paris in exciting finish|publisher=ChessBase|first=Alex|last=Yermolinsky|date=26 June 2017|access-date=29 June 2017|archive-date=28 June 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170628231646/http://en.chessbase.com/post/magnus-carlsen-wins-grand-chess-tour-paris-in-exciting-finish|url-status=live}}</ref>
From 28 June to 2 July, he played in the Leuven leg of the Grand Chess Tour. He won this tournament convincingly, scoring 11/18 (+3−1=5; two points for a win) in the rapid portion and 14½/18 (+12−1=5) in the blitz portion for an overall score of 25½/36, three points ahead of the runner-up, Wesley So.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://en.chessbase.com/post/grand-chess-tour-leuven-express-magnus-carlsen-runs-away-with-event|title=Grand Chess Tour – Leuven Express: Magnus Carlsen runs away with event|publisher=ChessBase|first=Alex|last=Yermolinsky|date=2 July 2017|access-date=3 July 2017|archive-date=2 July 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170702191231/http://en.chessbase.com/post/grand-chess-tour-leuven-express-magnus-carlsen-runs-away-with-event|url-status=live}}</ref> Carlsen's performance rating in the blitz portion of the tournament was 3018, which Garry Kasparov described as "phenomenal".<ref>{{cite web|url=https://twitter.com/Kasparov63/status/881561338052562944|quote=Phenomenal performance by Magnus. As I said during the round, it looked like everyone else was playing blitz while he was playing chess!|first=Garry|last=Kasparov|title=Garry Kasparov on Twitter|date=2 July 2017|access-date=2 July 2017|archive-date=16 May 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200516151252/https://twitter.com/Kasparov63/status/881561338052562944|url-status=live}}</ref>{{Primary source inline|date=November 2018}} Additionally, [[Leonard Barden]], writing for ''The Guardian'', said the performance was only matched by Fischer's 19/22 score at the [[World Blitz Chess Championship#Herceg Novi Blitz Tournament of 1970|1970 World Blitz Championship]].<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2017/jul/07/magnus-carlsen-leuven-blitz-chess|title=Magnus Carlsen blitzes the field with record-breaking display in Belgium|first=Leonard|last=Barden|website=The Guardian|date=7 July 2017|access-date=10 July 2017|archive-date=30 November 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20181130030446/https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2017/jul/07/magnus-carlsen-leuven-blitz-chess|url-status=live}}</ref>
From 2 to 11 August, Carlsen competed in the [[Sinquefield Cup#Sinquefield Cup 2017|Sinquefield Cup]], the first classical event of the Grand Chess Tour. He finished joint second with Anand, scoring 5½/9 (+3−1=5). He recorded three victories (against Karjakin, So and Aronian) and his one loss was to Vachier-Lagrave, who won the tournament with 6/9 (+3−0=6).<ref>{{cite web|url=http://en.chessbase.com/post/grand-chess-tour-sinquefield-cup-live|title=Sinquefield Cup: MVL WINS!|author=Staff writer(s)|publisher=ChessBase|date=11 August 2017|access-date=12 August 2017|archive-date=12 August 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170812101306/http://en.chessbase.com/post/grand-chess-tour-sinquefield-cup-live|url-status=live}}</ref> This result left Carlsen in first place in the Grand Chess Tour standings with 34 points, three points ahead of second place Vachier-Lagrave.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://grandchesstour.org/2017-tour-standings|title=2017 Tour Standings|publisher=Grand Chess Tour|date=11 August 2017|author=Staff writer(s)|access-date=15 August 2017|archive-date=15 August 2017|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170815115252/http://grandchesstour.org/2017-tour-standings|url-status=live}}</ref>
In September, he took part in the [[Chess World Cup 2017]]. His participation in the event as World Champion was unusual as the World Cup is part of the cycle to [[World Chess Championship 2018#Candidates Tournament|challenge the World Champion in 2018]].<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.chess.com/news/view/magnus-carlsen-to-play-fide-world-cup-1400|title=Magnus Carlsen To Play FIDE World Cup|first=Peter|last=Doggers|publisher=Chess.com|date=24 July 2017|access-date=9 September 2017|archive-date=6 April 2019|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190406204811/https://www.chess.com/news/view/magnus-carlsen-to-play-fide-world-cup-1400|url-status=live}}</ref> He defeated [[Oluwafemi Balogun]] +2−0=0 in the first round to advance to the second round, where he defeated [[Aleksey Dreev]] +2−0=0. He was then defeated in the third round by [[Bu Xiangzhi]] +0−1=1 and eliminated from the tournament.<ref>{{cite web|url =https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2017/sep/15/world-cup-grandmasters-tbilisi-chess|title =Cull of the grandmasters as World Cup quarter-final line-up is completed|first =Leonard|last =Barden|website =The Guardian|date =15 September 2017|access-date =1 October 2017|archive-date =14 April 2020|archive-url =https://web.archive.org/web/20200414092508/https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2017/sep/15/world-cup-grandmasters-tbilisi-chess|url-status =live}}</ref>
On 1 October, Carlsen won the Isle of Man Open, a tournament held from 23 September to 1 October. He scored 7½/9 (+6−0=3), half a point ahead of Nakamura and Anand, for a performance rating of 2903. This was Carlsen's first classical tournament victory in 435 days.<ref>{{cite web|url =https://www.chess.com/news/view/carlsen-wins-2017-chess-com-isle-of-man-international|title =Carlsen Wins 2017 Chess.com Isle of Man International|first =Mike|last =Klein|publisher =Chess.com|date =1 October 2017|access-date =1 October 2017|archive-date =15 January 2020|archive-url =https://web.archive.org/web/20200115160342/https://www.chess.com/news/view/carlsen-wins-2017-chess-com-isle-of-man-international|url-status =live}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url =https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2017/oct/06/magnus-carlsen-extends-world-rankings-lead|title =Magnus Carlsen extends gap at top of world rankings with Isle of Man win|first =Leonard|last =Barden|website =The Guardian|date =6 October 2017|access-date =8 October 2017|archive-date =15 January 2020|archive-url =https://web.archive.org/web/20200115160314/https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2017/oct/06/magnus-carlsen-extends-world-rankings-lead|url-status =live}}</ref>
From 9 to 14 November, Carlsen faced Ding Liren in the 2017 Champions Showdown, a match consisting of 10 rapid and 20 blitz games, hosted by the Saint Louis Chess Club.<ref>{{cite web|url =https://www.uschesschamps.com/news-2017-champions-showdown/2017-champions-showdown-day-6|title =2017 Champions Showdown – Day 6|website =uschesschamps.com|date =14 November 2017|access-date =14 December 2017|archive-date =30 November 2018|archive-url =https://web.archive.org/web/20181130030534/https://www.uschesschamps.com/news-2017-champions-showdown/2017-champions-showdown-day-6|url-status =live}}</ref> Carlsen won, scoring 22–8 (+16−2=12).<ref>{{cite web |url=https://chess24.com/en/watch/live-tournaments/champions-showdown-carlsen-ding-2017 |title=Carlsen vs. Ding Liren – Champions Showdown 2017 |website=chess24.com |access-date=14 December 2017 |archive-date=1 December 2017 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20171201040550/https://chess24.com/en/watch/live-tournaments/champions-showdown-carlsen-ding-2017 |url-status=live }}</ref>
From 1 to 11 December, Carlsen competed in the [[London Chess Classic#2017 Classic: 1–11 December|2017 London Chess Classic]], the final event of the 2017 Grand Chess Tour. He finished fifth, scoring 5/9 (+2−1=6). Caruana shared first place with Nepomniachtchi on 6/9 (+3−0=6) and won the tournament after defeating Nepomniachtchi 2½–1½ in the blitz tiebreak. Carlsen's placing awarded him 7 additional points in the Grand Chess Tour standings, which was enough to crown him the 2017 Grand Chess Tour champion.<ref>{{cite web|url =https://en.chessbase.com/post/london-chess-classic-2017-carlsen-wins-grand-chess-tour|title =Caruana wins London Classic, but Carlsen takes the Tour|first =Macauley|last =Peterson|publisher =ChessBase|date =12 December 2017|access-date =12 December 2017|archive-date =12 January 2018|archive-url =https://web.archive.org/web/20180112201031/https://en.chessbase.com/post/london-chess-classic-2017-carlsen-wins-grand-chess-tour|url-status =live}}</ref>
From 26 to 30 December, Carlsen played in the 2017 World Rapid and World Blitz Chess Championships, held in [[Riyadh]], Saudi Arabia. He finished fifth in the rapid event, scoring 10/15 (+8−3=4). Anand shared first place with [[Vladimir Fedoseev]] on 10½/15, and won the tournament after defeating Fedoseev on tiebreak.<ref>{{cite web|url =http://riyadh2017.fide.com/rapid-standings/|title =FINAL Standings Rapid OPEN|author =Staff writer(s)|publisher =FIDE|date =28 December 2017|access-date =30 December 2017|archive-date =10 November 2019|archive-url =https://web.archive.org/web/20191110084106/http://riyadh2017.fide.com/rapid-standings/|url-status =live}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url =https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2017/dec/29/vishy-anand-surprise-world-rapid-victory|title =Veteran Vishy Anand secures surprise title at world rapids in Riyadh|first =Leonard|last =Barden|website =The Guardian|date =29 December 2017|access-date =30 December 2017|archive-date =30 December 2019|archive-url =https://web.archive.org/web/20191230215732/https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2017/dec/29/vishy-anand-surprise-world-rapid-victory|url-status =live}}</ref> Carlsen won the blitz event, scoring 16/21 (+13−2=6), one and a half points ahead of his nearest competitors, Karjakin and Anand. This was Carlsen's third World Blitz Chess Championship victory.<ref>{{cite web|url =http://riyadh2017.fide.com/2017/12/30/carlsen-and-dzagnidze-are-world-blitz-champions/|title =Carlsen and Dzagnidze are World Blitz Champions!|author =Staff writer(s)|publisher =FIDE|date =30 December 2017|access-date =30 December 2017|archive-date =12 September 2019|archive-url =https://web.archive.org/web/20190912214051/http://riyadh2017.fide.com/2017/12/30/carlsen-and-dzagnidze-are-world-blitz-champions/|url-status =live}}</ref>
=== 2018 === [[File:Caruana-Carlsen 2018 Karlsruhe.jpg|thumb|Carlsen facing Caruana at the [[Grenke Chess Classic#2018|5th Grenke Chess Classic]], 31 March 2018]] From October 2017 to January 2018, Carlsen played in the second edition of Chess.com's Speed Chess Championship.<ref>{{cite web|url =https://www.chess.com/article/view/2018-speed-chess-championship-official-schedule-players-prizes-information|title =2018 Speed Chess Championship Official Schedule, Players, Prizes, Information|date =3 July 2018|website =chess.com|access-date =3 July 2018|archive-date =28 March 2020|archive-url =https://web.archive.org/web/20200328150048/https://www.chess.com/article/view/2018-speed-chess-championship-official-schedule-players-prizes-information|url-status =live}}</ref> He defeated [[Gadir Guseinov]], So and Grischuk in the first three rounds 20½–5½, 27½–9½ and 15½–10½, respectively. On 3 January he defeated Nakamura 18–9 in the final, thus winning the tournament for a consecutive time.<ref>{{cite web|url =http://theweekinchess.com/chessnews/events/chess.com-speed-chess-championship-2017|title =Chess.com Speed Chess Championship 2017|first =Mark|last =Crowther|date =4 January 2018|website =The Week in Chess|access-date =4 January 2018|archive-date =4 January 2018|archive-url =https://web.archive.org/web/20180104132437/http://theweekinchess.com/chessnews/events/chess.com-speed-chess-championship-2017|url-status =live}}</ref>
From 13 to 28 January, Carlsen competed in the [[80th Tata Steel Chess Tournament]]. He placed joint first with Giri, scoring 9/13 (+5−0=8).<ref name="Guardian Tata Steel 2018"/> Carlsen then defeated Giri 1½–½ in the blitz playoff, thus winning the tournament for a record sixth time.<ref name="Guardian Tata Steel 2018">{{cite web|url =https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2018/feb/02/magnus-carlsen-back-to-form-wijk-victory|title =Magnus Carlsen storms back into form with Wijk aan Zee victory|first =Leonard|last =Barden|website =The Guardian|date =2 February 2018|access-date =14 February 2018|archive-date =2 April 2019|archive-url =https://web.archive.org/web/20190402154210/https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2018/feb/02/magnus-carlsen-back-to-form-wijk-victory|url-status =live}}</ref> In February, Carlsen won the unofficial [[Chess960|Fischer Random]] Chess Championship, defeating Nakamura by a score of 14–10.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.frchess.com/|title=Fischer Random 2018|publisher=Fischer Random Chess|date=13 February 2018|author=Staff writer(s)|access-date=14 February 2018|archive-date=3 November 2019|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20191103023820/https://www.frchess.com/|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.chess.com/news/view/carlsen-beats-nakamura-14-10-in-fischer-random-chess|title=Carlsen Wins Fischer Random Chess Championship|last=Doggers|first=Peter|publisher=Chess.com|date=13 February 2018|access-date=14 February 2018|archive-date=23 January 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200123144846/https://www.chess.com/news/view/carlsen-beats-nakamura-14-10-in-fischer-random-chess|url-status=live}}</ref>
Carlsen placed second with a score of 5½/9 (+2−0=7) in the [[Grenke Chess Classic#2018|5th Grenke Chess Classic]], held from 31 March to 9 April. Caruana won the event with 6½/9 (+4−0=5).<ref>{{cite web|url =http://www.grenkechessclassic.de/en/grenke-chess-classic-2015/pairings-standings|title =GRENKE Chess Classic 2018: Pairings & standings|publisher =Grenke Chess|author =Staff writer(s)|date =9 April 2018|access-date =10 April 2018|archive-date =20 October 2019|archive-url =https://web.archive.org/web/20191020142306/http://grenkechessclassic.de/en/grenke-chess-classic-2015/pairings-standings|url-status =dead}}</ref> Carlsen won the [[Shamkir Chess#2018|fifth edition]] of Shamkir Chess, held from 18 to 28 April, finishing clear first with a score of 6/9 (+3−0=6).<ref>{{cite web|url =https://www.chess.com/news/view/carlsen-wins-shamkir-chess-after-quick-draw-with-ding|title =Carlsen Wins Shamkir Chess After Quick Draw With Ding|first =Peter|last =Doggers|publisher =Chess.com|date =28 April 2018|access-date =28 April 2018|archive-date =28 March 2020|archive-url =https://web.archive.org/web/20200328144615/https://www.chess.com/news/view/carlsen-wins-shamkir-chess-after-quick-draw-with-ding|url-status =live}}</ref> From 28 May to 7 June, he competed in the [[Norway Chess#2018|sixth edition]] of Norway Chess, placing second with 4½/8 (+2−1=5), half a point behind winner Caruana. He defeated Caruana and Aronian in rounds 1 and 3, respectively, but lost to So in round 6.<ref>{{cite web|url =https://www.chess.com/news/view/caruana-wins-altibox-norway-chess-6814|title =Caruana Wins Norway Chess|first =Peter|last =Doggers|publisher =Chess.com|date =7 June 2018|access-date =7 June 2018|archive-date =16 May 2020|archive-url =https://web.archive.org/web/20200516151300/https://www.chess.com/news/view/caruana-wins-altibox-norway-chess-6814|url-status =live}}</ref>
Carlsen participated in the [[Biel Chess Festival|51st Biel Grandmaster tournament]], held from 23 July to 1 August. He finished second on 6/10 (+3−1=6), one-and-a-half points behind the winner Mamedyarov.<ref>{{cite web|url =https://www.chess.com/news/view/carlsen-2nd-behind-mamedyarov-in-biel-as-georgiadis-blunders|title =Carlsen Finishes 2nd Behind Mamedyarov in Biel|first =Peter|last =Doggers|publisher =Chess.com|date =1 August 2018|access-date =10 August 2018|archive-date =27 January 2020|archive-url =https://web.archive.org/web/20200127174802/https://www.chess.com/news/view/carlsen-2nd-behind-mamedyarov-in-biel-as-georgiadis-blunders|url-status =live}}</ref> In August, he competed in the [[Sinquefield Cup#2018|6th Sinquefield Cup]]. He tied for first with Caruana and Aronian on 5½/9 (+2−0=7), and jointly won the tournament after the trio decided to share the title.<ref>{{cite web|url =https://en.chessbase.com/post/sinquefield-cup-2018-round-9|title =Sinquefield Cup: Three winners (one playoff)!|first =Venkatachalam|last =Saravanan|publisher =ChessBase|date =28 August 2018|access-date =28 August 2018|archive-date =27 August 2019|archive-url =https://web.archive.org/web/20190827181428/https://en.chessbase.com/post/sinquefield-cup-2018-round-9|url-status =live}}</ref> Carlsen represented Vålerenga sjakklubb at the 34th European Chess Club Cup in October. He scored 3½/6 (+1−0=5), as his team finished fifth.<ref>{{cite web|url =https://www.chess.com/news/view/european-chess-club-cup-st-petersburg-monaco-take-titles|title =European Chess Club Cup: St Petersburg, Monaco Take Titles|first =Peter|last =Doggers|publisher =Chess.com|date =19 October 2018|access-date =16 November 2018|archive-date =27 January 2020|archive-url =https://web.archive.org/web/20200127174331/https://www.chess.com/news/view/european-chess-club-cup-st-petersburg-monaco-take-titles|url-status =live}}</ref>
From 26 to 30 December, Carlsen played in the 2018 [[World Rapid Championship|World Rapid]] and [[World Blitz Championship|World Blitz]] Chess Championships, held in [[Saint Petersburg]], Russia. He lost three of his first eight games—including both of the first two—to significantly lower-rated opponents in the rapid event. Despite a strong recovery, he was unable to attain a medal, placing fifth with 10½/15 (+9−3=3).<ref>{{cite web|url =https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2018/dec/28/magnus-carlsen-recovers-dreadful-start-world-rapid-chess-championship|title =Magnus Carlsen pays for poor start as Daniil Dubov wins World Rapid Chess|first =Leonard|last =Barden|website =The Guardian|date =28 December 2018|access-date =3 January 2019|archive-date =14 August 2019|archive-url =https://web.archive.org/web/20190814221335/https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2018/dec/28/magnus-carlsen-recovers-dreadful-start-world-rapid-chess-championship|url-status =live}}</ref> He defended his blitz title, going unbeaten to finish clear first on a score of 17/21 (+13−0=8).<ref>{{cite web|url =https://chess24.com/en/read/news/carlsen-lagno-end-2018-as-world-blitz-champions|title =Carlsen & Lagno end 2018 as World Blitz Champions|first =Colin|last =McGourty|website =Chess24|date =31 December 2018|access-date =3 January 2019|archive-date =1 February 2020|archive-url =https://web.archive.org/web/20200201125410/https://chess24.com/en/read/news/carlsen-lagno-end-2018-as-world-blitz-champions|url-status =live}}</ref>
==== World Chess Championship 2018 ==== {{Main|World Chess Championship 2018}}
Carlsen faced [[Fabiano Caruana]] in the 2018 World Chess Championship in London. The 12-game match, organised by [[FIDE]], was played between 9 and 28 November. All 12 classical time control games were drawn. Carlsen retained his title by defeating Caruana 3–0 in rapid tiebreak games. Carlsen cited the first rapid game as "critical", and said he felt "very calm" after winning it.<ref>{{cite web|url =https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2018/nov/28/magnus-carlsen-beats-fabiano-caruana-in-world-chess-championship-tie-breakers|title =Magnus Carlsen beats Caruana in tie-breakers to retain World Chess crown|author =Sean Ingle|work =The Guardian|date =28 November 2018|access-date =28 November 2018|archive-date =8 May 2020|archive-url =https://web.archive.org/web/20200508012153/https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2018/nov/28/magnus-carlsen-beats-fabiano-caruana-in-world-chess-championship-tie-breakers|url-status =live}}</ref>
